We present PlayGen-MoG, an extensible framework for formation-conditioned play generation that addresses these challenges through three design choices: 1/ a Mixture-of-Gaussians (MoG) output head with shared mixture weights across all agents, where a single set of weights selects a play scenario that couples all players' trajectories, 2/ relative spatial attention that encodes pairwise player positions and distances as learned attention biases, and 3/ non-autoregressive prediction of absolute displacements from the initial formation, eliminating cumulative error drift and removing the dependence on observed trajectory history, enabling realistic play generation from a single static formation alone. On American football tracking data, PlayGen-MoG achieves 1.68 yard ADE and 3.98 yard FDE while maintaining full utilization of all 8 mixture components with entropy of 2.06 out of 2.08, and qualitatively confirming diverse generation without mode collapse.

AI.tldr: Proposes PlayGen-MoG, a formation-conditioned, multi-agent trajectory generator for team sports that uses a shared Mixture-of-Gaussians head with relative attention and non-autoregressive displacement prediction to generate diverse plays from a single initial formation. Achieves competitive ADE/FDE and high component utilization, suggesting effective multimodal, coordinated generation without mode collapse.
AI.motivation: Address the need for diverse, realistically coordinated multi-agent trajectories for play design in team sports, while avoiding posterior collapse and the requirement for long observed histories common in CVAE/diffusion models and forecasting regimes.
AI.method: Three design choices: (1) a Mixture-of-Gaussians output head with shared mixture weights across all agents to select a common play scenario that couples all trajectories; (2) relative spatial attention encoding pairwise player positions/distances as learned attention biases; (3) non-autoregressive prediction of absolute displacements from the initial formation, removing drift and history dependence and enabling generation from a single static formation.
AI.result: On American football tracking data, the method achieves 1.68 yard ADE and 3.98 yard FDE, with all 8 mixture components utilized (entropy 2.06/2.08), and qualitative evidence of diverse generation without mode collapse.
AI.conclusion: PlayGen-MoG provides a scalable, formation-conditioned, multimodal trajectory generation framework that can produce diverse, coordinated plays from a single initial formation, potentially applicable beyond football and suitable for play design tasks; future work could explore per-player customization, richer priors, or cross-domain generalization.

title: Homophily-aware Supervised Contrastive Counterfactual Augmented Fair Graph Neural Network
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication at the IEEE Conference on Secure and Trustworthy Machine Learning, 2026
summary: In recent years, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable success in tasks such as node classification, link prediction, and graph representation learning. However, they remain susceptible to biases that can arise not only from node attributes but also from the graph structure itself. Addressing fairness in GNNs has therefore emerged as a critical research challenge. In this work, we propose a novel model for training fairness-aware GNNs by improving the counterfactual augmented fair graph neural network framework (CAF). Specifically, our approach introduces a two-phase training strategy: in the first phase, we edit the graph to increase homophily ratio with respect to class labels while reducing homophily ratio with respect to sensitive attribute labels; in the second phase, we integrate a modified supervised contrastive loss and environmental loss into the optimization process, enabling the model to jointly improve predictive performance and fairness. Experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate that our model outperforms CAF and several state-of-the-art graph-based learning methods in both classification accuracy and fairness metrics.

AI.tldr: Two-phase fairness-aware GNN training: edit the graph to increase class homophily while reducing sensitive-attribute homophily, then use a modified supervised contrastive loss plus an environmental loss to jointly improve accuracy and fairness, outperforming CAF and baselines on five datasets.
AI.motivation: Fairness in GNNs is compromised by biases from both node attributes and the graph structure; there is a need for methods that decouple sensitive information from predictions while preserving predictive performance.
AI.method: Phase 1: edit the graph to raise class-label homophily and lower sensitive-attribute homophily. Phase 2: optimize with a modified supervised contrastive loss and an environmental loss, integrating both into training to boost accuracy and fairness.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five real-world datasets shows the proposed method outperforms CAF and several state-of-the-art graph-based methods in both classification accuracy and fairness metrics.
AI.conclusion: The two-phase CAF-based approach is effective for improving fairness in GNNs while maintaining performance, demonstrating the utility of graph editing combined with tailored loss objectives; future work could further explore robustness and generalizability across tasks and datasets.

title: A Comprehensive Framework for Long-Term Resiliency Investment Planning under Extreme Weather Uncertainty for Electric Utilities
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, submission to PowerUp 2026 conference
summary: Electric utilities must make massive capital investments in the coming years to respond to explosive growth in demand, aging assets and rising threats from extreme weather. Utilities today already have rigorous frameworks for capital planning, and there are opportunities to extend this capability to solve multi-objective optimization problems in the face of uncertainty. This work presents a four-part framework that 1) incorporates extreme weather as a source of uncertainty, 2) leverages a digital twin of the grid, 3) uses Monte Carlo simulation to capture variability and 4) applies a multi-objective optimization method for finding the optimal investment portfolio. We use this framework to investigate whether grid-aware optimization methods outperform model-free approaches. We find that, in fact, given the computational complexity of model-based metaheuristic optimization methods, the simpler net present value ranking method was able to find more optimal portfolios with only limited knowledge of the grid.

AI.tldr: Four-part framework for capital planning under extreme weather uncertainty using a grid digital twin, Monte Carlo simulation, and multi-objective optimization; finds that simple net present value (NPV) ranking can outperform grid-aware model-based optimization due to computational complexity.
AI.motivation: Utilities must make large, uncertain capital investments amid aging assets and increasing weather-related risks; extend planning frameworks to handle multi-objective optimization under uncertainty.
AI.method: Proposes a four-part framework: (1) treat extreme weather as a source of uncertainty; (2) leverage a digital twin of the grid; (3) use Monte Carlo simulation to capture variability; (4) apply a multi-objective optimization to identify investment portfolios; compare grid-aware optimization with model-free approaches.
AI.result: Model-based grid-aware metaheuristic optimization is computationally intensive; the study finds that a simpler net present value ranking method can identify more optimal investment portfolios with only limited grid knowledge.
AI.conclusion: Given computational constraints, simple model-free approaches (NPV ranking) may outperform complex grid-aware optimization; the framework clarifies trade-offs and suggests leveraging simpler methods or hybrid approaches when appropriate.

title: V2X-QA: A Comprehensive Reasoning Dataset and Benchmark for Multimodal Large Language Models in Autonomous Driving Across Ego, Infrastructure, and Cooperative Views
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential for autonomous driving, yet existing benchmarks remain largely ego-centric and therefore cannot systematically assess model performance in infrastructure-centric and cooperative driving conditions. In this work, we introduce V2X-QA, a real-world dataset and benchmark for evaluating MLLMs across vehicle-side, infrastructure-side, and cooperative viewpoints. V2X-QA is built around a view-decoupled evaluation protocol that enables controlled comparison under vehicle-only, infrastructure-only, and cooperative driving conditions within a unified multiple-choice question answering (MCQA) framework. The benchmark is organized into a twelve-task taxonomy spanning perception, prediction, and reasoning and planning, and is constructed through expert-verified MCQA annotation to enable fine-grained diagnosis of viewpoint-dependent capabilities. Benchmark results across ten representative state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source models show that viewpoint accessibility substantially affects performance, and infrastructure-side reasoning supports meaningful macroscopic traffic understanding. Results also indicate that cooperative reasoning remains challenging since it requires cross-view alignment and evidence integration rather than simply additional visual input. To address these challenges, we introduce V2X-MoE, a benchmark-aligned baseline with explicit view routing and viewpoint-specific LoRA experts. The strong performance of V2X-MoE further suggests that explicit viewpoint specialization is a promising direction for multi-view reasoning in autonomous driving. Overall, V2X-QA provides a foundation for studying multi-perspective reasoning, reliability, and cooperative physical intelligence in connected autonomous driving. The dataset and V2X-MoE resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/junwei0001/V2X-QA.

AI.tldr: Introduces V2X-QA, a real-world, view-decoupled MCQA benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs across vehicle, infrastructure, and cooperative viewpoints in autonomous driving; also proposes V2X-MoE, a viewpoint-aware baseline with explicit routing and LoRA experts.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks are largely ego-centric and fail to systematically assess model performance in infrastructure-centric and cooperative driving scenarios.
AI.method: A view-decoupled evaluation protocol within a unified MCQA framework, organized into a 12-task taxonomy spanning perception, prediction, reasoning, and planning; expert-verified MCQA annotations; evaluation across ten state-of-the-art models; introduction of V2X-MoE with explicit view routing and viewpoint-specific LoRA experts.
AI.result: Viewpoint accessibility markedly influences performance; infrastructure-side reasoning enables macroscopic traffic understanding; cooperative reasoning remains challenging due to the need for cross-view alignment and evidence integration; V2X-MoE achieves strong performance as a viewpoint-specialized baseline.
AI.conclusion: V2X-QA provides a foundation for studying multi-perspective reasoning and reliability in connected autonomous driving; resources (dataset and V2X-MoE) are publicly available for community use.

title: Street-Legal Physical-World Adversarial Rim for License Plates
comment: 20 pages, 8 figures, 5 tables, submitted to Security in Machine Learning Applications 2026
summary: Automatic license plate reader (ALPR) systems are widely deployed to identify and track vehicles. While prior work has demonstrated vulnerabilities in ALPR systems, far less attention has been paid to their legality and physical-world practicality. We investigate whether low-resourced threat actors can engineer a successful adversarial attack against a modern open-source ALPR system. We introduce the Street-legal Physical Adversarial Rim (SPAR), a physically realizable white-box attack against the popular ALPR system fast-alpr. SPAR requires no access to ALPR infrastructure during attack deployment and does not alter or obscure the attacker's license plate. Based on prior legislation and case law, we argue that SPAR is street-legal in the state of Texas. Under optimal conditions, SPAR reduces ALPR accuracy by 60% and achieves an 18% targeted impersonation rate. SPAR can be produced for under $100, and it was implemented entirely by commercial agentic coding assistants. These results highlight practical vulnerabilities in modern ALPR systems under realistic physical-world conditions and suggest new directions for both attack and defense.

AI.tldr: A street-legal, low-cost physical adversarial attack (SPAR) can significantly degrade or impersonate ALPR systems in real-world conditions, revealing practical vulnerabilities and guiding defense needs.
AI.motivation: Assess the legality, feasibility, and practicality of physical adversarial attacks on modern ALPR in real-world settings, and to push for robust defenses while considering legal boundaries.
AI.method: A white-box, physically realizable attack against the open-source ALPR fast-alpr, using a device called SPAR that does not require access to ALPR infrastructure, does not alter the attacker’s plate, and is inexpensive (<$100). Implementation leveraged commercial AI coding tools; no need for infrastructure access during deployment.
AI.result: Under optimal conditions, SPAR reduces ALPR accuracy by 60% and achieves an 18% targeted impersonation rate. The attack is feasible with minimal cost and effort, highlighting vulnerabilities in current ALPR systems.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates tangible, street-legal vulnerabilities in modern ALPR; prompts need for robust defenses in real-world deployments and further exploration of attack-defense dynamics in legally constrained, physical environments.

title: Haiku to Opus in Just 10 bits: LLMs Unlock Massive Compression Gains
summary:
We study the compression of LLM-generated text across lossless and lossy regimes, characterizing a compression-compute frontier where more compression is possible at the cost of more compute. For lossless compression, domain-adapted LoRA adapters can improve LLM-based arithmetic coding by 2x over compression with the base LLM alone. For lossy compression, prompting a model for a succinct rewrite then applying arithmetic coding can achieve compression ratios of approximately 0.03, a 2x improvement over compressing the original response.
  We further introduce Question-Asking compression (QA), an interactive lossy protocol inspired by the game 'Twenty Questions'. A small model iteratively refines its response by asking yes/no questions to a stronger model, transferring exactly one bit per answer. On 8 benchmarks spanning math, science, and code, 10 binary questions recover 23% to 72% of the capability gap between a small and large model on standard benchmarks and 7% to 38% on harder benchmarks, achieving compression ratios of 0.0006 to 0.004. This is over 100x smaller than prior LLM-based compression (Deletang et al., 2024), suggesting that interactive protocols can transfer knowledge far more efficiently than transmitting full responses.

AI.tldr: A study of lossless and lossy compression of LLM-generated text, introducing domain-adapted LoRA for arithmetic coding, prompt-based succinct rewrites, and a novel interactive QA protocol that transfers one bit per answer. Reported gains include ~2x improvement in lossless compression with LoRA, lossy compression to ~3% of original size, and an interactive QA approach achieving compression ratios as low as 0.0006–0.004 while recovering a substantial fraction of the capability gap on benchmarks. Claimed overall efficiency (>100x smaller than prior LLM-based methods) suggests interactive protocols can transfer knowledge far more efficiently than full response transmission.
AI.motivation: Characterize the compression-compute frontier for LLM-generated text across lossless and lossy regimes, and explore whether interactive, information-efficient protocols can meaningfully reduce the cost of transmitting or transferring model-generated content and capabilities.
AI.method: (1) Lossless: apply domain-adapted LoRA adapters to improve LLM-based arithmetic coding. (2) Lossy: prompt the model for a succinct rewrite, then apply arithmetic coding. (3) Question-Asking compression (QA): an interactive protocol where a small model asks yes/no questions to a stronger model, transferring exactly one bit per answer, to refine its output. Evaluation on benchmarks spanning math, science, and code.
AI.result: Lossless: LoRA adaptation yields about 2× improvement over base LLM for arithmetic coding. Lossy: succinct rewrite plus arithmetic coding achieves compression ratios around 0.03. QA protocol: across 8 benchmarks, 10 binary questions recover 23%–72% of the capability gap for standard benchmarks and 7%–38% for harder benchmarks; compression ratios range 0.0006–0.004. Claimed to be over 100× smaller than prior LLM-based compression (Deletang et al., 2024).
AI.conclusion: Interactive, information-efficient protocols can transfer knowledge much more efficiently than transmitting full responses, revealing a practical compression-compute frontier for LLM outputs and highlighting the potential of adaptive, dialogue-like methods for model compression and knowledge transfer.

title: Interpretable Deep Reinforcement Learning for Element-level Bridge Life-cycle Optimization
summary: The new Specifications for the National Bridge Inventory (SNBI), in effect from 2022, emphasize the use of element-level condition states (CS) for risk-based bridge management. Instead of a general component rating, element-level condition data use an array of relative CS quantities (i.e., CS proportions) to represent the condition of a bridge. Although this greatly increases the granularity of bridge condition data, it introduces challenges to set up optimal life-cycle policies due to the expanded state space from one single categorical integer to four-dimensional probability arrays. This study proposes a new interpretable reinforcement learning (RL) approach to seek optimal life-cycle policies based on element-level state representations. Compared to existing RL methods, the proposed algorithm yields life-cycle policies in the form of oblique decision trees with reasonable amounts of nodes and depth, making them directly understandable and auditable by humans and easily implementable into current bridge management systems. To achieve near-optimal policies, the proposed approach introduces three major improvements to existing RL methods: (a) the use of differentiable soft tree models as actor function approximators, (b) a temperature annealing process during training, and (c) regularization paired with pruning rules to limit policy complexity. Collectively, these improvements can yield interpretable life-cycle policies in the form of deterministic oblique decision trees. The benefits and trade-offs from these techniques are demonstrated in both supervised and reinforcement learning settings. The resulting framework is illustrated in a life-cycle optimization problem for steel girder bridges.

AI.tldr: Interpretable RL framework yielding near-optimal, auditable life-cycle policies from element-level bridge condition data, expressed as oblique decision trees.
AI.motivation: SNBI 2022 introduces element-level condition states (CS) for risk-based bridge management; the high-dimensional, four-dimensional CS probability arrays demand interpretable optimization to be actionable in practice.
AI.method: Three innovations: (a) differentiable soft-tree actor models as policy function approximators, (b) temperature annealing during training, (c) regularization with pruning to constrain policy complexity. Trains in supervised and RL settings to produce deterministic oblique decision trees; demonstrated on steel girder life-cycle optimization.
AI.result: Policies are represented as oblique decision trees with manageable complexity, achieving near-optimal lifecycle policies; key trade-offs and benefits shown in both supervised and RL contexts; applied to steel girder bridges.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers interpretable, auditable, and implementable life-cycle policies from high-dimensional element-level data, with potential applicability beyond bridges to other infrastructure assets.

title: Learning Locomotion on Complex Terrain for Quadrupedal Robots with Foot Position Maps and Stability Rewards
summary: Quadrupedal locomotion over complex terrain has been a long-standing research topic in robotics. While recent reinforcement learning-based locomotion methods improve generalizability and foot-placement precision, they rely on implicit inference of foot positions from joint angles, lacking the explicit precision and stability guarantees of optimization-based approaches. To address this, we introduce a foot position map integrated into the heightmap, and a dynamic locomotion-stability reward within an attention-based framework to achieve locomotion on complex terrain. We validate our method extensively on terrains seen during training as well as out-of-domain (OOD) terrains. Our results demonstrate that the proposed method enables precise and stable movement, resulting in improved locomotion success rates on both in-domain and OOD terrains.

AI.tldr: A foot-position-aware heightmap with a dynamic stability reward in an attention-based framework enables precise, stable quadrupedal locomotion on complex and out-of-domain terrains, achieving higher success rates than baseline RL approaches.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of explicit foot-placement precision and stability guarantees in standard RL-based locomotion, by introducing an explicit foot-position map and a stability-focused reward within an attention-based policy.
AI.method: Integrate a foot-position map into the terrain heightmap, use an attention-based policy, and apply a dynamic locomotion-stability reward; train on a spectrum of terrains and evaluate on both seen (in-domain) and out-of-domain terrains.
AI.result: The method yields precise and stable locomotion and improves locomotion success rates on both in-domain and OOD terrains compared to baseline RL approaches.
AI.conclusion: Combining explicit foot-position information with a stability-aware reward in an attention-based framework provides improved robustness and precision, narrowing the gap with optimization-based methods while maintaining RL flexibility for terrain generalization.

title: VERTIGO: Visual Preference Optimization for Cinematic Camera Trajectory Generation
summary: Cinematic camera control relies on a tight feedback loop between director and cinematographer, where camera motion and framing are continuously reviewed and refined. Recent generative camera systems can produce diverse, text-conditioned trajectories, but they lack this "director in the loop" and have no explicit supervision of whether a shot is visually desirable. This results in in-distribution camera motion but poor framing, off-screen characters, and undesirable visual aesthetics. In this paper, we introduce VERTIGO, the first framework for visual preference optimization of camera trajectory generators. Our framework leverages a real-time graphics engine (Unity) to render 2D visual previews from generated camera motion. A cinematically fine-tuned vision-language model then scores these previews using our proposed cyclic semantic similarity mechanism, which aligns renders with text prompts. This process provides the visual preference signals for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) post-training. Both quantitative evaluations and user studies on Unity renders and diffusion-based Camera-to-Video pipelines show consistent gains in condition adherence, framing quality, and perceptual realism. Notably, VERTIGO reduces the character off-screen rate from 38% to nearly 0% while preserving the geometric fidelity of camera motion. User study participants further prefer VERTIGO over baselines across composition, consistency, prompt adherence, and aesthetic quality, confirming the perceptual benefits of our visual preference post-training.

AI.tldr: VERTIGO introduces visual preference optimization for camera trajectory generators by rendering Unity previews and scoring with a fine-tuned vision-language model using cyclic semantic similarity, enabling DPO post-training to improve framing, composition, and avoid off-screen characters.
AI.motivation: Cinematic camera control requires director-in-loop supervision; generative camera systems lack explicit supervision of shot desirability, leading to poor framing and aesthetics.
AI.method: Render 2D previews in Unity from generated camera motion; score via cinematically fine-tuned vision-language model with cyclic semantic similarity to align renders with text prompts; apply visual preference signals in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) post-training.
AI.result: Quantitative and user studies show gains in condition adherence, framing quality, and perceptual realism; character off-screen rate drops from 38% to near 0% while preserving motion fidelity; users prefer VERTIGO across metrics.
AI.conclusion: Visual preference post-training yields perceptual benefits and better alignment with cinematic goals, validating the approach.

title: Characterizing WebGPU Dispatch Overhead for LLM Inference Across Four GPU Vendors, Three Backends, and Three Browsers
summary: WebGPU's security-focused design imposes per-operation validation that compounds across the many small dispatches in neural network inference, yet the true cost of this overhead is poorly characterized. We present a systematic characterization of WebGPU dispatch overhead for LLM inference at batch size 1, spanning four GPU vendors (NVIDIA, AMD, Apple, Intel), two native implementations (Dawn, wgpu-native) and three browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox), and two model sizes (Qwen2.5-0.5B and 1.5B). Our primary contribution is a sequential-dispatch methodology that reveals naive single-operation benchmarks overestimate dispatch cost by ${\sim}20\times$. The true per-dispatch cost of WebGPU API overhead alone is 24-36 $μ$s on Vulkan and 32-71 $μ$s on Metal, while the total per-operation overhead including Python cost is ${\sim}95$~$μ$s, which turns out to be a distinction critical for optimization. On Vulkan, kernel fusion improves throughput by 53%, while CUDA fusion provides no benefit, confirming that per-operation overhead is a primary differentiator. LLM inference was tested across three major operating systems (Linux, Windows, macOS). We built $\texttt{torch-webgpu}$, a PrivateUse1-based out-of-tree PyTorch backend and an FX-to-WebGPU compiler, which on our reference platform achieves 11--12% of CUDA performance. At dtype-matched float32, RTX PRO 2000 achieves 1.4$\times$ WebGPU's throughput despite ${\sim}6\times$ less compute than RTX 5090. For dispatch overhead, backend choice is the dominant factor, although implementation choice also matters substantially within a backend (2.2$\times$ for Metal). In terms of dispatch vs kernel compute efficiency, we conclude that at batch=1 with the current dispatch-heavy pipeline, per-operation overhead dominates regardless of kernel quality. All code, benchmarks, and raw data are open source.

AI.tldr: Systematic characterization of WebGPU dispatch overhead for LLM inference across vendors, runtimes, and OS, revealing per-operation overhead dominates at batch size 1 and naive benchmarks significantly underestimate cost; introduces a sequential-dispatch methodology, shows Vulkan kernel fusion helps while CUDA fusion does not, and provides an open-source PyTorch backend and compiler with performance around 11–12% of CUDA on a reference platform.
AI.motivation: Quantify real-world API and Python overheads in WebGPU-based LLM inference to guide backend design, optimization strategies, and cost-aware deployment across hardware and software stacks.
AI.method: Sequential-dispatch benchmarking of LLMs at batch size 1 across four GPUs (NVIDIA, AMD, Apple, Intel), two native backends (Dawn, wgpu-native), three browsers (Chrome, Safari, Firefox), two model sizes (Qwen2.5-0.5B and 1.5B), across Linux/Windows/macOS. Measure per-dispatch API overhead, Python overhead, and kernel compute, perform kernel fusion experiments, build a PrivateUse1-based out-of-tree PyTorch backend (torch-webgpu) and an FX-to-WebGPU compiler, and compare performance to CUDA on RTX-class GPUs.
AI.result: Naive single-operation benchmarks overestimate dispatch cost by ~20x. API overhead per dispatch: 24–36 μs (Vulkan) and 32–71 μs (Metal); total per-operation overhead including Python: ~95 μs. Vulkan kernel fusion increases throughput by 53%; CUDA fusion yields no benefit. Across OSes, backend choice dominates dispatch overhead (2.2× variation within Metal). With batch=1, per-operation overhead dominates over kernel compute. torch-webgpu achieves ~11–12% of CUDA performance on the reference platform; RTX PRO 2000 attains 1.4× WebGPU throughput with ~6× less compute than RTX 5090. All code, benchmarks, and data are open source.
AI.conclusion: Per-operation dispatch overhead is the primary bottleneck for batch-1 LLM inference on WebGPU; optimization should target reducing per-dispatch cost and backend efficiency. Kernel fusion offers substantial gains on Vulkan but not on CUDA-equivalent paths. The work provides a reusable methodology, demonstrates cross-vendor variability, and supplies open-source tooling that enables reproducibility and further improvements.

title: Competency Questions as Executable Plans: a Controlled RAG Architecture for Cultural Heritage Storytelling
comment: Accepted at the 23rd European Semantic Web Conference (ESWC 2026)
summary: The preservation of intangible cultural heritage is a critical challenge as collective memory fades over time. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising avenue for generating engaging narratives, their propensity for factual inaccuracies or "hallucinations" makes them unreliable for heritage applications where veracity is a central requirement. To address this, we propose a novel neuro-symbolic architecture grounded in Knowledge Graphs (KGs) that establishes a transparent "plan-retrieve-generate" workflow for story generation. A key novelty of our approach is the repurposing of competency questions (CQs) - traditionally design-time validation artifacts - into run-time executable narrative plans. This approach bridges the gap between high-level user personas and atomic knowledge retrieval, ensuring that generation is evidence-closed and fully auditable. We validate this architecture using a new resource: the Live Aid KG, a multimodal dataset aligning 1985 concert data with the Music Meta Ontology and linking to external multimedia assets. We present a systematic comparative evaluation of three distinct Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) strategies over this graph: a purely symbolic KG-RAG, a text-enriched Hybrid-RAG, and a structure-aware Graph-RAG. Our experiments reveal a quantifiable trade-off between the factual precision of symbolic retrieval, the contextual richness of hybrid methods, and the narrative coherence of graph-based traversal. Our findings offer actionable insights for designing personalised and controllable storytelling systems.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic, KG-grounded RAG framework for authentic storytelling about intangible heritage, repurposing competency questions for run-time planning, evaluated on Live Aid KG across three RAG strategies, revealing trade-offs between factual precision, contextual richness, and narrative coherence.
AI.motivation: Intangible cultural heritage stories require factual accuracy; LLM hallucinations undermine credibility. A transparent, auditable generation pipeline is needed that links user personas to knowledge retrieval via executable plans derived from competency questions.
AI.method: Develop a plan-retrieve-generate architecture that reuses competency questions as run-time narrative plans; integrate Live Aid KG with Music Meta Ontology; compare three Retrieval-Augmented Generation strategies: symbolic KG-RAG, text-enriched Hybrid-RAG, and structure-aware Graph-RAG; perform systematic evaluation focusing on factual precision, contextual richness, and narrative coherence.
AI.result: Demonstrated trade-offs among approaches: symbolic retrieval yields higher factual precision; Hybrid-RAG enhances contextual richness; Graph-RAG improves narrative coherence; the framework yields evidence-closed, auditable generation and actionable guidance for personalised storytelling in heritage contexts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed KG-grounded, plan-retrieve-generate architecture enables transparent, controllable storytelling for intangible heritage; Live Aid KG validation confirms feasibility; provides design guidance for selecting RAG configurations based on desired balance of precision, richness, and coherence.

title: Geometrically-Constrained Radar-Inertial Odometry via Continuous Point-Pose Uncertainty Modeling
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables, accepted to RA-L
summary: Radar odometry is crucial for robust localization in challenging environments; however, the sparsity of reliable returns and distinctive noise characteristics impede its performance. This paper introduces geometrically-constrained radar-inertial odometry and mapping that jointly consolidates point and pose uncertainty. We employ the continuous trajectory model to estimate the pose uncertainty at any arbitrary timestamp by propagating uncertainties of the control points. These pose uncertainties are continuously integrated with heteroscedastic measurement uncertainty during point projection, thereby enabling dynamic evaluation of observation confidence and adaptive down-weighting of uninformative radar points. By leveraging quantified uncertainties in radar mapping, we construct a high-fidelity map that improves odometry accuracy under imprecise radar measurements. Moreover, we reveal the effectiveness of explicit geometrical constraints in radar-inertial odometry when incorporated with the proposed uncertainty-aware mapping framework. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method, yielding substantial performance improvements in both accuracy and efficiency compared to existing baselines.

AI.tldr: A geometrically constrained radar-inertial odometry and mapping framework that propagates pose and measurement uncertainties to adaptively weight radar observations, yielding higher-fidelity maps and better odometry.
AI.motivation: Radar-based odometry is hampered by sparse, noisy returns; explicit propagation and fusion of pose and observation uncertainties are needed for robust localization and mapping.
AI.method: Use a continuous trajectory model to propagate pose uncertainty at arbitrary timestamps by tracking control-point uncertainties; integrate pose uncertainty with heteroscedastic measurement uncertainty during point projection to assess observation confidence and down-weight uninformative radar points; perform uncertainty-aware mapping with explicit geometrical constraints.
AI.result: Extensive real-world experiments show substantial improvements in both odometry accuracy and efficiency compared with existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicit geometrical constraints combined with uncertainty-aware mapping enhance radar-inertial odometry and mapping robustness and performance.

title: Hierarchical, Interpretable, Label-Free Concept Bottleneck Model
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) introduce interpretability to black-box deep learning models by predicting labels through human-understandable concepts. However, unlike humans, who identify objects at different levels of abstraction using both general and specific features, existing CBMs operate at a single semantic level in both concept and label space. We propose HIL-CBM, a Hierarchical Interpretable Label-Free Concept Bottleneck Model that extends CBMs into a hierarchical framework to enhance interpretability by more closely mirroring the human cognitive process. HIL-CBM enables classification and explanation across multiple semantic levels without requiring relational concept annotations. HIL-CBM aligns the abstraction level of concept-based explanations with that of model predictions, progressing from abstract to concrete. This is achieved by (i) introducing a gradient-based visual consistency loss that encourages abstraction layers to focus on similar spatial regions, and (ii) training dual classification heads, each operating on feature concepts at different abstraction levels. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that HIL-CBM outperforms state-of-the-art sparse CBMs in classification accuracy. Human evaluations further show that HIL-CBM provides more interpretable and accurate explanations, while maintaining a hierarchical and label-free approach to feature concepts.

AI.tldr: HIL-CBM extends CBMs with a hierarchical, label-free concept bottleneck framework, using gradient-based visual consistency and dual-level classifiers to yield multi-level explanations, improving accuracy and interpretability without relation annotations.
AI.motivation: To overcome single-level concepts in traditional CBMs and to align the abstraction level of explanations with model predictions in a hierarchical, data-efficient, label-free manner.
AI.method: Propose HIL-CBM, a Hierarchical Interpretable Label-Free CBM. It introduces a gradient-based visual consistency loss to align abstraction layers on similar spatial regions and trains two classification heads operating on concepts at different abstraction levels. It requires no relational concept annotations and demonstrates performance gains over sparse CBMs.
AI.result: HIL-CBM outperforms state-of-the-art sparse CBMs in classification accuracy on benchmark datasets. Human evaluations indicate more interpretable and accurate explanations, with the model maintaining a hierarchical, label-free concept structure.
AI.conclusion: HIL-CBM advances interpretable AI by combining hierarchical abstractions with label-free concept bottlenecks. It better matches human cognitive processing of objects at multiple levels and provides improved classification performance alongside more faithful explanations.

title: UI-Oceanus: Scaling GUI Agents with Synthetic Environmental Dynamics
summary: Scaling generalist GUI agents is hindered by the data scalability bottleneck of expensive human demonstrations and the "distillation ceiling" of synthetic teacher supervision. To transcend these limitations, we propose UI-Oceanus, a framework that shifts the learning focus from mimicking high-level trajectories to mastering interaction physics via ground-truth environmental feedback. Through a systematic investigation of self-supervised objectives, we identify that forward dynamics, defined as the generative prediction of future interface states, acts as the primary driver for scalability and significantly outweighs inverse inference. UI-Oceanus leverages this insight by converting low-cost autonomous exploration, which is verified directly by system execution, into high-density generative supervision to construct a robust internal world model. Experimental evaluations across a series of models demonstrate the decisive superiority of our approach: models utilizing Continual Pre-Training (CPT) on synthetic dynamics outperform non-CPT baselines with an average success rate improvement of 7% on offline benchmarks, which amplifies to a 16.8% gain in real-world online navigation. Furthermore, we observe that navigation performance scales with synthetic data volume. These results confirm that grounding agents in forward predictive modeling offers a superior pathway to scalable GUI automation with robust cross-domain adaptability and compositional generalization.

AI.tldr: UI-Oceanus leverages forward dynamics self-supervision to scale generalist GUI agents, outperforming baselines and enabling stronger cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Address the data scalability bottleneck from costly human demonstrations and the distillation ceiling of synthetic supervision by shifting learning toward ground-truth environmental feedback and forward-predictive modeling of interface states.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of self-supervised objectives identifies forward dynamics (predicting future interface states) as the primary driver. The approach uses autonomous exploration converted into high-density generative supervision to build a robust internal world model, with Continual Pre-Training (CPT) on synthetic dynamics.
AI.result: CPT on synthetic dynamics yields average offline benchmark improvements of 7% in success rate and 16.8% in real-world online navigation; navigation performance scales with synthetic data volume, demonstrating robustness and scalability.
AI.conclusion: Grounding GUI agents in forward predictive modeling offers a scalable pathway to generalist GUI automation with strong cross-domain adaptability and compositional generalization.

title: Mitigating LLM biases toward spurious social contexts using direct preference optimization
summary: LLMs are increasingly used for high-stakes decision-making, yet their sensitivity to spurious contextual information can introduce harmful biases. This is a critical concern when models are deployed for tasks like evaluating teachers' instructional quality, where biased assessment can affect teachers' professional development and career trajectories. We investigate model robustness to spurious social contexts using the largest publicly available dataset of U.S. classroom transcripts (NCTE) paired with expert rubric scores. Evaluating seven frontier and open-weight models across seven categories of spurious contexts -- including teacher experience, education level, demographic identity, and sycophancy-inducing framings -- we find that irrelevant contextual information can shift model predictions by up to 1.48 points on a 7-point scale, with larger models sometimes exhibiting greater sensitivity despite higher predictive accuracy. Mitigations using prompts and standard direct preference optimization (DPO) prove largely insufficient. We propose **Debiasing-DPO**,, a self-supervised training method that pairs neutral reasoning generated from the query alone, with the model's biased reasoning generated with both the query and additional spurious context. We further combine this objective with supervised fine-tuning on ground-truth labels to prevent losses in predictive accuracy. Applied to Llama 3B \& 8B and Qwen 3B \& 7B Instruct models, Debiasing-DPO reduces bias by 84\% and improves predictive accuracy by 52\% on average. Our findings from the educational case study highlight that robustness to spurious context is not a natural byproduct of model scaling and that our proposed method can yield substantial gains in both accuracy and robustness for prompt-based prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit susceptibility to spurious social context when predicting rubric-aligned scores for teacher evaluations; a debiasing training (Debiasing-DPO) combining neutral query-only reasoning with biased reasoning plus supervised fine-tuning markedly improves robustness and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Ensure safe deployment of LLMs in high-stakes educational assessment by assessing sensitivity to contextual biases and developing methods to reduce bias without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Evaluate seven frontier/open-weight models on seven spurious-context categories using the NCTE classroom transcripts with expert rubric scores; quantify prediction shifts; test prompt-based mitigations and direct preference optimization (DPO); introduce Debiasing-DPO (self-supervised objective pairing neutral reasoning from the query with biased reasoning from the query plus spurious context) plus supervised fine-tuning; apply to Llama 3B/8B and Qwen 3B/7B Instruct.
AI.result: Irrelevant contextual information can shift predictions by up to 1.48 on a 7-point scale; larger models can be more sensitive; prompts and standard DPO only modestly mitigate; Debiasing-DPO reduces bias by 84% and improves predictive accuracy by 52% on average across evaluated models.
AI.conclusion: Robustness to spurious context does not scale automatically with model size; Debiasing-DPO provides substantial gains in both fairness (bias reduction) and accuracy for prompt-based prediction in education, suggesting broader applicability to high-stakes NLP tasks.

title: OMNI-PoseX: A Fast Vision Model for 6D Object Pose Estimation in Embodied Tasks
summary: Accurate 6D object pose estimation is a fundamental capability for embodied agents, yet remains highly challenging in open-world environments. Many existing methods often rely on closed-set assumptions or geometry-agnostic regression schemes, limiting their generalization, stability, and real-time applicability in robotic systems. We present OMNI-PoseX, a vision foundation model that introduces a novel network architecture unifying open-vocabulary perception with an SO(3)-aware reflected flow matching pose predictor. The architecture decouples object-level understanding from geometry-consistent rotation inference, and employs a lightweight multi-modal fusion strategy that conditions rotation-sensitive geometric features on compact semantic embeddings, enabling efficient and stable 6D pose estimation. To enhance robustness and generalization, the model is trained on large-scale 6D pose datasets, leveraging broad object diversity, viewpoint variation, and scene complexity to build a scalable open-world pose backbone. Comprehensive evaluations across benchmark pose estimation, ablation studies, zero-shot generalization, and system-level robotic grasping integration demonstrate the effectiveness of OMNI-PoseX. The OMNI-PoseX achieves SOTA pose accuracy and real-time efficiency, while delivering geometrically consistent predictions that enable reliable grasping of diverse, previously unseen objects.

AI.tldr: OMNI-PoseX presents a foundation-model style approach for open-world 6D pose estimation combining open-vocabulary perception with an SO(3)-aware rotated flow predictor, decoupling object understanding from rotation inference for stable, real-time 6D pose estimation and robust grasping of unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Address open-world 6D pose estimation challenges: generalization to unseen objects, stability of rotation inference, and real-time applicability in robotic systems; close-set and geometry-agnostic methods hinder open-world deployment.
AI.method: Architecture unifies open-vocabulary perception with an SO(3)-aware reflected flow matching predictor; decouples object-level semantics from geometry-based rotation inference; uses lightweight multi-modal fusion that conditions rotation-sensitive geometric features on compact semantic embeddings; trained on large-scale 6D pose datasets to enhance diversity and robustness.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) pose accuracy and real-time efficiency; demonstrates geometrically consistent predictions; proves effectiveness via benchmark pose estimation, ablations, zero-shot generalization, and system-level robotic grasping integration.
AI.conclusion: OMNI-PoseX provides a scalable open-world 6D pose backbone with robust, real-time performance and geometrically consistent predictions that enable reliable grasping of diverse unseen objects, indicating strong potential for open-vocabulary, geometry-aware pose estimation.

title: Guideline2Graph: Profile-Aware Multimodal Parsing for Executable Clinical Decision Graphs
summary: Clinical practice guidelines are long, multimodal documents whose branching recommendations are difficult to convert into executable clinical decision support (CDS), and one-shot parsing often breaks cross-page continuity. Recent LLM/VLM extractors are mostly local or text-centric, under-specifying section interfaces and failing to consolidate cross-page control flow across full documents into one coherent decision graph. We present a decomposition-first pipeline that converts full-guideline evidence into an executable clinical decision graph through topology-aware chunking, interface-constrained chunk graph generation, and provenance-preserving global aggregation. Rather than relying on single-pass generation, the pipeline uses explicit entry/terminal interfaces and semantic deduplication to preserve cross-page continuity while keeping the induced control flow auditable and structurally consistent. We evaluate on an adjudicated prostate-guideline benchmark with matched inputs and the same underlying VLM backbone across compared methods. On the complete merged graph, our approach improves edge and triplet precision/recall from $19.6\%/16.1\%$ in existing models to $69.0\%/87.5\%$, while node recall rises from $78.1\%$ to $93.8\%$. These results support decomposition-first, auditable guideline-to-CDS conversion on this benchmark, while current evidence remains limited to one adjudicated prostate guideline and motivates broader multi-guideline validation.

AI.tldr: Decomposition-first pipeline converts full-guideline evidence into an executable, auditable clinical decision graph via topology-aware chunking and interface-constrained graph generation, improving cross-page continuity and edge/node accuracy over prior one-shot methods on a prostate-guideline benchmark.
AI.motivation: Clinical practice guidelines are long, multimodal, and yield branching recommendations that are hard to convert into executable CDS while maintaining cross-page continuity and auditable control flow.
AI.method: A decomposition-first pipeline with (1) topology-aware chunking of guideline content, (2) interface-constrained chunk graph generation, and (3) provenance-preserving global aggregation to produce a single executable decision graph. Explicit entry/terminal interfaces and semantic deduplication maintain cross-page continuity and auditable control flow rather than relying on single-pass generation.
AI.result: On a adjudicated prostate-guideline benchmark using the same VLM backbone, edge/triplet precision and recall improved from 19.6%/16.1% to 69.0%/87.5%; node recall increased from 78.1% to 93.8% on the complete merged graph.
AI.conclusion: Decomposition-first, auditable guideline-to-CDS conversion yields substantial accuracy gains and cross-page coherence compared with existing models, but broader multi-guideline validation is needed beyond the current adjudicated prostate guideline.

title: DrugPlayGround: Benchmarking Large Language Models and Embeddings for Drug Discovery
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are in the ascendancy for research in drug discovery, offering unprecedented opportunities to reshape drug research by accelerating hypothesis generation, optimizing candidate prioritization, and enabling more scalable and cost-effective drug discovery pipelines. However there is currently a lack of objective assessments of LLM performance to ascertain their advantages and limitations over traditional drug discovery platforms. To tackle this emergent problem, we have developed DrugPlayGround, a framework to evaluate and benchmark LLM performance for generating meaningful text-based descriptions of physiochemical drug characteristics, drug synergism, drug-protein interactions, and the physiological response to perturbations introduced by drug molecules. Moreover, DrugPlayGround is designed to work with domain experts to provide detailed explanations for justifying the predictions of LLMs, thereby testing LLMs for chemical and biological reasoning capabilities to push their greater use at the frontier of drug discovery at all of its stages.

AI.tldr: Introduction of DrugPlayGround, a framework to benchmark LLMs in drug discovery tasks and to collect expert-annotated explanations to test domain reasoning.
AI.motivation: Addresses absence of objective, domain-specific evaluation of LLMs vs traditional drug discovery, and the need to assess chemical/biological reasoning in LLMs across stages of discovery.
AI.method: Develops DrugPlayGround framework for evaluating LLM performance on generating text-based descriptions of physicochemical drug characteristics, drug synergism, drug-protein interactions, and physiological responses to perturbations; integrates collaboration with domain experts to provide explanations supporting predictions; targeted to multiple stages of drug discovery.
AI.result: A framework that enables objective benchmarking and explanation-based evaluation of LLMs in drug discovery tasks; not reporting empirical results in abstract. Provides a path to evaluate LLMs and their reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: DrugPlayGround has the potential to push broader adoption of LLMs in drug discovery by enabling objective assessment and domain-grounded explanations, facilitating rigorous evaluation of chemical and biological reasoning.

title: Do Audio-Visual Large Language Models Really See and Hear?
summary: Audio-Visual Large Language Models (AVLLMs) are emerging as unified interfaces to multimodal perception. We present the first mechanistic interpretability study of AVLLMs, analyzing how audio and visual features evolve and fuse through different layers of an AVLLM to produce the final text outputs. We find that although AVLLMs encode rich audio semantics at intermediate layers, these capabilities largely fail to surface in the final text generation when audio conflicts with vision. Probing analyses show that useful latent audio information is present, but deeper fusion layers disproportionately privilege visual representations that tend to suppress audio cues. We further trace this imbalance to training: the AVLLM's audio behavior strongly matches its vision-language base model, indicating limited additional alignment to audio supervision. Our findings reveal a fundamental modality bias in AVLLMs and provide new mechanistic insights into how multimodal LLMs integrate audio and vision.

AI.tldr: AVLLMs encode rich audio semantics in intermediate layers but final text outputs are dominated by vision when audio and vision conflict, indicating a modality bias. Training aligns audio behavior with the vision-language base model, suggesting limited audio-specific alignment.
AI.motivation: To perform a mechanistic interpretability study of AVLLMs to understand how audio and visual features evolve and fuse across layers and why audio cues may not surface in final outputs.
AI.method: Layer-wise mechanistic interpretability analyses across the network, probing to track audio/vision feature evolution and fusion, examining how conflicting cues are resolved, and tracing training-induced biases by comparing to the base vision-language model.
AI.result: Audio semantics are present at intermediate layers; final text generation often ignores audio cues when they conflict with vision; deeper fusion layers privilege visual representations, suppressing audio cues; latent audio information is detectable via probing but not utilized in final outputs; audio behavior resembles the base vision-language model, indicating limited extra alignment to audio supervision.
AI.conclusion: There exists a fundamental modality bias in AVLLMs, with incomplete integration of audio signals into final outputs. The findings provide mechanistic insights into multimodal fusion and suggest the need for stronger audio-centric alignment or architectural adjustments to balance modalities.

title: Vision-Based End-to-End Learning for UAV Traversal of Irregular Gaps via Differentiable Simulation
summary: -Navigation through narrow and irregular gaps is an essential skill in autonomous drones for applications such as inspection, search-and-rescue, and disaster response. However, traditional planning and control methods rely on explicit gap extraction and measurement, while recent end-to-end approaches often assume regularly shaped gaps, leading to poor generalization and limited practicality. In this work, we present a fully vision-based, end-to-end framework that maps depth images directly to control commands, enabling drones to traverse complex gaps within unseen environments. Operating in the Special Euclidean group SE(3), where position and orientation are tightly coupled, the framework leverages differentiable simulation, a Stop-Gradient operator, and a Bimodal Initialization Distribution to achieve stable traversal through consecutive gaps. Two auxiliary prediction modules-a gap-crossing success classifier and a traversability predictor-further enhance continuous navigation and safety. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate the approach's effectiveness, generalization capability, and practical robustness.

AI.tldr: A fully vision-based, end-to-end framework maps depth images to SE(3) control commands enabling drones to traverse complex, unseen gaps without explicit gap extraction.
AI.motivation: Autonomous gap navigation in cramped, irregular environments is essential for inspection, search-and-rescue, and disaster response; existing methods struggle to generalize to unseen gaps or rely on explicit gap extraction/regular shapes.
AI.method: The approach operates in SE(3) and uses depth-to-control mapping via differentiable simulation, a Stop-Gradient operator, and a Bimodal Initialization Distribution; includes two auxiliary predictors—gap-crossing success classifier and traversability predictor—to improve continuous navigation.
AI.result: Through extensive simulation and real-world experiments, the method demonstrates effective traversal through successive gaps, strong generalization to unseen environments, and practical robustness.
AI.conclusion: A vision-based, end-to-end SE(3) framework with differentiable simulation and auxiliary modules enables robust, generalizable gap traversal for drones in unknown environments.

title: Generating Satellite Imagery Data for Wildfire Detection through Mask-Conditioned Generative AI
summary:
The scarcity of labeled satellite imagery remains a fundamental bottleneck for deep-learning (DL)-based wildfire monitoring systems. This paper investigates whether a diffusion-based foundation model for Earth Observation (EO), EarthSynth, can synthesize realistic post-wildfire Sentinel-2 RGB imagery conditioned on existing burn masks, without task-specific retraining.
  Using burn masks derived from the CalFireSeg-50 dataset (Martin et al., 2025), we design and evaluate six controlled experimental configurations that systematically vary: (i) pipeline architecture (mask-only full generation vs. inpainting with pre-fire context), (ii) prompt engineering strategy (three hand-crafted prompts and a VLM-generated prompt via Qwen2-VL), and (iii) a region-wise color-matching post-processing step.
  Quantitative assessment on 10 stratified test samples uses four complementary metrics: Burn IoU, burn-region color distance (ΔC_burn), Darkness Contrast, and Spectral Plausibility. Results show that inpainting-based pipelines consistently outperform full-tile generation across all metrics, with the structured inpainting prompt achieving the best spatial alignment (Burn IoU = 0.456) and burn saliency (Darkness Contrast = 20.44), while color matching produces the lowest color distance (ΔC_burn = 63.22) at the cost of reduced burn saliency.
  VLM-assisted inpainting is competitive with hand-crafted prompts. These findings provide a foundation for incorporating generative data augmentation into wildfire detection pipelines.
  Code and experiments are available at: https://www.kaggle.com/code/valeriamartinh/genai-all-runned

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based Earth Observation foundation model EarthSynth is evaluated for synthesizing post-wildfire Sentinel-2 RGB imagery conditioned on burn masks without retraining. Six experimental configurations compare mask-only generation vs inpainting with pre-fire context, three handcrafted prompts plus a VLM-generated prompt, and a region-wise color-matching post-processing step. Inpainting outperforms full-tile generation; structured inpainting prompts yield best Burn IoU (0.456) and Darkness Contrast (20.44); color matching yields lowest color distance (ΔC_burn = 63.22) but lowers burn saliency. VLM-assisted inpainting is competitive with handcrafted prompts. Code available at Kaggle."
AI.motivation: The study addresses the bottleneck of labeled satellite imagery for deep learning in wildfire monitoring by testing whether a diffusion-based EO foundation model can synthesize realistic post-wildfire imagery conditioned on existing burn masks without requiring task-specific retraining, enabling data augmentation for downstream wildfire detection pipelines.
AI.method: Six configurations explore: (i) pipeline architecture (mask-only full generation vs inpainting with pre-fire context), (ii) prompt strategy (three handcrafted prompts + a VLM-generated prompt via Qwen2-VL), and (iii) a region-wise color-matching post-processing step. The burn masks come from CalFireSeg-50. Evaluation on 10 stratified test samples using Burn IoU, ΔC_burn, Darkness Contrast, and Spectral Plausibility. Code and experiments supplied at Kaggle.
AI.result: Inpainting-based pipelines consistently outperform full-tile generation across all metrics. The structured inpainting prompt achieves the best spatial alignment and burn saliency (Burn IoU = 0.456; Darkness Contrast = 20.44). Color matching yields the lowest color distance (ΔC_burn = 63.22) but at the cost of reduced saliency. VLM-assisted inpainting is competitive with handcrafted prompts.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a viable, non-retraining data-augmentation pathway for wildfire detection pipelines using diffusion-based EO synthesis conditioned on burn masks, laying groundwork for integrating generative data augmentation in wildfire monitoring. Code is publicly available.

title: FTimeXer: Frequency-aware Time-series Transformer with Exogenous variables for Robust Carbon Footprint Forecasting
comment: Accepted by The 5th International Conference on Electronics Technology and Artificial Intelligence (ETAI 2026)
summary: Accurate and up-to-date forecasting of the power grid's carbon footprint is crucial for effective product carbon footprint (PCF) accounting and informed decarbonization decisions. However, the carbon intensity of the grid exhibits high non-stationarity, and existing methods often struggle to effectively leverage periodic and oscillatory patterns. Furthermore, these methods tend to perform poorly when confronted with irregular exogenous inputs, such as missing data or misalignment. To tackle these challenges, we propose FTimeXer, a frequency-aware time-series Transformer designed with a robust training scheme that accommodates exogenous factors. FTimeXer features an Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)-driven frequency branch combined with gated time-frequency fusion, allowing it to capture multi-scale periodicity effectively. It also employs stochastic exogenous masking in conjunction with consistency regularization, which helps reduce spurious correlations and enhance stability. Experiments conducted on three real-world datasets show consistent improvements over strong baselines. As a result, these enhancements lead to more reliable forecasts of grid carbon factors, which are essential for effective PCF accounting and informed decision-making regarding decarbonization.

AI.tldr: A frequency-aware time-series Transformer (FTimeXer) improves grid carbon factor forecasting by integrating FFT-based frequency features and robust exogenous handling, yielding consistent gains on three real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Forecasting grid carbon intensity is challenging due to non-stationarity, periodicity, and irregular exogenous inputs; accurate PCF accounting requires reliable forecasts.
AI.method: FTimeXer architecture: FFT-driven frequency branch; gated time-frequency fusion; robust training using stochastic exogenous masking and consistency regularization to mitigate spurious correlations and handle missing/misaligned exogenous data.
AI.result: Three real-world datasets show consistent improvements over strong baselines, indicating better reliability for grid carbon factor forecasts.
AI.conclusion: Enhancements enable more reliable PCF accounting and informed decarbonization decisions by capturing multi-scale periodicity and robustly handling exogenous factors.

title: AutoVerifier: An Agentic Automated Verification Framework Using Large Language Models
comment: Winner of 2025-2026 Radiance Technologies Innovation Bowl
summary: Scientific and Technical Intelligence (S&TI) analysis requires verifying complex technical claims across rapidly growing literature, where existing approaches fail to bridge the verification gap between surface-level accuracy and deeper methodological validity. We present AutoVerifier, an LLM-based agentic framework that automates end-to-end verification of technical claims without requiring domain expertise. AutoVerifier decomposes every technical assertion into structured claim triples of the form (Subject, Predicate, Object), constructing knowledge graphs that enable structured reasoning across six progressively enriching layers: corpus construction and ingestion, entity and claim extraction, intra-document verification, cross-source verification, external signal corroboration, and final hypothesis matrix generation. We demonstrate AutoVerifier on a contested quantum computing claim, where the framework, operated by analysts with no quantum expertise, automatically identified overclaims and metric inconsistencies within the target paper, traced cross-source contradictions, uncovered undisclosed commercial conflicts of interest, and produced a final assessment. These results show that structured LLM verification can reliably evaluate the validity and maturity of emerging technologies, turning raw technical documents into traceable, evidence-backed intelligence assessments.

AI.tldr: AutoVerifier is an LLM-based agentic framework that converts technical claims into subject-predicate-object triples, builds knowledge graphs, and uses a six-layer verification pipeline for end-to-end, evidence-backed evaluation. Demonstrated on a contested quantum-computing claim by non-experts, identifying overclaims, inconsistencies, cross-source contradictions, and undisclosed conflicts of interest.
AI.motivation: To close the verification gap in Scientific and Technical Intelligence (S&TI) caused by rapid growth of literature, bridging surface-level accuracy and deeper methodological validity with automated, domain-agnostic verification.
AI.method: Decompose each technical assertion into (Subject, Predicate, Object) claim triples; construct knowledge graphs; six-layer pipeline: 1) corpus construction and ingestion, 2) entity/claim extraction, 3) intra-document verification, 4) cross-source verification, 5) external signal corroboration, 6) final hypothesis matrix generation; implemented as an LLM-based agentic framework enabling verification by non-domain experts.
AI.result: Applied to a contested quantum computing claim; AutoVerifier identified overclaims and metric inconsistencies within the target paper, traced cross-source contradictions, uncovered undisclosed commercial conflicts of interest, and produced a final assessment.
AI.conclusion: Structured LLM verification can reliably evaluate the validity and maturity of emerging technologies, turning raw technical documents into traceable, evidence-backed intelligence assessments.

title: QuadAgent: A Responsive Agent System for Vision-Language Guided Quadrotor Agile Flight
summary: We present QuadAgent, a training-free agent system for agile quadrotor flight guided by vision-language inputs. Unlike prior end-to-end or serial agent approaches, QuadAgent decouples high-level reasoning from low-level control using an asynchronous multi-agent architecture: Foreground Workflow Agents handle active tasks and user commands, while Background Agents perform look-ahead reasoning. The system maintains scene memory via the Impression Graph, a lightweight topological map built from sparse keyframes, and ensures safe flight with a vision-based obstacle avoidance network. Simulation results show that QuadAgent outperforms baseline methods in efficiency and responsiveness. Real-world experiments demonstrate that it can interpret complex instructions, reason about its surroundings, and navigate cluttered indoor spaces at speeds up to 5 m/s.

AI.tldr: A training-free, asynchronous multi-agent system (QuadAgent) decouples high-level reasoning from low-level control for vision-language-guided quadrotor flight; it uses foreground and background agents, an Impression Graph memory, and a vision-based obstacle avoidance network to improve efficiency and responsiveness, demonstrated in simulation and real-world up to 5 m/s.
AI.motivation: Current quadrotor systems relying on end-to-end or single-threaded control struggle to balance agility, safety, and interpretability when guided by diverse vision-language inputs. A decoupled, memory-augmented, look-ahead reasoning framework can enhance responsiveness and robustness for indoor navigation.
AI.method: QuadAgent employs an asynchronous multi-agent architecture with Foreground Workflow Agents handling active tasks and user commands, and Background Agents performing look-ahead reasoning. It maintains scene memory via the Impression Graph, a lightweight topological map built from sparse keyframes, and uses a vision-based obstacle avoidance network for safety. The design is training-free and validated through simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.result: In simulation, QuadAgent outperforms baseline methods in efficiency and responsiveness. Real-world tests show the system can interpret complex instructions, reason about its surroundings, and navigate cluttered indoor spaces at speeds up to 5 m/s.
AI.conclusion: QuadAgent demonstrates a practical, robust, training-free architecture that decouples reasoning and control for agile quadrotor operation, leveraging vision-language inputs, memory, and safety networks to achieve faster, safer navigation.

title: VLMs Need Words: Vision Language Models Ignore Visual Detail In Favor of Semantic Anchors
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) achieve impressive performance across a wide range of multimodal tasks. However, on some tasks that demand fine-grained visual perception, they often fail even when the required information is present in their internal representations. In this work, we demonstrate that this gap arises from their narrow training pipeline which focuses on moving visual information to the textual space. Consequently, VLMs can only reason about visual entities that can be mapped to known concepts in the language space, leaving vision-focused tasks such as visual correspondence and reasoning about novel visual entities poorly supported. As a result, VLMs are severely limited in several important multimodal capabilities because they rely on brittle, hallucinated textual descriptions of visual entities that they cannot map to textual representations. We verify this behavior through visual correspondence tasks, in which VLMs must detect matching entities between two images. Testing across semantic, shape, and face correspondence tasks, we find that VLMs perform much better when the relevant entities are nameable in language than when they are unnameable. Mechanistically, our Logit Lens analyses confirm that VLMs explicitly assign semantic labels to nameable entities and surface more unique corresponding tokens compared to unnameable entities. Furthermore, we show that teaching completely arbitrary names for unknown entities improves performance, yet task-specific finetuning yields even stronger generalization without relying on language priors. Our findings suggest that current VLM failures on visual tasks reflect learned shortcuts from their training, rather than a fundamental limitation of multimodal architectures.

AI.tldr: VLMs' reliance on mapping visuals to known language concepts hinders fine-grained visual reasoning; enabling arbitrary naming and task-specific finetuning can bridge the gap, showing the limitation is due to training shortcuts rather than architecture.
AI.motivation: To understand why Vision-Language Models fail on fine-grained visual tasks and whether failures arise from language-aligned training biases or intrinsic architectural limits.
AI.method: Evaluate on visual correspondence tasks (semantic, shape, and face) across VLMs; use Logit Lens analyses to inspect token-level semantic labeling; experiment with teaching arbitrary names for unknown entities; compare with task-specific finetuning.
AI.result: Nameable entities enable better performance; Logit Lens reveals explicit semantic labels and more unique tokens for nameable items; arbitrary naming improves performance; task-specific finetuning yields even stronger generalization without relying on language priors.
AI.conclusion: The observed failures are due to learned shortcuts from training rather than fundamental architectural limitations; appropriate training strategies (arbitrary naming and finetuning) can overcome these gaps and enhance visual reasoning in VLMs.

title: Contextual Intelligence The Next Leap for Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted to AAMAS 2025 (Blue Sky Ideas Track)
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has produced spectacular results in games, robotics, and continuous control. Yet, despite these successes, learned policies often fail to generalize beyond their training distribution, limiting real-world impact. Recent work on contextual RL (cRL) shows that exposing agents to environment characteristics -- contexts -- can improve zero-shot transfer. So far, the community has treated context as a monolithic, static observable, an approach that constrains the generalization capabilities of RL agents.
  To achieve contextual intelligence we first propose a novel taxonomy of contexts that separates allogenic (environment-imposed) from autogenic (agent-driven) factors. We identify three fundamental research directions that must be addressed to promote truly contextual intelligence: (1) Learning with heterogeneous contexts to explicitly exploit the taxonomy levels so agents can reason about their influence on the world and vice versa; (2) Multi-time-scale modeling to recognize that allogenic variables evolve slowly or remain static, whereas autogenic variables may change within an episode, potentially requiring different learning mechanisms; (3) Integration of abstract, high-level contexts to incorporate roles, resource & regulatory regimes, uncertainties, and other non-physical descriptors that crucially influence behavior.
  We envision context as a first-class modeling primitive, empowering agents to reason about who they are, what the world permits, and how both evolve over time. By doing so, we aim to catalyze a new generation of context-aware agents that can be deployed safely and efficiently in the real world.

AI.tldr: Proposes a taxonomy of contexts in reinforcement learning, separating allogenic (environment-imposed) from autogenic (agent-driven) factors, and outlines three research directions to enable truly contextual intelligence.
AI.motivation: RL policies generalize poorly beyond training distributions. Contextual RL (cRL) shows that exposing agents to environment characteristics can improve zero-shot transfer, but prior work treated context as a monolithic, static observable, limiting generalization.
AI.method: Introduce a novel taxonomy of contexts and identify three core research directions: (1) Learning with heterogeneous contexts to exploit taxonomy levels; (2) Multi-time-scale modeling to handle slow-changing allogenic vs. rapidly changing autogenic factors; (3) Integration of abstract, high-level contexts (roles, resource/regulatory regimes, uncertainties) into policy learning.
AI.result: A conceptual framework and strategic research agenda: taxonomy separating allogenic/autogenic contexts; three concrete research directions to advance contextual intelligence; emphasis on context as a first-class modeling primitive.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware RL agents should reason about who they are, what the world permits, and how both evolve, enabling safer and more efficient real-world deployment.

title: OntoKG: Ontology-Oriented Knowledge Graph Construction with Intrinsic-Relational Routing
summary:
Organizing a large-scale knowledge graph into a typed property graph requires structural decisions -- which entities become nodes, which properties become edges, and what schema governs these choices. Existing approaches embed these decisions in pipeline code or extract relations ad hoc, producing schemas that are tightly coupled to their construction process and difficult to reuse for downstream ontology-level tasks. We present an ontology-oriented approach in which the schema is designed from the outset for ontology analysis, entity disambiguation, domain customization, and LLM-guided extraction -- not merely as a byproduct of graph building. The core mechanism is intrinsic-relational routing, which classifies every property as either intrinsic or relational and routes it to the corresponding schema module. This routing produces a declarative schema that is portable across storage backends and independently reusable.
  We instantiate the approach on the January 2026 Wikidata dump. A rule-based cleaning stage identifies a 34.6M-entity core set from the full dump, followed by iterative intrinsic-relational routing that assigns each property to one of 94 modules organized into 8 categories. With tool-augmented LLM support and human review, the schema reaches 93.3% category coverage and 98.0% module assignment among classified entities. Exporting this schema yields a property graph with 34.0M nodes and 61.2M edges across 38 relationship types. We validate the ontology-oriented claim through five applications that consume the schema independently of the construction pipeline: ontology structure analysis, benchmark annotation auditing, entity disambiguation, domain customization, and LLM-guided extraction.

AI.tldr: Proposes an ontology-oriented approach to building typed property graphs from large knowledge graphs, using intrinsic-relational routing to produce portable schemas and modularization for ontology analysis and downstream tasks; demonstrated on Wikidata with high coverage and broad applicability.
AI.motivation: Current pipelines embed schema decisions in construction code and ad hoc relation extraction, yielding tight coupling and poor reuse for ontology-level tasks. There is a need for a portable, analysis-friendly schema designed from the outset.
AI.method: Introduce intrinsic-relational routing that classifies each property as intrinsic or relational and routes it to one of 94 modules organized into 8 categories, producing a declarative, storage-agnostic schema. Begin with a rule-based cleaning to obtain a core 34.6M-entity set, then iteratively apply routing with LLM support and human review to assign properties to modules. Export the resulting schema to construct a property graph (34.0M nodes, 61.2M edges, 38 relation types). Validate via five applications independent of the build pipeline: ontology analysis, benchmark annotation auditing, entity disambiguation, domain customization, and LLM-guided extraction.
