AI.result: Achieves 93.3% category coverage and 98.0% module assignment among classified entities; schema export yields a scalable property graph suitable for downstream ontology tasks; five autonomous applications demonstrate schema portability and usefulness beyond construction.
AI.conclusion: An ontology-oriented, portable schema with intrinsic-relational routing enables reusable ontology analyses and domain customization, decoupling schema design from graph-building pipelines and supporting LLM-assisted extraction.

title: Learning Structured Robot Policies from Vision-Language Models via Synthetic Neuro-Symbolic Supervision
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have recently demonstrated strong capabilities in mapping multimodal observations to robot behaviors. However, most current approaches rely on end-to-end visuomotor policies that remain opaque and difficult to analyze, limiting their use in safety-critical robotic applications. In contrast, classical robotic systems often rely on structured policy representations that provide interpretability, modularity, and reactive execution. This work investigates how foundation models can be specialized to generate structured robot policies grounded in multimodal perception, bridging high-dimensional learning and symbolic control. We propose a neuro-symbolic approach in which a VLM synthesizes executable Behavior Tree policies from visual observations, natural language instructions, and structured system specifications. To enable scalable supervision without manual annotation, we introduce an automated pipeline that generates a synthetic multimodal dataset of domain-randomized scenes paired with instruction-policy examples produced by a foundation model. Real-world experiments on two robotic manipulators show that structured policies learned entirely from synthetic supervision transfer successfully to physical systems. The results indicate that foundation models can be adapted to produce interpretable and structured robot policies, providing an alternative to opaque end-to-end approaches for multimodal robot decision making.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic framework uses a foundation model to synthesize executable Behavior Tree policies from multimodal inputs, trained via synthetic data, and successfully transfers to real robots for interpretable, structured control.
AI.motivation: Bridge high-dimensional perception with symbolic, interpretable control; address opacity of end-to-end visuomotor policies and leverage foundation models to generate structured policies grounded in multimodal data.
AI.method: A vision-language model generates executable Behavior Tree policies from visual observations, natural language instructions, and system specifications. An automated pipeline creates a synthetic, domain-randomized multimodal dataset with instruction-policy pairs produced by a foundation model, enabling scalable supervision without manual labeling. Real-world experiments on two robotic manipulators test transfer of synthetically learned policies to physical systems.
AI.result: Structured policies learned from synthetic supervision transfer successfully to real robotic manipulators, showing interpretability, modularity, and reactive execution aligned with the target tasks.
AI.conclusion: Foundation models can be specialized to produce interpretable, structured robot policies (e.g., Behavior Trees) from multimodal inputs, offering a viable alternative to opaque end-to-end approaches for multimodal robot decision making.

title: Token-Efficient Multimodal Reasoning via Image Prompt Packaging
summary: Deploying large multimodal language models at scale is constrained by token-based inference costs, yet the cost-performance behavior of visual prompting strategies remains poorly characterized. We introduce Image Prompt Packaging (IPPg), a prompting paradigm that embeds structured text directly into images to reduce text token overhead, and benchmark it across five datasets, three frontier models (GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet), and two task families (VQA and code generation). We derive a cost formulation decomposing savings by token type and show IPPg achieves 35.8--91.0\% inference cost reductions. Despite token compression of up to 96\%, accuracy remains competitive in many settings, though outcomes are highly model- and task-dependent: GPT-4.1 achieves simultaneous accuracy and cost gains on CoSQL, while Claude 3.5 incurs cost increases on several VQA benchmarks. Systematic error analysis yields a failure-mode taxonomy: spatial reasoning, non-English inputs, and character-sensitive operations are most vulnerable, while schema-structured tasks benefit most. A 125-configuration rendering ablation reveals accuracy shifts of 10--30 percentage points, establishing visual encoding choices as a first-class variable in multimodal system design.

AI.tldr: IPPg reduces multimodal inference costs by embedding structured prompts into images, achieving substantial cost savings with varying accuracy effects across models and tasks.
AI.motivation: Large token-based inference costs in multimodal LMs hinder scalability; visual prompting methods lack comprehensive cost-performance characterization.
AI.method: Introduce Image Prompt Packaging (IPPg); benchmark across five datasets, three frontier models (GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet), and two tasks (VQA, code generation); derive a token-type cost decomposition; conduct a 125-configuration rendering ablation; perform systematic error analysis.
AI.result: IPPg yields 35.8–91.0% inference cost reductions; token compression up to 96% with accuracy remaining competitive in many settings; model/task dependence observed (GPT-4.1 improves on CoSQL; Claude 3.5 often incurs higher costs on VQA); error taxonomy highlights vulnerabilities in spatial reasoning, non-English inputs, and character-sensitive operations; schema-structured tasks show the greatest gains; rendering ablation reveals 10–30 percentage point accuracy shifts across configurations.
AI.conclusion: Visual encoding choices are a critical variable in multimodal system design, enabling substantial cost savings with IPPg, though benefits are model- and task-dependent; systematic analysis and ablations establish a framework for optimizing visual prompt design.

title: OPRIDE: Offline Preference-based Reinforcement Learning via In-Dataset Exploration
summary: Preference-based reinforcement learning (PbRL) can help avoid sophisticated reward designs and align better with human intentions, showing great promise in various real-world applications. However, obtaining human feedback for preferences can be expensive and time-consuming, which forms a strong barrier for PbRL. In this work, we address the problem of low query efficiency in offline PbRL, pinpointing two primary reasons: inefficient exploration and overoptimization of learned reward functions. In response to these challenges, we propose a novel algorithm, \textbf{O}ffline \textbf{P}b\textbf{R}L via \textbf{I}n-\textbf{D}ataset \textbf{E}xploration (OPRIDE), designed to enhance the query efficiency of offline PbRL. OPRIDE consists of two key features: a principled exploration strategy that maximizes the informativeness of the queries and a discount scheduling mechanism aimed at mitigating overoptimization of the learned reward functions. Through empirical evaluations, we demonstrate that OPRIDE significantly outperforms prior methods, achieving strong performance with notably fewer queries. Moreover, we provide theoretical guarantees of the algorithm's efficiency. Experimental results across various locomotion, manipulation, and navigation tasks underscore the efficacy and versatility of our approach.

AI.tldr: OPRIDE is an offline PbRL algorithm that increases query efficiency by in-dataset exploration and a discount schedule to curb reward overfitting, achieving strong performance with fewer human preferences and providing theoretical efficiency guarantees.
AI.motivation: PbRL is hindered by expensive human feedback and the offline setting amplifies this issue. The paper identifies two main causes of low query efficiency: inefficient exploration and overoptimization of learned reward functions.
AI.method: OPRIDE introduces two components: (1) a principled exploration strategy that maximizes the informativeness of preference queries within the dataset, and (2) a discount scheduling mechanism to mitigate overfitting/overoptimization of the learned reward function during offline training.
AI.result: Empirical results show that OPRIDE outperforms prior methods across locomotion, manipulation, and navigation tasks, achieving strong performance with notably fewer queries; theoretical guarantees on efficiency are provided.
AI.conclusion: OPRIDE effectively enhances query efficiency in offline PbRL through in-dataset exploration and discount scheduling, demonstrating strong, versatile performance and theoretical backing.

title: Let's Have a Conversation: Designing and Evaluating LLM Agents for Interactive Optimization
summary: Optimization is as much about modeling the right problem as solving it. Identifying the right objectives, constraints, and trade-offs demands extensive interaction between researchers and stakeholders. Large language models can empower decision-makers with optimization capabilities through interactive optimization agents that can propose, interpret and refine solutions. However, it is fundamentally harder to evaluate a conversation-based interaction than traditional one-shot approaches. This paper proposes a scalable and replicable methodology for evaluating optimization agents through conversations. We build LLM-powered decision agents that role-play diverse stakeholders, each governed by an internal utility function but communicating like a real decision-maker. We generate thousands of conversations in a school scheduling case study. Results show that one-shot evaluation is severely limiting: the same optimization agent converges to much higher-quality solutions through conversations. Then, this paper uses this methodology to demonstrate that tailored optimization agents, endowed with domain-specific prompts and structured tools, can lead to significant improvements in solution quality in fewer interactions, as compared to general-purpose chatbots. These findings provide evidence of the benefits of emerging solutions at the AI-optimization interface to expand the reach of optimization technologies in practice. They also uncover the impact of operations research expertise to facilitate interactive deployments through the design of effective and reliable optimization agents.

AI.tldr: Scalable, replicable evaluation of optimization agents via interactive conversations with LLMs; demonstrates that conversation-based evaluation yields higher-quality solutions than one-shot, and that domain-tailored prompts/tools enhance performance.
AI.motivation: Evaluating conversation-based optimization agents is hard and costly; a scalable methodology is needed to study their performance, reliability, and deployment in practice.
AI.method: Develop LLM-powered decision agents that role-play diverse stakeholders with internal utilities; generate thousands of conversations in a school scheduling case study; compare one-shot evaluation versus conversation-based interaction; assess tailored domain-specific prompts/tools against general-purpose chatbots.
AI.result: Conversation-based interactions produce higher-quality solutions than one-shot evaluations; tailored optimization agents with domain prompts and structured tools achieve significant improvements with fewer interactions than generic chatbots; supports the potential of AI-optimization interfaces and the role of operations research expertise in deployments.
AI.conclusion: Interactive, conversation-driven optimization can broaden practical adoption of optimization technologies; domain-informed, tool-supported agents outperform generic assistants; provides a replicable framework for evaluating such agents across settings.

title: MFE: A Multimodal Hand Exoskeleton with Interactive Force, Pressure and Thermo-haptic Feedback
summary: Recent advancements in virtual reality and robotic teleoperation have greatly increased the variety of haptic information that must be conveyed to users. While existing haptic devices typically provide unimodal feedback to enhance situational awareness, a gap remains in their ability to deliver rich, multimodal sensory feedback encompassing force, pressure, and thermal sensations. To address this limitation, we present the Multimodal Feedback Exoskeleton (MFE), a hand exoskeleton designed to deliver hybrid haptic feedback. The MFE features 20 degrees of freedom for capturing hand pose. For force feedback, it employs an active mechanism capable of generating 3.5-8.1 N of pushing and pulling forces at the fingers' resting pose, enabling realistic interaction with deformable objects. The fingertips are equipped with flat actuators based on the electro-osmotic principle, providing pressure and vibration stimuli and achieving up to 2.47 kPa of contact pressure to render tactile sensations. For thermal feedback, the MFE integrates thermoelectric heat pumps capable of rendering temperatures from 10 to 55 degrees Celsius. We validated the MFE by integrating it into a robotic teleoperation system using the X-Arm 6 and Inspire Hand manipulator. In user studies, participants successfully recognized and manipulated deformable objects and differentiated remote objects with varying temperatures. These results demonstrate that the MFE enhances situational awareness, as well as the usability and transparency of robotic teleoperation systems.

AI.tldr: Multimodal Feedback Exoskeleton (MFE) delivering force, tactile, and thermal feedback for teleoperation; validated with robotic manipulators; enhances situational awareness and usability.
AI.motivation: To overcome unimodal haptic feedback limitations in VR/teleoperation and deliver richer perceptual cues.
AI.method: Design of a 20-DoF hand exoskeleton with: (i) active force feedback (3.5–8.1 N) at resting pose; (ii) electro-osmotic flat actuators for pressure up to 2.47 kPa and vibration; (iii) thermoelectric heat pumps 10–55 C; integration with X-Arm 6 and Inspire Hand; user studies.
AI.result: Participants could recognize and manipulate deformable objects and distinguish temperatures; improved situational awareness, usability, and transparency.
AI.conclusion: MFE demonstrates the viability of true multimodal haptic feedback in teleoperation; potential for enhanced realism; future work on optimization and scalability.

title: Delaunay Canopy: Building Wireframe Reconstruction from Airborne LiDAR Point Clouds via Delaunay Graph
summary: Reconstructing building wireframe from airborne LiDAR point clouds yields a compact, topology-centric representation that enables structural understanding beyond dense meshes. Yet a key limitation persists: conventional methods have failed to achieve accurate wireframe reconstruction in regions afflicted by significant noise, sparsity, or internal corners. This failure stems from the inability to establish an adaptive search space to effectively leverage the rich 3D geometry of large, sparse building point clouds. In this work, we address this challenge with Delaunay Canopy, which utilizes the Delaunay graph as a geometric prior to define a geometrically adaptive search space. Central to our approach is Delaunay Graph Scoring, which not only reconstructs the underlying geometric manifold but also yields region-wise curvature signatures to robustly guide the reconstruction. Built on this foundation, our corner and wire selection modules leverage the Delaunay-induced prior to focus on highly probable elements, thereby shaping the search space and enabling accurate prediction even in previously intractable regions. Extensive experiments on the Building3D Tallinn city and entry-level datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art wireframe reconstruction, delivering accurate predictions across diverse and complex building geometries.

AI.tldr: Introduces Delaunay Canopy, a Delaunay-graph–based geometric prior that enables adaptive search-space for wireframe reconstruction from noisy, sparse airborne LiDAR, achieving state-of-the-art results on Building3D Tallinn and related datasets.
AI.motivation: Conventional wireframe reconstruction struggles in regions with high noise, sparsity, or internal corners because the search space is not adaptive to 3D geometry; a geometry-aware prior is needed to leverage large, sparse point clouds.
AI.method: Proposes Delaunay Graph Scoring to reconstruct the manifold and extract region-wise curvature; uses the Delaunay-induced prior to drive corner and wire selection, shaping an adaptive search space for reconstruction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Building3D Tallinn city and entry-level datasets show state-of-the-art wireframe reconstruction with accurate predictions across diverse, complex geometries.
AI.conclusion: Delaunay-based priors enable robust wireframe reconstruction in challenging regions by focusing search on highly probable elements, suggesting broad applicability to geometry reconstruction in noisy, sparse data.

title: Differentiable Symbolic Planning: A Neural Architecture for Constraint Reasoning with Learned Feasibility
summary: Neural networks excel at pattern recognition but struggle with constraint reasoning -- determining whether configurations satisfy logical or physical constraints. We introduce Differentiable Symbolic Planning (DSP), a neural architecture that performs discrete symbolic reasoning while remaining fully differentiable. DSP maintains a feasibility channel (phi) that tracks constraint satisfaction evidence at each node, aggregates this into a global feasibility signal (Phi) through learned rule-weighted combination, and uses sparsemax attention to achieve exact-zero discrete rule selection. We integrate DSP into a Universal Cognitive Kernel (UCK) that combines graph attention with iterative constraint propagation. Evaluated on three constraint reasoning benchmarks -- graph reachability, Boolean satisfiability, and planning feasibility -- UCK+DSP achieves 97.4% accuracy on planning under 4x size generalization (vs. 59.7% for ablated baselines), 96.4% on SAT under 2x generalization, and maintains balanced performance on both positive and negative classes where standard neural approaches collapse. Ablation studies reveal that global phi aggregation is critical: removing it causes accuracy to drop from 98% to 64%. The learned phi signal exhibits interpretable semantics, with values of +18 for feasible cases and -13 for infeasible cases emerging without supervision.

AI.tldr: Differentiable Symbolic Planning (DSP) enables neural networks to perform discrete constraint reasoning by maintaining a per-node feasibility signal (phi) and a global Phi via learned rule-weighted aggregation with sparsemax-based discrete rule selection. Integrating DSP into a Universal Cognitive Kernel (UCK) yields strong generalization in constraint reasoning benchmarks, including planning feasibility, SAT, and graph reachability.
AI.motivation: Neural networks excel at pattern recognition but struggle with constraint reasoning that requires discrete symbolic manipulation. The authors aim to bridge differentiable learning with exact logical/constraint reasoning by introducing a differentiable-symbolic planning mechanism that preserves discrete decision points (rule selection) within a differentiable framework.
AI.method: Maintain node-level feasibility channels (phi) that track constraint-satisfaction evidence; aggregate these via learned rule-weighted combination into a global feasibility Phi. Apply sparsemax attention to enable exact-zero discrete rule selection. Integrate DSP into a Universal Cognitive Kernel (UCK) that combines graph attention with iterative constraint propagation.
AI.result: On three benchmarks (graph reachability, SAT, planning feasibility), UCK+DSP achieves 97.4% planning accuracy under 4x size generalization (vs 59.7% for ablated baselines) and 96.4% SAT accuracy under 2x generalization, while maintaining balanced positive/negative class performance where standard neural baselines fail. Ablation shows removing global Phi aggregation drops accuracy from 98% to 64%. Learned phi signals are interpretable, with +18 for feasible cases and -13 for infeasible cases emerging without supervision.
AI.conclusion: DSP effectively enables differentiable discrete constraint reasoning within a graph-based cognitive kernel, yielding strong generalization, interpretable constraint signals, and critical dependence on global feasibility aggregation.

title: GrandCode: Achieving Grandmaster Level in Competitive Programming via Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Competitive programming remains one of the last few human strongholds in coding against AI. The best AI system to date still underperforms the best humans competitive programming: the most recent best result, Google's Gemini~3 Deep Think, attained 8th place even not being evaluated under live competition conditions. In this work, we introduce GrandCode, a multi-agent RL system designed for competitive programming. The capability of GrandCode is attributed to two key factors: (1) It orchestrates a variety of agentic modules (hypothesis proposal, solver, test generator, summarization, etc) and jointly improves them through post-training and online test-time RL; (2) We introduce Agentic GRPO specifically designed for multi-stage agent rollouts with delayed rewards and the severe off-policy drift that is prevalent in agentic RL. GrandCode is the first AI system that consistently beats all human participants in live contests of competitive programming: in the most recent three Codeforces live competitions, i.e., Round~1087 (Mar 21, 2026), Round~1088 (Mar 28, 2026), and Round~1089 (Mar 29, 2026), GrandCode placed first in all of them, beating all human participants, including legendary grandmasters. GrandCode shows that AI systems have reached a point where they surpass the strongest human programmers on the most competitive coding tasks.

AI.tldr: GrandCode is a multi-agent RL system for competitive programming that claims to beat all human participants in Codeforces live rounds via orchestrated agent modules and a novel Agentic GRPO for delayed rewards; evaluation across three rounds suggests AI surpasses top human programmers in this domain.
AI.motivation: Push AI to surpass human peak performance in high-skill, fast-paced programming tasks and address off-policy drift in multi-agent RL through a structured, interactive agent architecture.
AI.method: A modular, multi-agent architecture combining hypothesis proposal, solvers, test generation, and summarization. Trains via post-training and online test-time RL. Introduces Agentic GRPO for multi-stage rollouts with delayed rewards and off-policy drift mitigation.
AI.result: In three Codeforces live rounds (1087–1089, Mar 2026), GrandCode placed first in all rounds, ahead of all human participants including grandmasters.
AI.conclusion: Claims that AI can consistently exceed the strongest human programmers on competitive programming tasks; emphasizes the potential of agentic RL with multi-module orchestration, while inviting scrutiny on reproducibility, baseline comparisons, and generalization.

title: Goal-Conditioned Neural ODEs with Guaranteed Safety and Stability for Learning-Based All-Pairs Motion Planning
summary: This paper presents a learning-based approach for all-pairs motion planning, where the initial and goal states are allowed to be arbitrary points in a safe set. We construct smooth goal-conditioned neural ordinary differential equations (neural ODEs) via bi-Lipschitz diffeomorphisms. Theoretical results show that the proposed model can provide guarantees of global exponential stability and safety (safe set forward invariance) regardless of goal location. Moreover, explicit bounds on convergence rate, tracking error, and vector field magnitude are established. Our approach admits a tractable learning implementation using bi-Lipschitz neural networks and can incorporate demonstration data. We illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method on a 2D corridor navigation task.

AI.tldr: Learning-based all-pairs motion planning using bi-Lipschitz neural ODEs yields global stability and safety guarantees for arbitrary goal locations within a safe set; learning is tractable and can use demonstrations; validated on a 2D corridor task.
AI.motivation: Extend motion planning to arbitrary goal points within a safe set while ensuring formal guarantees on stability and safety, regardless of goal location; provide explicit performance bounds.
AI.method: Construct smooth, goal-conditioned neural ordinary differential equations via bi-Lipschitz diffeomorphisms using bi-Lipschitz neural networks; incorporate demonstration data; derive explicit bounds on convergence rate, tracking error, and vector field magnitude.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees: global exponential stability and safe set forward invariance independent of goal location; explicit convergence, tracking, and vector field bounds; tractable learning implementation; demonstrated on a 2D corridor navigation task.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a safe, stable, and learnable framework for all-pairs motion planning, combining neural ODEs with bi-Lipschitz mappings; suitable for demonstration-augmented learning; applicability to other safe planning problems and higher dimensions warrants further study.

title: An Explainable Vision-Language Model Framework with Adaptive PID-Tversky Loss for Lumbar Spinal Stenosis Diagnosis
summary: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis (LSS) diagnosis remains a critical clinical challenge, with diagnosis heavily dependent on labor-intensive manual interpretation of multi-view Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), leading to substantial inter-observer variability and diagnostic delays. Existing vision-language models simultaneously fail to address the extreme class imbalance prevalent in clinical segmentation datasets while preserving spatial accuracy, primarily due to global pooling mechanisms that discard crucial anatomical hierarchies. We present an end-to-end Explainable Vision-Language Model framework designed to overcome these limitations, achieved through two principal objectives. We propose a Spatial Patch Cross-Attention module that enables precise, text-directed localization of spinal anomalies with spatial precision. A novel Adaptive PID-Tversky Loss function by integrating control theory principles dynamically further modifies training penalties to specifically address difficult, under-segmented minority instances. By incorporating foundational VLMs alongside an Automated Radiology Report Generation module, our framework demonstrates considerable performance: a diagnostic classification accuracy of 90.69%, a macro-averaged Dice score of 0.9512 for segmentation, and a CIDEr score of 92.80%. Furthermore, the framework shows explainability by converting complex segmentation predictions into radiologist-style clinical reports, thereby establishing a new benchmark for transparent, interpretable AI in clinical medical imaging that keeps essential human supervision while enhancing diagnostic capabilities.

AI.tldr: End-to-end explainable Vision-Language Model for Lumbar Spinal Stenosis that uses Spatial Patch Cross-Attention and Adaptive PID-Tversky Loss to address spatial precision and severe class imbalance, with automated radiology report generation; achieves 90.69% diagnostic accuracy, macro Dice 0.9512, CIDEr 92.80.
AI.motivation: Diagnosing LSS from multi-view MRI is labor-intensive and prone to inter-observer variability and delays. Existing vision-language models struggle to maintain spatial fidelity under extreme class imbalance due to global pooling. There is a need for interpretable AI that preserves anatomical hierarchies and can generate radiologist-style reports.
AI.method: Introduce a Spatial Patch Cross-Attention module for text-directed, spatially precise localization of spinal anomalies. Propose an Adaptive PID-Tversky Loss that dynamically tunes penalties to better learn hard, under-represented minority classes. Integrate foundational Vision-Language Models with an Automated Radiology Report Generation module.
AI.result: Performance includes diagnostic accuracy of 90.69%, macro-averaged Dice of 0.9512 for segmentation, and CIDEr score of 92.80. The approach also provides explainability by converting segmentation results into radiologist-style reports, acting as a transparent AI benchmark with preserved human supervision.
AI.conclusion: The framework advances interpretable, accurate clinical AI for spinal imaging by combining precise spatial localization, dynamic loss control to address class imbalance, and automatic report generation, setting a new standard for transparent VLM-based medical imaging analysis.

title: Modeling and Controlling Deployment Reliability under Temporal Distribution Shift
comment: 19 pages, 5 figures, 7 tables. Empirical study on temporally indexed credit-risk dataset (1.35M samples, 2007-2018)
summary:
Machine learning models deployed in non-stationary environments are exposed to temporal distribution shift, which can erode predictive reliability over time. While common mitigation strategies such as periodic retraining and recalibration aim to preserve performance, they typically focus on average metrics evaluated at isolated time points and do not explicitly model how reliability evolves during deployment.
  We propose a deployment-centric framework that treats reliability as a dynamic state composed of discrimination and calibration. The trajectory of this state across sequential evaluation windows induces a measurable notion of volatility, allowing deployment adaptation to be formulated as a multi-objective control problem that balances reliability stability against cumulative intervention cost.
  Within this framework, we define a family of state-dependent intervention policies and empirically characterize the resulting cost-volatility Pareto frontier. Experiments on a large-scale, temporally indexed credit-risk dataset (1.35M loans, 2007-2018) show that selective, drift-triggered interventions can achieve smoother reliability trajectories than continuous rolling retraining while substantially reducing operational cost.
  These findings position deployment reliability under temporal shift as a controllable multi-objective system and highlight the role of policy design in shaping stability-cost trade-offs in high-stakes tabular applications.

AI.tldr: Deployment-centric reliability under temporal shift: frame reliability as a dynamic state (discrimination + calibration) and optimize drift-triggered interventions via a multi-objective control approach to balance stability and intervention cost; demonstrated on a large credit dataset with improved cost-efficiency vs. rolling retraining.
AI.motivation: Non-stationary deployment environments erode predictive reliability. Snapshot metrics miss evolution over time; need a framework that models reliability dynamics to guide when and how to intervene.
AI.method: Define a state-dependent intervention policy class within a multi-objective control framework. Track reliability dynamics (discrimination and calibration) across sequential evaluation windows, quantify volatility, and pose an intervention cost. Empirically characterize the cost-volatility Pareto frontier. Validate on a large temporally-indexed credit-risk dataset (1.35M loans, 2007-2018) comparing drift-triggered interventions to continuous rolling retraining.
AI.result: Selective, drift-triggered interventions yield smoother reliability trajectories (lower volatility) and substantially reduce operational cost compared with continuous rolling retraining.
AI.conclusion: Deployment reliability under temporal shift is controllable via policy design. The stability-cost trade-off can be shaped to favor smoother performance with reduced intervention costs, with implications for high-stakes tabular applications.

title: DeltaLogic: Minimal Premise Edits Reveal Belief-Revision Failures in Logical Reasoning Models
comment: ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models
summary: Reasoning benchmarks typically evaluate whether a model derives the correct answer from a fixed premise set, but they under-measure a closely related capability that matters in dynamic environments: belief revision under minimal evidence change. We introduce DeltaLogic, a benchmark transformation protocol that converts natural-language reasoning examples into short revision episodes. Each episode first asks for an initial conclusion under premises P, then applies a minimal edit δ(P), and finally asks whether the previous conclusion should remain stable or be revised. We instantiate DeltaLogic from FOLIO and ProofWriter and evaluate small causal language models with constrained label scoring. On a completed 30-episode Qwen evaluation subset, stronger initial reasoning still does not imply stronger revision behavior: Qwen3-1.7B reaches 0.667 initial accuracy but only 0.467 revision accuracy, with inertia rising to 0.600 on episodes where the gold label should change, while Qwen3-0.6B collapses into near universal abstention. There, Qwen3-4B preserves the same inertial failure pattern (0.650 initial, 0.450 revised, 0.600 inertia), whereas Phi-4-mini-instruct is substantially stronger (0.950 initial, 0.850 revised) but still exhibits non-trivial abstention and control instability. These results suggest that logical competence under fixed premises does not imply disciplined belief revision after local evidence edits. DeltaLogic therefore targets a distinct and practically important reasoning capability that complements existing logical inference and belief-updating benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DeltaLogic introduces a benchmark to assess belief revision under minimal edits in natural-language reasoning. Strong initial reasoning does not imply robust revision across models; belief-update capability is distinct and not captured by traditional fixed-premise logic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Belief revision under minimal evidence change is critical in dynamic environments, where small edits to premises should not lead to unstable conclusions. Existing benchmarks focus on static inference and overlook revision discipline.
AI.method: DeltaLogic transforms natural-language reasoning tasks into short revision episodes: start with an initial conclusion under premises P, apply a minimal edit δ(P), then judge whether the prior conclusion should stay or be revised. The protocol is instantiated from FOLIO and ProofWriter and evaluated on small causal LMs with constrained label scoring on a completed 30-episode Qwen subset.
AI.result: Stronger initial reasoning does not predict stronger revision behavior. Qwen3-1.7B: 0.667 initial accuracy, 0.467 revision accuracy, inertia 0.600 when the gold label changes. Qwen3-0.6B collapses to near universal abstention. Qwen3-4B shows the same inertial pattern. Phi-4-mini-instruct achieves 0.950 initial and 0.850 revised accuracy but still exhibits non-trivial abstention and control instability.
AI.conclusion: Logical competence under fixed premises does not imply disciplined belief revision after local evidence edits. DeltaLogic targets a distinct, practically important reasoning capability that complements existing logical inference and belief-updating benchmarks.

title: Orientation Matters: Learning Radiation Patterns of Multi-Rotor UAVs In-Flight to Enhance Communication Availability Modeling
summary: The paper presents an approach for learning antenna Radiation Patterns (RPs) of a pair of heterogeneous quadrotor Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) by calibration flight data. RPs are modeled either as a Spherical Harmonics series or as a weighted average over inducing samples. Linear regression of polynomial coefficients simultaneously decouples the two independent UAVs' RPs. A joint calibration trajectory exploits available flight time in an obstacle-free anechoic altitude. Evaluation on a real-world dataset demonstrates the feasibility of learning both radiation patterns, achieving 3.6 dB RMS error, the measurement noise level. The proposed RP learning and decoupling can be exploited in rapid recalibration upon payload changes, thereby enabling precise autonomous path planning and swarm control in real-world applications where setup changes are expected.

AI.tldr: A method to learn and decouple radiation patterns of two heterogeneous quadrotor UAVs using calibration flight data; models RP with spherical harmonics or sample-weighted averages; uses linear regression on polynomial coefficients to separate RPs; achieves 3.6 dB RMS error on real data; enables rapid recalibration for payload changes.
AI.motivation: Accurate radiation pattern knowledge is essential for reliable communication, sensing, and coordination in UAV swarms. Heterogeneous payloads and platform differences make RP estimation challenging; a decoupled, calibration-based approach can enable fast re-tuning for different configurations.
AI.method: Collect calibration flight data in an obstacle-free anechoic altitude; model each UAV's RP as either a spherical harmonics (SH) series or a weighted average over inducing samples; perform linear regression on polynomial coefficients to decouple the two UAVs' RPs; design a joint calibration trajectory to maximize information within flight time.
AI.result: Feasibility demonstrated on a real-world dataset; learned RPs with 3.6 dB RMS error, matching the measurement noise level; rapid recalibration potential upon payload changes; enables more accurate autonomous path planning and swarm control under varying setups.
AI.conclusion: RP learning with decoupling supports fast calibration when payloads vary, enabling precise autonomous planning and swarm coordination in real-world operations where configurations change.

title: Rapidly deploying on-device eye tracking by distilling visual foundation models
summary: Eye tracking (ET) plays a critical role in augmented and virtual reality applications. However, rapidly deploying high-accuracy, on-device gaze estimation for new products remains challenging because hardware configurations (e.g., camera placement, camera pose, and illumination) often change across device generations. Visual foundation models (VFMs) are a promising direction for rapid training and deployment, and they excel on natural-image benchmarks; yet we find that off-the-shelf VFMs still struggle to achieve high accuracy on specialized near-eye infrared imagery. To address this gap, we introduce DistillGaze, a framework that distills a foundation model by leveraging labeled synthetic data and unlabeled real data for rapid and high-performance on-device gaze estimation. DistillGaze proceeds in two stages. First, we adapt a VFM into a domain-specialized teacher using self-supervised learning on labeled synthetic and unlabeled real images. Synthetic data provides scalable, high-quality gaze supervision, while unlabeled real data helps bridge the synthetic-to-real domain gap. Second, we train an on-device student using both teacher guidance and self-training. Evaluated on a large-scale, crowd-sourced dataset spanning over 2,000 participants, DistillGaze reduces median gaze error by 58.62% relative to synthetic-only baselines while maintaining a lightweight 256K-parameter model suitable for real-time on-device deployment. Overall, DistillGaze provides an efficient pathway for training and deploying ET models that adapt to hardware changes, and offers a recipe for combining synthetic supervision with unlabeled real data in on-device regression tasks.

AI.tldr: DistillGaze distills a foundation model for on-device gaze estimation via a two-stage pipeline that uses labeled synthetic data and unlabeled real data to bridge the synthetic–real gap, achieving a 58.62% median error reduction over synthetic-only baselines with a lightweight 256K-parameter model.
AI.motivation: High-accuracy gaze estimation on resource-constrained devices must adapt to hardware changes (camera placement, pose, illumination) across generations. Off-the-shelf VFMs struggle on near-eye infrared imagery, necessitating efficient on-device adaptation.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) adapt a VFM into a domain-specialized teacher using self-supervised learning on labeled synthetic and unlabeled real images; synthetic data provides scalable gaze supervision, unlabeled real data bridges synthetic–real gap. (2) train an on-device student using teacher guidance and self-training; leverage synthetic supervision and unlabeled real data for robust, lightweight regression.
AI.result: Evaluated on a large-scale crowd-sourced dataset (>2,000 participants). DistillGaze reduces median gaze error by 58.62% relative to synthetic-only baselines and yields a lightweight 256K-parameter model capable of real-time on-device deployment.
AI.conclusion: DistillGaze offers an efficient framework for adapting ET models to hardware changes by combining synthetic supervision with unlabeled real data, presenting a practical recipe for on-device regression tasks.

title: An Initial Exploration of Contrastive Prompt Tuning to Generate Energy-Efficient Code
comment: Published at the Third International Workshop on Large Language Models for Code (LLM4Code 2026)
summary: Although LLMs are capable of generating functionally correct code, they also tend to produce less energy-efficient code in comparison to human-written solutions. As these inefficiencies lead to higher computational overhead, they are in direct conflict with Green Software Development (GSD) efforts, which aim to reduce the energy consumption of code. To support these efforts, this study aims to investigate whether and how LLMs can be optimized to promote the generation of energy-efficient code. To this end, we employ Contrastive Prompt Tuning (CPT). CPT combines Contrastive Learning techniques, which help the model to distinguish between efficient and inefficient code, and Prompt Tuning, a Parameter-Efficient Fine Tuning (PEFT) approach that requires only a fraction of the cost of traditional fine tuning. This study evaluates CPT on Python, Java and C++ coding problems across three different models to provide a comprehensive evaluation. The method achieves consistent improvements in code accuracy for two models but efficiency gains vary by model, language and task complexity, indicating that improvements are not uniformly reliable.

AI.tldr: Contrastive Prompt Tuning (CPT) improves code accuracy for LLM-generated code on Python/Java/C++, but energy-efficiency gains are inconsistent across models, languages, and task complexity.
AI.motivation: Address Green Software Development (GSD) goals by reducing energy consumption in code produced by LLMs, using a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach that can bias models towards energy-efficient solutions.
AI.method: Apply CPT, combining contrastive learning to differentiate efficient versus inefficient code with Prompt Tuning (a PEFT technique), evaluated across Python, Java, and C++ coding problems on three models.
AI.result: CPT yields consistent code accuracy improvements for two models; energy-efficiency (efficiency) gains are variable and not uniformly reliable, depending on model, language, and task complexity.
AI.conclusion: CPT shows potential to steer LLM code generation toward energy efficiency, but results are heterogeneous; further work is needed to stabilize improvements and assess energy impact across broader settings.

title: Aligning Progress and Feasibility: A Neuro-Symbolic Dual Memory Framework for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong potential in long-horizon decision-making tasks, such as embodied manipulation and web interaction. However, agents frequently struggle with endless trial-and-error loops or deviate from the main objective in complex environments. We attribute these failures to two fundamental errors: global Progress Drift and local Feasibility Violation. Existing methods typically attempt to address both issues simultaneously using a single paradigm. However, these two challenges are fundamentally distinct: the former relies on fuzzy semantic planning, while the latter demands strict logical constraints and state validation. The inherent limitations of such a single-paradigm approach pose a fundamental challenge for existing models in handling long-horizon tasks. Motivated by this insight, we propose a Neuro-Symbolic Dual Memory Framework that explicitly decouples semantic progress guidance from logical feasibility verification. Specifically, during the inference phase, the framework invokes both memory mechanisms synchronously: on one hand, a neural-network-based Progress Memory extracts semantic blueprints from successful trajectories to guide global task advancement; on the other hand, a symbolic-logic-based Feasibility Memory utilizes executable Python verification functions synthesized from failed transitions to perform strict logical validation. Experiments demonstrate that this method significantly outperforms existing competitive baselines on ALFWorld, WebShop, and TextCraft, while drastically reducing the invalid action rate and average trajectory length.

AI.tldr: A neuro-symbolic dual-memory framework decouples semantic progress guidance from logical feasibility verification to improve long-horizon tasks; neural Progress Memory guides global planning, while symbolic Feasibility Memory enforces state validity using synthesized Python checks.
AI.motivation: To address two fundamental errors in long-horizon LLM-driven agents—global Progress Drift (semantic planning drift) and local Feasibility Violation (state-validity failures)—which are not well tackled by a single paradigm.
AI.method: During inference, run two memory streams in parallel: (1) Progress Memory (neural) extracts semantic blueprints from successful trajectories to steer global task advancement; (2) Feasibility Memory (symbolic) uses executable Python verification functions synthesized from failed transitions to enforce strict logical validation of states and actions.
AI.result: The proposed method significantly outperforms competitive baselines on ALFWorld, WebShop, and TextCraft, while drastically reducing invalid action rate and shortening average trajectory length.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semantic guidance from feasibility verification via a dual-memory neuro-symbolic framework yields robust long-horizon decision capabilities and better sample efficiency, with potential applicability to other long-horizon tasks.

title: Feature Attribution Stability Suite: How Stable Are Post-Hoc Attributions?
comment: Accepted in the proceedings track of XAI4CV Workshop at CVPR 2026. It has 2 images, 5 tables, 6 equations, and 35 references in the main paper and 12 figures, 15 tables, and 3 references in the supplementary material
summary: Post-hoc feature attribution methods are widely deployed in safety-critical vision systems, yet their stability under realistic input perturbations remains poorly characterized. Existing metrics evaluate explanations primarily under additive noise, collapse stability to a single scalar, and fail to condition on prediction preservation, conflating explanation fragility with model sensitivity. We introduce the Feature Attribution Stability Suite (FASS), a benchmark that enforces prediction-invariance filtering, decomposes stability into three complementary metrics: structural similarity, rank correlation, and top-k Jaccard overlap-and evaluates across geometric, photometric, and compression perturbations. Evaluating four attribution methods (Integrated Gradients, GradientSHAP, Grad-CAM, LIME) across four architectures and three datasets-ImageNet-1K, MS COCO, and CIFAR-10, FASS shows that stability estimates depend critically on perturbation family and prediction-invariance filtering. Geometric perturbations expose substantially greater attribution instability than photometric changes, and without conditioning on prediction preservation, up to 99% of evaluated pairs involve changed predictions. Under this controlled evaluation, we observe consistent method-level trends, with Grad-CAM achieving the highest stability across datasets.

AI.tldr: FASS introduces a prediction-invariance-filtered benchmark for evaluating stability of post-hoc attribution methods, using three metrics (structural similarity, rank correlation, top-k Jaccard) across geometric, photometric, and compression perturbations on ImageNet-1K, MS COCO, and CIFAR-10; Grad-CAM shows highest stability, geometric perturbations are more destabilizing, and ignoring prediction preservation can misrepresent stability (up to 99% of pairs change predictions).
AI.motivation: Current attribution stability studies rely on additive noise and single scalar metrics, conflating model sensitivity with explanation fragility. There is a need for a benchmark that enforces that explanations remain stable when predictions are preserved, and that decomposes stability across multiple perturbation families and datasets.
AI.method: Introduce FASS: apply controlled perturbations (geometric, photometric, compression) to inputs and enforce prediction invariance by filtering perturbed pairs whose predicted label changes are allowed. Compute three complementary metrics on attribution maps (structural similarity, rank correlation, top-k Jaccard of salient regions). Evaluate four attribution methods (Integrated Gradients, GradientSHAP, Grad-CAM, LIME) across four architectures on ImageNet-1K, MS COCO, and CIFAR-10.
AI.result: Stability depends critically on perturbation family and the prediction-preservation filter. Geometric perturbations induce greater attribution instability than photometric ones. Without prediction-preservation filtering, up to 99% of evaluated pairs involve changed predictions. Under the controlled evaluation, Grad-CAM displays the highest stability across datasets.
AI.conclusion: Prediction-invariance filtering plus a multi-metric decomposition yields a robust framework for attribution stability evaluation. The findings reveal method-level trends (Grad-CAM most stable) and underscore the importance of perturbation design in stability assessments for safety-critical vision systems.

title: Improving Role Consistency in Multi-Agent Collaboration via Quantitative Role Clarity
summary: In large language model (LLM)-driven multi-agent systems, disobey role specification (failure to adhere to the defined responsibilities and constraints of an assigned role, potentially leading to an agent behaving like another) is a major failure mode \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2503-13657}. To address this issue, in the present paper, we propose a quantitative role clarity to improve role consistency. Firstly, we construct a role assignment matrix $S(φ)=[s_{ij}(φ)]$, where $s_{ij}(φ)$ is the semantic similarity between the $i$-th agent's behavior trajectory and the $j$-th agent's role description. Then we define role clarity matrix $M(φ)$ as $\text{softmax}(S(φ))-I$, where $\text{softmax}(S(φ))$ is a row-wise softmax of $S(φ)$ and $I$ is the identity matrix. The Frobenius norm of $M(φ)$ quantifies the alignment between agents' role descriptions and their behaviors trajectory. Moreover, we employ the role clarity matrix as a regularizer during lightweight fine-tuning to improve role consistency, thereby improving end-to-end task performance. Experiments on the ChatDev multi-agent system show that our method substantially improves role consistency and task performance: with Qwen and Llama, the role overstepping rate decreases from $46.4\%$ to $8.4\%$ and from $43.4\%$ to $0.2\%$, respectively, and the role clarity score increases from $0.5328$ to $0.9097$ and from $0.5007$ to $0.8530$, respectively, the task success rate increases from $0.6769$ to $0.6909$ and from $0.6174$ to $0.6763$, respectively.

AI.tldr: A quantitative role-clarity framework for LLM-driven multi-agent systems; defines a role-assignment matrix and a derived role-clarity regularizer to align agent behavior with roles, improving role consistency and task performance in ChatDev across Qwen and Llama.
AI.motivation: Disobedience to role specifications is a major failure mode in LLM-driven multi-agent systems; improving role consistency can enhance task coordination and performance.
AI.method: Construct role assignment matrix S(φ) using semantic similarity between each agent's behavior trajectory and each agent's role description. Define role clarity matrix M(φ) = softmax(S(φ)) - I, applying row-wise softmax to S(φ) and subtracting the identity. Use the Frobenius norm of M(φ) as a metric of alignment and employ M(φ) as a regularizer during lightweight fine-tuning to promote role adherence.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on ChatDev with Qwen and Llama shows substantial reductions in role overstepping: from 46.4% to 8.4% (Qwen) and 43.4% to 0.2% (Llama). Role clarity scores improve from 0.5328 to 0.9097 (Qwen) and 0.5007 to 0.8530 (Llama). Task success rates rise from 0.6769 to 0.6909 (Qwen) and 0.6174 to 0.6763 (Llama).
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, regularized approach using a softmax-derived role-clarity matrix meaningfully improves role consistency and end-to-end task performance in multi-agent LLM systems.

title: RAGE: A Tightly Coupled Radar-Aided Grip Estimator For Autonomous Race Cars
summary: Real-time estimation of vehicle-tire-road friction is critical for allowing autonomous race cars to safely and effectively operate at their physical limits. Traditional approaches to measure tire grip often depend on costly, specialized sensors that require custom installation, limiting scalability and deployment. In this work, we introduce RAGE, a novel real-time estimator that simultaneously infers the vehicle velocity, slip angles of the tires and the lateral forces that act on them, using only standard sensors, such as IMUs and RADARs, which are commonly available on most of modern autonomous platforms. We validate our approach through both high-fidelity simulations and real-world experiments conducted on the EAV-24 autonomous race car, demonstrating the accuracy and effectiveness of our method in estimating the vehicle lateral dynamics.

AI.tldr: RAGE: real-time estimation of vehicle velocity, tire slip angles, and lateral forces using only standard sensors (IMU, radar) for autonomous racing; validated in simulations and on EAV-24.
AI.motivation: Real-time tire–road friction estimation is essential for safe operation at the vehicle’s physical limits, but traditional methods depend on costly, specialized sensors; a scalable solution using widely available sensors is needed.
AI.method: A real-time estimator (RAGE) that jointly infers vehicle velocity, tire slip angles, and lateral forces from standard sensors (IMUs and radars), validated through high-fidelity simulations and real-world experiments on the EAV-24 autonomous race car.
AI.result: Demonstrates accuracy and effectiveness in estimating lateral dynamics via simulations and real-world testing.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of real-time lateral-dynamics estimation with standard sensors, enabling scalable deployment for autonomous racing.

title: Overconfidence and Calibration in Medical VQA: Empirical Findings and Hallucination-Aware Mitigation
summary: As vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in clinical decision support, more than accuracy is required: knowing when to trust their predictions is equally critical. Yet, a comprehensive and systematic investigation into the overconfidence of these models remains notably scarce in the medical domain. We address this gap through a comprehensive empirical study of confidence calibration in VLMs, spanning three model families (Qwen3-VL, InternVL3, LLaVA-NeXT), three model scales (2B--38B), and multiple confidence estimation prompting strategies, across three medical visual question answering (VQA) benchmarks. Our study yields three key findings: First, overconfidence persists across model families and is not resolved by scaling or prompting, such as chain-of-thought and verbalized confidence variants. Second, simple post-hoc calibration approaches, such as Platt scaling, reduce calibration error and consistently outperform the prompt-based strategy. Third, due to their (strict) monotonicity, these post-hoc calibration methods are inherently limited in improving the discriminative quality of predictions, leaving AUROC at the same level. Motivated by these findings, we investigate hallucination-aware calibration (HAC), which incorporates vision-grounded hallucination detection signals as complementary inputs to refine confidence estimates. We find that leveraging these hallucination signals improves both calibration and AUROC, with the largest gains on open-ended questions. Overall, our findings suggest post-hoc calibration as standard practice for medical VLM deployment over raw confidence estimates, and highlight the practical usefulness of hallucination signals to enable more reliable use of VLMs in medical VQA.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc calibration (e.g., Platt scaling) improves medical VLM calibration; prompt-based strategies fail to fix overconfidence; hallucination-aware calibration (HAC) improves calibration and AUROC, especially for open-ended questions.
AI.motivation: Medical vision-language models require reliable confidence estimates, not just accuracy, to be safely deployed in decision support. Existing literature in the medical domain lacks a systematic study of overconfidence and calibration for VLMs.
AI.method: Empirical study across three model families (Qwen3-VL, InternVL3, LLaVA-NeXT), three model scales (2B–38B), and multiple confidence-estimation prompting strategies, evaluated on three medical VQA benchmarks. PEG: assess calibration via post-hoc methods (e.g., Platt scaling) and prompt-based confidence variants; introduce hallucination-aware calibration (HAC) that uses vision-grounded hallucination-detection signals as inputs to refine confidence estimates.
AI.result: Overconfidence persists across model families and scales; prompting (including chain-of-thought or verbalized confidence) does not resolve it. Post-hoc calibration reduces calibration error and outperforms prompt-based strategies. However, due to monotonicity, post-hoc methods do not improve discriminative quality (AUROC) substantially. HAC leveraging hallucination signals improves both calibration and AUROC, with larger gains on open-ended questions.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc calibration should be standard practice for medical VLM deployment to obtain reliable confidence estimates, and hallucination signals offer practical gains in calibration and discriminative performance, enabling more trustworthy medical VQA.

title: From Broad Exploration to Stable Synthesis: Entropy-Guided Optimization for Autoregressive Image Generation
summary: Combining Chain-of-Thought (CoT) with Reinforcement Learning (RL) improves text-to-image (T2I) generation, yet the underlying interaction between CoT's exploration and RL's optimization remains unclear. We present a systematic entropy-based analysis that yields three key insights: (1) CoT expands the generative exploration space, while RL contracts it toward high-reward regions; (2) final reward is strongly negatively correlated with both the mean and variance of image-token entropy, highlighting the need to reduce uncertainty and instability; and (3) the entropy of the textual CoT directly governs downstream image quality, with lower-entropy CoTs leading to better generations. Motivated by these findings, we propose Entropy-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (EG-GRPO), a fine-tuning strategy that reallocates optimization budget by uncertainty: low-entropy tokens are excluded from reward-driven updates to preserve stability, while high-entropy tokens receive an entropy bonus that encourages structured exploration without collapse. Experiments on standard T2I benchmarks demonstrate that EG-GRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Entropy-guided fine-tuning of chain-of-thought with reinforcement learning for text-to-image generation; key findings: CoT broadens exploration while RL narrows it to high-reward regions; lower entropy in CoT (and image tokens) correlates with higher rewards and better image quality; introduces EG-GRPO to reallocate optimization by token uncertainty, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: To clarify how CoT exploration interacts with RL optimization in text-to-image generation, quantify the role of token entropy, and address instability and suboptimal exploration.
AI.method: Systematic entropy-based analysis of CoT and image-token distributions; development of Entropy-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (EG-GRPO) which excludes low-entropy tokens from reward updates and adds entropy bonuses to high-entropy tokens; empirical evaluation on standard T2I benchmarks.
AI.result: Final reward is negatively correlated with mean and variance of image-token entropy; lower-entropy CoTs predict better downstream image quality; EG-GRPO yields state-of-the-art performance on standard T2I benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-aware reallocation of optimization in CoT+RL improves stability and image quality; controlling textual entropy is crucial for high-quality T2I generation; the proposed EG-GRPO effectively harnesses entropy guidance to balance exploration and exploitation.

title: CharTool: Tool-Integrated Visual Reasoning for Chart Understanding
summary: Charts are ubiquitous in scientific and financial literature for presenting structured data. However, chart reasoning remains challenging for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) due to the lack of high-quality training data, as well as the need for fine-grained visual grounding and precise numerical computation. To address these challenges, we first propose DuoChart, a scalable dual-source data pipeline that combines synthesized charts with real-world charts to construct diverse, high-quality chart training data. We then introduce CharTool, which equips MLLMs with external tools, including image cropping for localized visual perception and code-based computation for accurate numerical reasoning. Through agentic reinforcement learning on DuoChart, CharTool learns tool-integrated reasoning grounded in chart content. Extensive experiments on six chart benchmarks show that our method consistently improves over strong MLLM baselines across model scales. Notably, CharTool-7B outperforms the base model by **+8.0%** on CharXiv (Reasoning) and **+9.78%** on ChartQAPro, while achieving competitive performance with substantially larger or proprietary models. Moreover, CharTool demonstrates positive generalization to out-of-domain visual math reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DuoChart combines synthesized and real-world charts to create diverse, high-quality chart training data; CharTool equips MLLMs with tools (image cropping for localized perception and code-based computation for accurate numerical reasoning); via agentic RL on DuoChart, tool-integrated reasoning grounded in chart content improves performance across benchmarks; CharTool-7B achieves notable gains (+8.0% CharXiv Reasoning, +9.78% ChartQAPro) and generalizes to out-of-domain visual math reasoning.
AI.motivation: Overcome scarcity of high-quality chart reasoning data and the need for precise visual grounding and numerical computation in multimodal LLMs.
AI.method: DuoChart: scalable dual-source data pipeline combining synthesized and real-world charts. CharTool: external tools (image cropping for localized vision; code-based computation for arithmetic). Agentic reinforcement learning on DuoChart to learn tool-integrated, chart-grounded reasoning.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over strong baselines across model scales on six chart benchmarks. CharTool-7B outperforms the base model by +8.0% on CharXiv (Reasoning) and +9.78% on ChartQAPro, while remaining competitive with substantially larger or proprietary models. Demonstrates positive generalization to out-of-domain visual math reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Tool-augmented, data-efficient chart reasoning is achievable with dual-source data and integrated external tools, yielding solid cross-benchmark gains and generalization.

title: Learning Task-Invariant Properties via Dreamer: Enabling Efficient Policy Transfer for Quadruped Robots
comment: Accepted by IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: Achieving quadruped robot locomotion across diverse and dynamic terrains presents significant challenges, primarily due to the discrepancies between simulation environments and real-world conditions. Traditional sim-to-real transfer methods often rely on manual feature design or costly real-world fine-tuning. To address these limitations, this paper proposes the DreamTIP framework, which incorporates Task-Invariant Properties learning within the Dreamer world model architecture to enhance sim-to-real transfer capabilities. Guided by large language models, DreamTIP identifies and leverages Task-Invariant Properties, such as contact stability and terrain clearance, which exhibit robustness to dynamic variations and strong transferability across tasks. These properties are integrated into the world model as auxiliary prediction targets, enabling the policy to learn representations that are insensitive to underlying dynamic changes. Furthermore, an efficient adaptation strategy is designed, employing a mixed replay buffer and regularization constraints to rapidly calibrate to real-world dynamics while effectively mitigating representation collapse and catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments on complex terrains, including Stair, Climb, Tilt, and Crawl, demonstrate that DreamTIP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both simulated and real-world environments. Our method achieves an average performance improvement of 28.1% across eight distinct simulated transfer tasks. In the real-world Climb task, the baseline method achieved only a 10\ success rate, whereas our method attained a 100% success rate. These results indicate that incorporating Task-Invariant Properties into Dreamer learning offers a novel solution for achieving robust and transferable robot locomotion.

AI.tldr: DreamTIP adds Task-Invariant Properties (TIP) into the Dreamer world model to boost sim-to-real transfer for quadruped locomotion by learning robust, task-invariant representations and using an efficient adaptation strategy.
AI.motivation: Address the sim-to-real gap in legged robots, avoiding manual feature design and expensive real-world fine-tuning by leveraging robust, transferable properties.
AI.method: Identify Task-Invariant Properties with guidance from large language models; incorporate TIP as auxiliary prediction targets within the Dreamer world model; employ a mixed replay buffer and regularization to adapt to real dynamics and avoid representation collapse and forgetting.
AI.result: Significant performance gains: ~28.1% average improvement over eight simulated transfer tasks; real-world Climb task shows 100% success vs 10% for the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Integrating Task-Invariant Properties into Dreamer enables robust, transferable locomotion across diverse terrains and demonstrates the effectiveness of guided, auxiliary-task representations and efficient adaptation for sim-to-real transfer.

title: Contrastive Language-Colored Pointmap Pretraining for Unified 3D Scene Understanding
summary: Pretraining 3D encoders by aligning with Contrastive Language Image Pretraining (CLIP) has emerged as a promising direction to learn generalizable representations for 3D scene understanding. In this paper, we propose UniScene3D, a transformer-based encoder that learns unified scene representations from multi-view colored pointmaps, jointly modeling image appearance and geometry. For robust colored pointmap representation learning, we introduce novel cross-view geometric alignment and grounded view alignment to enforce cross-view geometry and semantic consistency. Extensive low-shot and task-specific fine-tuning evaluations on viewpoint grounding, scene retrieval, scene type classification, and 3D VQA demonstrate our state-of-the-art performance. These results highlight the effectiveness of our approach for unified 3D scene understanding. https://yebulabula.github.io/UniScene3D/

AI.tldr: UniScene3D trains a transformer-based 3D encoder that learns unified representations from multi-view colored pointmaps by aligning with CLIP. It uses cross-view geometric alignment and grounded view alignment to enforce geometry and semantic consistency, achieving state-of-the-art results in low-shot and fine-tuned tasks like viewpoint grounding, scene retrieval, scene type classification, and 3D VQA.
AI.motivation: There is a need for unified 3D scene representations that simultaneously capture appearance and geometry. Prior 3D encoders often lack cross-view consistency and do not leverage strong cross-modal pretraining such as CLIP. By aligning 3D representations with CLIP and enforcing cross-view geometric and semantic consistency, the model can generalize better across tasks and viewpoints.
AI.method: A transformer-based encoder (UniScene3D) processes multi-view colored pointmaps to produce unified scene representations. It introduces: (1) cross-view geometric alignment to enforce geometric consistency across views; (2) grounded view alignment to enforce semantic consistency across views; (3) CLIP-like pretraining objectives to align 3D representations with 2D image appearance and language semantics, enabling unified learning of appearance and geometry.
AI.result: Extensive low-shot and task-specific fine-tuning experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on viewpoint grounding, scene retrieval, scene type classification, and 3D VQA, validating the effectiveness of the unified 3D understanding approach.
AI.conclusion: UniScene3D shows that a CLIP-aligned, transformer-based 3D encoder with cross-view geometric and grounded semantic alignment can learn robust, unified scene representations applicable across multiple 3D understanding tasks.

title: YC Bench: a Live Benchmark for Forecasting Startup Outperformance in Y Combinator Batches
summary: Forecasting startup success is notoriously difficult, partly because meaningful outcomes, such as exits, large funding rounds, and sustained revenue growth, are rare and can take years to materialize. As a result, signals are sparse and evaluation cycles are slow. Y Combinator batches offer a unique mitigation: each batch comprises around 200 startups, funded simultaneously, with evaluation at Demo Day only three months later. We introduce YC Bench, a live benchmark for forecasting early outperformance within YC batches. Using the YC W26 batch as a case study (196 startups), we measure outperformance with a Pre-Demo Day Score, a KPI combining publicly available traction signals and web visibility. This short-term metric enables rapid evaluation of forecasting models. As a baseline, we take Google mentions prior to the YC W26 application deadline, a simple proxy for prior brand recognition, recovering 6 of 11 top performers at YC Demo Day (55% recall). YC Bench provides a live benchmark for studying startup success forecasting, with iteration cycles measured in months rather than years. Code and Data are available on GitHub: https://github.com/benstaf/ycbench

AI.tldr: YC Bench is a live benchmark for forecasting early startup outperformance within YC batches, using a short-term Pre-Demo Day Score that blends traction signals and web visibility; a Google mentions baseline yields 55% recall (6 of 11 top performers); code/data available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Forecasting startup success is difficult due to sparse meaningful outcomes and long evaluation cycles. YC batches provide a controlled, rapid evaluation environment (≈200 startups per batch, Demo Day in ~3 months).
AI.method: Define a Pre-Demo Day Score (PDS) from publicly available traction signals and web visibility; apply to the YC W26 batch (196 startups) to forecast Demo Day outperformance; compare against a simple baseline (Google mentions prior to the application deadline) to assess predictive power.
AI.result: Baseline Google mentions recover 6 of 11 top performers at Demo Day (55% recall). YC Bench enables live, rapid iteration of forecasting models on a monthly-to-quarterly cycle; dataset and code are available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: YC Bench provides a live, fast-cycle benchmarking platform for startup success forecasting, enabling rapid model evaluation and reproducible research via open-source code and data.

title: ESL-Bench: An Event-Driven Synthetic Longitudinal Benchmark for Health Agents
summary: Longitudinal health agents must reason across multi-source trajectories that combine continuous device streams, sparse clinical exams, and episodic life events - yet evaluating them is hard: real-world data cannot be released at scale, and temporally grounded attribution questions seldom admit definitive answers without structured ground truth. We present ESL-Bench, an event-driven synthesis framework and benchmark providing 100 synthetic users, each with a 1-5 year trajectory comprising a health profile, a multi-phase narrative plan, daily device measurements, periodic exam records, and an event log with explicit per-indicator impact parameters. Each indicator follows a baseline stochastic process driven by discrete events with sigmoid-onset, exponential-decay kernels under saturation and projection constraints; a hybrid pipeline delegates sparse semantic artifacts to LLM-based planning and dense indicator dynamics to algorithmic simulation with hard physiological bounds. Users are each paired with 100 evaluation queries across five dimensions - Lookup, Trend, Comparison, Anomaly, Explanation - stratified into Easy, Medium, and Hard tiers, with all ground-truth answers programmatically computable from the recorded event-indicator relationships. Evaluating 13 methods spanning LLMs with tools, DB-native agents, and memory-augmented RAG, we find that DB agents (48-58%) substantially outperform memory RAG baselines (30-38%), with the gap concentrated on Comparison and Explanation queries where multi-hop reasoning and evidence attribution are required.

AI.tldr: Synthetic ESL-Bench benchmark enables evaluation of longitudinal health-agent reasoning on multi-source data with ground-truth event-indicator relations; DB agents outperform memory RAG, especially on multi-hop tasks.
AI.motivation: Address evaluation challenges for longitudinal health agents by providing scalable, ground-truth synthetic data with explicit event-indicator relationships and diverse query types.
AI.method: Generate 100 synthetic users with 1-5 year trajectories; indicators follow baseline stochastic processes with sigmoid-onset, exponential-decay kernels under saturation and projection constraints; hybrid pipeline uses LLM planning for sparse semantics and algorithmic simulation for dense dynamics with hard physiological bounds; evaluate 13 methods across 5 query dimensions (Lookup, Trend, Comparison, Anomaly, Explanation) stratified into Easy/Medium/Hard; ground-truth answers computed from event-indicator relationships.
AI.result: DB-native agents achieve 48-58% accuracy vs memory RAG 30-38%; the performance gap is largest on Comparison and Explanation queries requiring multi-hop reasoning and evidence attribution.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the value of an event-driven synthetic benchmark for evaluating longitudinal health agents and clarifies where different architectures (DB agents vs RAG) excel, enabling reproducible, ground-truth evaluation.

title: Open-Loop Planning, Closed-Loop Verification: Speculative Verification for VLA
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, as large foundation models for embodied control, have shown strong performance in manipulation tasks. However, their performance comes at high inference cost. To improve efficiency, recent methods adopt action chunking, which predicts a sequence of future actions for open-loop execution. Although effective for reducing computation, open-loop execution is sensitive to environmental changes and prone to error accumulation due to the lack of close-loop feedback. To address this limitation, we propose Speculative Verification for VLA Control (SV-VLA), a framework that combines efficient open-loop long-horizon planning with lightweight closed-loop online verification. Specifically, SV-VLA uses a heavy VLA as a low-frequency macro-planner to generate an action chunk together with a planning context, while a lightweight verifier continuously monitors execution based on the latest observations. Conditioned on both the current observation and the planning context, the verifier compares the planned action against a closed-loop reference action and triggers replanning only when necessary. Experiments demonstrate that SV-VLA combines the efficiency of chunked prediction with the robustness of closed-loop control, enabling efficient and reliable VLA-based control in dynamic environments. Code is available: https://github.com/edsad122/SV-VLA.

AI.tldr: SV-VLA combines open-loop long-horizon planning with a lightweight online verifier to maintain robustness in dynamic environments, achieving efficient VLA control through action chunking while enabling replanning only when necessary.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference cost of Vision-Language-Action models and mitigate error accumulation in open-loop chunked predictions by introducing a lightweight closed-loop verification mechanism.
AI.method: A heavy Vision-Language-Action model serves as a macro-planner to generate an action chunk and planning context. A lightweight verifier continuously monitors execution using current observations, comparing the planned action to a closed-loop reference action and triggering replanning only when discrepancies are detected.
AI.result: Experimental results show that SV-VLA preserves the efficiency of chunked prediction while improving robustness via lightweight online verification, enabling efficient and reliable VLA-based control in dynamic environments. Code is released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Speculative verification enables a practical balance between open-loop efficiency and closed-loop robustness in VLA control, suggesting a viable direction for scalable embodied AI systems.

title: WSVD: Weighted Low-Rank Approximation for Fast and Efficient Execution of Low-Precision Vision-Language Models
summary: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) has become an important technique for reducing the computational burden of Vision Language Models (VLMs), which play a central role in tasks such as image captioning and visual question answering. Although multiple prior works have proposed efficient SVD variants to enable low-rank operations, we find that in practice it remains difficult to achieve substantial latency reduction during model execution. To address this limitation, we introduce a new computational pattern and apply SVD at a finer granularity, enabling real and measurable improvements in execution latency.
