Furthermore, recognizing that weight elements differ in their relative importance, we adaptively allocate relative importance to each element during SVD process to better preserve accuracy, then extend this framework with quantization applied to both weights and activations, resulting in a highly efficient VLM. Collectively, we introduce~\textit{Weighted SVD} (WSVD), which outperforms other approaches by achieving over $1.8\times$ decoding speedup while preserving accuracy. We open source our code at: \href{https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/WSVD}{\texttt{https://github.com/SAI-Lab-NYU/WSVD}

AI.tldr: Weighted SVD (WSVD) introduces a finer-grained SVD-based compression for Vision-Language Models, using adaptive element-wise importance weighting and quantization to accelerate decoding while preserving accuracy; it achieves ~1.8x speedup and is open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Standard SVD-based acceleration struggles to deliver substantial latency reductions in practice for VLMs; a finer-grained pattern and importance-aware weighting can better preserve accuracy under compression.
AI.method: Proposes a new computational pattern and apply SVD at a finer granularity; adaptively allocate relative importance to each weight element during the SVD process; extend with quantization for weights and activations; result is WSVD.
AI.result: WSVD yields over 1.8× decoding speedup with preserved accuracy, outperforming other approaches; code released.
AI.conclusion: WSVD demonstrates that adaptive, fine-grained SVD with quantization can substantially accelerate VLMs in a practical manner, offering a viable deployment-ready approach.

title: Dynamical structure of vanishing gradient and overfitting in multi-layer perceptrons
summary: Vanishing gradient and overfitting are two of the most extensively studied problems in the literature about machine learning. However, they are frequently considered in some asymptotic setting, which obscure the underlying dynamical mechanisms responsible for their emergence. In this paper, we aim to provide a clear dynamical description of learning in multi-layer perceptrons. To this end, we introduce a minimal model, inspired by studies by Fukumizu and Amari, to investigate vanishing gradients and overfitting in MLPs trained via gradient descent. Within this model, we show that the learning dynamics may pass through plateau regions and near-optimal regions during training, both of which consist of saddle structures, before ultimately converging to the overfitting region. Under suitable conditions on the training dataset, we prove that, with high probability, the overfitting region collapses to a single attractor modulo symmetry, which corresponds to the overfitting. Moreover, we show that any MLP trained on a finite noisy dataset cannot converge to the theoretical optimum and instead necessarily converges to an overfitting solution.

AI.tldr: A minimal dynamical model for MLPs under gradient descent reveals that learning trajectories traverse saddle- and plateau-like regions before converging to an overfitting attractor; with finite noisy data, the optimum is unattainable and overfitting is the default outcome.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamical mechanisms behind vanishing gradients and overfitting in multi-layer perceptrons beyond asymptotic analyses, by providing a tractable dynamical description of gradient-based learning.
AI.method: Introduce a minimal model (inspired by Fukumizu and Amari) to study gradient-descent training of MLPs. Analyze the resulting dynamics to identify plateau and near-optimal saddle regions, and prove that the overfitting region collapses to a single attractor (modulo symmetry) under suitable data assumptions. Show that with finite noisy data, convergence to the theoretical optimum is impossible, and the algorithm converges to an overfitting solution.
AI.result: The dynamics can pass through saddle/plateau regions before reaching an overfitting attractor. Under certain data conditions, the overfitting region becomes a unique attractor (up to symmetries). Finite noisy datasets prevent convergence to the theoretical optimum, forcing convergence to overfitting.
AI.conclusion: Learning dynamics of MLPs under gradient descent are governed by saddle structures and attractoried overfitting states; for finite noisy data, overfitting is inevitable and inevitable optimum is unattainable, highlighting a dynamical mechanism for vanishing gradients and overfitting.

title: EMS: Multi-Agent Voting via Efficient Majority-then-Stopping
summary: Majority voting is the standard for aggregating multi-agent responses into a final decision. However, traditional methods typically require all agents to complete their reasoning before aggregation begins, leading to significant computational overhead, as many responses become redundant once a majority consensus is achieved. In this work, we formulate the multi-agent voting as a reliability-aware agent scheduling problem, and propose an Efficient Majority-then-Stopping (EMS) to improve reasoning efficiency. EMS prioritizes agents based on task-aware reliability and terminates the reasoning pipeline the moment a majority is achieved from the following three critical components. Specifically, we introduce Agent Confidence Modeling (ACM) to estimate agent reliability using historical performance and semantic similarity, Adaptive Incremental Voting (AIV) to sequentially select agents with early stopping, and Individual Confidence Updating (ICU) to dynamically update the reliability of each contributing agent. Extensive evaluations across six benchmarks demonstrate that EMS consistently reduces the average number of invoked agents by 32%.

AI.tldr: Efficient majority-then-stopping (EMS) reduces multi-agent reasoning cost by 32% by stopping once a majority is reached, using reliability-aware scheduling.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational overhead in aggregating multi-agent responses by avoiding unnecessary reasoning after a majority consensus is formed, rather than requiring all agents to complete.
AI.method: EMS introduces three components: (1) Agent Confidence Modeling (ACM) to estimate agent reliability from historical performance and semantic similarity; (2) Adaptive Incremental Voting (AIV) to sequentially select agents with early stopping; (3) Individual Confidence Updating (ICU) to dynamically update each agent's reliability during the process.
AI.result: EMS reduces the average number of invoked agents by 32% across six benchmarks, demonstrating improved reasoning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A reliability-aware, majority-based stopping framework (EMS) can substantially improve efficiency in multi-agent reasoning with minimal delay to consensus, aided by ACM, AIV, and ICU.

title: A Flow Matching Framework for Soft-Robot Inverse Dynamics
summary: Learning the inverse dynamics of soft continuum robots remains challenging due to high-dimensional nonlinearities and complex actuation coupling. Conventional feedback-based controllers often suffer from control chattering due to corrective oscillations, while deterministic regression-based learners struggle to capture the complex nonlinear mappings required for accurate dynamic tracking. Motivated by these limitations, we propose an inverse-dynamics framework for open-loop feedforward control that learns the system's differential dynamics as a generative transport map. Specifically, inverse dynamics is reformulated as a conditional flow-matching problem, and Rectified Flow (RF) is adopted as a lightweight instance to generate physically consistent control inputs rather than conditional averages. Two variants are introduced to further enhance physical consistency: RF-Physical, utilizing a physics-based prior for residual modeling; and RF-FWD, integrating a forward-dynamics consistency loss during flow matching. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our framework reduces trajectory tracking RMSE by over 50% compared to standard regression baselines (MLP, LSTM, Transformer). The system sustains stable open-loop execution at a peak end-effector velocity of 1.14 m/s with sub-millisecond inference latency (0.995 ms). This work demonstrates flow matching as a robust, high-performance paradigm for learning differential inverse dynamics in soft robotic systems.

AI.tldr: Reframes inverse dynamics of soft continuum robots as a conditional flow-matching task using Rectified Flow to generate physically consistent feedforward control, with two physics-informed variants, achieving significant RMSE reductions and millisecond-scale inference.
AI.motivation: Overcome chattering in feedback controllers and the difficulty of capturing high-dimensional nonlinear dynamics with deterministic regressors; enable accurate, fast open-loop trajectory tracking for soft robots through a learned transport map.
AI.method: Formulates inverse dynamics as a conditional flow-matching problem. Uses Rectified Flow (RF) as a lightweight generator to produce control inputs. Introduces two variants: RF-Physical (physics-based residual prior) and RF-FWD (forward-dynamics consistency loss) to improve physical realism.
AI.result: Achieves >50% reduction in trajectory tracking RMSE compared with MLP, LSTM, and Transformer baselines. Demonstrates stable open-loop execution at 1.14 m/s peak end-effector velocity with ~1 ms inference latency.
AI.conclusion: Flow matching is an effective, high-performance paradigm for learning differential inverse dynamics in soft robots, offering robust open-loop control with fast, physically consistent predictions.

title: FusionBERT: Multi-View Image-3D Retrieval via Cross-Attention Visual Fusion and Normal-Aware 3D Encoder
summary: We propose FusionBERT, a novel multi-view visual fusion framework for image-3D multimodal retrieval. Existing image-3D representation learning methods predominantly focus on feature alignment of a single object image and its 3D model, limiting their applicability in realistic scenarios where an object is typically observed and captured from multiple viewpoints. Although multi-view observations naturally provide complementary geometric and appearance cues, existing multimodal large models rarely explore how to effectively fuse such multi-view visual information for better cross-modal retrieval. To address this limitation, we introduce a multi-view image-3D retrieval framework named FusionBERT, which innovatively utilizes a cross-attention-based multi-view visual aggregator to adaptively integrate features from multi-view images of an object. The proposed multi-view visual encoder fuses inter-view complementary relationships and selectively emphasizes informative visual cues across multiple views to get a more robustly fused visual feature for better 3D model matching. Furthermore, FusionBERT proposes a normal-aware 3D model encoder that can further enhance the 3D geometric feature of an object model by jointly encoding point normals and 3D positions, enabling a more robust representation learning for textureless or color-degraded 3D models. Extensive image-3D retrieval experiments demonstrate that FusionBERT achieves significantly higher retrieval accuracy than SOTA multimodal large models under both single-view and multi-view settings, establishing a strong baseline for multi-view multimodal retrieval.

AI.tldr: FusionBERT introduces a multi-view image-3D retrieval framework that fuses features from multiple object views via a cross-attention-based visual aggregator and uses a normal-aware 3D encoder to enhance geometry—yielding state-of-the-art retrieval performance in both single-view and multi-view settings.
AI.motivation: Current image-3D retrieval methods largely rely on single-view representation, missing complementary cues from multiple viewpoints in realistic scenarios; effective multi-view fusion is needed to improve cross-modal retrieval robustness.
AI.method: A cross-attention-based multi-view visual aggregator integrates features from multiple views; a multi-view visual encoder captures inter-view relationships and emphasizes informative cues; a normal-aware 3D model encoder encodes point normals together with 3D positions to strengthen textureless/color-degraded 3D models.
AI.result: Experimental results show FusionBERT achieves significantly higher retrieval accuracy than state-of-the-art multimodal large models under both single-view and multi-view settings, establishing a strong baseline for multi-view multimodal retrieval.
AI.conclusion: FusionBERT provides robust cross-view fusion for image-3D retrieval, improving 3D geometric feature quality and enabling better performance in challenging scenarios; it sets a strong baseline for future multi-view multimodal retrieval research.

title: Self-Directed Task Identification
summary: In this work, we present a novel machine learning framework called Self-Directed Task Identification (SDTI), which enables models to autonomously identify the correct target variable for each dataset in a zero-shot setting without pre-training. SDTI is a minimal, interpretable framework demonstrating the feasibility of repurposing core machine learning concepts for a novel task structure. To our knowledge, no existing architectures have demonstrated this ability. Traditional approaches lack this capability, leaving data annotation as a time-consuming process that relies heavily on human effort. Using only standard neural network components, we show that SDTI can be achieved through appropriate problem formulation and architectural design. We evaluate the proposed framework on a range of benchmark tasks and demonstrate its effectiveness in reliably identifying the ground truth out of a set of potential target variables. SDTI outperformed baseline architectures by 14% in F1 score on synthetic task identification benchmarks. These proof-of-concept experiments highlight the future potential of SDTI to reduce dependence on manual annotation and to enhance the scalability of autonomous learning systems in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Self-Directed Task Identification (SDTI) is a zero-shot, minimal, interpretable framework enabling models to autonomously identify the correct target variable for each dataset without pre-training, achieving improvement on synthetic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on manual data annotation by enabling autonomous identification of the ground-truth target variable; address the gap where traditional architectures cannot select targets without prior training.
AI.method: A zero-shot framework using standard neural network components with a carefully designed problem formulation and architecture to allow autonomous target-variable identification without pre-training; emphasizes interpretability and minimalism.
AI.result: SDTI outperformed baseline architectures by 14% in F1 score on synthetic task identification benchmarks and reliably identified the ground-truth target among multiple candidates across a range of synthetic tasks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of repurposing core ML concepts for a novel task structure; suggests potential to reduce manual annotation and enhance scalability of autonomous learning systems in real-world applications.

title: Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning for Tool-Calling Agents with Iterative Reward Calibration
summary: Training tool-calling agents with reinforcement learning on multi-turn tasks remains challenging due to sparse outcome rewards and difficult credit assignment across conversation turns. We present the first application of MT-GRPO (Multi-Turn Group Relative Policy Optimization) combined with GTPO (Generalized Token-level Policy Optimization) for training a tool-calling agent on realistic customer service tasks with an LLM-based user simulator. Through systematic analysis of training rollouts, we discover that naively designed dense per-turn rewards degrade performance by up to 14 percentage points due to misalignment between reward discriminativeness and advantage direction. We introduce Iterative Reward Calibration, a methodology for designing per-turn rewards using empirical discriminative analysis of rollout data, and show that our GTPO hybrid advantage formulation eliminates the advantage misalignment problem. Applied to the Tau-Bench airline benchmark, our approach improves Qwen3.5-4B from 63.8 percent to 66.7 percent (+2.9pp) and Qwen3-30B-A3B from 58.0 percent to 69.5 percent (+11.5pp) -- with the trained 4B model exceeding GPT-4.1 (49.4 percent) and GPT-4o (42.8 percent) despite being 50 times smaller, and the 30.5B MoE model approaching Claude Sonnet 4.5 (70.0 percent). To our knowledge, these are the first published RL training results on Tau-Bench. We release our code, reward calibration analysis, and training recipes.

AI.tldr: RL training for tool-calling agents via MT-GRPO+GTPO with Iterative Reward Calibration on Tau-Bench; yields state-of-the-art results with smaller models; code and calibration tools released.
AI.motivation: Training tool-calling agents in multi-turn dialogues suffers from sparse rewards and credit assignment across turns. Dense per-turn rewards can degrade performance due to misalignment between reward discriminativeness and the policy advantage direction.
AI.method: Combine MT-GRPO (Multi-Turn Group Relative Policy Optimization) with GTPO (Generalized Token-level Policy Optimization); introduce Iterative Reward Calibration using empirical rollout discriminative analysis; apply a GTPO hybrid advantage to fix misalignment; evaluate on Tau-Bench airline tasks with an LLM-based user simulator; release code and training recipes.
AI.result: On Tau-Bench: Qwen3.5-4B improves from 63.8% to 66.7% (+2.9pp); Qwen3-30B-A3B improves from 58.0% to 69.5% (+11.5pp). The 4B model exceeds GPT-4.1 (49.4%) and GPT-4o (42.8%) despite being ~50x smaller; the 30.5B MoE model approaches Claude Sonnet 4.5 (70.0%). First published RL results on Tau-Bench.
AI.conclusion: Iterative Reward Calibration with GTPO resolves the reward-advantage misalignment and enables effective RL training for tool-calling agents; the approach yields strong Tau-Bench performance with smaller models and is accompanied by open-sourced code and training recipes.

title: Asymptotically-Bounded 3D Frontier Exploration enhanced with Bayesian Information Gain
comment: Submitted for review to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Robotic exploration in large-scale environments is computationally demanding due to the high overhead of processing extensive frontiers. This article presents an OctoMap-based frontier exploration algorithm with predictable, asymptotically bounded performance. Unlike conventional methods whose complexity scales with environment size, our approach maintains a complexity of $\mathcal{O}(|\mathcal{F}|)$, where $|\mathcal{F}|$ is the number of frontiers. This is achieved through strategic forward and inverse sensor modeling, which enables approximate yet efficient frontier detection and maintenance. To further enhance performance, we integrate a Bayesian regressor to estimate information gain, circumventing the need to explicitly count unknown voxels when prioritizing viewpoints. Simulations show the proposed method is more computationally efficient than the existing OctoMap-based methods and achieves computational efficiency comparable to baselines that are independent of OctoMap. Specifically, the Bayesian-enhanced framework achieves up to a $54\%$ improvement in total exploration time compared to standard deterministic frontier-based baselines across varying spatial scales, while guaranteeing task completion. Real-world experiments confirm the computational bounds as well as the effectiveness of the proposed enhancement.

AI.tldr: An OctoMap-based frontier exploration framework with O(|F|) complexity, leveraging forward/inverse sensor modeling and a Bayesian information gain estimator to prioritize viewpoints; shows up to 54% reduction in exploration time and is validated in simulation and real-world experiments, with guarantees on task completion.
AI.motivation: Frontier-based exploration in large-scale environments is computationally demanding due to processing extensive frontiers. The work seeks scalable, bounded-performance exploration with practical guarantees.
AI.method: OctoMap-based frontier detection and maintenance with complexity linear in the number of frontiers |F|, achieved via forward and inverse sensor modeling for approximate frontier detection; introduces a Bayesian regressor to estimate information gain to prioritize viewpoints without explicit unknown voxel counting.
AI.result: Simulations indicate the method is more computationally efficient than existing OctoMap-based methods and comparable to baselines independent of OctoMap; Bayesian enhancement yields up to 54% faster total exploration time across varying spatial scales, with task completion guaranteed; real-world experiments validate the computational bounds and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides predictable, asymptotically bounded performance for large-scale exploration, combining OctoMap-based frontier handling with Bayesian gain estimation for efficiency, and is validated by simulations and real-world tests.

title: TrackerSplat: Exploiting Point Tracking for Fast and Robust Dynamic 3D Gaussians Reconstruction
summary: Recent advancements in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have demonstrated its potential for efficient and photorealistic 3D reconstructions, which is crucial for diverse applications such as robotics and immersive media. However, current Gaussian-based methods for dynamic scene reconstruction struggle with large inter-frame displacements, leading to artifacts and temporal inconsistencies under fast object motions. To address this, we introduce \textit{TrackerSplat}, a novel method that integrates advanced point tracking methods to enhance the robustness and scalability of 3DGS for dynamic scene reconstruction. TrackerSplat utilizes off-the-shelf point tracking models to extract pixel trajectories and triangulate per-view pixel trajectories onto 3D Gaussians to guide the relocation, rotation, and scaling of Gaussians before training. This strategy effectively handles large displacements between frames, dramatically reducing the fading and recoloring artifacts prevalent in prior methods. By accurately positioning Gaussians prior to gradient-based optimization, TrackerSplat overcomes the quality degradation associated with large frame gaps when processing multiple adjacent frames in parallel across multiple devices, thereby boosting reconstruction throughput while preserving rendering quality. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm the robustness of TrackerSplat in challenging scenarios with significant displacements, achieving superior throughput under parallel settings and maintaining visual quality compared to baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/yindaheng98/TrackerSplat.

AI.tldr: TrackerSplat integrates off-the-shelf 2D/3D point tracking with 3D Gaussian splatting to robustly relocate and transform Gaussians across frames, enabling dynamic 3D reconstructions with large inter-frame displacements and improved throughput.
AI.motivation: Current 3D Gaussian splatting for dynamic scenes suffers from artifacts and temporal inconsistency under large inter-frame displacements; there is a need for robust, scalable reconstruction that remains high-quality with fast object motions.
AI.method: Use pre-trained point tracking models to extract pixel trajectories; triangulate per-view trajectories onto 3D Gaussians to guide relocation, rotation, and scaling prior to optimization; enable parallel processing across frames/devices to improve throughput while reducing artifacts from large frame gaps.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world datasets shows robustness to large displacements, higher throughput under parallel settings, and maintained visual quality compared with baselines; code released.
AI.conclusion: TrackerSplat effectively mitigates artifacts caused by large frame gaps in dynamic 3D Gaussian splatting, improving both reconstruction quality and throughput, and demonstrates practical applicability with open-source code.

title: Mitigating Data Scarcity in Spaceflight Applications for Offline Reinforcement Learning Using Physics-Informed Deep Generative Models
summary: The deployment of reinforcement learning (RL)-based controllers on physical systems is often limited by poor generalization to real-world scenarios, known as the simulation-to-reality (sim-to-real) gap. This gap is particularly challenging in spaceflight, where real-world training data are scarce due to high cost and limited planetary exploration data. Traditional approaches, such as system identification and synthetic data generation, depend on sufficient data and often fail due to modeling assumptions or lack of physics-based constraints. We propose addressing this data scarcity by introducing physics-based learning bias in a generative model. Specifically, we develop the Mutual Information-based Split Variational Autoencoder (MI-VAE), a physics-informed VAE that learns differences between observed system trajectories and those predicted by physics-based models. The latent space of the MI-VAE enables generation of synthetic datasets that respect physical constraints. We evaluate MI-VAE on a planetary lander problem, focusing on limited real-world data and offline RL training. Results show that augmenting datasets with MI-VAE samples significantly improves downstream RL performance, outperforming standard VAEs in statistical fidelity, sample diversity, and policy success rate. This work demonstrates a scalable strategy for enhancing autonomous controller robustness in complex, data-constrained environments.

AI.tldr: Proposes MI-VAE, a physics-informed VAE using mutual information to capture residuals between data and physics models, generating physics-consistent synthetic data to improve offline RL in data-scarce spaceflight tasks.
AI.motivation: Sim-to-real gap and data scarcity in spaceflight; traditional system identification and synthetic data methods rely on strong modeling assumptions and may fail without sufficient data or physics constraints.
AI.method: Mutual Information-based Split Variational Autoencoder (MI-VAE) that learns differences (residuals) between observed trajectories and physics-based model predictions; latent space encodes residuals; latent samples generate synthetic trajectories that respect physical constraints; evaluated on a planetary lander problem with offline RL.
AI.result: Incorporating MI-VAE-generated data significantly improves downstream RL performance and outperforms standard VAEs in statistical fidelity, dataset diversity, and policy success rate.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable approach for enhancing autonomous controller robustness in data-constrained, complex environments by leveraging physics-informed generative modeling to augment training data.

title: Analysis of Optimality of Large Language Models on Planning Problems
summary: Classic AI planning problems have been revisited in the Large Language Model (LLM) era, with a focus of recent benchmarks on success rates rather than plan efficiency. We examine the degree to which frontier models reason optimally versus relying on simple, heuristic, and possibly inefficient strategies. We focus on the Blocksworld domain involving towers of labeled blocks which have to be moved from an initial to a goal configuration via a set of primitive actions. We also study a formally equivalent task, the generalized Path-Star ($P^*$) graph, in order to isolate true topological reasoning from semantic priors. We systematically manipulate problem depth (the height of block towers), width (the number of towers), and compositionality (the number of goal blocks). Reasoning-enhanced LLMs significantly outperform traditional satisficing planners (e.g., LAMA) in complex, multi-goal configurations. Although classical search algorithms hit a wall as the search space expands, LLMs track theoretical optimality limits with near-perfect precision, even when domain-specific semantic hints are stripped away. To explain these surprising findings, we consider (and find evidence to support) two hypotheses: an active Algorithmic Simulation executed via reasoning tokens and a Geometric Memory that allows models to represent the $P^*$ topology as a navigable global geometry, effectively bypassing exponential combinatorial complexity.

AI.tldr: Frontier LLMs show near-optimal planning in Blocksworld and P* graphs, outperforming satisficing planners in large, multi-goal settings, aided by active reasoning simulation and a geometric memory of topology.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLM planning success reflects genuine optimization rather than heuristics, and isolate topological reasoning from semantic priors in planning tasks.
AI.method: Systematically vary problem depth (tower height), width (number of towers), and compositionality (number of goal blocks) in Blocksworld and its equivalent P* graph; compare reasoning-enhanced LLMs to traditional planners (e.g., LAMA); strip domain-specific semantic hints to test true reasoning; analyze evidence for two proposed mechanisms: Algorithmic Simulation via reasoning tokens and Geometric Memory of P* topology.
AI.result: Reasoning-enhanced LLMs significantly outperform traditional satisficing planners in complex, multi-goal configurations; classical search methods stall as search space grows, while LLMs track theoretical optimality limits with near-perfect precision even without semantic hints; evidence supports two mechanisms: (1) active Algorithmic Simulation via reasoning tokens, and (2) Geometric Memory enabling a navigable representation of P* topology.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can achieve near-optimal planning in domains with combinatorial complexity by leveraging internal reasoning and topology-aware representations; the findings motivate leveraging reasoning tokens and geometric memory in future planning models and extending evaluation to broader domains.

title: Behavior-Constrained Reinforcement Learning with Receding-Horizon Credit Assignment for High-Performance Control
summary: Learning high-performance control policies that remain consistent with expert behavior is a fundamental challenge in robotics. Reinforcement learning can discover high-performing strategies but often departs from desirable human behavior, whereas imitation learning is limited by demonstration quality and struggles to improve beyond expert data. We propose a behavior-constrained reinforcement learning framework that improves beyond demonstrations while explicitly controlling deviation from expert behavior. Because expert-consistent behavior in dynamic control is inherently trajectory-level, we introduce a receding-horizon predictive mechanism that models short-term future trajectories and provides look-ahead rewards during training. To account for the natural variability of human behavior under disturbances and changing conditions, we further condition the policy on reference trajectories, allowing it to represent a distribution of expert-consistent behaviors rather than a single deterministic target. Empirically, we evaluate the approach in high-fidelity race car simulation using data from professional drivers, a domain characterized by extreme dynamics and narrow performance margins. The learned policies achieve competitive lap times while maintaining close alignment with expert driving behavior, outperforming baseline methods in both performance and imitation quality. Beyond standard benchmarks, we conduct human-grounded evaluation in a driver-in-the-loop simulator and show that the learned policies reproduce setup-dependent driving characteristics consistent with the feedback of top-class professional race drivers. These results demonstrate that our method enables learning high-performance control policies that are both optimal and behavior-consistent, and can serve as reliable surrogates for human decision-making in complex control systems.

AI.tldr: A behavior-constrained RL framework with receding-horizon predictions that learns high-performance, expert-consistent control policies, validated in professional-driver racing simulations and driver-in-the-loop tests; outperforms baselines in performance and imitation quality.
AI.motivation: RL can achieve strong performance but may diverge from desirable human behavior; imitation learning is limited by demonstration quality and cannot easily surpass experts. There is a need for trajectory-level, expert-consistent policies that are robust to disturbances and reflect a distribution of expert behaviors.
AI.method: Integrates a receding-horizon predictive mechanism to model short-term future trajectories and provide look-ahead rewards during training; conditions the policy on reference trajectories to capture a distribution of expert-consistent behaviors rather than a single target; trained in high-fidelity race car simulations using professional-driver data; includes human-grounded evaluation in a driver-in-the-loop setup.
AI.result: Policies achieve competitive lap times while closely aligning with expert behavior; outperform baseline methods in both performance and imitation quality; human-in-the-loop evaluation shows reproduction of setup-dependent driving characteristics aligned with feedback from top drivers.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that high-performance, behavior-consistent control policies can be learned and deployed as reliable surrogates for human decision-making in complex dynamic systems, with potential applicability beyond racing to other robotics domains.

title: Moondream Segmentation: From Words to Masks
summary: We present Moondream Segmentation, a referring image segmentation extension of Moondream 3, a vision-language model. Given an image and a referring expression, the model autoregressively decodes a vector path and iteratively refines the rasterized mask into a final detailed mask. We introduce a reinforcement learning stage that resolves ambiguity in the supervised signal by directly optimizing mask quality. Rollouts from this stage produce coarse-to-ground-truth targets for the refiner. To mitigate evaluation noise from polygon annotations, we release RefCOCO-M, a cleaned RefCOCO validation split with boundary-accurate masks. Moondream Segmentation achieves a cIoU of 80.2% on RefCOCO (val) and 62.6% mIoU on LVIS (val).

AI.tldr: Extends Moondream 3 to referential segmentation by autoregressively generating a vector path to refine masks, with an RL stage to optimize mask quality; introduces RefCOCO-M; achieves competitive results on RefCOCO and LVIS.
AI.motivation: Address ambiguity in supervision and evaluation noise in referring image segmentation; improve boundary-accurate mask fidelity; provide a cleaned benchmark (RefCOCO-M).
AI.method: Autoregressive vector-path decoding to produce an initial mask, iterative rasterized mask refinement, and a reinforcement learning stage with rollouts that generate coarse-to-ground-truth targets for the refiner; dataset refinement (RefCOCO-M) to reduce boundary annotation noise.
AI.result: cIoU 80.2% on RefCOCO (val); 62.6% mIoU on LVIS (val).
AI.conclusion: RL-guided refinement improves mask quality and robustness to annotation noise; release of RefCOCO-M addresses evaluation noise; demonstrates strong segmentation performance in referring image segmentation.

title: Matrix Profile for Time-Series Anomaly Detection: A Reproducible Open-Source Benchmark on TSB-AD
summary: Matrix Profile (MP) methods are an interpretable and scalable family of distance-based methods for time-series anomaly detection, but strong benchmark performance still depends on design choices beyond a vanilla nearest-neighbor profile. This technical report documents an open-source Matrix Profile for Anomaly Detection (MMPAD) submission to TSB-AD, a benchmark that covers both univariate and multivariate time series. The submitted system combines pre-sorted multidimensional aggregation, efficient exclusion-zone-aware k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) retrieval for repeated anomalies, and moving-average post-processing. To serve as a reproducible reference for MP-based anomaly detection on TSB-AD, we detail the released implementation, the hyperparameter settings for the univariate and multivariate tracks, and the corresponding benchmark results. We further analyze how the system performs on the aggregate leaderboard and across specific dataset characteristics.The open-source implementation is available at https://github.com/mcyeh/mmpad_tsb.

AI.tldr: MMPAD submission to TSB-AD shows a reproducible MP-based anomaly detector using pre-sorted multidimensional aggregation, exclusion-zone-aware kNN for repeated anomalies, and moving-average post-processing, with detailed hyperparameters and benchmark results.
AI.motivation: Provide a reproducible, well-documented reference for Matrix Profile–based anomaly detection on TSB-AD and understand how design choices beyond vanilla MP affect performance across univariate and multivariate time series.
AI.method: Combine pre-sorted multidimensional aggregation, exclusion-zone-aware kNN retrieval to handle repeated anomalies, and moving-average post-processing. Release an open-source implementation with explicit hyperparameters for univariate and multivariate tracks; evaluate on TSB-AD.
AI.result: Benchmark results on aggregate leaderboard and dataset-characteristic analysis; demonstration of performance trends and baselines; publicly available code.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a reproducible MP-based anomaly-detection reference for TSB-AD and offers insights into how specific design choices influence effectiveness, supported by comprehensive benchmark results.

title: AgentHazard: A Benchmark for Evaluating Harmful Behavior in Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents extend language models from text generation to persistent action over tools, files, and execution environments. Unlike chat systems, they maintain state across interactions and translate intermediate outputs into concrete actions. This creates a distinct safety challenge in that harmful behavior may emerge through sequences of individually plausible steps, including intermediate actions that appear locally acceptable but collectively lead to unauthorized actions. We present \textbf{AgentHazard}, a benchmark for evaluating harmful behavior in computer-use agents. AgentHazard contains \textbf{2,653} instances spanning diverse risk categories and attack strategies. Each instance pairs a harmful objective with a sequence of operational steps that are locally legitimate but jointly induce unsafe behavior. The benchmark evaluates whether agents can recognize and interrupt harm arising from accumulated context, repeated tool use, intermediate actions, and dependencies across steps. We evaluate AgentHazard on Claude Code, OpenClaw, and IFlow using mostly open or openly deployable models from the Qwen3, Kimi, GLM, and DeepSeek families. Our experimental results indicate that current systems remain highly vulnerable. In particular, when powered by Qwen3-Coder, Claude Code exhibits an attack success rate of \textbf{73.63\%}, suggesting that model alignment alone does not reliably guarantee the safety of autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: AgentHazard is a 2,653-item benchmark for assessing harmful behavior in computer-use agents, revealing that current models remain vulnerable to multi-step, context-accumulating attacks; high attack success observed on Claude Code with Qwen3-Coder.
AI.motivation: Addresses the safety risk unique to stateful, tool-using agents, where harmful behavior can emerge from sequences of locally acceptable actions. Highlights that alignment alone may not prevent unsafe plans that exploit context, tooling, and cross-step dependencies.
AI.method: Construct 2,653 instances; each pairs a harmful objective with a sequence of steps that are locally legitimate but collectively unsafe. Evaluate across Claude Code, OpenClaw, and IFlow using open/deployable models from the Qwen3, Kimi, GLM, and DeepSeek families, measuring recognition/interruption of harm from accumulated context, repeated tool use, intermediate actions, and step dependencies.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial vulnerabilities in current systems; e.g., with Qwen3-Coder powering Claude Code, attack success rate reaches 73.63%.
AI.conclusion: Model alignment alone does not ensure safety for autonomous agents. AgentHazard provides a rigorous benchmark to drive safer design and evaluation of computer-use agents.

title: ARM: Advantage Reward Modeling for Long-Horizon Manipulation
summary: Long-horizon robotic manipulation remains challenging for reinforcement learning (RL) because sparse rewards provide limited guidance for credit assignment. Practical policy improvement thus relies on richer intermediate supervision, such as dense progress rewards, which are costly to obtain and ill-suited to non-monotonic behaviors such as backtracking and recovery. To address this, we propose Advantage Reward Modeling (ARM), a framework that shifts from hard-to-quantify absolute progress to estimating relative advantage. We introduce a cost-effective tri-state labeling strategy -- Progressive, Regressive, and Stagnant -- that reduces human cognitive overhead while ensuring high cross-annotator consistency. By training on these intuitive signals, ARM enables automated progress annotation for both complete demonstrations and fragmented DAgger-style data. Integrating ARM into an offline RL pipeline allows for adaptive action-reward reweighting, effectively filtering suboptimal samples. Our approach achieves a 99.4% success rate on a challenging long-horizon towel-folding task, demonstrating improved stability and data efficiency over current VLA baselines with near-zero human intervention during policy training.

AI.tldr: Advantage Reward Modeling (ARM) uses tri-state relative signals—Progressive, Regressive, and Stagnant—to guide RL for long-horizon tasks, enabling offline RL with reweighted rewards and reduced supervision, achieving near-perfect towel-folding success with minimal labeling.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon robotic manipulation suffers from sparse rewards; dense progress rewards are costly and fail to capture non-monotonic behaviors like backtracking. A low-cost, consistent supervisory signal is needed.
AI.method: Introduce ARM framework with tri-state labeling; collect/annotate progress via humans using three categories; train a model to estimate relative advantage; integrate into offline RL to reweight actions; applicable to full demonstrations and fragmented DAgger data.
AI.result: Achieves 99.4% success on a long-horizon towel-folding task; improved stability and data efficiency vs. VLA baselines; near-zero human intervention during policy training.
AI.conclusion: ARM provides a practical, scalable alternative to absolute progress rewards, enabling robust credit assignment in long-horizon RL with reduced labeling effort.

title: Rascene: High-Fidelity 3D Scene Imaging with mmWave Communication Signals
summary: Robust 3D environmental perception is critical for applications such as autonomous driving and robot navigation. However, optical sensors such as cameras and LiDAR often fail under adverse conditions, including smoke, fog, and non-ideal lighting. Although specialized radar systems can operate in these environments, their reliance on bespoke hardware and licensed spectrum limits scalability and cost-effectiveness. This paper introduces Rascene, an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) framework that leverages ubiquitous mmWave OFDM communication signals for 3D scene imaging. To overcome the sparse and multipath-ambiguous nature of individual radio frames, Rascene performs multi-frame, spatially adaptive fusion with confidence-weighted forward projection, enabling the recovery of geometric consensus across arbitrary poses. Experimental results demonstrate that our method reconstructs 3D scenes with high precision, offering a new pathway toward low-cost, scalable, and robust 3D perception.

AI.tldr: ISAC-based mmWave OFDM framework (Rascene) for robust 3D scene imaging using multi-frame fusion to overcome sparse/multipath ambiguities and adverse conditions.
AI.motivation: Need robust, low-cost 3D perception under smoke, fog, and poor lighting; traditional optical sensors fail; standalone radars are costly and spectrum-constrained; leveraging ubiquitous mmWave communications enables scalable sensing.
AI.method: Proposes Rascene, an integrated sensing and communication system that uses existing mmWave OFDM signals; performs multi-frame spatially adaptive fusion with confidence-weighted forward projection to align frames across arbitrary poses and resolve geometric consensus.
AI.result: Experiments show high-precision 3D reconstruction under challenging conditions; demonstrates feasibility of low-cost, scalable ISAC-based 3D perception.
AI.conclusion: Rascene provides a path toward robust, scalable 3D sensing by repurposing mmWave communications for imaging, potentially reducing hardware costs and enabling widespread deployment.

title: Do We Need Frontier Models to Verify Mathematical Proofs?
summary: Advances in training, post-training, and inference-time methods have enabled frontier reasoning models to win gold medals in math competitions and settle challenging open problems. Gaining trust in the responses of these models requires that natural language proofs be checked for errors. LLM judges are increasingly being adopted to meet the growing demand for evaluating such proofs. While verification is considered easier than generation, what model capability does reliable verification actually require? We systematically evaluate four open-source and two frontier LLMs on datasets of human-graded natural language proofs of competition-level problems. We consider two key metrics: verifier accuracy and self-consistency (the rate of agreement across repeated judgments on the same proof). We observe that smaller open-source models are only up to ~10% behind frontier models in accuracy but they are up to ~25% more inconsistent. Furthermore, we see that verifier accuracy is sensitive to prompt choice across all models. We then demonstrate that the smaller models, in fact, do possess the mathematical capabilities to verify proofs at the level of frontier models, but they struggle to reliably elicit these capabilities with general judging prompts. Through an LLM-guided prompt search, we synthesize an ensemble of specialized prompts that overcome the specific failure modes of smaller models, boosting their performance by up to 9.1% in accuracy and 15.9% in self-consistency. These gains are realized across models and datasets, allowing models like Qwen3.5-35B to perform on par with frontier models such as Gemini 3.1 Pro for proof verification.

AI.tldr: Smaller open-source LLMs nearly match frontier models in proof-verification accuracy but lag in self-consistency; prompt engineering with an LLM-guided ensemble of specialized prompts closes the gap, enabling smaller models to reach frontier-level verification performance.
AI.motivation: Assess whether verifier capability is truly lacking in smaller models or merely hampered by prompting; evaluate accuracy and self-consistency across model sizes; determine if specialized prompts can unlock latent verification capabilities.
AI.method: Evaluate four open-source and two frontier LLMs on datasets of human-graded natural language proofs from competition problems; measure verifier accuracy and self-consistency; perform LLM-guided prompt search to create an ensemble of prompts; compare performance across models and datasets; analyze the role of prompt choice and ensemble effects.
AI.result: Open-source models are up to ~10% behind frontier in accuracy but up to ~25% more inconsistent; prompt choice heavily impacts accuracy; LLM-guided prompt search yields improvements up to 9.1% in accuracy and 15.9% in self-consistency; enabling models like Qwen3.5-35B to rival Gemini 3.1 Pro in proof verification.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, ensemble prompts can unlock latent verification abilities in smaller LLMs, reducing the performance gap with frontier models and enabling cost-effective proof verification pipelines.

title: FoE: Forest of Errors Makes the First Solution the Best in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Recent Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) like DeepSeek-R1 have demonstrated remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks, exhibiting human-like patterns in exploring multiple alternative solutions. Upon closer inspection, however, we uncover a surprising phenomenon: The First is The Best, where alternative solutions are not merely suboptimal but potentially detrimental. This observation challenges widely accepted test-time scaling laws, leading us to hypothesize that errors within the reasoning path scale concurrently with test time. Through comprehensive empirical analysis, we characterize errors as a forest-structured Forest of Errors (FoE) and conclude that FoE makes the First the Best, which is underpinned by rigorous theoretical analysis. Leveraging these insights, we propose RED, a self-guided efficient reasoning framework comprising two components: I) Refining First, which suppresses FoE growth in the first solution; and II) Discarding Subs, which prunes subsequent FoE via dual-consistency. Extensive experiments across five benchmarks and six backbone models demonstrate that RED outperforms eight competitive baselines, achieving performance gains of up to 19.0% while reducing token consumption by 37.7% ~ 70.4%. Moreover, comparative experiments on FoE metrics shed light on how RED achieves effectiveness.

title: Enhancing Multi-Robot Exploration Using Probabilistic Frontier Prioritization with Dirichlet Process Gaussian Mixtures
comment: Submitted for review IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L)
summary: Multi-agent autonomous exploration is essential for applications such as environmental monitoring, search and rescue, and industrial-scale surveillance. However, effective coordination under communication constraints remains a significant challenge. Frontier exploration algorithms analyze the boundary between the known and unknown regions to determine the next-best view that maximizes exploratory gain. This article proposes an enhancement to existing frontier-based exploration algorithms by introducing a probabilistic approach to frontier prioritization. By leveraging Dirichlet process Gaussian mixture model (DP-GMM) and a probabilistic formulation of information gain, the method improves the quality of frontier prioritization. The proposed enhancement, integrated into two state-of-the-art multi-agent exploration algorithms, consistently improves performance across environments of varying clutter, communication constraints, and team sizes. Simulations showcase an average gain of $10\%$ and $14\%$ for the two algorithms across all combinations. Successful deployment in real-world experiments with a dual-drone system further corroborates these findings.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic frontier prioritization using DP-GMM and probabilistic information gain improves two frontier-based multi-agent exploration algorithms, with ~10–14% gains, validated in simulations and dual-drone real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: Efficient frontier-based exploration is hampered by communication constraints and heterogeneous team sizes; improving frontier prioritization can boost exploration efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Integrate Dirichlet process Gaussian mixture models to model frontier distributions and apply a probabilistic information-gain formulation to rank frontiers; embedded into two state-of-the-art multi-agent exploration algorithms; tested under varying clutter and communication constraints.
AI.result: Simulations show average gains of 10% and 14% for the two algorithms across all combinations; real-world dual-drone experiments validate the approach.
AI.conclusion: The probabilistic DP-GMM frontier prioritization approach improves exploration performance across environments and constraints and is transferable to existing frontier-based multi-agent systems.

title: Unlocking Multi-Site Clinical Data: A Federated Approach to Privacy-First Child Autism Behavior Analysis
comment: Accepted on the CVPR 2026 Workshop on Computer Vision for Children (CV4CHL)
summary: Automated recognition of autistic behaviors in children is essential for early intervention and objective clinical assessment. However, the development of robust models is severely hindered by strict privacy regulations (e.g., HIPAA) and the sensitive nature of pediatric data, which prevents the centralized aggregation of clinical datasets. Furthermore, individual clinical sites often suffer from data scarcity, making it difficult to learn generalized behavior patterns or tailor models to site-specific patient distributions. To address these challenges, we observe that Federated Learning (FL) can decouple model training from raw data access, enabling multi-site collaboration while maintaining strict data residency. In this paper, we present the first study exploring Federated Learning for pose-based child autism behavior recognition. Our framework employs a two-layer privacy protection mechanism: utilizing human skeletal abstraction to remove identifiable visual information from the raw RGB videos and FL to ensure sensitive pose data remains within the clinic. This approach leverages distributed clinical data to learn generalized representations while providing the flexibility for site-specific personalization. Experimental results on the MMASD benchmark demonstrate that our framework achieves high recognition accuracy, outperforming traditional federated baselines and providing a robust, privacy-first solution for multi-site clinical analysis.

AI.tldr: Federated learning with skeletal-based privacy for pose-based autism recognition across multiple sites, achieving strong accuracy on MMASD and outperforming standard FL baselines.
AI.motivation: Address privacy regulations and data scarcity in pediatric autism data by enabling multi-site collaboration without centralizing raw data, while enabling site-specific personalization.
AI.method: A two-layer privacy approach: (1) human skeletal abstraction to remove identifiable visual information from RGB videos, (2) federated learning to keep sensitive pose data within each clinic, enabling distributed learning and site personalization.
AI.result: On the MMASD benchmark, the framework achieves high recognition accuracy and outperforms traditional federated baselines, offering a privacy-first solution for multi-site clinical analysis.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and benefit of privacy-preserving FL for pediatric autism behavior recognition; supports generalized representations across sites with room for further enhancements in handling data heterogeneity and privacy leakage risks.

title: On the Geometric Structure of Layer Updates in Deep Language Models
summary:
We study the geometric structure of layer updates in deep language models. Rather than analyzing what information is encoded in intermediate representations, we ask how representations change from one layer to the next. We show that layerwise updates admit a decomposition into a dominant tokenwise component and a residual that is not captured by restricted tokenwise function classes.
  Across multiple architectures, including Transformers and state-space models, we find that the full layer update is almost perfectly aligned with the tokenwise component, while the residual exhibits substantially weaker alignment, larger angular deviation, and significantly lower projection onto the dominant tokenwise subspace. This indicates that the residual is not merely a small correction, but a geometrically distinct component of the transformation.
  This geometric separation has functional consequences: approximation error under the restricted tokenwise model is strongly associated with output perturbation, with Spearman correlations often exceeding 0.7 and reaching up to 0.95 in larger models. Together, these results suggest that most layerwise updates behave like structured reparameterizations along a dominant direction, while functionally significant computation is concentrated in a geometrically distinct residual component.
  Our framework provides a simple, architecture-agnostic method for probing the geometric and functional structure of layer updates in modern language models.

AI.tldr: Layer updates in language models decompose into a dominant tokenwise direction plus a distinct residual; the residual carries functional computation and is geometrically separate from the tokenwise component; restricted tokenwise models show perturbation-aligned errors, framework is architecture-agnostic.
AI.motivation: Understand how layer updates evolve across layers by analyzing their geometric structure, not just the encoded representations.
AI.method: Geometric decomposition of layer updates into a dominant tokenwise component and a residual; alignment analyses (angular deviation, projection onto tokenwise subspace) across Transformer and state-space models; correlation of restricted-model approximation error with output perturbation via Spearman correlations; architecture-agnostic framework.
AI.result: Full layer updates align almost perfectly with the tokenwise component; the residual is geometrically distinct, with weaker alignment, larger angular deviation, and lower projection onto the tokenwise subspace; restricted tokenwise approximations show strong perturbation-related errors (Spearman 0.7–0.95); results hold across multiple architectures.
AI.conclusion: Layerwise updates largely reparameterize along a dominant direction, while functional computation concentrates in a geometrically distinct residual; the proposed framework provides a simple, architecture-agnostic method to probe geometric and functional structure of layer updates.

title: InfoSeeker: A Scalable Hierarchical Parallel Agent Framework for Web Information Seeking
summary: Recent agentic search systems have made substantial progress by emphasising deep, multi-step reasoning. However, this focus often overlooks the challenges of wide-scale information synthesis, where agents must aggregate large volumes of heterogeneous evidence across many sources. As a result, most existing large language model agent systems face severe limitations in data-intensive settings, including context saturation, cascading error propagation, and high end-to-end latency. To address these challenges, we present \framework, a hierarchical framework based on principle of near-decomposability, containing a strategic \textit{Host}, multiple \textit{Managers} and parallel \textit{Workers}. By leveraging aggregation and reflection mechanisms at the Manager layer, our framework enforces strict context isolation to prevent saturation and error propagation. Simultaneously, the parallelism in worker layer accelerates the speed of overall task execution, mitigating the significant latency. Our evaluation on two complementary benchmarks demonstrates both efficiency ($ 3-5 \times$ speed-up) and effectiveness, achieving a $8.4\%$ success rate on WideSearch-en and $52.9\%$ accuracy on BrowseComp-zh. The code is released at https://github.com/agent-on-the-fly/InfoSeeker

AI.tldr: InfoSeeker: a hierarchical near-decomposable framework for large-scale information synthesis that uses a Host, multiple Managers, and parallel Workers to minimize context saturation, error propagation, and latency; achieves 3–5× speed-up and competitive accuracy on WideSearch-en and BrowseComp-zh.
AI.motivation: To address data-intensive information synthesis with large volumes of heterogeneous evidence, where existing LLM agent systems struggle with context saturation, cascading errors, and high end-to-end latency.
AI.method: Proposes a Host–Manager–Worker architecture; Managers perform aggregation and reflection to enforce context isolation; Workers execute tasks in parallel to accelerate processing; near-decomposability guides system design.
AI.result: Reported 3–5× speed-up; 8.4% success on WideSearch-en; 52.9% accuracy on BrowseComp-zh; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates improved efficiency and effectiveness for data-intensive information synthesis tasks and offers a practical architecture for scalable LLM-based agents.

title: Joint Prediction of Human Motions and Actions in Human-Robot Collaboration
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures. Submitted to IEEE AIM 2026
summary: Fluent human--robot collaboration requires robots to continuously estimate human behaviour and anticipate future intentions. This entails reasoning jointly about \emph{continuous movements} and \emph{discrete actions}, which are still largely modelled in isolation. In this paper, we introduce \textsf{MA-HERP}, a hierarchical and recursive probabilistic framework for the \emph{joint estimation and prediction} of human movements and actions. The model combines: (i) a hierarchical representation in which movements compose into actions through admissible Allen interval relations, (ii) a unified probabilistic factorisation coupling continuous dynamics, discrete labels, and durations, and (iii) a recursive inference scheme inspired by Bayesian filtering, alternating top-down action prediction with bottom-up sensory evidence. We present a preliminary experimental evaluation based on neural models trained on musculoskeletal simulations of reaching movements, showing accurate motion prediction, robust action inference under noise, and computational performance compatible with on-line human--robot collaboration.

AI.tldr: MA-HERP: a hierarchical, recursive probabilistic model for joint estimation and prediction of human movements and actions, combining continuous dynamics with discrete action labels via Allen interval relations and Bayesian-like recursion; validated on musculoskeletal reaching simulations with promising real-time performance.
AI.motivation: To enable fluent human–robot collaboration by jointly reasoning about continuous movements and discrete actions, addressing the limitation of modeling them in isolation.
AI.method: Introduce MA-HERP: a hierarchical representation where movements compose into actions using admissible Allen interval relations; a unified probabilistic factorisation that couples continuous dynamics, discrete labels, and durations; a recursive inference scheme inspired by Bayesian filtering that alternates top-down action prediction with bottom-up sensory evidence.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments using neural models trained on musculoskeletal simulations of reaching movements show accurate motion prediction, robust action inference under noise, and computation compatible with online HRI.
AI.conclusion: MA-HERP demonstrates feasibility of joint continuous-discrete reasoning for fluent HRI and provides a foundation for extending to real robots and broader action repertoires, with future work focusing on scalability and real-time deployment.

title: Smart Transfer: Leveraging Vision Foundation Model for Rapid Building Damage Mapping with Post-Earthquake VHR Imagery
summary: Living in a changing climate, human society now faces more frequent and severe natural disasters than ever before. As a consequence, rapid disaster response during the "Golden 72 Hours" of search and rescue becomes a vital humanitarian necessity and community concern. However, traditional disaster damage surveys routinely fail to generalize across distinct urban morphologies and new disaster events. Effective damage mapping typically requires exhaustive and time-consuming manual data annotation. To address this issue, we introduce Smart Transfer, a novel Geospatial Artificial Intelligence (GeoAI) framework, leveraging state-of-the-art vision Foundation Models (FMs) for rapid building damage mapping with post-earthquake Very High Resolution (VHR) imagery. Specifically, we design two novel model transfer strategies: first, Pixel-wise Clustering (PC), ensuring robust prototype-level global feature alignment; second, a Distance-Penalized Triplet (DPT), integrating patch-level spatial autocorrelation patterns by assigning stronger penalties to semantically inconsistent yet spatially adjacent patches. Extensive experiments and ablations from the recent 2023 Turkiye-Syria earthquake show promising performance in multiple cross-region transfer settings, namely Leave One Domain Out (LODO) and Specific Source Domain Combination (SSDC). Moreover, Smart Transfer provides a scalable, automated GeoAI solution to accelerate building damage mapping and support rapid disaster response, offering new opportunities to enhance disaster resilience in climate-vulnerable regions and communities. The data and code are publicly available at https://github.com/ai4city-hkust/SmartTransfer.

AI.tldr: A GeoAI framework, Smart Transfer, uses vision foundation models to map post-earthquake building damage rapidly with two novel transfer strategies (Pixel-wise Clustering and Distance-Penalized Triplet). Demonstrates cross-region generalization (LODO/SSDC) on the 2023 Turkiye-Syria earthquake; provides public data/code.
AI.motivation: Address generalization gaps of traditional disaster surveys across urban morphologies and new events; reduce reliance on manual annotation for rapid damage mapping during post-disaster response.
AI.method: Two transfer strategies built on foundation models: Pixel-wise Clustering (PC) for prototype-level global feature alignment; Distance-Penalized Triplet (DPT) incorporating patch-level spatial autocorrelation by penalizing semantically inconsistent yet spatially adjacent patches; evaluated on post-earthquake post-event Very High Resolution imagery using Leave One Domain Out (LODO) and Specific Source Domain Combination (SSDC).
AI.result: Promising cross-region transfer performance with extensive experiments and ablations; data and code publicly available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Smart Transfer provides a scalable, automated GeoAI solution for rapid building damage mapping to support disaster response and resilience in climate-vulnerable regions; demonstrates improved cross-domain transferability; resources available publicly.

title: VALOR: Value-Aware Revenue Uplift Modeling with Treatment-Gated Representation for B2B Sales
summary: B2B sales organizations must identify "persuadable" accounts within zero-inflated revenue distributions to optimize expensive human resource allocation. Standard uplift frameworks struggle with treatment signal collapse in high-dimensional spaces and a misalignment between regression calibration and the ranking of high-value "whales." We introduce VALOR (Value Aware Learning of Optimized (B2B) Revenue), a unified framework featuring a Treatment-Gated Sparse-Revenue Network that uses bilinear interaction to prevent causal signal collapse. The framework is optimized via a novel Cost-Sensitive Focal-ZILN objective that combines a focal mechanism for distributional robustness with a value-weighted ranking loss that scales penalties based on financial magnitude. To provide interpretability for high-touch sales programs, we further derive Robust ZILN-GBDT, a tree based variant utilizing a custom splitting criterion for uplift heterogeneity. Extensive evaluations confirm VALOR's dominance, achieving a 20% improvement in rankability over state-of-the-art methods on public benchmarks and delivering a validated 2.7x increase in incremental revenue per account in a rigorous 4-month production A/B test.

AI.tldr: VALOR is a value-aware uplift framework for B2B revenue that combines a Treatment-Gated Sparse-Revenue Network with a Cost-Sensitive Focal-ZILN objective, delivering robust, value-weighted ranking and interpretability via a Robust ZILN-GBDT variant. It reports 20% gains in rankability and 2.7x incremental revenue per account in production.
AI.motivation: B2B sales must identify 'persuadable' accounts within zero-inflated revenue distributions. Standard uplift methods suffer from (i) causal signal collapse in high-dimensional spaces and (ii) misalignment between regression calibration and ranking of high-value 'whales'; thus a unified, value-aware approach is needed.
AI.method: VALOR introduces a Treatment-Gated Sparse-Revenue Network using bilinear interaction to preserve causal signal in high dimensions. It optimizes a novel Cost-Sensitive Focal-ZILN objective that blends distributional robustness (focal mechanism) with a value-weighted ranking loss scaling penalties by financial magnitude. For interpretability, Robust ZILN-GBDT (tree-based) with a custom splitting criterion captures uplift heterogeneity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows VALOR achieves a 20% improvement in rankability over state-of-the-art methods on public benchmarks and a 2.7x increase in incremental revenue per account in a 4-month production A/B test.
AI.conclusion: VALOR provides a dominant, robust framework for B2B uplift with an interpretable variant, addressing signal collapse and miscalibration while delivering substantial revenue gains.

title: Agentic-MME: What Agentic Capability Really Brings to Multimodal Intelligence?
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are evolving from passive observers into active agents, solving problems through Visual Expansion (invoking visual tools) and Knowledge Expansion (open-web search). However, existing evaluations fall short: they lack flexible tool integration, test visual and search tools separately, and evaluate primarily by final answers. Consequently, they cannot verify if tools were actually invoked, applied correctly, or used efficiently. To address this, we introduce Agentic-MME, a process-verified benchmark for Multimodal Agentic Capabilities. It contains 418 real-world tasks across 6 domains and 3 difficulty levels to evaluate capability synergy, featuring over 2,000 stepwise checkpoints that average 10+ person-hours of manual annotation per task. Each task includes a unified evaluation framework supporting sandboxed code and APIs, alongside a human reference trajectory annotated with stepwise checkpoints along dual-axis: S-axis and V-axis. To enable true process-level verification, we audit fine-grained intermediate states rather than just final answers, and quantify efficiency via an overthinking metric relative to human trajectories. Experimental results show the best model, Gemini3-pro, achieves 56.3% overall accuracy, which falls significantly to 23.0% on Level-3 tasks, underscoring the difficulty of real-world multimodal agentic problem solving.

AI.tldr: Agentic-MME: a process-verified benchmark for Multimodal Agentic Capabilities with 418 tasks, 6 domains, 3 difficulty levels, 2000+ stepwise checkpoints; audits intermediate states and overthinking efficiency; Gemini3-pro scores 56.3% overall, 23.0% on Level-3.
AI.motivation: Fill gaps in current MLLM evaluation that do not verify tool use or process, by enabling end-to-end tool invocation verification and state tracking across visual and knowledge tools.
AI.method: Create unified evaluation framework with sandboxed code/APIs; 418 real-world tasks across 6 domains and 3 difficulty levels; human reference trajectories with stepwise checkpoints on S-axis (state) and V-axis (vision); audit intermediate states; compute overthinking metric vs. human trajectories.
AI.result: Gemini3-pro achieves 56.3% overall accuracy; Level-3 tasks drop to 23.0%; demonstrates challenge of real-world multimodal agentic tasks and limitations of current models.
AI.conclusion: Provides a comprehensive, process-oriented benchmark for evaluating multimodal agentic capabilities, highlighting tool invocation, state tracking, and efficiency; sets a challenging standard and highlights gaps for future research.

title: Flash-Mono: Feed-Forward Accelerated Gaussian Splatting Monocular SLAM
summary: Monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting SLAM suffers from critical limitations in time efficiency, geometric accuracy, and multi-view consistency. These issues stem from the time-consuming $\textit{Train-from-Scratch}$ optimization and the lack of inter-frame scale consistency from single-frame geometry priors. We contend that a feed-forward paradigm, leveraging multi-frame context to predict Gaussian attributes directly, is crucial for addressing these challenges. We present Flash-Mono, a system composed of three core modules: a feed-forward prediction frontend, a 2D Gaussian Splatting mapping backend, and an efficient hidden-state-based loop closure module. We trained a recurrent feed-forward frontend model that progressively aggregates multi-frame visual features into a hidden state via cross attention and jointly predicts camera poses and per-pixel Gaussian properties. By directly predicting Gaussian attributes, our method bypasses the burdensome per-frame optimization required in optimization-based GS-SLAM, achieving a $\textbf{10x}$ speedup while ensuring high-quality rendering. The power of our recurrent architecture extends beyond efficient prediction. The hidden states act as compact submap descriptors, facilitating efficient loop closure and global $\mathrm{Sim}(3)$ optimization to mitigate the long-standing challenge of drift. For enhanced geometric fidelity, we replace conventional 3D Gaussian ellipsoids with 2D Gaussian surfels. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Flash-Mono achieves state-of-the-art performance in both tracking and mapping quality, highlighting its potential for embodied perception and real-time reconstruction applications. Project page: https://victkk.github.io/flash-mono.

AI.tldr: Flash-Mono replaces train-from-scratch optimization in monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting SLAM with a recurrent feed-forward pipeline that aggregates multi-frame context to predict Gaussian attributes, achieving ~10x speedup and improved drift handling via hidden-state loop closure and 2D Gaussian surfels.
AI.motivation: To resolve critical bottlenecks in time efficiency, geometric accuracy, and multi-view consistency in GS-SLAM by leveraging multi-frame context and avoiding per-frame optimization.
AI.method: A three-module system: (1) a recurrent feed-forward frontend that aggregates multi-frame visual features into a hidden state via cross-attention to jointly predict camera poses and per-pixel Gaussian properties; (2) a 2D Gaussian splatting mapping backend; (3) a hidden-state-based loop closure module that enables compact submap descriptors for efficient loop closure and global Sim(3) optimization. Replacing 3D Gaussian ellipsoids with 2D Gaussian surfels.
AI.result: Achieves ~10x speedup over optimization-based GS-SLAM while delivering high-quality rendering; hidden states serve as compact submap descriptors enabling efficient loop closure and drift mitigation; state-of-the-art performance in tracking and mapping quality demonstrated on experiments.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of end-to-end learned GS-SLAM with multi-frame context, offering real-time capable, drift-resistant reconstruction; the approach may impact embodied perception and real-time 3D reconstruction while inviting further work on generalization and dynamic scenes.

title: Cross-Vehicle 3D Geometric Consistency for Self-Supervised Surround Depth Estimation on Articulated Vehicles
summary: Surround depth estimation provides a cost-effective alternative to LiDAR for 3D perception in autonomous driving. While recent self-supervised methods explore multi-camera settings to improve scale awareness and scene coverage, they are primarily designed for passenger vehicles and rarely consider articulated vehicles or robotics platforms. The articulated structure introduces complex cross-segment geometry and motion coupling, making consistent depth reasoning across views more challenging. In this work, we propose \textbf{ArticuSurDepth}, a self-supervised framework for surround-view depth estimation on articulated vehicles that enhances depth learning through cross-view and cross-vehicle geometric consistency guided by structural priors from vision foundation model. Specifically, we introduce multi-view spatial context enrichment strategy and a cross-view surface normal constraint to improve structural coherence across spatial and temporal contexts. We further incorporate camera height regularization with ground plane-awareness to encourage metric depth estimation, together with cross-vehicle pose consistency that bridges motion estimation between articulated segments. To validate our proposed method, an articulated vehicle experiment platform was established with a dataset collected over it. Experiment results demonstrate state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance of depth estimation on our self-collected dataset as well as on DDAD, nuScenes, and KITTI benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Self-supervised surround-view depth estimation for articulated vehicles using cross-view and cross-vehicle geometric consistency, enhanced by structural priors from a vision foundation model. It introduces multi-view spatial context enrichment, cross-view surface normal constraints, ground-plane-aware height regularization, and cross-vehicle pose consistency, achieving state-of-the-art results on a self-collected articulated-vehicle dataset and on DDAD, nuScenes, and KITTI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Articulated vehicles introduce complex cross-segment geometry and motion coupling that challenge depth reasoning across views. Existing self-supervised methods focus on passenger vehicles and often lack metric depth guarantees and cross-vehicle consistency. Leveraging vision foundation model priors can guide structural learning to improve coherence and generalization.
AI.method: A self-supervised framework (ArticuSurDepth) that (1) enriches multi-view spatial context, (2) imposes cross-view surface normal consistency, (3) uses camera height regularization with ground-plane awareness to promote metric depth, and (4) enforces cross-vehicle pose consistency to bridge articulated segments. Structural priors from a vision foundation model guide depth learning. Evaluation uses a self-collected articulated-vehicle dataset and benchmarks DDAD, nuScenes, and KITTI.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art depth estimation performance on the authors’ articulated-vehicle dataset and on DDAD, nuScenes, and KITTI benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating cross-view and cross-vehicle geometry, plus structural priors and ground-plane-aware constraints, yields robust, coherent, and metric-depth capable surround-view depth estimation for articulated vehicles, with strong generalization across standard benchmarks.

title: Time-Warping Recurrent Neural Networks for Transfer Learning
summary:
Dynamical systems describe how a physical system evolves over time. Physical processes can evolve faster or slower in different environmental conditions. We use time-warping as rescaling the time in a model of a physical system. This thesis proposes a new method of transfer learning for Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) based on time-warping. We prove that for a class of linear, first-order differential equations known as time lag models, an LSTM can approximate these systems with any desired accuracy, and the model can be time-warped while maintaining the approximation accuracy.
  The Time-Warping method of transfer learning is then evaluated in an applied problem on predicting fuel moisture content (FMC), an important concept in wildfire modeling.
