An RNN with LSTM recurrent layers is pretrained on fuels with a characteristic time scale of 10 hours, where there are large quantities of data available for training. The RNN is then modified with transfer learning to generate predictions for fuels with characteristic time scales of 1 hour, 100 hours, and 1000 hours. The Time-Warping method is evaluated against several known methods of transfer learning. The Time-Warping method produces predictions with an accuracy level comparable to the established methods, despite modifying only a small fraction of the parameters that the other methods modify.

AI.tldr: Time-Warping enables transfer learning for RNNs by rescaling time; LSTM can approximate linear time-lag models with any accuracy; tested on FMC for wildfire modeling across multiple time scales; achieves comparable accuracy with fewer parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Environmental processes evolve at different time scales; efficient transfer learning for RNNs is needed; exploit invariance to time rescaling in dynamical systems.
AI.method: Prove LSTM can approximate time-lag models; time-warping preserves accuracy under rescaled time; pretrained on data with 10h scale; fine-tune to 1h, 100h, 1000h; compare to standard transfer methods; measure accuracy and parameter changes.
AI.result: Time-Warping yields accuracy comparable to established methods; modifies only a small fraction of parameters; effective across the tested time scales.
AI.conclusion: Time-Warping is a viable transfer-learning approach for RNNs in dynamical systems with varying time scales, enabling efficient adaptation with minimal parameter updates.

title: Automatic Textbook Formalization
summary: We present a case study where an automatic AI system formalizes a textbook with more than 500 pages of graduate-level algebraic combinatorics to Lean. The resulting formalization represents a new milestone in textbook formalization scale and proficiency, moving from early results in undergraduate topology and restructuring of existing library content to a full standalone formalization of a graduate textbook. The formalization comprises 130K lines of code and 5900 Lean declarations and was conducted within one week by a total of 30K Claude 4.5 Opus agents collaborating in parallel on a shared code base via version control, simultaneously setting a record in multi-agent software engineering with usable results. The inference cost matches or undercuts what we estimate as the salaries required for a team of human experts, and we expect there is still the potential for large efficiencies to be made without the need for better models. We make our code, the resulting Lean code base and a side-by-side blueprint website available open-source.

AI.tldr: A large-scale case study demonstrates automatic formalization of a graduate-level algebra textbook into Lean using collaborative AI agents, achieving a 130K-line, 5900-declaration formalization in one week, with open-source release and cost-efficient implications.
AI.motivation: To push the boundaries of textbook formalization scale and demonstrate the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of automated formalization via cooperative AI agents, suggesting potential for large-scale library development and faster verification of mathematical content.
AI.method: Parallel automated formalization where 30,000 Claude 4.5 Opus agents collaboratively work on a shared Lean repository, using version control, to translate a >500-page graduate text into Lean, resulting in 130K LOC and 5900 declarations in about one week.
AI.result: A new milestone in textbook formalization scale: a standalone Lean formalization of a graduate-level algebraic combinatorics textbook, with sizable codebase, rapid completion, and open-source release; inference cost reportedly competitive with human salaries.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates high scalability and potential efficiency gains for machine-assisted formalization of mathematical texts, while leaving room for further optimization and validation; the open release supports reproducibility and future work in large-scale, multi-agent formalization.

title: An Open-Source LiDAR and Monocular Off-Road Autonomous Navigation Stack
summary: Off-road autonomous navigation demands reliable 3D perception for robust obstacle detection in challenging unstructured terrain. While LiDAR is accurate, it is costly and power-intensive. Monocular depth estimation using foundation models offers a lightweight alternative, but its integration into outdoor navigation stacks remains underexplored. We present an open-source off-road navigation stack supporting both LiDAR and monocular 3D perception without task-specific training. For the monocular setup, we combine zero-shot depth prediction (Depth Anything V2) with metric depth rescaling using sparse SLAM measurements (VINS-Mono). Two key enhancements improve robustness: edge-masking to reduce obstacle hallucination and temporal smoothing to mitigate the impact of SLAM instability. The resulting point cloud is used to generate a robot-centric 2.5D elevation map for costmap-based planning. Evaluated in photorealistic simulations (Isaac Sim) and real-world unstructured environments, the monocular configuration matches high-resolution LiDAR performance in most scenarios, demonstrating that foundation-model-based monocular depth estimation is a viable LiDAR alternative for robust off-road navigation. By open-sourcing the navigation stack and the simulation environment, we provide a complete pipeline for off-road navigation as well as a reproducible benchmark. Code available at https://github.com/LARIAD/Offroad-Nav.

AI.tldr: Monocular depth estimation via foundation models (Depth Anything V2) with metric-rescaled depth from sparse SLAM (VINS-Mono) and robustness boosts (edge masking, temporal smoothing) enables a LiDAR-like perception stack for open-source off-road navigation; validated in photorealistic sims and real-world tests; provides a reproducible benchmark.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on costly LiDAR for off-road navigation by leveraging monocular depth from foundation models, enabling lightweight, robust perception in unstructured terrain.
AI.method: Use zero-shot monocular depth from Depth Anything V2, scale to metric depth using sparse SLAM measurements from VINS-Mono, apply edge-masking and temporal smoothing to improve robustness, convert to a 3D point cloud, build a robot-centric 2.5D elevation map for costmap-based planning; open-source implementation and simulation environment.
AI.result: Monocular setup achieves performance comparable to high-resolution LiDAR in most scenarios, across photorealistic simulations and real-world unstructured environments.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model-based monocular depth estimation is a viable LiDAR alternative for robust off-road navigation; the open-source stack and simulation environment enable reproducible benchmarks and practical deployment.

title: Drift-Resilient Temporal Priors for Visual Tracking
summary: Temporal information is crucial for visual tracking, but existing multi-frame trackers are vulnerable to model drift caused by naively aggregating noisy historical predictions. In this paper, we introduce DTPTrack, a lightweight and generalizable module designed to be seamlessly integrated into existing trackers to suppress drift. Our framework consists of two core components: (1) a Temporal Reliability Calibrator (TRC) mechanism that learns to assign a per-frame reliability score to historical states, filtering out noise while anchoring on the ground-truth template; and (2) a Temporal Guidance Synthesizer (TGS) module that synthesizes this calibrated history into a compact set of dynamic temporal priors to provide predictive guidance. To demonstrate its versatility, we integrate DTPTrack into three diverse tracking architectures--OSTrack, ODTrack, and LoRAT-and show consistent, significant performance gains across all baselines. Our best-performing model, built upon an extended LoRATv2 backbone, sets a new state-of-the-art on several benchmarks, achieving a 77.5% Success rate on LaSOT and an 80.3% AO on GOT-10k.

AI.tldr: DTPTrack introduces two modular components, Temporal Reliability Calibrator (TRC) and Temporal Guidance Synthesizer (TGS), to suppress drift in multi-frame visual trackers. It is lightweight and generalizable, and integrates into OSTrack, ODTrack, and LoRAT to yield consistent improvements. The best model on extended LoRATv2 achieves state-of-the-art results (LaSOT 77.5% SR; GOT-10k 80.3% AO).
AI.motivation: Temporal information is crucial for tracking but naive aggregation of noisy historical predictions leads to model drift. A principled method to assess historical frame reliability and to synthesize compact temporal priors is needed to improve robustness without heavy computational overhead.
AI.method: Two components: (1) Temporal Reliability Calibrator (TRC) assigns per-frame reliability scores to historical states, filtering noise and anchoring on a ground-truth template; (2) Temporal Guidance Synthesizer (TGS) converts the calibrated history into a compact set of dynamic temporal priors that guide prediction. The framework is lightweight and demonstrated by integrating DTPTrack into OSTrack, ODTrack, and LoRAT, yielding consistent performance gains.
AI.result: Across all three baselines, DTPTrack provides significant and consistent performance gains. The strongest model, based on an extended LoRATv2 backbone, achieves a new SOTA on several benchmarks: 77.5% Suc (LaSOT) and 80.3% AO (GOT-10k).
AI.conclusion: The proposed TRC+TGS module effectively suppresses drift by leveraging calibrated history as dynamic priors, and demonstrates high generality across diverse tracker architectures, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks.

title: SEDGE: Structural Extrapolated Data Generation
summary: This paper proposes a framework for Structural Extrapolated Data GEneration (SEDGE) based on suitable assumptions on the underlying data generating process. We provide conditions under which data satisfying new specifications can be generated reliably, together with the approximate identifiability of the distribution of such data under certain ``conservative" assumptions. On the algorithmic side, we develop practical methods to achieve extrapolated data generation, based on the structure-informed optimization strategy or diffusion posterior sampling, respectively. We verify the extrapolation performance on synthetic data and also consider extrapolated image generation as a real-world scenario to illustrate the validity of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: SEDGE provides a framework for extrapolated data generation under structured assumptions about the data-generating process, with two algorithmic paths (structure-informed optimization and diffusion posterior sampling) and theoretical/identifiability conditions, validated on synthetic data and extrapolated images.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable generation of data beyond the observed distribution by leveraging underlying process structure and conservative identifiability assumptions, addressing data insufficiency for downstream tasks requiring out-of-distribution samples.
AI.method: (i) Derive conditions under which extrapolated data can be generated reliably given the assumed structure; (ii) propose two practical algorithms: a structure-informed optimization approach and diffusion posterior sampling; (iii) validate on synthetic data and demonstrate extrapolated image generation in real-world-like settings.
AI.result: The paper establishes theoretical conditions for reliable extrapolation and approximate identifiability under conservative assumptions; it provides practical algorithms and demonstrates extrapolation performance on synthetic data and extrapolated images.
AI.conclusion: SEDGE offers a principled framework linking structural assumptions to extrapolated data generation with tractable algorithms and empirical validation, while highlighting dependencies on model accuracy and the conservativeness of identifiability assumptions.

title: Chart-RL: Policy Optimization Reinforcement Learning for Enhanced Visual Reasoning in Chart Question Answering with Vision Language Models
comment: In Proceedings of the 32nd ACM-SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary: The recent advancements in Vision Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated progress toward true intelligence requiring robust reasoning capabilities. Beyond pattern recognition, linguistic reasoning must integrate with visual comprehension, particularly for Chart Question Answering (CQA) tasks involving complex data visualizations. Current VLMs face significant limitations in CQA, including imprecise numerical extraction, difficulty interpreting implicit visual relationships, and inadequate attention mechanisms for capturing spatial relationships in charts. In this work, we address these challenges by presenting Chart-RL, a novel reinforcement learning framework that enhances VLMs chart understanding through feedback-driven policy optimization of visual perception and logical inference. Our key innovation includes a comprehensive framework integrating Reinforcement Learning (RL) from Policy Optimization techniques along with adaptive reward functions, that demonstrates superior performance compared to baseline foundation models and competitive results against larger state-of-the-art architectures. We also integrated Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning through Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) in the RL framework that only requires single GPU configurations while preserving performance integrity. We conducted extensive benchmarking across open-source, proprietary, and state-of-the-art closed-source models utilizing the ChartQAPro dataset. The RL fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct model achieved an answer accuracy of 0.634, surpassing the 0.580 accuracy of the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct foundation model despite utilizing half the parameter count, while simultaneously reducing inference latency from 31 seconds to 9 seconds.

AI.tldr: Chart-RL introduces a reinforcement-learning–driven framework to improve chart-based visual-language reasoning (ChartQA) by optimizing perception and inference policies with adaptive rewards, plus LoRA-based parameter-efficient fine-tuning. It achieves stronger accuracy and faster inference than larger baselines using fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Address core bottlenecks in chart question answering: imprecise numerical extraction, difficulty interpreting implicit visual relationships, and inadequate capture of spatial relationships in charts by current vision-language models.
AI.method: A reinforcement learning framework with policy optimization and adaptive reward shaping to guide visual perception and logical inference. It incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, enabling single-GPU training. The approach is evaluated by fine-tuning Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct on ChartQAPro and comparing against larger models and baselines.
AI.result: On ChartQAPro, RL-fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct achieves 0.634 accuracy, surpassing Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct's 0.580 accuracy, while using half the parameters and reducing inference latency from 31s to 9s.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that policy-optimized RL with adaptive rewards and LoRA can yield superior chart understanding efficiency, offering competitive performance relative to larger models and highlighting the potential of RL-augmented VLMs for chart reasoning.

title: FSUNav: A Cerebrum-Cerebellum Architecture for Fast, Safe, and Universal Zero-Shot Goal-Oriented Navigation
summary: Current vision-language navigation methods face substantial bottlenecks regarding heterogeneous robot compatibility, real-time performance, and navigation safety. Furthermore, they struggle to support open-vocabulary semantic generalization and multimodal task inputs. To address these challenges, this paper proposes FSUNav: a Cerebrum-Cerebellum architecture for fast, safe, and universal zero-shot goal-oriented navigation, which innovatively integrates vision-language models (VLMs) with the proposed architecture. The cerebellum module, a high-frequency end-to-end module, develops a universal local planner based on deep reinforcement learning, enabling unified navigation across heterogeneous platforms (e.g., humanoid, quadruped, wheeled robots) to improve navigation efficiency while significantly reducing collision risk. The cerebrum module constructs a three-layer reasoning model and leverages VLMs to build an end-to-end detection and verification mechanism, enabling zero-shot open-vocabulary goal navigation without predefined IDs and improving task success rates in both simulation and real-world environments. Additionally, the framework supports multimodal inputs (e.g., text, target descriptions, and images), further enhancing generalization, real-time performance, safety, and robustness. Experimental results on MP3D, HM3D, and OVON benchmarks demonstrate that FSUNav achieves state-of-the-art performance on object, instance image, and task navigation, significantly outperforming existing methods. Real-world deployments on diverse robotic platforms further validate its robustness and practical applicability.

AI.tldr: FSUNav introduces a Cerebrum-Cerebellum architecture that unifies VLM-enabled perception with a high-frequency local planner for fast, safe, and universal zero-shot navigation across heterogeneous robots, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks and real-world deployments.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language navigation methods struggle with heterogeneous robot compatibility, real-time performance, safety, and open-vocabulary generalization for multimodal inputs. A unified architecture that leverages VLMs while maintaining fast, platform-agnostic planning could address these bottlenecks.
AI.method: Proposes FSUNav with two modules: (1) cerebellum — a high-frequency end-to-end local planner for universal navigation across humanoid, quadruped, and wheeled robots using deep reinforcement learning; (2) cerebrum — a three-layer reasoning model that uses VLMs for end-to-end detection and verification to enable zero-shot open-vocabulary goal navigation without predefined IDs, plus multimodal inputs (text, descriptions, images).
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on MP3D, HM3D, and OVON benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance for object, instance image, and task navigation. Real-world deployments on diverse robotic platforms demonstrate robustness and practical applicability, with improvements in navigation efficiency and collision risk reduction.
AI.conclusion: FSUNav demonstrates a scalable, universal navigation framework that combines VLM-based perception with a fast, safe local planner, enabling zero-shot, open-vocabulary navigation across heterogeneous robots and modalities, with strong performance in simulation and real-world deployments.

title: Efficient3D: A Unified Framework for Adaptive and Debiased Token Reduction in 3D MLLMs
summary: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have expanded reasoning capabilities into 3D domains, enabling fine-grained spatial understanding. However, the substantial size of 3D MLLMs and the high dimensionality of input features introduce considerable inference overhead, which limits practical deployment on resource constrained platforms. To overcome this limitation, this paper presents Efficient3D, a unified framework for visual token pruning that accelerates 3D MLLMs while maintaining competitive accuracy. The proposed framework introduces a Debiased Visual Token Importance Estimator (DVTIE) module, which considers the influence of shallow initial layers during attention aggregation, thereby producing more reliable importance predictions for visual tokens. In addition, an Adaptive Token Rebalancing (ATR) strategy is developed to dynamically adjust pruning strength based on scene complexity, preserving semantic completeness and maintaining balanced attention across layers. Together, they enable context-aware token reduction that maintains essential semantics with lower computation. Comprehensive experiments conducted on five representative 3D vision and language benchmarks, including ScanRefer, Multi3DRefer, Scan2Cap, ScanQA, and SQA3D, demonstrate that Efficient3D achieves superior performance compared with unpruned baselines, with a +2.57% CIDEr improvement on the Scan2Cap dataset. Therefore, Efficient3D provides a scalable and effective solution for efficient inference in 3D MLLMs. The code is released at: https://github.com/sol924/Efficient3D

AI.tldr: Efficient3D presents a unified framework for visual token pruning in 3D MLLMs, combining a Debiased Visual Token Importance Estimator (DVTIE) that accounts for shallow-layer influence during attention, with an Adaptive Token Rebalancing (ATR) mechanism that adjusts pruning strength based on scene complexity, achieving competitive accuracy with lower computation (notably +2.57% CIDEr on Scan2Cap).
AI.motivation: Reduce the substantial inference overhead and high dimensionality of 3D MLLMs while preserving semantic richness and task performance; enable practical deployment on resource-constrained platforms through token-level pruning guided by reliable importance estimates.
AI.method: DVTIE: debiases token importance by incorporating influence from early layers in attention aggregation to produce more reliable token rankings. ATR: adaptively tunes pruning strength according to scene complexity, ensuring semantic completeness and balanced multi-layer attention. Framework applied to 3D-LLMs across multiple benchmarks; code released.
AI.result: On five 3D vision-language benchmarks (ScanRefer, Multi3DRefer, Scan2Cap, ScanQA, SQA3D), Efficient3D achieves superior performance compared with unpruned baselines, exemplified by a +2.57% CIDEr improvement on Scan2Cap. The approach demonstrates effective context-aware token reduction with reduced computation.
AI.conclusion: Efficient3D offers a scalable, effective approach for acceleration of 3D MLLMs via intelligent token pruning, balancing efficiency and semantic fidelity; its reported gains on standard benchmarks and public code release suggest practical impact.

title: Causal-Audit: A Framework for Risk Assessment of Assumption Violations in Time-Series Causal Discovery
comment: 28 pages, 10 figures, 15 tables. Being submitted to Journal of Causal Inference JCI
summary: Time-series causal discovery methods rely on assumptions such as stationarity, regular sampling, and bounded temporal dependence. When these assumptions are violated, structure learning can produce confident but misleading causal graphs without warning. We introduce Causal-Audit, a framework that formalizes assumption validation as calibrated risk assessment. The framework computes effect-size diagnostics across five assumption families (stationarity, irregularity, persistence, nonlinearity, and confounding proxies), aggregates them into four calibrated risk scores with uncertainty intervals, and applies an abstention-aware decision policy that recommends methods (e.g., PCMCI+, VAR-based Granger causality) only when evidence supports reliable inference. The semi-automatic diagnostic stage can also be used independently for structured assumption auditing in individual studies. Evaluation on a synthetic atlas of 500 data-generating processes (DGPs) spanning 10 violation families demonstrates well-calibrated risk scores (AUROC > 0.95), a 62% false positive reduction among recommended datasets, and 78% abstention on severe-violation cases. On 21 external evaluations from TimeGraph (18 categories) and CausalTime (3 domains), recommend-or-abstain decisions are consistent with benchmark specifications in all cases. An open-source implementation of our framework is available.

AI.tldr: Causal-Audit introduces calibrated risk assessment for validating assumptions in time-series causal discovery, using five assumption families to derive four calibrated risk scores with uncertainty, plus an abstention policy for reliable method selection; validated on synthetic and external datasets with strong calibration and reduced misinference; open-source implementation available.
AI.motivation: To prevent confident but incorrect causal graphs when key time-series assumptions (stationarity, sampling regularity, etc.) are violated, by auditing assumptions and allowing abstention from inference when evidence is weak.
AI.method: Compute effect-size diagnostics across five assumption families (stationarity, irregularity, persistence, nonlinearity, confounding proxies); synthesize into four calibrated risk scores with uncertainty intervals; apply abstention-aware decision policy to recommend methods (e.g., PCMCI+, VAR-based Granger causality) only when reliable; semi-automatic diagnostic stage usable independently; open-source implementation.
AI.result: On a synthetic atlas of 500 DGPs across 10 violation families, risk scores show AUROC > 0.95, 62% reduction in false positives among recommended datasets, and 78% abstention on severe-violation cases; external evaluations from TimeGraph (18 categories) and CausalTime (3 domains) show decisions align with benchmarks in all cases.
AI.conclusion: Causal-Audit provides calibrated, interpretable risk assessments for assumption validation in time-series causal discovery and a robust abstention mechanism, improving reliability; framework is open-source for broader adoption.

title: Coupled Control, Structured Memory, and Verifiable Action in Agentic AI (SCRAT -- Stochastic Control with Retrieval and Auditable Trajectories): A Comparative Perspective from Squirrel Locomotion and Scatter-Hoarding
summary: Agentic AI is increasingly judged not by fluent output alone but by whether it can act, remember, and verify under partial observability, delay, and strategic observation. Existing research often studies these demands separately: robotics emphasizes control, retrieval systems emphasize memory, and alignment or assurance work emphasizes checking and oversight. This article argues that squirrel ecology offers a sharp comparative case because arboreal locomotion, scatter-hoarding, and audience-sensitive caching couple all three demands in one organism. We synthesize evidence from fox, eastern gray, and, in one field comparison, red squirrels, and impose an explicit inference ladder: empirical observation, minimal computational inference, and AI design conjecture. We introduce a minimal hierarchical partially observed control model with latent dynamics, structured episodic memory, observer-belief state, option-level actions, and delayed verifier signals. This motivates three hypotheses: (H1) fast local feedback plus predictive compensation improves robustness under hidden dynamics shifts; (H2) memory organized for future control improves delayed retrieval under cue conflict and load; and (H3) verifiers and observer models inside the action-memory loop reduce silent failure and information leakage while remaining vulnerable to misspecification. A downstream conjecture is that role-differentiated proposer/executor/checker/adversary systems may reduce correlated error under asymmetric information and verification burden. The contribution is a comparative perspective and benchmark agenda: a disciplined program of falsifiable claims about the coupling of control, memory, and verifiable action.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework unifying control, memory, and verification in agentic AI, using squirrel ecology as a benchmark to propose a minimal hierarchical POMDP with latent dynamics, episodic memory, observer beliefs, and delayed verification; tests three hypotheses and a benchmark agenda.
AI.motivation: Current AI research treats control, memory, and verification separately; there is a need for integrated models operating under partial observability and delayed feedback. Squirrel ecology provides an integrated, comparative case to study coupling of these demands, enabling falsifiable claims about agentic behavior.
AI.method: Introduce a minimal hierarchical partially observed control model with latent dynamics, structured episodic memory, observer-belief state, option-level actions, and delayed verifier signals; synthesize evidence from fox and two squirrel species; apply an explicit inference ladder: empirical observation, minimal computational inference, and AI design conjecture; formulate three hypotheses (H1–H3) and a downstream conjecture on role-differentiated proposer/executor/checker/adversary systems; propose a benchmark agenda for falsifiable claims.
AI.result: Proposes a conceptual framework and three testable hypotheses (H1–H3); outlines a downstream architecture and a benchmark program, but provides no empirical results yet.
AI.conclusion: Offers a disciplined, falsifiable program to study how control, memory, and verification co-evolve and interact within agentic AI, using a comparative biological benchmark to guide architecture design and evaluation.

title: Multi-View Video Diffusion Policy: A 3D Spatio-Temporal-Aware Video Action Model
summary: Robotic manipulation requires understanding both the 3D spatial structure of the environment and its temporal evolution, yet most existing policies overlook one or both. They typically rely on 2D visual observations and backbones pretrained on static image--text pairs, resulting in high data requirements and limited understanding of environment dynamics. To address this, we introduce MV-VDP, a multi-view video diffusion policy that jointly models the 3D spatio-temporal state of the environment. The core idea is to simultaneously predict multi-view heatmap videos and RGB videos, which 1) align the representation format of video pretraining with action finetuning, and 2) specify not only what actions the robot should take, but also how the environment is expected to evolve in response to those actions. Extensive experiments show that MV-VDP enables data-efficient, robust, generalizable, and interpretable manipulation. With only ten demonstration trajectories and without additional pretraining, MV-VDP successfully performs complex real-world tasks, demonstrates strong robustness across a range of model hyperparameters, generalizes to out-of-distribution settings, and predicts realistic future videos. Experiments on Meta-World and real-world robotic platforms demonstrate that MV-VDP consistently outperforms video-prediction--based, 3D-based, and vision--language--action models, establishing a new state of the art in data-efficient multi-task manipulation.

AI.tldr: MV-VDP is a multi-view video diffusion policy that jointly models 3D spatio-temporal state by predicting multi-view heatmap videos and RGB videos, enabling data-efficient, robust, and interpretable manipulation with strong performance on Meta-World and real robots using only ten demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Current robotic manipulation relies heavily on 2D observations and static pretraining, leading to high data requirements and poor modeling of environment dynamics. There's a need to capture both the 3D spatial structure and its temporal evolution to improve data efficiency, generalization, and interpretability.
AI.method: MV-VDP introduces a diffusion-policy framework that jointly predicts multi-view heatmap videos (capturing 3D structure across viewpoints) and RGB videos, aligning video-language pretraining with action finetuning and providing forward-looking predictions of environment evolution in response to actions. Training uses only ten demonstration trajectories with no additional pretraining.
AI.result: The approach yields data-efficient, robust, generalizable, and interpretable manipulation. It demonstrates strong robustness to hyperparameters, generalizes to out-of-distribution settings, and predicts realistic future videos. In experiments on Meta-World and real robotic platforms, MV-VDP outperforms video-prediction–based, 3D-based, and vision-language–action models, achieving state-of-the-art performance in data-efficient multi-task manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling 3D spatio-temporal state via a diffusion-based policy enables efficient learning, robust generalization, and interpretable future-state predictions for manipulation tasks. This suggests promising avenues for data-efficient, visually guided robotic control and potential extensions to more complex tasks, planning integration, and broader modalities.

title: Parser-Oriented Structural Refinement for a Stable Layout Interface in Document Parsing
summary: Accurate document parsing requires both robust content recognition and a stable parser interface. In explicit Document Layout Analysis (DLA) pipelines, downstream parsers do not consume the full detector output. Instead, they operate on a retained and serialized set of layout instances. However, on dense pages with overlapping regions and ambiguous boundaries, unstable layout hypotheses can make the retained instance set inconsistent with its parser input order, leading to severe downstream parsing errors. To address this issue, we introduce a lightweight structural refinement stage between a DETR-style detector and the parser to stabilize the parser interface. Treating raw detector outputs as a compact hypothesis pool, the proposed module performs set-level reasoning over query features, semantic cues, box geometry, and visual evidence. From a shared refined structural state, it jointly determines instance retention, refines box localization, and predicts parser input order before handoff. We further introduce retention-oriented supervision and a difficulty-aware ordering objective to better align the retained instance set and its order with the final parser input, especially on structurally complex pages. Extensive experiments on public benchmarks show that our method consistently improves page-level layout quality. When integrated into a standard end-to-end parsing pipeline, the stabilized parser interface also substantially reduces sequence mismatch, achieving a Reading Order Edit of 0.024 on OmniDocBench.

AI.tldr: A lightweight structural refinement stage bridges a DETR-style detector and a parser to stabilize parser input quality by jointly deciding which instances to retain, refining boxes, and predicting parser order; includes retention-oriented supervision and difficulty-aware ordering, yielding improved page-level layout quality and reduced Reading Order Edit (ROE) on OmniDocBench (0.024).
AI.motivation: In explicit DLA pipelines, downstream parsers do not consume the full detector output. Dense pages with overlapping regions and ambiguous boundaries yield unstable layout hypotheses that can become inconsistent with the parser input order, causing substantial downstream parsing errors.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight refinement module that treats detector outputs as a compact hypothesis pool and performs set-level reasoning over query features, semantic cues, box geometry, and visual evidence. From a shared refined structural state, it jointly determines instance retention, refines box localization, and predicts parser input order before handoff. Includes retention-oriented supervision and a difficulty-aware ordering objective to align retained instances and their order with the final parser input on structurally complex pages.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public benchmarks show consistent improvements in page-level layout quality. Integrated into a standard end-to-end parsing pipeline, the stabilized parser interface substantially reduces sequence mismatch, achieving a Reading Order Edit of 0.024 on OmniDocBench.
AI.conclusion: Stabilizing the parser interface via a structural refinement stage improves alignment between retained instances and parser input, enhancing parsing reliability on dense, structured pages.

title: Re-analysis of the Human Transcription Factor Atlas Recovers TF-Specific Signatures from Pooled Single-Cell Screens with Missing Controls
summary: Public pooled single-cell perturbation atlases are valuable resources for studying transcription factor (TF) function, but downstream re-analysis can be limited by incomplete deposited metadata and missing internal controls. Here we re-analyze the human TF Atlas dataset (GSE216481), a MORF-based pooled overexpression screen spanning 3,550 TF open reading frames and 254,519 cells, with a reproducible pipeline for quality control, MORF barcode demultiplexing, per-TF differential expression, and functional enrichment. From 77,018 cells in the pooled screen, we assign 60,997 (79.2\%) to 87 TF identities. Because the deposited barcode mapping lacks the GFP and mCherry negative controls present in the original library, we use embryoid body (EB) cells as an external baseline and remove shared batch/transduction artifacts by background subtraction. This strategy recovers TF-specific signatures for 59 of 61 testable TFs, compared with 27 detected by one-vs-rest alone, showing that robust TF-level signal can be rescued despite missing intra-pool controls. HOPX, MAZ, PAX6, FOS, and FEZF2 emerge as the strongest transcriptional remodelers, while per-TF enrichment links FEZF2 to regulation of differentiation, EGR1 to Hippo and cardiac programs, FOS to focal adhesion, and NFIC to collagen biosynthesis. Condition-level analyses reveal convergent Wnt, neurogenic, EMT, and Hippo signatures, and Harmony indicates minimal confounding batch effects across pooled replicates. Our per-TF effect sizes significantly agree with Joung et al.'s published rankings (Spearman $ρ= -0.316$, $p = 0.013$; negative because lower rank indicates stronger effect). Together, these results show that the deposited TF Atlas data can support validated TF-specific transcriptional and pathway analyses when paired with principled external controls, artifact removal, and reproducible computation.

AI.tldr: Reanalysis of the human TF Atlas (GSE216481) MORF-based pooled overexpression screen using an external baseline (embryoid body cells) to recover TF-specific signatures despite missing intra-pool controls. Demonstrates robust TF-level signal recovery (59/61 TFs) and convergent pathway signatures, with substantial agreement to Joung et al. rankings.
AI.motivation: Incomplete deposited metadata and absence of internal pool controls hinder downstream TF-level analyses; the study aims to devise a reproducible pipeline with external baselines and artifact removal to recover TF-specific transcriptional programs.
AI.method: Reanalysis pipeline including quality control, MORF barcode demultiplexing, per-TF differential expression, and functional enrichment. External baseline from embryoid body cells used for background subtraction to remove batch/transduction artifacts; use Harmony for batch-effect assessment; cross-validation against published rankings.
AI.result: From 77,018 pool-annotated cells, 60,997 (79.2%) assigned to 87 TFs. After baseline/background subtraction, TF-specific signatures recovered for 59 of 61 testable TFs (vs 27 by one-vs-rest). Key remodelers include HOPX, MAZ, PAX6, FOS, FEZF2; enriched programs link FEZF2 to differentiation, EGR1 to Hippo/cardiac, FOS to focal adhesion, NFIC to collagen biosynthesis. Convergent condition-level Wnt, neurogenic, EMT, Hippo signatures; minimal batch confounding by Harmony. Per-TF effects correlate with Joung et al. rankings (Spearman ρ = -0.316, p = 0.013).
AI.conclusion: Deposited TF Atlas data can support validated TF-specific transcriptional and pathway analyses when coupled with principled external controls, artifact removal, and reproducible computation; external baselines enable recovery of TF-level signals despite missing internal controls.

title: The Compression Gap: Why Discrete Tokenization Limits Vision-Language-Action Model Scaling
summary: Scaling Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by upgrading the vision encoder is expected to improve downstream manipulation performance--as it does in vision-language modeling. We show that this expectation fails when actions are represented as discrete tokens, and explain why through an information-theoretic principle we call the Compression Gap: in any visuomotor pipeline, scaling behavior is governed by the location of the tightest information bottleneck. When actions are continuous (e.g., Diffusion Policy), the vision encoder is the binding constraint, and upgrading it directly improves performance. When actions are discretized through a fixed-capacity codebook (e.g., OAT), the codebook becomes the binding constraint, and encoder improvements cannot propagate past it--regardless of how rich the upstream representation is. We validate this principle on the LIBERO benchmark with three lines of evidence: a factorial experiment showing that encoder upgrades improve Diffusion Policy by over 21 percentage points while OAT gains are substantially attenuated across model scales; an encoder quality gradient across four encoders confirming that Diffusion Policy tracks encoder quality monotonically while OAT remains flat; and a codebook size experiment demonstrating that relaxing codebook capacity partially recovers encoder sensitivity, providing causal evidence for the bottleneck hypothesis. Our findings reveal that scaling in Physical AI requires identifying where information bottlenecks lie in the pipeline, rather than uniformly increasing model or data size.

AI.tldr: Scaling VLA models by simply upgrading the vision encoder does not uniformly boost downstream manipulation when actions are discretized; a bottleneck, the Compression Gap, limits gains. Continuous actions (Diffusion Policy) benefit from better encoders, while discrete action codes (OAT) cap improvements regardless of encoder quality.
AI.motivation: Understand why scaling vision encoders in Vision-Language-Action systems does not always translate to better downstream manipulation when actions are discretized into a fixed-capacity codebook, and formalize this via an information-theoretic compression gap.
AI.method: Factorial experiments on LIBERO comparing continuous-action (Diffusion Policy) vs discrete-action (OAT) pipelines across model scales, encoder quality gradient across four encoders, and a codebook-size manipulation to test bottlenecks and causal influence of encoder quality on downstream performance.
AI.result: Continuous-action Diffusion Policy shows strong sensitivity to encoder upgrades (≈+21 percentage points with scaling); discrete-action OAT gains are substantially attenuated as models scale. Encoder quality correlates monotonically with Diffusion Policy but remains flat for OAT. Increasing codebook capacity partially recovers encoder sensitivity, supporting the bottleneck hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: Scaling in Physical AI should target the true information bottlenecks in the pipeline rather than uniformly increasing model or data size; identifying where bottlenecks lie is essential to predict where scaling will help.

title: DocShield: Towards AI Document Safety via Evidence-Grounded Agentic Reasoning
summary: The rapid progress of generative AI has enabled increasingly realistic text-centric image forgeries, posing major challenges to document safety. Existing forensic methods mainly rely on visual cues and lack evidence-based reasoning to reveal subtle text manipulations. Detection, localization, and explanation are often treated as isolated tasks, limiting reliability and interpretability. To tackle these challenges, we propose DocShield, the first unified framework formulating text-centric forgery analysis as a visual-logical co-reasoning problem. At its core, a novel Cross-Cues-aware Chain of Thought (CCT) mechanism enables implicit agentic reasoning, iteratively cross-validating visual anomalies with textual semantics to produce consistent, evidence-grounded forensic analysis. We further introduce a Weighted Multi-Task Reward for GRPO-based optimization, aligning reasoning structure, spatial evidence, and authenticity prediction. Complementing the framework, we construct RealText-V1, a multilingual dataset of document-like text images with pixel-level manipulation masks and expert-level textual explanations. Extensive experiments show DocShield significantly outperforms existing methods, improving macro-average F1 by 41.4% over specialized frameworks and 23.4% over GPT-4o on T-IC13, with consistent gains on the challenging T-SROIE benchmark. Our dataset, model, and code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: A unified, cross-cues chain-of-thought framework (DocShield) for text-centric forgery analysis in documents, combining visual anomalies and textual semantics with a GRPO-optimized multi-task objective, plus RealText-V1 dataset with pixel-level manipulation masks and explanations; shows large performance gains and will release code and data.
AI.motivation: Current document forgery tools rely on visual cues and treat detection, localization, and explanation as separate tasks, offering limited evidence-based, interpretable reasoning for text-centric manipulations. There is a need for integrated, explainable analysis that jointly reasons about visuals and text.
AI.method: Introduce Cross-Cues-aware Chain of Thought (CCT) to iteratively cross-validate visual anomalies with textual semantics for evidence-grounded reasoning. Employ a Weighted Multi-Task Reward for GRPO-based optimization to align reasoning structure, spatial evidence, and authenticity prediction. Build RealText-V1, a multilingual document-like text image dataset with pixel-level manipulation masks and expert-level textual explanations.
AI.result: DocShield achieves substantial improvements, including a macro F1 increase of 41.4% over specialized baselines and 23.4% over GPT-4o on T-IC13, with consistent gains on T-SROIE. RealText-V1 and the model-code will be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: The framework unifies detection, localization, and explanation under a single, evidence-grounded reasoning paradigm for text-centric forgeries, advancing interpretability and reliability in document forensics.

title: AdaHOP: Fast and Accurate Low-Precision Training via Outlier-Pattern-Aware Rotation
summary: Low-precision training (LPT) commonly employs Hadamard transforms to suppress outliers and mitigate quantization error in large language models (LLMs). However, prior methods apply a fixed transform uniformly, despite substantial variation in outlier structures across tensors. Through the first systematic study of outlier patterns across weights, activations, and gradients of LLMs, we show that this strategy is fundamentally flawed: the effectiveness of Hadamard-based suppression depends on how the transform's smoothing direction aligns with the outlier structure of each operand -- a property that varies substantially across layers and computation paths. We characterize these patterns into three types: Row-wise, Column-wise, and None. Each pair requires a tailored transform direction or outlier handling strategy to minimize quantization error. Based on this insight, we propose AdaHOP (Adaptive Hadamard transform with Outlier-Pattern-aware strategy), which assigns each matrix multiplication its optimal strategy: Inner Hadamard Transform (IHT) where inner-dimension smoothing is effective, or IHT combined with selective Outlier Extraction (OE) -- routing dominant outliers to a high-precision path -- where it is not. Combined with hardware-aware Triton kernels, AdaHOP achieves BF16 training quality at MXFP4 precision while delivering up to 3.6X memory compression and 1.8X kernel acceleration} over BF16 full-precision training.

AI.tldr: AdaHOP adapts Hadamard-based outlier suppression to the observed outlier patterns in LLM training, aligning transform direction to row-wise, column-wise, or none patterns per operand, enabling selective outlier extraction and inner-Hadamard transforms to improve quantization and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Fixed Hadamard transforms in low-precision training poorly handle diverse outlier structures across weights, activations, and gradients, leading to suboptimal quantization error and efficiency. A systematic characterization of outlier patterns motivates an adaptive strategy.
AI.method: Conducted a systematic study of outlier patterns in weights, activations, and gradients of LLMs. Classified patterns into three types: Row-wise, Column-wise, and None. Proposed AdaHOP (Adaptive Hadamard transform with Outlier-Pattern-aware strategy) that selects between Inner Hadamard Transform (IHT) and IHT with selective Outlier Extraction (OE) per matrix multiplication. Implemented with hardware-aware Triton kernels to optimize performance.
AI.result: AdaHOP delivers BF16 training quality at MXFP4 precision, with up to 3.6× memory compression and 1.8× kernel acceleration compared with BF16 full-precision training.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, pattern-aware Hadamard strategies significantly reduce quantization error and improve training efficiency in LPT for LLMs, outperforming fixed-transform approaches.

title: Safety-Critical Centralized Nonlinear MPC for Cooperative Payload Transportation by Two Quadrupedal Robots
summary: This paper presents a safety-critical centralized nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) framework for cooperative payload transportation by two quadrupedal robots. The interconnected robot-payload system is modeled as a discrete-time nonlinear differential-algebraic system, capturing the coupled dynamics through holonomic constraints and interaction wrenches. To ensure safety in complex environments, we develop a control barrier function (CBF)-based NMPC formulation that enforces collision avoidance constraints for both the robots and the payload. The proposed approach retains the interaction wrenches as decision variables, resulting in a structured DAE-constrained optimal control problem that enables efficient real-time implementation. The effectiveness of the algorithm is validated through extensive hardware experiments on two Unitree Go2 platforms performing cooperative payload transportation in cluttered environments under mass and inertia uncertainty and external push disturbances.

AI.tldr: A safety-critical NMPC with a control barrier function (CBF) enforces collision avoidance for two quadrotors transporting a payload on a discrete-time nonlinear DAE model, treating interaction wrenches as decision variables; demonstrated in hardware for real-time operation in cluttered environments.
AI.motivation: Ensure safety and robustness in cooperative payload transport under uncertainty and disturbances by integrating CBFs with NMPC to satisfy collision-avoidance and safety constraints for both agents and payload.
AI.method: Model the interconnected robot-payload system as a discrete-time nonlinear differential-algebraic system with holonomic constraints. Retain interaction wrenches as decision variables within a CBF-based NMPC framework, forming a structured DAE-constrained OCP for real-time solve and collision avoidance. Validate via hardware experiments on two Unitree Go2 platforms performing cooperative payload transport under mass/inertia uncertainty and external pushes.
AI.result: The approach yields successful hardware validation: two Go2 robots cooperatively transport payloads in cluttered environments with robustness to mass/inertia uncertainty and push disturbances, achieving real-time performance.
AI.conclusion: A CBF-based NMPC on a DAE formulation enables safe, real-time cooperative payload transport with coupled dynamics and interaction wrenches, demonstrated on quadrotor platforms in cluttered environments.

title: XrayClaw: Cooperative-Competitive Multi-Agent Alignment for Trustworthy Chest X-ray Diagnosis
summary: Chest X-ray (CXR) interpretation is a fundamental yet complex clinical task that increasingly relies on artificial intelligence for automation. However, traditional monolithic models often lack the nuanced reasoning required for trustworthy diagnosis, frequently leading to logical inconsistencies and diagnostic hallucinations. While multi-agent systems offer a potential solution by simulating collaborative consultations, existing frameworks remain susceptible to consensus-based errors when instantiated by a single underlying model. This paper introduces XrayClaw, a novel framework that operationalizes multi-agent alignment through a sophisticated cooperative-competitive architecture. XrayClaw integrates four specialized cooperative agents to simulate a systematic clinical workflow, alongside a competitive agent that serves as an independent auditor. To reconcile these distinct diagnostic pathways, we propose Competitive Preference Optimization, a learning objective that penalizes illogical reasoning by enforcing mutual verification between analytical and holistic interpretations. Extensive empirical evaluations on the MS-CXR-T, MIMIC-CXR, and CheXbench benchmarks demonstrate that XrayClaw achieves state-of-the-art performance in diagnostic accuracy, clinical reasoning fidelity, and zero-shot domain generalization. Our results indicate that XrayClaw effectively mitigates cumulative hallucinations and enhances the overall reliability of automated CXR diagnosis, establishing a new paradigm for trustworthy medical imaging analysis.

AI.tldr: XrayClaw introduces a cooperative-competitive multi-agent framework for chest X-ray interpretation, using four cooperative agents plus an auditor and Competitive Preference Optimization to ensure logical, verified reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art results and better generalization while reducing hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Monolithic models in medical imaging often produce inconsistent or hallucinated reasoning. Existing multi-agent systems still suffer from consensus-based errors when driven by a single underlying model; there is a need for trustworthy, verifiable AI that can simulate clinical workflows and mitigate cumulative reasoning errors.
AI.method: Four cooperative agents model a clinical workflow; a competitive auditor agent provides independent evaluation. Competitive Preference Optimization enforces mutual verification between analytical and holistic interpretations, penalizes illogical reasoning, and fosters cross-checks. Evaluations on MS-CXR-T, MIMIC-CXR, CheXbench with emphasis on diagnostic accuracy, reasoning fidelity, and zero-shot generalization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art diagnostic performance, improved clinical reasoning fidelity, robust zero-shot domain generalization, reduced cumulative hallucinations, and higher reliability in automated CXR diagnosis on the evaluated benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: XrayClaw demonstrates a new paradigm for trustworthy medical imaging analysis via cooperative-competitive multi-agent alignment, with potential to enhance reliability and generalization in AI-assisted radiology.

title: Jump Start or False Start? A Theoretical and Empirical Evaluation of LLM-initialized Bandits
summary: The recent advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) offers new opportunities to generate user preference data to warm-start bandits. Recent studies on contextual bandits with LLM initialization (CBLI) have shown that these synthetic priors can significantly lower early regret. However, these findings assume that LLM-generated choices are reasonably aligned with actual user preferences. In this paper, we systematically examine how LLM-generated preferences perform when random and label-flipping noise is injected into the synthetic training data. For aligned domains, we find that warm-starting remains effective up to 30% corruption, loses its advantage around 40%, and degrades performance beyond 50%. When there is systematic misalignment, even without added noise, LLM-generated priors can lead to higher regret than a cold-start bandit. To explain these behaviors, we develop a theoretical analysis that decomposes the effect of random label noise and systematic misalignment on the prior error driving the bandit's regret, and derive a sufficient condition under which LLM-based warm starts are provably better than a cold-start bandit. We validate these results across multiple conjoint datasets and LLMs, showing that estimated alignment reliably tracks when warm-starting improves or degrades recommendation quality.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how robust LLM-initialized warm starts for contextual bandits are to noise and misalignment in synthetic preference data, offering a theoretical decomposition, a sufficient-condition guarantee for benefit, and empirical validation across datasets and LLMs.
AI.motivation: Understand when LLM-generated preferences help or hurt warm-started bandits, especially under noisy or misaligned training data.
AI.method: Theoretical decomposition of prior error into random label noise and systematic misalignment; derivation of a sufficient condition for improvement over cold-start; empirical validation on conjoint datasets using multiple LLMs with injected noise.
AI.result: In aligned domains, warm starts remain beneficial up to about 30% label corruption, become ineffective around 40%, and decline beyond 50% corruption. With systematic misalignment, priors can increase regret even without noise. Alignment estimates track when warm-starts improve or degrade, and the sufficient condition predicts when LLM-based warm starts are provably better.
AI.conclusion: Alignment quality of LLM-generated priors is crucial for the success of warm-started contextual bandits. The paper provides a theoretical framework and empirical evidence, guiding when to rely on LLM priors and highlighting directions for robust, adaptive warm-start strategies.

title: VBGS-SLAM: Variational Bayesian Gaussian Splatting Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has shown promising results for 3D scene modeling using mixtures of Gaussians, yet its existing simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) variants typically rely on direct, deterministic pose optimization against the splat map, making them sensitive to initialization and susceptible to catastrophic forgetting as map evolves. We propose Variational Bayesian Gaussian Splatting SLAM (VBGS-SLAM), a novel framework that couples the splat map refinement and camera pose tracking in a generative probabilistic form. By leveraging conjugate properties of multivariate Gaussians and variational inference, our method admits efficient closed-form updates and explicitly maintains posterior uncertainty over both poses and scene parameters. This uncertainty-aware method mitigates drift and enhances robustness in challenging conditions, while preserving the efficiency and rendering quality of existing 3DGS. Our experiments demonstrate superior tracking performance and robustness in long sequence prediction, alongside efficient, high-quality novel view synthesis across diverse synthetic and real-world scenes.

AI.tldr: Introduces VBGS-SLAM, a probabilistic variational Bayesian SLAM framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting that maintains posterior uncertainty over camera poses and scene parameters, improving robustness and reducing drift while preserving rendering quality.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting SLAM methods rely on direct, deterministic pose optimization against the splat map, which is sensitive to initialization and prone to forgetting as the map evolves.
AI.method: Couples splat-map refinement with camera pose tracking in a generative probabilistic model. Leverages conjugate multivariate Gaussian properties and variational inference to enable closed-form updates and explicit posterior uncertainty over poses and scene parameters.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior tracking performance and robustness in long sequence prediction, along with efficient, high-quality novel view synthesis across diverse synthetic and real-world scenes.
AI.conclusion: An uncertainty-aware VB approach mitigates drift and enhances robustness while preserving the efficiency and rendering quality of 3D Gaussian Splatting.

title: A Spectral Framework for Multi-Scale Nonlinear Dimensionality Reduction
summary: Dimensionality reduction (DR) is characterized by two longstanding trade-offs. First, there is a global-local preservation tension: methods such as t-SNE and UMAP prioritize local neighborhood preservation, yet may distort global manifold structure, while methods such as Laplacian Eigenmaps preserve global geometry but often yield limited local separation. Second, there is a gap between expressiveness and analytical transparency: many nonlinear DR methods produce embeddings without an explicit connection to the underlying high-dimensional structure, limiting insight into the embedding process. In this paper, we introduce a spectral framework for nonlinear DR that addresses these challenges. Our approach embeds high-dimensional data using a spectral basis combined with cross-entropy optimization, enabling multi-scale representations that bridge global and local structure. Leveraging linear spectral decomposition, the framework further supports analysis of embeddings through a graph-frequency perspective, enabling examination of how spectral modes influence the resulting embedding. We complement this analysis with glyph-based scatterplot augmentations for visual exploration. Quantitative evaluations and case studies demonstrate that our framework improves manifold continuity while enabling deeper analysis of embedding structure through spectral mode contributions.

AI.tldr: A spectral nonlinear dimensionality reduction framework that uses a spectral basis and cross-entropy optimization to produce multi-scale embeddings with graph-frequency analysis and glyph-based visualization, improving manifold continuity and interpretability.
AI.motivation: DR methods face two persistent tensions: preserving global versus local structure, and achieving expressiveness with analytical transparency. Local methods (e.g., t-SNE/UMAP) may distort global geometry, while global methods (e.g., Laplacian Eigenmaps) can limit local separation and offer little insight into the embedding process. The paper seeks a framework that (i) captures multi-scale global-local structure and (ii) provides explicit connections to the high-dimensional data.
AI.method: Embed high-dimensional data using a spectral basis combined with cross-entropy optimization to enable multi-scale representations. Utilize linear spectral decomposition to enable a graph-frequency perspective for analyzing how spectral modes influence the embedding. Include glyph-based scatterplot augmentations for interactive visual exploration.
AI.result: Quantitative evaluations and case studies indicate improved manifold continuity. The approach also enables deeper analysis of embedding structure through spectral-mode contributions, and offers visualization tools that aid interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed spectral nonlinear DR framework bridges global and local structure while enhancing interpretability. Spectral-mode analysis provides insights into embedding formation, and glyph-based visuals support exploration of spectral contributions.

title: STRNet: Visual Navigation with Spatio-Temporal Representation through Dynamic Graph Aggregation
summary: Visual navigation requires the robot to reach a specified goal such as an image, based on a sequence of first-person visual observations. While recent learning-based approaches have made significant progress, they often focus on improving policy heads or decision strategies while relying on simplistic feature encoders and temporal pooling to represent visual input. This leads to the loss of fine-grained spatial and temporal structure, ultimately limiting accurate action prediction and progress estimation. In this paper, we propose a unified spatio-temporal representation framework that enhances visual encoding for robotic navigation. Our approach extracts features from both image sequences and goal observations, and fuses them using the designed spatio-temporal fusion module. This module performs spatial graph reasoning within each frame and models temporal dynamics using a hybrid temporal shift module combined with multi-resolution difference-aware convolution. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach consistently improves navigation performance and offers a generalizable visual backbone for goal-conditioned control. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/hren20/STRNet}{https://github.com/hren20/STRNet}.

AI.tldr: Unified spatio-temporal representation STRNet improves visual encoding for goal-conditioned navigation by per-frame spatial graph reasoning and temporal modeling via hybrid temporal shift and multi-resolution difference-aware conv, integrating image sequences and goal observations; shows consistent navigation gains; code released.
AI.motivation: Current approaches rely on simplistic encoders and temporal pooling, losing fine-grained spatial/temporal structure; better representations can improve action prediction and progress estimation.
AI.method: Extract features from image sequences and goals; apply spatio-temporal fusion module; within-frame spatial graph reasoning; temporal dynamics via hybrid temporal shift module plus multi-resolution difference-aware convolution; end-to-end backbone for goal-conditioned control.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent navigation performance gains; generalizable visual backbone; code release.
AI.conclusion: STRNet provides improved visual encoding for goal-conditioned navigation and serves as a reusable backbone; potential to generalize to other vision-based robotics tasks.

title: ExploreVLA: Dense World Modeling and Exploration for End-to-End Autonomous Driving
comment: The code and demo will be publicly available at https://zihaosheng.github.io/ExploreVLA/
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving models based on Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architectures have shown promising results by learning driving policies through behavior cloning on expert demonstrations. However, imitation learning inherently limits the model to replicating observed behaviors without exploring diverse driving strategies, leaving it brittle in novel or out-of-distribution scenarios. Reinforcement learning (RL) offers a natural remedy by enabling policy exploration beyond the expert distribution. Yet VLA models, typically trained on offline datasets, lack directly observable state transitions, necessitating a learned world model to anticipate action consequences. In this work, we propose a unified understanding-and-generation framework that leverages world modeling to simultaneously enable meaningful exploration and provide dense supervision. Specifically, we augment trajectory prediction with future RGB and depth image generation as dense world modeling objectives, requiring the model to learn fine-grained visual and geometric representations that substantially enrich the planning backbone. Beyond serving as a supervisory signal, the world model further acts as a source of intrinsic reward for policy exploration: its image prediction uncertainty naturally measures a trajectory's novelty relative to the training distribution, where high uncertainty indicates out-of-distribution scenarios that, if safe, represent valuable learning opportunities. We incorporate this exploration signal into a safety-gated reward and optimize the policy via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Experiments on the NAVSIM and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, achieving a state-of-the-art PDMS score of 93.7 and an EPDMS of 88.8 on NAVSIM. The code and demo will be publicly available at https://zihaosheng.github.io/ExploreVLA/.

AI.tldr: A unified VLA driving framework that learns a world model through dense trajectory prediction (future RGB/depth) to enable exploration via intrinsic rewards, and optimizes with GRPO, achieving state-of-the-art NAVSIM PDMS/EPDMS and strong results on NAVSIM/nuScenes with public code.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning confines policies to observed expert behavior and fails in out-of-distribution scenarios; offline VLA models lack observable state transitions and require a world model for planning. RL can explore but benefits from joint learning of world dynamics and policy.
AI.method: Augment trajectory prediction with future RGB and depth image generation as dense world-modeling objectives; use the world-model's prediction uncertainty as an intrinsic reward to promote exploration; apply a safety-gated reward; optimize the policy with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art PDMS 93.7 and EPDMS 88.8 on NAVSIM; demonstrates effectiveness on NAVSIM and nuScenes benchmarks; code and demo to be released publicly.
AI.conclusion: The approach enriches planning with rich visual-geometry representations and provides a built-in exploration signal via world-model uncertainty, improving robustness and learning efficiency in VLA driving.

title: Fast NF4 Dequantization Kernels for Large Language Model Inference
comment: 7 pages, 4 figures, EMC2 Workshop at ASPLOS 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have grown beyond the memory capacity of single GPU devices, necessitating quantization techniques for practical deployment. While NF4 (4-bit NormalFloat) quantization enables 4$\times$ memory reduction, inference on current NVIDIA GPUs (e.g., Ampere A100) requires expensive dequantization back to FP16 format, creating a critical performance bottleneck. This paper presents a lightweight shared memory optimization that addresses this gap through principled memory hierarchy exploitation while maintaining full ecosystem compatibility. We compare our technique against the open-source BitsAndBytes implementation, achieving 2.0--2.2$\times$ kernel speedup across three models (Gemma 27B, Qwen3 32B, and Llama3.3 70B) and up to 1.54$\times$ end-to-end improvement by leveraging the 12--15$\times$ latency advantage of shared memory over global memory access. Our optimization reduces instruction counts through simplified indexing logic while using only 64 bytes of shared memory per thread block, demonstrating that lightweight optimizations can deliver substantial performance gains with minimal engineering effort. This work provides a plug-and-play solution for the HuggingFace ecosystem that democratizes access to advanced models on existing GPU infrastructure.

AI.tldr: NF4 quantization with a lightweight shared-memory kernel yields substantial kernel-level and end-to-end speedups for 4-bit LLM inference, while preserving ecosystem compatibility.
AI.motivation: 4-bit NF4 reduces model memory but requires dequantization to FP16 on current GPUs, creating a significant bottleneck. A memory-hierarchy-aware optimization can bypass or amortize dequantization bottlenecks and accelerate inference within existing ecosystems.
AI.method: Develop a lightweight shared-memory kernel that exploits memory hierarchy and uses minimal per-block shared memory (64 bytes) to operate on NF4 data with simplified indexing, compared against BitsAndBytes. It aims for plug-and-play compatibility with HuggingFace.
AI.result: Achieves 2.0–2.2x kernel speedup over BitsAndBytes across Gemma 27B, Qwen3 32B, and Llama3 70B, and up to 1.54x end-to-end speedup by leveraging the latency (12–15x) advantage of shared memory over global memory; reduces instruction counts via simplified indexing.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, memory-hierarchy-aware optimization can deliver large performance gains with minimal engineering effort and broad ecosystem adoption, enabling more accessible NF4-based LLM deployment on existing GPUs.

title: MOMO: Mars Orbital Model Foundation Model for Mars Orbital Applications
summary: We introduce MOMO, the first multi-sensor foundation model for Mars remote sensing. MOMO uses model merge to integrate representations learned independently from three key Martian sensors (HiRISE, CTX, and THEMIS), spanning resolutions from 0.25 m/pixel to 100 m/pixel. Central to our method is our novel Equal Validation Loss (EVL) strategy, which aligns checkpoints across sensors based on validation loss similarity before fusion via task arithmetic. This ensures models are merged at compatible convergence stages, leading to improved stability and generalization. We train MOMO on a large-scale, high-quality corpus of $\sim 12$ million samples curated from Mars orbital data and evaluate it on 9 downstream tasks from Mars-Bench. MOMO achieves better overall performance compared to ImageNet pre-trained, earth observation foundation model, sensor-specific pre-training, and fully-supervised baselines. Particularly on segmentation tasks, MOMO shows consistent and significant performance improvement. Our results demonstrate that model merging through an optimal checkpoint selection strategy provides an effective approach for building foundation models for multi-resolution data. The model weights, pretraining code, pretraining data, and evaluation code are available at: https://github.com/kerner-lab/MOMO.

AI.tldr: MOMO is a multi-sensor Mars foundation model created via a novel Equal Validation Loss guided model merging across HiRISE, CTX, and THEMIS representations, achieving superior multi-task performance including segmentation on Mars-Bench.
AI.motivation: Address multi-resolution, multi-sensor Mars data; standard foundation models trained on single sensor or Earth imagery lack cross-sensor fusion; aim to exploit complementary resolutions via merge at compatible convergence points.
AI.method: 3 independent sensor-specific representations learned; EVL aligns checkpoints based on validation loss similarity prior to fusion via task arithmetic; training on ~12M samples; evaluated on 9 downstream tasks from Mars-Bench.
AI.result: Outperforms ImageNet pre-trained, Earth observation foundation model, sensor-specific pretraining, and fully-supervised baselines; notably strong gains on segmentation tasks; demonstrates effectiveness of model merging with optimal checkpoint selection for multi-resolution data.
AI.conclusion: Model merging with an optimal checkpoint selection strategy is a viable approach to build foundation models for multi-resolution remote sensing; code and data are publicly released for reproducibility.

title: Communication-Efficient Distributed Learning with Differential Privacy
summary: We address nonconvex learning problems over undirected networks. In particular, we focus on the challenge of designing an algorithm that is both communication-efficient and that guarantees the privacy of the agents' data. The first goal is achieved through a local training approach, which reduces communication frequency. The second goal is achieved by perturbing gradients during local training, specifically through gradient clipping and additive noise. We prove that the resulting algorithm converges to a stationary point of the problem within a bounded distance. Additionally, we provide theoretical privacy guarantees within a differential privacy framework that ensure agents' training data cannot be inferred from the trained model shared over the network. We show the algorithm's superior performance on a classification task under the same privacy budget, compared with state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A communication-efficient, privacy-preserving distributed nonconvex learning algorithm over networks using local training and differentially private gradient perturbations, with provable convergence and empirical advantage under a fixed privacy budget.
AI.motivation: To develop distributed nonconvex learning methods that are both communication-efficient and data-privacy-preserving in networked environments where agents hold sensitive data.
AI.method: Employ local training to reduce communication rounds; perturb gradients via clipping and additive noise to achieve differential privacy; prove convergence to a stationary point within a bounded distance; provide formal DP guarantees; compare against state-of-the-art under the same privacy budget.
AI.result: The algorithm converges to a stationary point within a bounded distance from the optimum; differential privacy guarantees hold; empirically outperforms state-of-the-art methods on a classification task under the same privacy budget.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully integrates communication efficiency and privacy protection in distributed nonconvex learning, with solid convergence guarantees and demonstrated empirical gains.

title: THOM: Generating Physically Plausible Hand-Object Meshes From Text
summary: The generation of 3D hand-object interactions (HOIs) from text is crucial for dexterous robotic grasping and VR/AR content generation, requiring both high visual fidelity and physical plausibility. Nevertheless, the ill-posed problem of mesh extraction from text-generated Gaussians, and physics-based optimization on the erroneous meshes pose challenges. To address these issues, we introduce THOM, a training-free framework that generates photorealistic, physically plausible 3D HOI meshes without the need for a template object mesh. THOM employs a two-stage pipeline, initially generating the hand and object Gaussians, followed by physics-based HOI optimization. Our new mesh extraction method and vertex-to-Gaussian mapping explicitly assign Gaussian elements to mesh vertices, allowing topology-aware regularization. Furthermore, we improve the physical plausibility of interactions by VLM-guided translation refinement and contact-aware optimization. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that THOM consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods in terms of text alignment, visual realism, and interaction plausibility.

AI.tldr: THOM is a training-free framework for text-to-3D hand-object interactions that uses Gaussian representations, a two-stage pipeline, topology-aware mesh extraction with vertex-Gaussian mapping, and VLM-guided optimization to achieve photorealistic, physically plausible HOI meshes without a template object mesh.
AI.motivation: To solve the ill-posed mesh extraction when converting text-generated Gaussians into meshes and the ensuing physics-based optimization on flawed meshes in text-to-3D HOI generation.
AI.method: THOM uses a two-stage pipeline: (1) generate hand and object Gaussians from text; (2) perform physics-based HOI optimization. It introduces a mesh extraction method with explicit vertex-to-Gaussian mapping to assign Gaussians to mesh vertices for topology-aware regularization, along with VLM-guided translation refinement and contact-aware optimization to improve physical plausibility.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show that THOM consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods in text alignment, visual realism, and interaction plausibility.
AI.conclusion: THOM enables photorealistic, physically plausible 3D HOI meshes from text without requiring a template object mesh, representing a training-free advance that outperforms prior approaches in alignment, realism, and feasibility of hand-object interactions.

title: ROMAN: A Multiscale Routing Operator for Convolutional Time Series Models
summary: We introduce ROMAN (ROuting Multiscale representAtioN), a deterministic operator for time series that maps temporal scale and coarse temporal position into an explicit channel structure while reducing sequence length. ROMAN builds an anti-aliased multiscale pyramid, extracts fixed-length windows from each scale, and stacks them as pseudochannels, yielding a compact representation on which standard convolutional classifiers can operate.
