In this way, ROMAN provides a simple mechanism to control the inductive bias of downstream models: it can reduce temporal invariance, make temporal pooling implicitly coarse-position-aware, and expose multiscale interactions through channel mixing, while often improving computational efficiency by shortening the processed time axis. We formally analyze the ROMAN operator and then evaluate it in two complementary ways by measuring its impact as a preprocessing step for four representative convolutional classifiers: MiniRocket, MultiRocket, a standard CNN-based classifier, and a fully convolutional network (FCN) classifier. First, we design synthetic time series classification tasks that isolate coarse position awareness, long-range correlation, multiscale interaction, and full positional invariance, showing that ROMAN behaves consistently with its intended mechanism and is most useful when class information depends on temporal structure that standard pooled convolution tends to suppress. Second, we benchmark the same models with and without ROMAN on long-sequence subsets of the UCR and UEA archives, showing that ROMAN provides a practically useful alternative representation whose effect on accuracy is task-dependent, but whose effect on efficiency is often favorable. Code is available at https://github.com/gon-uri/ROMAN

AI.tldr: ROMAN is a deterministic time-series operator that builds an anti-aliased multiscale pyramid, extracts fixed-length windows per scale, and stacks them as pseudochannels to form a compact representation for standard conv classifiers. It reduces sequence length, injects explicit scale/position information, and can improve efficiency and control inductive bias. Evaluated as preprocessing for four classifiers on synthetic tasks and long-sequence UCR/UEA datasets; results show task-dependent accuracy effects and generally favorable efficiency; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: To address the mismatch between temporal structure in data and the invariances/pooling of standard CNNs, by injecting multiscale, position-aware structure while reducing computation.
AI.method: Construct an anti-aliased multiscale pyramid; extract fixed-length windows from each scale; stack as channels; analyze theoretically; evaluate as preprocessing input to MiniRocket, MultiRocket, CNN, FCN; use synthetic tasks to probe coarse position awareness, long-range correlation, multiscale interaction, and full positional invariance; benchmark on long-sequence subsets of UCR and UEA.
AI.result: ROMAN behaves as intended: introduces coarse-position awareness, reduces temporal invariance, exposes multiscale interactions via channel mixing, and often improves efficiency by shortening the time axis; accuracy effects are task-dependent; provides a practically useful alternative representation; code available.
AI.conclusion: ROMAN offers a simple yet effective way to modulate the inductive bias of time-series models through a deterministic, multiscale, concise representation. It is particularly beneficial when temporal structure is informative but can be suppressed by standard pooled convolutions; validated on synthetic and real long-sequence benchmarks; open-source implementation.

title: Visual Instruction-Finetuned Language Model for Versatile Brain MR Image Tasks
summary: LLMs have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in linguistic reasoning and are increasingly adept at vision-language tasks. The integration of image tokens into transformers has enabled direct visual input and output, advancing research from image-to-text descriptions to text-to-image generation. However, simple text-to-image generation holds limited clinical utility. In medical imaging, tasks such as image segmentation for localizing pathologies or image translation for reconstructing missing sequences have much greater clinical importance. Despite this, integrating these diverse, clinically relevant tasks within a single, versatile language model remains unexplored. Our method, LLaBIT (Large Language Model for Brain Image Translation), extends the visual reasoning of LLMs to these clinically meaningful tasks in the brain MRI domain. To mitigate the spatial information loss inherent in image tokenization, we incorporate a mechanism to reuse feature maps from the image encoder, minimizing data degradation. We also generate text data using LLMs with strict predefined instructions to augment limited image-text paired data in brain MRI. We comprehensively evaluated our method on five brain MRI datasets across four distinct tasks: report generation, visual question answering, image segmentation, and image translation. Our model not only demonstrated superior performance across all tasks but also outperformed specialized, task-specific models in direct comparisons, highlighting its efficacy and versatility

AI.tldr: LLaBIT extends LLMs to brain MRI tasks (report generation, VQA, segmentation, translation) by reusing image encoder feature maps and synthetic text data, achieving state-of-the-art performance across five datasets and surpassing task-specific models.
AI.motivation: Unlock clinically meaningful capabilities beyond text-to-image generation by unifying segmentation and translation tasks within a single vision-language model for brain MRI; address information loss from image tokenization and data scarcity via synthetic data.
AI.method: Integrates image encoder feature map reuse into an LLM-based framework; uses strict instruction-driven text data generation by LLMs to augment paired data; applies to multi-task brain MRI evaluation.
AI.result: Outperforms specialized models across four tasks on five datasets; demonstrates versatility and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Validated LLaBIT's ability to generalize across clinically relevant brain MRI tasks, highlighting the potential of unified medical imaging LLMs and data-efficient training through synthetic text data.

title: VoxelCodeBench: Benchmarking 3D World Modeling Through Code Generation
summary: Evaluating code generation models for 3D spatial reasoning requires executing generated code in realistic environments and assessing outputs beyond surface-level correctness. We introduce a platform VoxelCode, for analyzing code generation capabilities for 3D understanding and environment creation. Our platform integrates natural language task specification, API-driven code execution in Unreal Engine, and a unified evaluation pipeline supporting both automated metrics and human assessment. To demonstrate its utility, we construct VoxelCodeBench, a benchmark of voxel manipulation tasks spanning three reasoning dimensions: symbolic interpretation, geometric construction, and artistic composition. Evaluating leading code generation models, we find that producing executable code is far easier than producing spatially correct outputs, with geometric construction and multi-object composition proving particularly challenging. By open-sourcing our platform and benchmark, we provide the community with extensible infrastructure for developing new 3D code generation benchmarks and probing spatial reasoning in future models.

AI.tldr: VoxelCode: a platform to evaluate code-generation models for 3D spatial reasoning via executable Unreal Engine code, plus VoxelCodeBench benchmark spanning symbolic, geometric, and artistic tasks; finds executable code easier than correct spatial outputs; open-source infrastructure for future 3D code-generation research.
AI.motivation: Assess 3D understanding and environment creation capabilities of code-generation models using realistic execution environments and comprehensive evaluation beyond surface correctness.
AI.method: A platform that combines natural language task specifications, API-driven code execution in Unreal Engine, and a unified evaluation pipeline with automated metrics and human assessment; a benchmark (VoxelCodeBench) covering voxel manipulation across symbolic interpretation, geometric construction, and artistic composition.
AI.result: Leading code-generation models more easily produce executable code than outputs that are spatially correct; geometric construction and multi-object composition are particularly challenging.
AI.conclusion: Open-sourcing the platform and benchmark provides extensible infrastructure for developing new 3D code-generation benchmarks and probing spatial reasoning in future models.

title: Differentiable Stroke Planning with Dual Parameterization for Efficient and High-Fidelity Painting Creation
summary: In stroke-based rendering, search methods often get trapped in local minima due to discrete stroke placement, while differentiable optimizers lack structural awareness and produce unstructured layouts. To bridge this gap, we propose a dual representation that couples discrete polylines with continuous Bézier control points via a bidirectional mapping mechanism. This enables collaborative optimization: local gradients refine global stroke structures, while content-aware stroke proposals help escape poor local optima. Our representation further supports Gaussian-splatting-inspired initialization, enabling highly parallel stroke optimization across the image. Experiments show that our approach reduces the number of strokes by 30-50%, achieves more structurally coherent layouts, and improves reconstruction quality, while cutting optimization time by 30-40% compared to existing differentiable vectorization methods.

AI.tldr: A dual discrete-continuous representation for stroke-based vectorization, coupling polylines with Bézier control points via bidirectional mapping to enable collaborative optimization, Gaussian-splatting-inspired initialization, achieving fewer strokes, better structure, improved reconstruction, and faster optimization compared to prior differentiable methods.
AI.motivation: Stroke-based rendering optimization suffers from local minima due to discrete stroke placement and lacks structural awareness in differentiable optimizers. A hybrid representation aims to fuse discrete and continuous elements to enable cooperative optimization and scalable initialization.
AI.method: Introduce a dual representation that links discrete polylines with continuous Bézier control points through bidirectional mappings. Use collaborative optimization where local gradients refine global stroke layouts and content-aware stroke proposals escape poor optima. Employ Gaussian-splatting-inspired initialization to enable highly parallel stroke optimization across the image.
AI.result: Achieves 30–50% reduction in the number of strokes, more structurally coherent layouts, improved reconstruction quality, and 30–40% faster optimization than existing differentiable vectorization methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges discrete and continuous optimization in stroke-based rendering, enabling efficient, structured, and high-quality vectorization with scalable parallelism.

title: WGFINNs: Weak formulation-based GENERIC formalism informed neural networks'
summary: Data-driven discovery of governing equations from noisy observations remains a fundamental challenge in scientific machine learning. While GENERIC formalism informed neural networks (GFINNs) provide a principled framework that enforces the laws of thermodynamics by construction, their reliance on strong-form loss formulations makes them highly sensitive to measurement noise. To address this limitation, we propose weak formulation-based GENERIC formalism informed neural networks (WGFINNs), which integrate the weak formulation of dynamical systems with the structure-preserving architecture of GFINNs. WGFINNs significantly enhance robustness to noisy data while retaining exact satisfaction of GENERIC degeneracy and symmetry conditions. We further incorporate a state-wise weighted loss and a residual-based attention mechanism to mitigate scale imbalance across state variables. Theoretical analysis contrasts quantitative differences between the strong-form and the weak-form estimators. Mainly, the strong-form estimator diverges as the time step decreases in the presence of noise, while the weak-form estimator can be accurate even with noisy data if test functions satisfy certain conditions. Numerical experiments demonstrate that WGFINNs consistently outperform GFINNs at varying noise levels, achieving more accurate predictions and reliable recovery of physical quantities.

AI.tldr: WGFINNs introduce a weak-form GENERIC-inspired NN to robustly learn governing equations from noisy data, outperforming GFINNs by preserving thermodynamic structure while mitigating noise through weak formulation, state-wise weighting, and residual attention.
AI.motivation: To enable robust data-driven discovery of GENERIC-structured dynamics under measurement noise. GFINNs enforce thermodynamic laws but suffer from high sensitivity to noise due to strong-form losses; WGFINNs aim to retain exact GENERIC degeneracy/symmetry while improving noise robustness.
AI.method: Combine weak formulation of dynamical systems with the structure-preserving GFINN architecture to enforce GENERIC constraints. Add a state-wise weighted loss and a residual-based attention mechanism to address scale imbalances across state variables. Provide theoretical analysis contrasting strong-form vs weak-form estimators and derive conditions under which the weak form remains accurate with noisy data.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show WGFINNs consistently outperform GFINNs across noise levels, offering more accurate state predictions and more reliable recovery of physical quantities while maintaining GENERIC structure.
AI.conclusion: Weak-form GENERIC-informed neural networks yield robust data-driven learning under noise without sacrificing thermodynamic structure. The approach suggests practical guidelines for test-function choices and loss design, and can potentially be extended to other structure-preserving learned dynamics.

title: DeCo-DETR: Decoupled Cognition DETR for efficient Open-Vocabulary Object Detection
summary: Open-vocabulary Object Detection (OVOD) enables models to recognize objects beyond predefined categories, but existing approaches remain limited in practical deployment. On the one hand, multimodal designs often incur substantial computational overhead due to their reliance on text encoders at inference time. On the other hand, tightly coupled training objectives introduce a trade-off between closed-set detection accuracy and open-world generalization. Thus, we propose Decoupled Cognition DETR (DeCo-DETR), a vision-centric framework that addresses these challenges through a unified decoupling paradigm. Instead of depending on online text encoding, DeCo-DETR constructs a hierarchical semantic prototype space from region-level descriptions generated by pre-trained LVLMs and aligned via CLIP, enabling efficient and reusable semantic representation. Building upon this representation, the framework further disentangles semantic reasoning from localization through a decoupled training strategy, which separates alignment and detection into parallel optimization streams. Extensive experiments on standard OVOD benchmarks demonstrate that DeCo-DETR achieves competitive zero-shot detection performance while significantly improving inference efficiency. These results highlight the effectiveness of decoupling semantic cognition from detection, offering a practical direction for scalable OVOD systems.

AI.tldr: DeCo-DETR presents a vision-centric OVOD framework that decouples semantic cognition from detection. It replaces online text encoding with a hierarchical semantic prototype space built from LVLM-derived region descriptions aligned by CLIP, and trains alignment and detection in parallel, achieving competitive zero-shot performance with better inference efficiency.
AI.motivation: OVOD suffers from heavy inference cost due to text encoders and a trade-off between closed-set accuracy and open-world generalization due to tightly coupled objectives. A decoupled, vision-centric approach could improve efficiency and scalability while preserving generalization.
AI.method: Construct a hierarchical semantic prototype space from region-level descriptions generated by pre-trained LVLMs and aligned via CLIP. Use a decoupled training strategy that separately optimizes semantic alignment and object detection in parallel inference streams, thereby decoupling reasoning from localization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard OVOD benchmarks show competitive zero-shot detection performance and substantially improved inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semantic cognition from detection is effective for scalable OVOD systems, offering a practical, efficient, and reusable semantic representation framework for open-world object detection.

title: Steerable but Not Decodable: Function Vectors Operate Beyond the Logit Lens
summary: Function vectors (FVs) -- mean-difference directions extracted from in-context learning demonstrations -- can steer large language model behavior when added to the residual stream. We hypothesized that FV steering failures reflect an absence of task-relevant information: the logit lens would fail alongside steering. We were wrong. In the most comprehensive cross-template FV transfer study to date - 4,032 pairs across 12 tasks, 6 models from 3 families (Llama-3.1-8B, Gemma-2-9B, Mistral-7B-v0.3; base and instruction-tuned), 8 templates per task - we find the opposite dissociation: FV steering succeeds even when the logit lens cannot decode the correct answer at any layer. This steerability-without-decodability pattern is universal: steering exceeds logit lens accuracy for every task on every model, with gaps as large as -0.91. Only 3 of 72 task-model instances show the predicted decodable-without-steerable pattern, all in Mistral. FV vocabulary projection reveals that FVs achieving over 0.90 steering accuracy still project to incoherent token distributions, indicating FVs encode computational instructions rather than answer directions. FVs intervene optimally at early layers (L2-L8); the logit lens detects correct answers only at late layers (L28-L32). The previously reported negative cosine-transfer correlation (r=-0.572) dissolves at scale: pooled r ranges from -0.199 to +0.126, and cosine adds less than 0.011 in R-squared beyond task identity. Post-steering analysis reveals a model-family divergence: Mistral FVs rewrite intermediate representations; Llama/Gemma FVs produce near-zero changes despite successful steering. Activation patching confirms causal localization: easy tasks achieve perfect recovery at targeted layers; hard tasks show zero recovery everywhere.

AI.tldr: Cross-template FV transfer across 12 tasks and 6 LLM families shows FV steering succeeds where logit-lens decodeability fails; effects are universal, involve early-layer interventions, with model-family differences; cosine-transfer correlation collapses at scale.
AI.motivation: Test the widely held view that FV steering requires task-relevant information accessible via logit-level decoding; examine the relationship between steerability and decodability across architectures and templates.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical study: 4,032 FV-task pairs, 12 tasks, 6 models (Llama-3.1-8B, Gemma-2-9B, Mistral-7B-v0.3), base and instruction-tuned; 8 templates per task. Analyze steering accuracy vs logit-lens accuracy across layers; FV vocabulary projection; layer-wise activation patching; examine model-family differences.
AI.result: FV steering outperforms logit-lens decodeability across all task-models (except 3 instances in Mistral). FVs with >0.9 steering can map to incoherent distributions; FVs act like instructions to computations rather than answer directions. Steering intervenes in early layers (L2-L8); logit lens decodes only late (L28-L32). Cosine-transfer correlations collapse; pooled correlations near zero; model-family differences: Mistral FVs rewrite representations; Llama/Gemma FVs cause minimal changes yet steer effectively; activation patching shows causal localization: easy tasks recover at targeted layers; hard tasks show no recovery.
AI.conclusion: FVs reveal that steerability does not require reliable decodability of the final answer; FVs encode task-structuring information that affects intermediate representations; demonstrate distinct, architecture-dependent mechanisms; implications for understanding in-context learning and alignment diagnostics.

title: InverseDraping: Recovering Sewing Patterns from 3D Garment Surfaces via BoxMesh Bridging
summary: Recovering sewing patterns from draped 3D garments is a challenging problem in human digitization research. In contrast to the well-studied forward process of draping designed sewing patterns using mature physical simulation engines, the inverse process of recovering parametric 2D patterns from deformed garment geometry remains fundamentally ill-posed for existing methods. We propose a two-stage framework that centers on a structured intermediate representation, BoxMesh, which serves as the key to bridging the gap between 3D garment geometry and parametric sewing patterns. BoxMesh encodes both garment-level geometry and panel-level structure in 3D, while explicitly disentangling intrinsic panel geometry and stitching topology from draping-induced deformations. This representation imposes a physically grounded structure on the problem, significantly reducing ambiguity. In Stage I, a geometry-driven autoregressive model infers BoxMesh from the input 3D garment. In Stage II, a semantics-aware autoregressive model parses BoxMesh into parametric sewing patterns. We adopt autoregressive modeling to naturally handle the variable-length and structured nature of panel configurations and stitching relationships. This decomposition separates geometric inversion from structured pattern inference, leading to more accurate and robust recovery. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the GarmentCodeData benchmark and generalizes effectively to real-world scans and single-view images.

AI.tldr: A two-stage BoxMesh framework to recover parametric sewing patterns from 3D draped garments. Stage I uses a geometry-driven autoregressive model to infer BoxMesh from 3D geometry; Stage II uses a semantics-aware autoregressive model to convert BoxMesh into parametric sewing patterns. The approach yields state-of-the-art results on GarmentCodeData and generalizes to real-world scans and single-view images.
AI.motivation: Inverse problem of recovering 2D sewing patterns from draped 3D garments is ill-posed and underconstrained. Existing methods lack a structured intermediate representation bridging 3D geometry and 2D parametric patterns, leading to ambiguity and poor generalization. A physically grounded, structured intermediate representation can reduce ambiguity and improve robustness.
AI.method: Introduce BoxMesh as an intermediate representation that encodes garment-level geometry and panel-level structure, disentangling intrinsic panel geometry and stitching topology from draping deformations. Stage I: geometry-driven autoregressive model infers BoxMesh from input 3D garment. Stage II: semantics-aware autoregressive model parses BoxMesh into parametric sewing patterns. Autoregressive modeling handles variable-length and structured panel configurations and stitching relationships, effectively separating geometric inversion from pattern inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on the GarmentCodeData benchmark and strong generalization to real-world scans and single-view images.
AI.conclusion: BoxMesh provides a physically grounded, structured bridge between 3D garment geometry and 2D parametric sewing patterns. The two-stage autoregressive framework improves accuracy and robustness in recovering sewing patterns from draped garments, addressing the ill-posed nature of the inverse problem.

title: Complex-Valued GNNs for Distributed Basis-Invariant Control of Planar Systems
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, submitted to CDC 2026 main track
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are a well-regarded tool for learned control of networked dynamical systems due to their ability to be deployed in a distributed manner. However, current distributed GNN architectures assume that all nodes in the network collect geometric observations in compatible bases, which limits the usefulness of such controllers in GPS-denied and compass-denied environments. This paper presents a GNN parametrization that is globally invariant to choice of local basis. 2D geometric features and transformations between bases are expressed in the complex domain. Inside each GNN layer, complex-valued linear layers with phase-equivariant activation functions are used. When viewed from a fixed global frame, all policies learned by this architecture are strictly invariant to choice of local frames. This architecture is shown to increase the data efficiency, tracking performance, and generalization of learned control when compared to a real-valued baseline on an imitation learning flocking task.

AI.tldr: A basis-invariant GNN for distributed control. Complex-valued GNN with phase-equivariant activations yields policies invariant to local frame choices, improving data efficiency, tracking, and generalization in flocking tasks versus a real-valued baseline.
AI.motivation: Current distributed GNNs assume compatible local bases, which fails in GPS/compass-denied settings. A globally invariant, basis-agnostic policy enables robust, decentralized control.
AI.method: Express 2D geometry in the complex domain; use complex-valued linear layers with phase-equivariant activations in each GNN layer to achieve frame invariance when viewed in a fixed global frame.
AI.result: Compared to a real-valued baseline on an imitation-learning flocking task, the proposed architecture improves data efficiency, tracking performance, and generalization.
AI.conclusion: A complex, phase-equivariant GNN provides global frame invariance to local basis choices, enabling more robust distributed control in GPS/compass-denied environments and better sample efficiency and generalization.

title: Generalized Small Object Detection:A Point-Prompted Paradigm and Benchmark
summary: Small object detection (SOD) remains challenging due to extremely limited pixels and ambiguous object boundaries. These characteristics lead to challenging annotation, limited availability of large-scale high-quality datasets, and inherently weak semantic representations for small objects. In this work, we first address the data limitation by introducing TinySet-9M, the first large-scale, multi-domain dataset for small object detection. Beyond filling the gap in large-scale datasets, we establish a benchmark to evaluate the effectiveness of existing label-efficient detection methods for small objects. Our evaluation reveals that weak visual cues further exacerbate the performance degradation of label-efficient methods in small object detection, highlighting a critical challenge in label-efficient SOD. Secondly, to tackle the limitation of insufficient semantic representation, we move beyond training-time feature enhancement and propose a new paradigm termed Point-Prompt Small Object Detection (P2SOD). This paradigm introduces sparse point prompts at inference time as an efficient information bridge for category-level localization, enabling semantic augmentation. Building upon the P2SOD paradigm and the large-scale TinySet-9M dataset, we further develop DEAL (DEtect Any smalL object), a scalable and transferable point-prompted detection framework that learns robust, prompt-conditioned representations from large-scale data. With only a single click at inference time, DEAL achieves a 31.4% relative improvement over fully supervised baselines under strict localization metrics (e.g., AP75) on TinySet-9M, while generalizing effectively to unseen categories and unseen datasets. Our project is available at https://zhuhaoraneis.github.io/TinySet-9M/.

AI.tldr: TinySet-9M enables large-scale, multi-domain small object detection; introduces Point-Prompt Small Object Detection (P2SOD) and the DEAL framework; reports 31.4% relative AP75 gain with a single click and good generalization to unseen categories/datasets.
AI.motivation: Small object detection suffers from data scarcity and weak semantic representations. Label-efficient methods degrade due to limited supervision and weak cues; a large-scale dataset and inference-time semantic augmentation are needed.
AI.method: (1) TinySet-9M: a large-scale, multi-domain dataset and benchmark for SOD to assess label-efficient methods. (2) P2SOD: inference-time sparse point prompts to provide semantic augmentation for category-level localization. (3) DEAL: a scalable, transferable point-prompted detection framework that learns prompt-conditioned representations from large-scale data, requiring only a single click at inference.
AI.result: DEAL achieves a 31.4% relative improvement over fully supervised baselines on TinySet-9M under strict AP75 localization, and generalizes to unseen categories and datasets.
AI.conclusion: The approach addresses data scarcity and semantic representation gaps in SOD by coupling a large-scale dataset with a light-touch, inference-time prompt mechanism, yielding strong improvements and transferable representations.

title: Analytic Drift Resister for Non-Exemplar Continual Graph Learning
summary: Non-Exemplar Continual Graph Learning (NECGL) seeks to eliminate the privacy risks intrinsic to rehearsal-based paradigms by retaining solely class-level prototype representations rather than raw graph examples for mitigating catastrophic forgetting. However, this design choice inevitably precipitates feature drift. As a nascent alternative, Analytic Continual Learning (ACL) capitalizes on the intrinsic generalization properties of frozen pre-trained models to bolster continual learning performance. Nonetheless, a key drawback resides in the pronounced attenuation of model plasticity. To surmount these challenges, we propose Analytic Drift Resister (ADR), a novel and theoretically grounded NECGL framework. ADR exploits iterative backpropagation to break free from the frozen pre-trained constraint, adapting to evolving task graph distributions and fortifying model plasticity. Since parameter updates trigger feature drift, we further propose Hierarchical Analytic Merging (HAM), performing layer-wise merging of linear transformations in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) via ridge regression, thereby ensuring absolute resistance to feature drift. On this basis, Analytic Classifier Reconstruction (ACR) enables theoretically zero-forgetting class-incremental learning. Empirical evaluation on four node classification benchmarks demonstrates that ADR maintains strong competitiveness against existing state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: ADR is a theoretically grounded NECGL framework integrating iterative backpropagation to escape frozen pre-trained representations, hierarchical analytic merging (HAM) to resist feature drift via ridge-regressed layer-wise merging of GNN linear transforms, and analytic classifier reconstruction (ACR) to achieve zero-forgetting in class-incremental learning; empirically competitive on four node classification benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address privacy concerns in rehearsal-free continual graph learning by avoiding raw graph exemplars; combat feature drift from prototype-only representations and the limited plasticity of frozen pre-trained models; provide a principled framework that maintains plasticity while resisting drift.
AI.method: Propose ADR to enable iterative backpropagation despite starting from a frozen pre-trained model; introduce HAM for drift-resilient, layer-wise ridge-regression-based merging of linear GNN components; implement ACR to reconstruct classifiers with zero-forgetting properties; evaluate on standard node classification benchmarks to compare against state-of-the-art NECGL/ACL baselines.
AI.result: ADR demonstrates strong competitiveness against existing state-of-the-art methods across four node classification benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The ADR framework, complemented by HAM and ACR, offers a theoretically grounded and practical solution for non-exemplar continual graph learning, balancing privacy-preserving representations with sufficient plasticity to adapt to evolving task distributions.

title: A Unified Perspective on Adversarial Membership Manipulation in Vision Models
summary: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) aim to determine whether a specific data point was part of a model's training set, serving as effective tools for evaluating privacy leakage of vision models. However, existing MIAs implicitly assume honest query inputs, and their adversarial robustness remains unexplored. We show that MIAs for vision models expose a previously overlooked adversarial surface: adversarial membership manipulation, where imperceptible perturbations can reliably push non-member images into the "member" region of state-of-the-art MIAs. In this paper, we provide the first unified perspective on this phenomenon by analyzing its mechanism and implications. We begin by demonstrating that adversarial membership fabrication is consistently effective across diverse architectures and datasets. We then reveal a distinctive geometric signature - a characteristic gradient-norm collapse trajectory - that reliably separates fabricated from true members despite their nearly identical semantic representations. Building on this insight, we introduce a principled detection strategy grounded in gradient-geometry signals and develop a robust inference framework that substantially mitigates adversarial manipulation. Extensive experiments show that fabrication is broadly effective, while our detection and robust inference strategies significantly enhance resilience. This work establishes the first comprehensive framework for adversarial membership manipulation in vision models.

AI.tldr: Adversarial membership manipulation exposes a new vulnerability in vision-model MIAs; a gradient-geometry signature enables detection and a robust inference framework mitigates the attack across architectures and datasets.
AI.motivation: MIAs show privacy leakage for vision models but assume honest inputs; adversarial perturbations can fabricate membership. The work seeks to unify the mechanism, validate across models/datasets, and provide defenses.
AI.method: Analyze the mechanism of adversarial membership fabrication; identify a gradient-norm collapse trajectory as a diagnostic signature; propose gradient-geometry-based detection and a robust inference framework; validate with extensive experiments across architectures and datasets.
AI.result: Fabrication of membership is broadly effective; proposed detection strategy reduces vulnerability and the robust inference framework improves resilience; gradient-geometry signals persist across settings.
AI.conclusion: First comprehensive framework for adversarial membership manipulation in vision models, highlighting the need for defenses in MIAs and providing practical detection and robustness tools.

title: AXELRAM: Quantize Once, Never Dequantize
summary:
We propose AXELRAM, a smart SRAM macro architecture that computes attention scores directly from quantized KV cache indices without dequantization. The key enabler is a design-time fixed codebook: orthogonal-transform-based quantization concentrates each coordinate's distribution to N(0,1/d), so the optimal quantizer depends only on dimension d and bit-width b, not on input data. The asymmetric path design -- transform on write, table-lookup on read with no inverse transform -- reduces per-query multiplications by 102.4x (a mathematical identity).
  Through multi-seed evaluation (10 seeds x 3 models), we discover that sign pattern sensitivity causes catastrophic PPL spikes (Delta > 50) on certain models (Qwen2.5-3B), while others (LLaMA-3.1-8B) are fully stable. This phenomenon extends SpinQuant's observation of rotation variance in weight quantization to the KV cache domain, where the effect is qualitatively more severe. We trace the root cause to layer-wise norm heterogeneity and propose a gradient-free sign pattern selection (200 candidates, 8 calibration samples, one-time) that eliminates catastrophic spikes with zero additional hardware cost. All source code is available at https://github.com/Axelidea/AXELRAM.

AI.tldr: AXELRAM enables attention computation directly from quantized KV cache indices using a fixed, design-time codebook, cutting per-query multiplications by ~102x via an asymmetric write/read path and no dequantization. It reveals model-dependent sign-pattern stability: some models suffer catastrophic PPL spikes, others are stable. A gradient-free sign-pattern selection (200 candidates, 8 calibration samples) fixes spikes with zero hardware cost. Open-source at the provided repo.
AI.motivation: Reduce compute and hardware cost for transformer attention by avoiding dequantization of KV caches; enable fast, SRAM-based attention through a fixed codebook and efficient quantization that does not depend on input data.
AI.method: Orthogonal-transform-based quantization with a design-time fixed codebook; transform on write, lookup on read with no inverse transform; asymmetric path to avoid multiplications; multi-seed evaluation (10 seeds x 3 models) to study stability; analysis of sign-pattern sensitivity; gradient-free sign-pattern selection (200 candidates, 8 calibration samples, one-time) to eliminate spikes.
AI.result: Substantial computational reduction: ~102.4x fewer per-query multiplications. Model-dependent sign-pattern sensitivity causes catastrophic PPL spikes in some models (e.g., Qwen2.5-3B) but not in others (LLaMA-3.1-8B). Root cause tied to layer-wise norm heterogeneity. Gradient-free sign-pattern selection eliminates spikes with zero additional hardware cost. All source code available at the linked GitHub repo.
AI.conclusion: AXELRAM presents a hardware-friendly method for quantized KV-cache attention with major speedups, but stability is model-dependent due to norm heterogeneity. The proposed sign-pattern search provides a robust fix with negligible cost, and the work is complemented by open-source code for reproducibility.

title: EnsemHalDet: Robust VLM Hallucination Detection via Ensemble of Internal State Detectors
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at multimodal tasks, but they remain vulnerable to hallucinations that are factually incorrect or ungrounded in the input image. Recent work suggests that hallucination detection using internal representations is more efficient and accurate than approaches that rely solely on model outputs. However, existing internal-representation-based methods typically rely on a single representation or detector, limiting their ability to capture diverse hallucination signals. In this paper, we propose EnsemHalDet, an ensemble-based hallucination detection framework that leverages multiple internal representations of VLMs, including attention outputs and hidden states. EnsemHalDet trains independent detectors for each representation and combines them through ensemble learning. Experimental results across multiple VQA datasets and VLMs show that EnsemHalDet consistently outperforms prior methods and single-detector models in terms of AUC. These results demonstrate that ensembling diverse internal signals significantly improves robustness in multimodal hallucination detection.

AI.tldr: EnsemHalDet uses an ensemble of detectors over multiple internal representations to robustly detect multimodal hallucinations, achieving higher AUC than single-detector methods.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in vision-language models are problematic and internal representations offer signals for detection, but relying on a single representation limits effectiveness; ensemble signals could capture diverse cues.
AI.method: Train independent detectors for each internal representation (e.g., attention outputs, hidden states) and combine their predictions via ensemble learning to detect hallucinations.
AI.result: Across multiple VQA datasets and VLMs, EnsemHalDet yields higher AUC than prior methods and single-detector baselines, demonstrating robustness gains from diverse internal signals.
AI.conclusion: Ensembling diverse internal signals significantly improves robustness of multimodal hallucination detection.

title: Conditional Sampling via Wasserstein Autoencoders and Triangular Transport
summary: We present Conditional Wasserstein Autoencoders (CWAEs), a framework for conditional simulation that exploits low-dimensional structure in both the conditioned and the conditioning variables. The key idea is to modify a Wasserstein autoencoder to use a (block-) triangular decoder and impose an appropriate independence assumption on the latent variables. We show that the resulting model gives an autoencoder that can exploit low-dimensional structure while simultaneously the decoder can be used for conditional simulation. We explore various theoretical properties of CWAEs, including their connections to conditional optimal transport (OT) problems. We also present alternative formulations that lead to three architectural variants forming the foundation of our algorithms. We present a series of numerical experiments that demonstrate that our different CWAE variants achieve substantial reductions in approximation error relative to the low-rank ensemble Kalman filter (LREnKF), particularly in problems where the support of the conditional measures is truly low-dimensional.

AI.tldr: Conditional Wasserstein Autoencoders (CWAEs) introduce a block-triangular decoder with latent independence to perform conditional simulation by exploiting low-dimensional structure; they connect to conditional optimal transport and yield three architectural variants, showing improved approximation over LREnKF when conditional supports are low-dimensional.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient conditional simulation by leveraging low-dimensional structure in both conditioned and conditioning variables, and to unify conditional simulation with OT-based theory.
AI.method: Modify a Wasserstein autoencoder to use a (block-) triangular decoder and impose an independence assumption on the latent variables; derive connections to conditional OT; present three architectural variants; provide numerical experiments.
AI.result: CWAEs achieve substantial reductions in approximation error compared to the low-rank ensemble Kalman filter (LREnKF) in problems where the conditional measures have truly low-dimensional support.
AI.conclusion: CWAEs offer a principled, OT-connected framework for conditional simulation with practical variants that improve approximation quality over LREnKF in low-dimensional settings.

title: CANDLE: Illumination-Invariant Semantic Priors for Color Ambient Lighting Normalization
comment: CVPRW 2026 Camera Ready; NTIRE 2026 Ambient Lighting Normalization (2nd & 3rd in Color & White Light Track)
summary: Color ambient lighting normalization under multi-colored illumination is challenging due to severe chromatic shifts, highlight saturation, and material-dependent reflectance. Existing geometric and low-level priors are insufficient for recovering object-intrinsic color when illumination-induced chromatic bias dominates. We observe that DINOv3's self-supervised features remain highly consistent between colored-light inputs and ambient-lit ground truth, motivating their use as illumination-robust semantic priors. We propose CANDLE (Color Ambient Normalization with DINO Layer Enhancement), which introduces DINO Omni-layer Guidance (D.O.G.) to adaptively inject multi-layer DINOv3 features into successive encoder stages, and a color-frequency refinement design (BFACG + SFFB) to suppress decoder-side chromatic collapse and detail contamination. Experiments on CL3AN show a +1.22 dB PSNR gain over the strongest prior method. CANDLE achieves 3rd place on the NTIRE 2026 ALN Color Lighting Challenge and 2nd place in fidelity on the White Lighting track with the lowest FID, confirming strong generalization across both chromatic and luminance-dominant illumination conditions. Code is available at https://github.com/ron941/CANDLE.

AI.tldr: CANDLE combines DINOv3-based illumination-robust priors with a multi-layer guidance scheme and color-frequency refinement to normalize color under multi-colored illumination. It improves PSNR and achieves top placements in NTIRE 2026 ALN challenges, with code released.
AI.motivation: Color ambient lighting normalization is hampered by chromatic shifts, highlight saturation, and material-dependent reflectance. Existing priors fail when illumination biases dominate. Observing DINOv3 features remain consistent across colored-light inputs and ambient ground-truth motivates using them as illumination-robust semantic priors.
AI.method: Introduce DINO Omni-layer Guidance (D.O.G.) to inject multi-layer DINOv3 features into encoder stages. Implement color-frequency refinement BFACG + SFFB to suppress decoder-side chromatic collapse and detail contamination. The overall architecture is named CANDLE (Color Ambient Normalization with DINO Layer Enhancement).
AI.result: On CL3AN, achieves +1.22 dB PSNR over the strongest prior. In NTIRE 2026 ALN Color Lighting Challenge: 3rd place; White Lighting track Fidelity: 2nd place with the lowest FID, indicating strong generalization across chromatic and luminance-dominant illumination. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The approach validates that DINO-based illumination-robust features can guide color normalization under complex lighting, with effective multi-layer feature integration and frequency-aware refinement leading to strong performance and generalization.

title: Communication-free Sampling and 4D Hybrid Parallelism for Scalable Mini-batch GNN Training
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are widely used for learning on graph datasets derived from various real-world scenarios. Learning from extremely large graphs requires distributed training, and mini-batching with sampling is a popular approach for parallelizing GNN training. Existing distributed mini-batch approaches have significant performance bottlenecks due to expensive sampling methods and limited scaling when using data parallelism. In this work, we present ScaleGNN, a 4D parallel framework for scalable mini-batch GNN training that combines communication-free distributed sampling, 3D parallel matrix multiplication (PMM), and data parallelism. ScaleGNN introduces a uniform vertex sampling algorithm, enabling each process (GPU device) to construct its local mini-batch, i.e., subgraph partitions without any inter-process communication. 3D PMM enables scaling mini-batch training to much larger GPU counts than vanilla data parallelism with significantly lower communication overheads. We also present additional optimizations to overlap sampling with training, reduce communication overhead by sending data in lower precision, kernel fusion, and communication-computation overlap. We evaluate ScaleGNN on five graph datasets and demonstrate strong scaling up to 2048 GPUs on Perlmutter, 2048 GCDs on Frontier, and 1024 GPUs on Tuolumne. On Perlmutter, ScaleGNN achieves 3.5x end-to-end training speedup over the SOTA baseline on ogbn-products.

AI.tldr: ScaleGNN proposes a 4D parallel framework to scale mini-batch GNN training by combining communication-free sampling, 3D PMM, and data parallelism, achieving strong scaling on large GPUs and substantial speedups over baselines.
AI.motivation: Learning on extremely large graphs requires distributed training; existing mini-batch sampling bottlenecks and limited scalability with data parallelism hinder performance. There is a need for faster, scalable GNN training frameworks.
AI.method: Introduce ScaleGNN, a 4D parallel framework: uniform vertex sampling to create local mini-batches without inter-process communication; 3D parallel matrix multiplication (PMM) to scale computation across GPUs; data parallelism; additional optimizations including sampling-training overlap, low-precision communication, kernel fusion, and overlap of communication and computation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five graph datasets shows strong scaling up to 2048 GPUs on Perlmutter, 2048 GCDs on Frontier, and 1024 GPUs on Tuolumne; on Perlmutter, achieves 3.5x end-to-end training speedup over SOTA baseline on ogbn-products.
AI.conclusion: ScaleGNN effectively addresses sampling bottlenecks and communication overheads in distributed GNN training, enabling near-linear scaling to thousands of GPUs and substantial speedups over the state-of-the-art.

title: LumaFlux: Lifting 8-Bit Worlds to HDR Reality with Physically-Guided Diffusion Transformers
summary: The rapid adoption of HDR-capable devices has created a pressing need to convert the 8-bit Standard Dynamic Range (SDR) content into perceptually and physically accurate 10-bit High Dynamic Range (HDR). Existing inverse tone-mapping (ITM) methods often rely on fixed tone-mapping operators that struggle to generalize to real-world degradations, stylistic variations, and camera pipelines, frequently producing clipped highlights, desaturated colors, or unstable tone reproduction. We introduce LumaFlux, a first physically and perceptually guided diffusion transformer (DiT) for SDR-to-HDR reconstruction by adapting a large pretrained DiT. Our LumaFlux introduces (1) a Physically-Guided Adaptation (PGA) module that injects luminance, spatial descriptors, and frequency cues into attention through low-rank residuals; (2) a Perceptual Cross-Modulation (PCM) layer that stabilizes chroma and texture via FiLM conditioning from vision encoder features; and (3) an HDR Residual Coupler that fuses physical and perceptual signals under a timestep- and layer-adaptive modulation schedule. Finally, a lightweight Rational-Quadratic Spline decoder reconstructs smooth, interpretable tone fields for highlight and exposure expansion, enhancing the output of the VAE decoder to generate HDR. To enable robust HDR learning, we curate the first large-scale SDR-HDR training corpus. For fair and reproducible comparison, we further establish a new evaluation benchmark, comprising HDR references and corresponding expert-graded SDR versions. Across benchmarks, LumaFlux outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving superior luminance reconstruction and perceptual color fidelity with minimal additional parameters.

AI.tldr: Introduces LumaFlux, a diffusion-transformer-based SDR-to-HDR method that is physically and perceptually guided.
AI.motivation: HDR-capable devices require reliable SDR-to-HDR conversion; existing ITMs struggle with real-world degradations, leading to clipped highlights and desaturated colors.
AI.method: Adapt a pretrained Diffusion-in-Transformer (DiT) with: (1) Physically-Guided Adaptation (PGA) injecting luminance, spatial descriptors, and frequency cues via low-rank residuals into attention; (2) Perceptual Cross-Modulation (PCM) using FiLM conditioning from a vision encoder to stabilize chroma/texture; (3) HDR Residual Coupler fusing physical and perceptual signals with timestep- and layer-adaptive modulation; additionally, a Rational-Quadratic Spline decoder reconstructs smooth tone fields; training includes a large SDR-HDR corpus and a new evaluation benchmark with expert-graded SDR references.
AI.result: LumaFlux outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks, achieving superior luminance reconstruction and perceptual color fidelity with minimal extra parameters.
AI.conclusion: A physically- and perceptually-guided diffusion approach for SDR-to-HDR yields robust, high-fidelity HDR reconstruction; introduces a large training corpus and benchmark to enable fair evaluation.

title: Generalization Limits of Reinforcement Learning Alignment
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables, accepted at JSAI 2026
summary: The safety of large language models (LLMs) relies on alignment techniques such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). However, recent theoretical analyses suggest that reinforcement learning-based training does not acquire new capabilities but merely redistributes the utilization probabilities of existing ones. In this study, we propose ``compound jailbreaks'' targeting OpenAI gpt-oss-20b, which exploit the generalization failures of alignment. This approach combines multiple attack techniques -- each individually defended against -- to saturate the instruction hierarchy maintenance process. Our evaluation shows that the attack success rate (ASR) increased from 14.3\% with individual methods to 71.4\% with the combined approach. These results provide empirical evidence for the hypothesis that safety training does not generalize as broadly as model capabilities, highlighting the need for multifaceted safety evaluations using compound attack scenarios.

AI.tldr: Compound jailbreaks combining multiple, individually defended attacks significantly raise the attack success rate on gpt-oss-20b (14.3% for individual methods vs. 71.4% for the combined approach), suggesting safety training via RLHF may not generalize as broadly as model capabilities.
AI.motivation: Assess the limits of alignment-based safety (e.g., RLHF) and the generalization of safety training by evaluating how robust safety defenses are when exposed to multifaceted attack strategies.
AI.method: Introduce 'compound jailbreaks' that combine several attack techniques, each separately defended against, to saturate the model's instruction hierarchy maintenance. Evaluate on OpenAI gpt-oss-20b and measure attack success rate (ASR) for both individual methods and the combined approach.
AI.result: ASR rises from 14.3% with individual methods to 71.4% with the combined attack, providing empirical support for the hypothesis that safety training does not generalize to broader capabilities and highlighting the need for multifaceted safety evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Safety alignment may be more brittle than model capabilities; single-defense approaches are insufficient. The work advocates multifaceted safety evaluations using compound attack scenarios to better probe alignment robustness.

title: UNICA: A Unified Neural Framework for Controllable 3D Avatars
summary: Controllable 3D human avatars have found widespread applications in 3D games, the metaverse, and AR/VR scenarios. The conventional approach to creating such a 3D avatar requires a lengthy, intricate pipeline encompassing appearance modeling, motion planning, rigging, and physical simulation. In this paper, we introduce UNICA (UNIfied neural Controllable Avatar), a skeleton-free generative model that unifies all avatar control components into a single neural framework. Given keyboard inputs akin to video game controls, UNICA generates the next frame of a 3D avatar's geometry through an action-conditioned diffusion model operating on 2D position maps. A point transformer then maps the resulting geometry to 3D Gaussian Splatting for high-fidelity free-view rendering. Our approach naturally captures hair and loose clothing dynamics without manually designed physical simulation, and supports extra-long autoregressive generation. To the best of our knowledge, UNICA is the first model to unify the workflow of "motion planning, rigging, physical simulation, and rendering". Code is released at https://github.com/zjh21/UNICA.

AI.tldr: A skeleton-free neural avatar model UNICA unifies motion planning, rigging, simulation, and rendering into a diffusion-based 2D position map framework with 3D Gaussian splatting for view-consistent rendering, enabling long-horizon autoregressive controllable avatar generation from simple inputs.
AI.motivation: To reduce the lengthy pipeline for controllable 3D avatars by integrating control components into a single neural model, enabling end-to-end controllable avatar generation from simple inputs.
AI.method: An action-conditioned diffusion model operating on 2D position maps to generate next-frame geometry from keyboard controls; a point transformer maps geometry to 3D Gaussian Splatting for rendering; hair/clothes dynamics emerge from diffusion without explicit physics; autoregressive generation supports long horizons. Skeleton-free and unified workflow claim.
AI.result: UNICA can produce next-frame geometry given inputs and render via Gaussian splatting; demonstrates hair/clothing dynamics without manual physics; supports extra-long autoregressive generation; code released.
AI.conclusion: UNICA offers a unified, skeleton-free framework for controllable avatars, potentially simplifying and accelerating avatar creation and animation pipelines, with end-to-end controllable generation from game-like inputs.

title: Product-Stability: Provable Convergence for Gradient Descent on the Edge of Stability
summary: Empirically, modern deep learning training often occurs at the Edge of Stability (EoS), where the sharpness of the loss exceeds the threshold below which classical convergence analysis applies. Despite recent progress, existing theoretical explanations of EoS either rely on restrictive assumptions or focus on specific squared-loss-type objectives. In this work, we introduce and study a structural property of loss functions that we term product-stability. We show that for losses with product-stable minima, gradient descent applied to objectives of the form $(x,y) \mapsto l(xy)$ can provably converge to the local minimum even when training in the EoS regime. This framework substantially generalizes prior results and applies to a broad class of losses, including binary cross entropy. Using bifurcation diagrams, we characterize the resulting training dynamics, explain the emergence of stable oscillations, and precisely quantify the sharpness at convergence. Together, our results offer a principled explanation for stable EoS training for a wider class of loss functions.

AI.tldr: Introduces product-stable minima and shows gradient descent on l(xy) converges to local minima in EoS for a broad loss class, including binary cross entropy; uses bifurcation diagrams to explain dynamics and quantify sharpness.
AI.motivation: EoS observed in modern deep learning; existing theory limited by restrictive assumptions or squared-loss focus; seeks a structural property that guarantees convergence in EoS for a wide class of losses.
AI.method: Define product-stable minima; analyze objectives of form (x,y) -> l(xy) under gradient descent; prove convergence to local minima in EoS; generalize prior results; apply to BCE; use bifurcation diagrams to characterize dynamics and stable oscillations; quantify convergence sharpness.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees of convergence under EoS for product-stable losses; broader applicability including BCE; explanation of stable oscillations; precise quantification of sharpness at convergence.
AI.conclusion: Provides principled explanation for stable EoS training across a wider loss family and offers a framework to analyze training dynamics via product-stability and bifurcation analysis.

title: PaveBench: A Versatile Benchmark for Pavement Distress Perception and Interactive Vision-Language Analysis
summary: Pavement condition assessment is essential for road safety and maintenance. Existing research has made significant progress. However, most studies focus on conventional computer vision tasks such as classification, detection, and segmentation. In real-world applications, pavement inspection requires more than visual recognition. It also requires quantitative analysis, explanation, and interactive decision support. Current datasets are limited. They focus on unimodal perception. They lack support for multi-turn interaction and fact-grounded reasoning. They also do not connect perception with vision-language analysis. To address these limitations, we introduce PaveBench, a large-scale benchmark for pavement distress perception and interactive vision-language analysis on real-world highway inspection images. PaveBench supports four core tasks: classification, object detection, semantic segmentation, and vision-language question answering. It provides unified task definitions and evaluation protocols. On the visual side, PaveBench provides large-scale annotations and includes a curated hard-distractor subset for robustness evaluation. It contains a large collection of real-world pavement images. On the multimodal side, we introduce PaveVQA, a real-image question answering (QA) dataset that supports single-turn, multi-turn, and expert-corrected interactions. It covers recognition, localization, quantitative estimation, and maintenance reasoning. We evaluate several state-of-the-art methods and provide a detailed analysis. We also present a simple and effective agent-augmented visual question answering framework that integrates domain-specific models as tools alongside vision-language models. The dataset is available at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/MML-Group/PaveBench.

AI.tldr: Introduces PaveBench, a large-scale benchmark for pavement distress perception and interactive vision-language analysis, with four tasks (classification, detection, segmentation, and VQA) and the PaveVQA dataset for single/multi-turn expert-corrected QA; also proposes an agent-augmented VQA framework; dataset released.
AI.motivation: Pavement inspection requires quantitative analysis, explanations, and interactive decision support. Existing datasets are largely unimodal, lack multi-turn interaction, fact-grounded reasoning, and vision-language integration, hindering real-world decision-making.
AI.method: Develop PaveBench with unified task definitions and evaluation protocols for four visual tasks; provide large-scale real-world pavement images with extensive annotations and a hard-distractor subset; create PaveVQA for single-turn, multi-turn, and expert-corrected QA; evaluate SOTA methods and present an agent-augmented VQA framework that uses domain-specific tools alongside vision-language models.
AI.result: Establishes a comprehensive benchmark and dataset suite (PaveBench and PaveVQA); includes evaluation of several state-of-the-art methods; provides insights through detailed analysis and robustness evaluation; dataset is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: PaveBench enables robust, interactive, multimodal pavement distress analysis and supports enhanced decision-making in road maintenance; the framework and datasets offer a foundation for future research and real-world deployment.

title: Low-Rank Compression of Pretrained Models via Randomized Subspace Iteration
summary: The massive scale of pretrained models has made efficient compression essential for practical deployment. Low-rank decomposition based on the singular value decomposition (SVD) provides a principled approach for model reduction, but its exact computation is expensive for large weight matrices. Randomized alternatives such as randomized SVD (RSVD) improve efficiency, yet they can suffer from poor approximation quality when the singular value spectrum decays slowly, a regime commonly observed in modern pretrained models. In this work, we address this limitation from both theoretical and empirical perspectives. First, we establish a connection between low-rank approximation error and predictive performance by analyzing softmax perturbations, showing that deviations in class probabilities are controlled by the spectral error of the compressed weights. Second, we demonstrate that RSVD is inadequate, and we propose randomized subspace iteration (RSI) as a more effective alternative. By incorporating multiple power iterations, RSI improves spectral separation and provides a controllable mechanism for enhancing approximation quality. We evaluate our approach on both convolutional networks and transformer-based architectures. Our results show that RSI achieves near-optimal approximation quality while outperforming RSVD in predictive accuracy under aggressive compression, enabling efficient model compression.

AI.tldr: RSI with power iterations improves randomized low-rank approximations for large pretrained models, linking spectral error to predictive performance and outperforming RSVD under aggressive compression.
AI.motivation: Need efficient, accurate low-rank compression for large pretrained models; RSVD underperforms with slowly decaying spectra; establish link to classifier perturbations.
AI.method: Proposes randomized subspace iteration (RSI) with multiple power iterations; analyzes softmax perturbations; experiments on CNNs and transformers.
AI.result: RSI achieves near-optimal spectral approximation and higher predictive accuracy than RSVD under aggressive compression.
AI.conclusion: RSI is a practical, controllable approach for model compression in large models, improving efficiency without sacrificing accuracy; provides theoretical and empirical support.

title: CMCC-ReID: Cross-Modality Clothing-Change Person Re-Identification
summary: Person Re-Identification (ReID) faces severe challenges from modality discrepancy and clothing variation in long-term surveillance scenario. While existing studies have made significant progress in either Visible-Infrared ReID (VI-ReID) or Clothing-Change ReID (CC-ReID), real-world surveillance system often face both challenges simultaneously. To address this overlooked yet realistic problem, we define a new task, termed Cross-Modality Clothing-Change Re-Identification (CMCC-ReID), which targets pedestrian matching across variations in both modality and clothing. To advance research in this direction, we construct a new benchmark SYSU-CMCC, where each identity is captured in both visible and infrared domains with distinct outfits, reflecting the dual heterogeneity of long-term surveillance. To tackle CMCC-ReID, we propose a Progressive Identity Alignment Network (PIA) that progressively mitigates the issues of clothing variation and modality discrepancy. Specifically, a Dual-Branch Disentangling Learning (DBDL) module separates identity-related cues from clothing-related factors to achieve clothing-agnostic representation, and a Bi-Directional Prototype Learning (BPL) module performs intra-modality and inter-modality contrast in the embedding space to bridge the modality gap while further suppressing clothing interference. Extensive experiments on the SYSU-CMCC dataset demonstrate that PIA establishes a strong baseline for this new task and significantly outperforms existing methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cross-Modality Clothing-Change Re-Identification (CMCC-ReID) and SYSU-CMCC dataset; presents Progressive Identity Alignment Network (PIA) with modules for disentangling clothing and aligning modalities, achieving strong baseline results.
AI.motivation: Address the joint challenges of modality discrepancy (visible vs. infrared) and clothing variation in long-term surveillance, which prior work treats separately (VI-ReID or CC-ReID).
AI.method: PIA uses Dual-Branch Disentangling Learning (DBDL) to separate identity cues from clothing; and Bi-Directional Prototype Learning (BPL) to perform intra- and inter-modality contrastive learning in the embedding space, with a progressive alignment framework.
AI.result: Experiments on the SYSU-CMCC benchmark show that PIA establishes a strong baseline for CMCC-ReID and significantly outperforms existing methods.
AI.conclusion: PIA provides a robust, clothing-agnostic and modality-bridging representation for CMCC-ReID, and the SYSU-CMCC dataset offers a realistic evaluation setting for long-term surveillance with dual heterogeneity.

title: A Numerical Method for Coupling Parameterized Physics-Informed Neural Networks and FDM for Advanced Thermal-Hydraulic System Simulation
summary: Severe accident analysis using system-level codes such as MELCOR is indispensable for nuclear safety assessment, yet the computational cost of repeated simulations poses a significant bottleneck for parametric studies and uncertainty quantification. Existing surrogate models accelerate these analyses but depend on large volumes of simulation data, while physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) enable data-free training but must be retrained for every change in problem parameters. This study addresses both limitations by developing the Parameterized PINNs coupled with FDM (P2F) method, a node-assigned hybrid framework for MELCOR's Control Volume Hydrodynamics/Flow Path (CVH/FP) module. In the P2F method, a parameterized Node-Assigned PINN (NA-PINN) accepts the water-level difference, initial velocity, and time as inputs, learning a solution manifold so that a single trained network serves as a data-free surrogate for the momentum conservation equation across all flow paths without retraining. This PINN is coupled with a finite difference method (FDM) solver that advances the mass conservation equation at each time step, ensuring exact discrete mass conservation while replacing the iterative nonlinear momentum solve with a single forward pass. Verification on a six-tank gravity-driven draining scenario yields a water level mean absolute error of $7.85 \times 10^{-5}$ m and a velocity mean absolute error of $3.21 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s under the nominal condition with $Δt = 1.0$ s. The framework maintains consistent accuracy across time steps ranging from 0.2 to 1.0 s and generalizes to five distinct initial conditions, all without retraining or simulation data. This work introduces a numerical coupling methodology for integrating parameterized PINNs with FDM within a nuclear thermal-hydraulic system code framework.

AI.tldr: P2F introduces a Parameterized PINN with a finite-difference mass solver for MELCOR CVH/FP, delivering a data-free, parametric surrogate across all flow paths with exact mass conservation and high accuracy, avoiding retraining for parameter changes.
AI.motivation: High computational cost of repeated MELCOR simulations limits parametric studies and uncertainty quantification; existing surrogates require large data; PINNs are data-free but typically need retraining for different parameters. A parametric PINN coupled with a mass-conserving solver offers data-free generalization across problem parameters without retraining.
AI.method: A parameterized Node-Assigned PINN (NA-PINN) takes water-level difference, initial velocity, and time as inputs and learns a solution manifold for the momentum equation across all flow paths. It is coupled with a finite-difference method (FDM) solver that advances the mass conservation equation at each time step, ensuring exact discrete mass conservation while replacing the nonlinear momentum solve with a single forward pass.
AI.result: Validation on a six-tank gravity-driven draining setup yields water-level MAE = 7.85e-5 m and velocity MAE = 3.21e-3 m/s under nominal Δt = 1.0 s. Accuracy remains steady for Δt in [0.2, 1.0] s and generalizes to five distinct initial conditions, with no retraining or simulation data required.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a robust numerical framework that couples parameterized PINNs with FDM within a nuclear thermal-hydraulic code, enabling data-free, parameterizable surrogates that preserve mass conservation across flow paths.

title: QAPruner: Quantization-Aware Vision Token Pruning for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong reasoning ability, but their high computational and memory costs hinder deployment in resource-constrained settings. While Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) and vision token pruning are standard compression techniques, they are usually treated as independent optimizations. In this paper, we show that these two techniques are strongly coupled: naively applying semantic-based token pruning to PTQ-optimized MLLMs can discard activation outliers that are important for numerical stability and thus worsen quantization errors in low-bit regimes (\textit{e.g.}, W4A4). To address this issue, we propose a quantization-aware vision token pruning framework. Our method introduces a lightweight hybrid sensitivity metric that combines simulated group-wise quantization error with outlier intensity. By combining this metric with standard semantic relevance scores, the method retains tokens that are both semantically informative and robust to quantization. Experiments on standard LLaVA architectures show that our method consistently outperforms naive integration baselines. At an aggressive pruning ratio that retains only 12.5\% of visual tokens, our framework improves accuracy by 2.24\% over the baseline and even surpasses dense quantization without pruning. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first method that explicitly co-optimizes vision token pruning and PTQ for accurate low-bit MLLM inference.

AI.tldr: Joint PTQ-aware vision token pruning for MLLMs; co-optimizes pruning and quantization via a hybrid sensitivity metric to retain semantically informative and quantization-robust tokens, improving low-bit accuracy.
AI.motivation: MLLMs are powerful but costly; PTQ and vision-token pruning are typically treated separate, but naive pruning under low-bit quantization can remove activation outliers critical for numerical stability, worsening quantization errors.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight hybrid sensitivity metric that blends simulated group-wise quantization error with outlier intensity, combined with semantic relevance scores. This guides pruning to retain tokens that are both informative and robust to quantization, enabling effective low-bit inference.
AI.result: On standard LLaVA architectures, the method consistently outperforms naive integration baselines. At 12.5% token retention, accuracy improves by 2.24% over the baseline and surpasses dense quantization without pruning.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a first explicit co-optimization of vision token pruning and PTQ for accurate low-bit MLLM inference, highlighting the importance of considering quantization robustness in pruning decisions and enabling more efficient deployment.

title: Cross-subject Muscle Fatigue Detection via Adversarial and Supervised Contrastive Learning with Inception-Attention Network
comment: This work has been submitted to ICARM 2026 for possible publication. 6 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables
summary: Muscle fatigue detection plays an important role in physical rehabilitation. Previous researches have demonstrated that sEMG offers superior sensitivity in detecting muscle fatigue compared to other biological signals. However, features extracted from sEMG may vary during dynamic contractions and across different subjects, which causes unstability in fatigue detection. To address these challenges, this research proposes a novel neural network comprising an Inception-attention module as a feature extractor, a fatigue classifier and a domain classifier equipped with a gradient reversal layer. The integrated domain classifier encourages the network to learn subject-invariant common fatigue features while minimizing subject-specific features. Furthermore, a supervised contrastive loss function is also employed to enhance the generalization capability of the model. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieved outstanding performance in three-class classification tasks, reaching 93.54% accuracy, 92.69% recall and 92.69% F1-score, providing a robust solution for cross-subject muscle fatigue detection, offering significant guidance for rehabilitation training and assistance.

AI.tldr: Cross-subject fatigue detection from sEMG using an Inception-attention feature extractor, a fatigue classifier, and a domain-adversarial module with gradient reversal plus supervised contrastive loss; achieves 93.54% accuracy, 92.69% recall, 92.69% F1 on three-class tasks.
AI.motivation: sEMG fatigue features vary with dynamic contractions and across subjects, causing instability in fatigue detection. A domain-adversarial approach can learn subject-invariant fatigue features, while a contrastive loss aims to enhance generalization.
AI.method: A neural network with an Inception-attention feature extractor, a fatigue classifier, and a domain classifier equipped with a gradient reversal layer. A supervised contrastive loss is employed to improve generalization. Training targets cross-subject fatigue classification (three classes).
AI.result: Three-class classification performance: accuracy 93.54%, recall 92.69%, F1-score 92.69%. Demonstrates robust cross-subject fatigue detection.
AI.conclusion: The architecture integrates hierarchy-aware feature extraction, domain-adversarial learning, and supervised contrastive learning to address inter-subject variability in sEMG fatigue detection, yielding strong cross-subject performance and practical rehabilitation guidance.

title: MMPhysVideo: Scaling Physical Plausibility in Video Generation via Joint Multimodal Modeling
summary: Despite advancements in generating visually stunning content, video diffusion models (VDMs) often yield physically inconsistent results due to pixel-only reconstruction. To address this, we propose MMPhysVideo, the first framework to scale physical plausibility in video generation through joint multimodal modeling. We recast perceptual cues, specifically semantics, geometry, and spatio-temporal trajectory, into a unified pseudo-RGB format, enabling VDMs to directly capture complex physical dynamics. To mitigate cross-modal interference, we propose a Bidirectionally Controlled Teacher architecture, which utilizes parallel branches to fully decouple RGB and perception processing and adopts two zero-initialized control links to gradually learn pixel-wise consistency. For inference efficiency, the teacher's physical prior is distilled into a single-stream student model via representation alignment. Furthermore, we present MMPhysPipe, a scalable data curation and annotation pipeline tailored for constructing physics-rich multimodal datasets. MMPhysPipe employs a vision-language model (VLM) guided by a chain-of-visual-evidence rule to pinpoint physical subjects, enabling expert models to extract multi-granular perceptual information. Without additional inference costs, MMPhysVideo consistently improves physical plausibility and visual quality over advanced models across various benchmarks and achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: A multimodal framework (MMPhysVideo) improves physical plausibility in video diffusion by encoding semantics, geometry, and spatio-temporal cues into a pseudo-RGB format, using a Bidirectionally Controlled Teacher and a distillation-based single-stream student, plus MMPhysPipe for data curation; achieves state-of-the-art results without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Address physical inconsistency in video diffusion caused by pixel-only reconstruction, and enable scalable, physics-aware video generation by integrating multimodal perceptual cues and a physical prior.
AI.method: Recast semantics, geometry, and spatio-temporal trajectory into a unified pseudo-RGB representation for the diffusion model; employ a Bidirectionally Controlled Teacher with parallel RGB/perception branches and two zero-initialized control links to learn pixel-level consistency; distill the teacher's physical prior into a single-stream student for efficient inference; introduce MMPhysPipe, a data curation/annotation pipeline guided by vision-language models and a chain-of-visual-evidence rule to identify physical subjects, enabling expert extraction of multi-granular perceptual information.
