summary: Routing algorithms are crucial for efficient computer network operations, and in many settings they must be able to react to traffic bursts within milliseconds. Live telemetry data can provide informative signals to routing algorithms, and recent work has trained neural networks to exploit such signals for traffic-aware routing. Yet, aggregating network-wide information is subject to communication delays, and existing neural approaches either assume unrealistic delay-free global states, or restrict routers to purely local telemetry. This leaves their deployability in real-world environments unclear. We cast telemetry-aware routing as a delay-aware closed-loop control problem and introduce a framework that trains and evaluates neural routing algorithms, while explicitly modeling communication and inference delays. On top of this framework, we propose LOGGIA, a scalable graph neural routing algorithm that predicts log-space link weights from attributed topology-and-telemetry graphs. It utilizes a data-driven pre-training stage, followed by on-policy Reinforcement Learning. Across synthetic and real network topologies, and unseen mixed TCP/UDP traffic sequences, LOGGIA consistently outperforms shortest-path baselines, whereas neural baselines fail once realistic delays are enforced. Our experiments further suggest that neural routing algorithms like LOGGIA perform best when deployed fully locally, i.e., observing network states and inferring actions at every router individually, as opposed to centralized decision making.

AI.tldr: A delay-aware framework for telemetry-driven routing; introduces LOGGIA, an on-policy RL-enhanced graph neural network that predicts log-link weights from topology+telemetry graphs to improve routing under realistic delays, with local router deployment favored.
AI.motivation: Existing neural routing approaches rely on delay-free global states or purely local views, hindering real-world deployability; need to handle millisecond traffic bursts using live telemetry despite communication/inference delays.
AI.method: Propose a delay-aware closed-loop control framework; LOGGIA uses a graph neural network to map topology+telemetry to log-space link weights; training proceeds in two stages: data-driven pre-training and on-policy reinforcement learning; evaluation across synthetic and real topologies with mixed traffic, comparing to shortest-path baselines and other neural methods.
AI.result: LOGGIA outperforms shortest-path baselines across tested topologies and traffic sequences; neural baselines fail under realistic delays; best performance when routing decisions are made locally at each router rather than centrally.
AI.conclusion: Modeling communication and inference delays is crucial for deployable neural routing; local, fully distributed deployment can maximize performance; the framework and LOGGIA demonstrate practicality of neural routing in real networks.

title: Toward an Artificial General Teacher: Procedural Geometry Data Generation and Visual Grounding with Vision-Language Models
summary: We study visual explanation in geometry education as a Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) problem: given a diagram and a natural language description, the task is to produce a pixel-level mask for the referred geometric element. However, existing RIS models trained on natural image benchmarks such as RefCOCO fail catastrophically on geometric diagrams due to the fundamental domain shift between photographic scenes and abstract, textureless schematics. To address the absence of suitable training data, we present a fully automated procedural data engine that generates over 200,000 synthetic geometry diagrams with pixel-perfect segmentation masks and linguistically diverse referring expressions, requiring zero manual annotation. We further propose domain-specific fine-tuning of vision-language models (VLMs), demonstrating that a fine-tuned Florence-2 achieves 49% IoU and 85% Buffered IoU (BIoU), compared to <1% IoU in zero-shot settings. We introduce Buffered IoU, a geometry-aware evaluation metric that accounts for thin-structure localization, and show that it better reflects true segmentation quality than standard IoU. Our results establish a foundation for building Artificial General Teachers (AGTs) capable of providing visually grounded, step-by-step explanations of geometry problems.

AI.tldr: A study reframes geometric diagram understanding as referring image segmentation, introducing a synthetic data engine and domain-tuned vision-language models with a new 'Buffered IoU' metric; achieving meaningful gains in diagram-specific RIS tasks and arguing for AGT-enabled geometry explanations.
AI.motivation: Address domain shift and data scarcity: RIS models trained on natural images fail on geometry diagrams; need large, diverse, annotated data without manual labeling; enable reliable visual grounding for geometry education.
AI.method: 1) Fully automated procedural generator producing 200k+ synthetic geometry diagrams with pixel-perfect segmentation masks and diverse referring expressions. 2) Domain-specific fine-tuning of Florence-2 VLM to adapt to diagrams. 3) Introduce Buffered IoU to better capture thin structures. 4) Demonstrate AGT potential with step-by-step geometry explanations.
AI.result: Fine-tuned Florence-2 achieves 49% IoU and 85% Buffered IoU (BIoU); zero-shot performance is <1% IoU. The proposed metric correlates better with segmentation quality for thin geometry elements.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a dataset and tuning approach enabling visually grounded geometry explanations; supports the development of Artificial General Teachers (AGTs) for geometry problem solving.

title: Explainable Machine Learning Reveals 12-Fold Ucp1 Upregulation and Thermogenic Reprogramming in Female Mouse White Adipose Tissue After 37 Days of Microgravity: First AI/ML Analysis of NASA OSD-970
comment: 11 pages, 9 figures, 5 tables. First AI/ML analysis of NASA OSD-970 (GLDS-790). Code available at https://github.com/Rashadul22/NASA_OSD970_Complete_Output
summary: Microgravity induces profound metabolic adaptations in mammalian physiology, yet the molecular mechanisms governing thermogenesis in female white adipose tissue (WAT) remain poorly characterized. This paper presents the first machine learning (ML) analysis of NASA Open Science Data Repository (OSDR) dataset OSD-970, derived from the Rodent Research-1 (RR-1) mission. Using RT-qPCR data from 89 adipogenesis and thermogenesis pathway genes in gonadal WAT of 16 female C57BL/6J mice (8 flight, 8 ground control) following 37 days aboard the International Space Station (ISS), we applied differential expression analysis, multiple ML classifiers with Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOO-CV), and Explainable AI via SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). The most striking finding is a dramatic 12.21-fold upregulation of Ucp1 (Delta-Delta-Ct = -3.61, p = 0.0167) in microgravity-exposed WAT, accompanied by significant activation of the thermogenesis pathway (mean pathway fold-change = 3.24). The best-performing model (Random Forest with top-20 features) achieved AUC = 0.922, Accuracy = 0.812, and F1 = 0.824 via LOO-CV. SHAP analysis consistently ranked Ucp1 among the top predictive features, while Angpt2, Irs2, Jun, and Klf-family transcription factors emerged as dominant consensus classifiers. Principal component analysis (PCA) revealed clear separation between flight and ground samples, with PC1 explaining 69.1% of variance. These results suggest rapid thermogenic reprogramming in female WAT as a compensatory response to microgravity. This study demonstrates the power of explainable AI for re-analysis of newly released NASA space biology datasets, with direct implications for female astronaut health on long-duration missions and for Earth-based obesity and metabolic disease research.

AI.tldr: Explainable AI on RR-1 NASA data reveals rapid thermogenic remodeling in female gonadal WAT during spaceflight, with strong Ucp1 upregulation and a high-performing ML classifier; SHAP highlights Ucp1 and other regulators.
AI.motivation: Characterize thermogenic regulation in female white adipose tissue under microgravity and demonstrate the utility of ML/SHAP analyses on NASA Open Science Data Repository datasets.
AI.method: RT-qPCR on 89 adipogenesis/thermogenesis genes in gonadal WAT from 16 female C57BL/6J mice (8 flight, 8 ground) after 37 days on ISS. Analyses included differential expression, multiple ML classifiers with Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation, SHAP explainability, and PCA.
AI.result: Ucp1 upregulated 12.21-fold (Delta-Delta-Ct -3.61, p=0.0167); mean thermogenesis pathway fold-change 3.24. Best model: Random Forest with top-20 features; AUC 0.922, Accuracy 0.812, F1 0.824. SHAP ranked Ucp1 highly; Angpt2, Irs2, Jun, and Klf TFs also predictive. PCA: PC1 explains 69.1% variance, clearly separating flight vs ground.
AI.conclusion: Indicates rapid thermogenic reprogramming in female WAT as a microgravity response; demonstrates the value of explainable AI for re-analyzing NASA space biology datasets and has implications for female astronaut health and Earth-based obesity/metabolic disease research.

title: EvaNet: Towards More Efficient and Consistent Infrared and Visible Image Fusion Assessment
summary: Evaluation is essential in image fusion research, yet most existing metrics are directly borrowed from other vision tasks without proper adaptation. These traditional metrics, often based on complex image transformations, not only fail to capture the true quality of the fusion results but also are computationally demanding. To address these issues, we propose a unified evaluation framework specifically tailored for image fusion. At its core is a lightweight network designed efficiently to approximate widely used metrics, following a divide-and-conquer strategy. Unlike conventional approaches that directly assess similarity between fused and source images, we first decompose the fusion result into infrared and visible components. The evaluation model is then used to measure the degree of information preservation in these separated components, effectively disentangling the fusion evaluation process. During training, we incorporate a contrastive learning strategy and inform our evaluation model by perceptual scene assessment provided by a large language model. Last, we propose the first consistency evaluation framework, which measures the alignment between image fusion metrics and human visual perception, using both independent no-reference scores and downstream tasks performance as objective references. Extensive experiments show that our learning-based evaluation paradigm delivers both superior efficiency (up to 1,000 times faster) and greater consistency across a range of standard image fusion benchmarks. Our code will be publicly available at https://github.com/AWCXV/EvaNet.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, unified evaluation framework for image fusion (EvaNet) that decomposes fused images into infrared and visible components, learns a metric via a lightweight network trained with contrastive learning and LLM-guided perceptual signals, and introduces a consistency evaluation framework to align metrics with human perception, achieving up to 1000x speedup and improved consistency.
AI.motivation: Traditional image fusion metrics are often borrowed from other vision tasks, are computationally heavy, and poorly reflect fusion quality. There is a need for an efficient, task-aligned evaluation framework that correlates with human perception.
AI.method: 1) A lightweight network to approximate standard fusion metrics. 2) Divide-and-conquer: decompose fused results into infrared and visible components and evaluate information preservation in each. 3) Train with contrastive learning; leverage perceptual guidance from a large language model. 4) Propose a consistency evaluation framework using no-reference scores and downstream task performance as references.
AI.result: The learning-based evaluation paradigm is up to 1,000 times faster than traditional metrics and shows greater consistency across standard image fusion benchmarks. Code will be released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers efficient, consistent evaluation for image fusion, with disentangled component-wise assessment and alignment to human perception, offering a solid foundation for future metric design and practice.

title: Mitigating Reward Hacking in RLHF via Advantage Sign Robustness
summary: Reward models (RMs) used in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) are vulnerable to reward hacking: as the policy maximizes a learned proxy reward, true quality plateaus or degrades. We make the assumption that reward hacking is often caused by flipped advantage signs: instead of reducing the likelihood of a bad response, a flipped sign causes the update to increase it. By considering an adversarial perturbation in the RM parameter space, we can derive a certified sign-preservation radius, which is the smallest perturbation that can flip the advantage sign during policy optimization. Based on this formulation, we propose Sign-Certified Policy Optimization (SignCert-PO), down-weighting non-robust completions in the policy gradient update. Unlike prior approaches that require multiple RMs or access to the RM training data, SignCert-PO is lightweight and operates purely at the policy optimization stage using only the RM parameters and on-policy completions. On TL;DR summarization and AlpacaFarm benchmarks, SignCert-PO consistently achieves a better win rate than baselines and reduces reward hacking.

AI.tldr: Introduces Sign-Certified Policy Optimization (SignCert-PO), a lightweight method to mitigate reward hacking in RLHF by certifying sign preservation under RM perturbations and down-weighting non-robust completions; shows improved win rates on TL;DR and AlpacaFarm benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Reward models in RLHF can be exploited through reward hacking when the policy optimizes a learned proxy reward. The proposed approach targets the root cause—possible sign flips in advantage updates—by providing a certified robustness notion and a policy-optimization technique that handles this at training time without extra RMs.
AI.method: Model perturbation analysis in RM parameter space to derive a certified sign-preservation radius (the smallest perturbation that can flip the advantage sign). Use this radius to down-weight non-robust completions in the policy gradient update. SignCert-PO operates purely at the policy optimization stage, requiring only RM parameters and on-policy completions; no need for multiple RMs or RM training data.
AI.result: Empirically, SignCert-PO achieves a better win rate than baselines and reduces reward hacking on the TL;DR summarization task and AlpacaFarm benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SignCert-PO is lightweight and robust, achieving sign-preserving policy updates without access to RM training data and solely using RM parameters and on-policy data; it mitigates reward hacking by down-weighting non-robust completions during policy optimization.

title: FedSQ: Optimized Weight Averaging via Fixed Gating
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative training across organizations without sharing raw data, but it is hindered by statistical heterogeneity (non-i.i.d.\ client data) and by instability of naive weight averaging under client drift. In many cross-silo deployments, FL is warm-started from a strong pretrained backbone (e.g., ImageNet-1K) and then adapted to local domains. Motivated by recent evidence that ReLU-like gating regimes (structural knowledge) stabilize earlier than the remaining parameter values (quantitative knowledge), we propose FedSQ (Federated Structural-Quantitative learning), a transfer-initialized neural federated procedure based on a DualCopy, piecewise-linear view of deep networks. FedSQ freezes a structural copy of the pretrained model to induce fixed binary gating masks during federated fine-tuning, while only a quantitative copy is optimized locally and aggregated across rounds. Fixing the gating reduces learning to within-regime affine refinements, which stabilizes aggregation under heterogeneous partitions. Experiments on two convolutional neural network backbones under i.i.d.\ and Dirichlet splits show that FedSQ improves robustness and can reduce rounds-to-best validation performance relative to standard baselines while preserving accuracy in the transfer setting.

AI.tldr: FedSQ uses a DualCopy approach with a fixed structural copy (binary gating masks) and a trainable quantitative copy to stabilize federated fine-tuning on pretrained backbones, improving robustness and reducing rounds-to-best in non-i.i.d. settings.
AI.motivation: Statistical heterogeneity (non-i.i.d. client data) and unstable weight averaging in federated learning hinder convergence, especially when warm-started from a pretrained backbone. ReLU-like gating regimes suggest separating structural (binary) from quantitative (continuous) knowledge to stabilize learning.
AI.method: A transfer-initialized neural FL method that introduces DualCopy: a fixed structural copy providing gating masks and a trainable quantitative copy. The gating masks are fixed during federated fine-tuning, effectively producing a piecewise-linear, gating-based architecture. Computation updates occur only on the quantitative copy and are aggregated across rounds.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on two CNN backbones under i.i.d. and Dirichlet non-iid splits shows FedSQ improves robustness and reduces rounds-to-best validation compared with standard baselines, while preserving transfer accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Fixing structural gating reduces learning to regime-limited affine refinements, stabilizing aggregation under heterogeneity and making transfer-initialized FL more robust and efficient. The approach is effective across architectures and data partitions, though it relies on a meaningful structural copy and gating.

title: UniSpector: Towards Universal Open-set Defect Recognition via Spectral-Contrastive Visual Prompting
summary: Although industrial inspection systems should be capable of recognizing unprecedented defects, most existing approaches operate under a closed-set assumption, which prevents them from detecting novel anomalies. While visual prompting offers a scalable alternative for industrial inspection, existing methods often suffer from prompt embedding collapse due to high intra-class variance and subtle inter-class differences. To resolve this, we propose UniSpector, which shifts the focus from naive prompt-to-region matching to the principled design of a semantically structured and transferable prompt topology. UniSpector employs the Spatial-Spectral Prompt Encoder to extract orientation-invariant, fine-grained representations; these serve as a solid basis for the Contrastive Prompt Encoder to explicitly regularize the prompt space into a semantically organized angular manifold. Additionally, Prompt-guided Query Selection generates adaptive object queries aligned with the prompt. We introduce Inspect Anything, the first benchmark for visual-prompt-based open-set defect localization, where UniSpector significantly outperforms baselines by at least 19.7% and 15.8% in AP50b and AP50m, respectively. These results show that our method enable a scalable, retraining-free inspection paradigm for continuously evolving industrial environments, while offering critical insights into the design of generic visual prompting.

AI.tldr: UniSpector proposes a semantically structured, transferable prompt topology for open-set defect localization in industrial inspection, leveraging a Spatial-Spectral Prompt Encoder, a Contrastive Prompt Encoder, and a Prompt-guided Query Selection, plus a new Inspect Anything benchmark; it achieves substantial AP improvements and offers a retraining-free, scalable solution.
AI.motivation: Open-set defect detection in manufacturing suffers from closed-set assumptions, prompt embedding collapse due to high intra-class variance and subtle inter-class differences; need scalable, robust visual prompting that preserves semantic structure and transferability.
AI.method: Introduce UniSpector architecture: Spatial-Spectral Prompt Encoder for orientation-invariant, fine-grained features; Contrastive Prompt Encoder to regularize the prompt space into a semantically organized angular manifold; Prompt-guided Query Selection to align object queries with prompts; propose Inspect Anything benchmark for open-set defect localization.
AI.result: Empirically outperform baselines by at least 19.7% AP50b and 15.8% AP50m on Inspect Anything; demonstrates retraining-free, scalable inspection in evolving industrial environments.
AI.conclusion: Semantically structured prompt topology improves open-set defect localization; provides design insights for generic visual prompting in industrial inspection and a new benchmark to evaluate openness and transferability.

title: Generating DDPM-based Samples from Tilted Distributions
summary: Given $n$ independent samples from a $d$-dimensional probability distribution, our aim is to generate diffusion-based samples from a distribution obtained by tilting the original, where the degree of tilt is parametrized by $θ\in \mathbb{R}^d$. We define a plug-in estimator and show that it is minimax-optimal. We develop Wasserstein bounds between the distribution of the plug-in estimator and the true distribution as a function of $n$ and $θ$, illustrating regimes where the output and the desired true distribution are close. Further, under some assumptions, we prove the TV-accuracy of running Diffusion on these tilted samples. Our theoretical results are supported by extensive simulations. Applications of our work include finance, weather and climate modelling, and many other domains, where the aim may be to generate samples from a tilted distribution that satisfies practically motivated moment constraints.

AI.tldr: A framework to generate diffusion-based samples from a tilted distribution parameterized by θ, via a minimax-optimal plug-in estimator, with Wasserstein-distance bounds to the true tilted distribution as a function of n and θ, and TV-accuracy results for diffusion on these samples; supported by simulations and applicable to finance, weather, and climate modeling.
AI.motivation: To sample from distributions subject to tilt or moment constraints using diffusion models, with theoretical guarantees on approximation quality and finite-sample behavior.
AI.method: Define a plug-in estimator for the tilted distribution; establish minimax-optimality; derive Wasserstein distance bounds between the plug-in-induced distribution and the true tilted distribution as functions of n and θ; analyze TV-accuracy of running diffusion on samples from the tilted distribution under certain assumptions; validate with simulations.
AI.result: Proves minimax-optimality of the plug-in estimator; provides finite-sample Wasserstein bounds showing when the generated output approximates the tilted target; shows TV-accuracy of the diffusion process on tilted samples under assumptions; simulations corroborate theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a principled, theory-backed method for sampling from tilted distributions via diffusion models, with explicit sample-size and tilt-parameter regimes guaranteeing close distributional performance; broad applicability to finance, weather, climate, and other domains requiring tilted or constrained sampling.

title: SentiAvatar: Towards Expressive and Interactive Digital Humans
summary: We present SentiAvatar, a framework for building expressive interactive 3D digital humans, and use it to create SuSu, a virtual character that speaks, gestures, and emotes in real time. Achieving such a system remains challenging, as it requires jointly addressing three key problems: the lack of large-scale, high-quality multimodal data, robust semantic-to-motion mapping, and fine-grained frame-level motion-prosody synchronization. To solve these problems, first, we build SuSuInterActs (21K clips, 37 hours), a dialogue corpus captured via optical motion capture around a single character with synchronized speech, full-body motion, and facial expressions. Second, we pre-train a Motion Foundation Model on 200K+ motion sequences, equipping it with rich action priors that go well beyond the conversation. We then propose an audio-aware plan-then-infill architecture that decouples sentence-level semantic planning from frame-level prosody-driven interpolation, so that generated motions are both semantically appropriate and rhythmically aligned with speech. Experiments show that SentiAvatar achieves state-of-the-art on both SuSuInterActs (R@1 43.64%, nearly 2 times the best baseline) and BEATv2 (FGD 4.941, BC 8.078), producing 6s of output in 0.3s with unlimited multi-turn streaming. The source code, model, and dataset are available at https://sentiavatar.github.io.

AI.tldr: SentiAvatar demonstrates an end-to-end framework for building expressive interactive 3D digital humans, combining a specialized dataset (SuSuInterActs), a Motion Foundation Model, and an audio-aware plan-then-infill pipeline to achieve real-time semantically correct and prosody-aligned motion; achieves state-of-the-art on SuSuInterActs and BEATv2.
AI.motivation: Three core challenges hinder expressive 3D avatars: data scarcity of large, high-quality multimodal datasets; robust mapping from semantic plan to motion; and fine-grained synchronization of motion with speech. Address by constructing a dialogue-focused motion dataset, large-scale motion pretraining, and decoupled planning/interpolation.
AI.method: Create SuSuInterActs dataset with 21K clips, 37 hours, synchronized speech, full-body motion, facial expressions; pre-train a Motion Foundation Model on 200K+ motion sequences; propose audio-aware plan-then-infill architecture that separates sentence-level planning from frame-level prosody-driven interpolation to ensure semantic relevance and speech rhythm alignment.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on SuSuInterActs (R@1 43.64%, ~2x baseline) and BEATv2 (FGD 4.941, BC 8.078); capable of producing 6 seconds of output in 0.3 seconds with unlimited multi-turn streaming.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable path to scalable real-time expressive avatars; the dataset, model, and code are released; potential impact on interactive digital humans; future work could extend data diversity and generalization across characters.

title: Co-Evolution of Policy and Internal Reward for Language Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents learn by interacting with environments, but long-horizon training remains fundamentally bottlenecked by sparse and delayed rewards. Existing methods typically address this challenge through post-hoc credit assignment or external reward models, which provide limited guidance at inference time and often separate reward improvement from policy improvement. We propose Self-Guide, a self-generated internal reward for language agents that supports both inference-time guidance and training-time supervision. Specifically, the agent uses Self-Guide as a short self-guidance signal to steer the next action during inference, and converts the same signal into step-level internal reward for denser policy optimization during training. This creates a co-evolving loop: better policy produces better guidance, and better guidance further improves policy as internal reward. Across three agent benchmarks, inference-time self-guidance already yields clear gains, while jointly evolving policy and internal reward with GRPO brings further improvements (8\%) over baselines trained solely with environment reward. Overall, our results suggest that language agents can improve not only by collecting more experience, but also by learning to generate and refine their own internal reward during acting and learning.

AI.tldr: Self-Guide introduces a self-generated internal reward that serves as short-term guidance during inference and as a signal for denser policy optimization during training, creating a co-evolving loop where better policy yields better guidance and vice versa. Inference gains appear first, with further gains (~8%) when jointly evolving policy and internal reward via GRPO, surpassing environment-reward baselines.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agent training is bottlenecked by sparse and delayed rewards. Existing approaches rely on post-hoc credit assignment or external reward models, which offer limited inference-time guidance and decouple reward improvement from policy improvement. A unified internal reward signal can provide guidance at both inference and learning time.
AI.method: Introduce Self-Guide as a short self-guidance signal that steers the next action during inference. Convert the same signal into step-level internal reward to drive policy optimization during training. Establish a co-evolving loop: improved policy improves guidance, which in turn further improves policy. Employ GRPO to jointly evolve policy and the internal reward. Evaluate on three agent benchmarks, comparing inference-time guidance and training-time joint evolution.
AI.result: Inference-time self-guidance yields clear performance gains. Jointly evolving policy and internal reward with GRPO yields additional improvements, about 8% over baselines trained only with environment rewards, across three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Language agents can improve not only by collecting more experience but also by generating and refining their own internal reward during acting and learning. Self-generated rewards can provide both inference-time guidance and training-time supervision, enabling a co-evolution of policy and reward that enhances performance beyond environment-reward baselines.

title: GP-4DGS: Probabilistic 4D Gaussian Splatting from Monocular Video via Variational Gaussian Processes
summary: We present GP-4DGS, a novel framework that integrates Gaussian Processes (GPs) into 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) for principled probabilistic modeling of dynamic scenes. While existing 4DGS methods focus on deterministic reconstruction, they are inherently limited in capturing motion ambiguity and lack mechanisms to assess prediction reliability. By leveraging the kernel-based probabilistic nature of GPs, our approach introduces three key capabilities: (i) uncertainty quantification for motion predictions, (ii) motion estimation for unobserved or sparsely sampled regions, and (iii) temporal extrapolation beyond observed training frames. To scale GPs to the large number of Gaussian primitives in 4DGS, we design spatio-temporal kernels that capture the correlation structure of deformation fields and adopt variational Gaussian Processes with inducing points for tractable inference. Our experiments show that GP-4DGS enhances reconstruction quality while providing reliable uncertainty estimates that effectively identify regions of high motion ambiguity. By addressing these challenges, our work takes a meaningful step toward bridging probabilistic modeling and neural graphics.

AI.tldr: A GP-4DGS introduces Gaussian Processes into 4D Gaussian Splatting to model dynamic scenes probabilistically, enabling uncertainty quantification, motion estimation in unobserved regions, and temporal extrapolation while remaining scalable via spatio-temporal kernels and inducing points.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting methods yield deterministic reconstructions and struggle with motion ambiguity and reliability assessment. A probabilistic formulation can quantify uncertainty, guide observation strategies, handle sparse data, and support extrapolation.
AI.method: Integrate Gaussian Processes into 4D Gaussian Splatting. Design spatio-temporal kernels to capture correlations in deformation fields. Use variational GP with inducing points to scale to many Gaussian primitives. Train on dynamic scenes to obtain predictive means and uncertainties, perform motion estimation in unobserved regions, and extrapolate temporally beyond training frames.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show improved reconstruction quality and reliable uncertainty estimates. The framework identifies regions with high motion ambiguity and provides plausible motion estimates where data are sparse, demonstrating the benefit of probabilistic modeling in neural graphics.
AI.conclusion: GP-4DGS bridges probabilistic modeling and neural graphics, offering a scalable, uncertainty-aware approach to dynamic scene representation that extends 4D Gaussian Splatting with principled probabilistic reasoning.

title: Self-Distilled RLVR
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) has become a popular training paradigm in the LLM community. This paradigm selects a larger model as the teacher to provide dense, fine-grained signals for each sampled trajectory, in contrast to reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), which only obtains sparse signals from verifiable outcomes in the environment. Recently, the community has explored on-policy self-distillation (OPSD), where the same model serves as both teacher and student, with the teacher receiving additional privileged information such as reference answers to enable self-evolution. This paper demonstrates that learning signals solely derived from the privileged teacher result in severe information leakage and unstable long-term training. Accordingly, we identify the optimal niche for self-distillation and propose \textbf{RLSD} (\textbf{RL}VR with \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{D}istillation). Specifically, we leverage self-distillation to obtain token-level policy differences for determining fine-grained update magnitudes, while continuing to use RLVR to derive reliable update directions from environmental feedback (e.g., response correctness). This enables RLSD to simultaneously harness the strengths of both RLVR and OPSD, achieving a higher convergence ceiling and superior training stability.

AI.tldr: Introduces RLSD, a hybrid RL + self-distillation framework that combines token-level updates from privileged self-distillation with environment-driven updates from RLVR, aiming to improve stability and convergence over pure OPSD or RLVR alone.
AI.motivation: To address instability and information leakage associated with on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) and to exploit the complementary strengths of RL-based verification (RLVR) and self-distillation.
AI.method: RLSD uses self-distillation to derive token-level policy differences to modulate update magnitudes, while continuing to use RLVR to obtain reliable update directions from environmental feedback (e.g., response correctness); integrates both sources to guide training.
AI.result: Claimed improvement: higher convergence ceiling and superior training stability compared to existing OPSD/RLVR approaches.
AI.conclusion: RLSD effectively blends RLVR and OPSD, achieving better performance and stability by combining token-level, privileged-information signals with environment-based guidance.

title: BEVPredFormer: Spatio-temporal Attention for BEV Instance Prediction in Autonomous Driving
summary: A robust awareness of how dynamic scenes evolve is essential for Autonomous Driving systems, as they must accurately detect, track, and predict the behaviour of surrounding obstacles. Traditional perception pipelines that rely on modular architectures tend to suffer from cumulative errors and latency. Instance Prediction models provide a unified solution, performing Bird's-Eye-View segmentation and motion estimation across current and future frames using information directly obtained from different sensors. However, a key challenge in these models lies in the effective processing of the dense spatial and temporal information inherent in dynamic driving environments. This level of complexity demands architectures capable of capturing fine-grained motion patterns and long-range dependencies without compromising real-time performance. We introduce BEVPredFormer, a novel camera-only architecture for BEV instance prediction that uses attention-based temporal processing to improve temporal and spatial comprehension of the scene and relies on an attention-based 3D projection of the camera information. BEVPredFormer employs a recurrent-free design that incorporates gated transformer layers, divided spatio-temporal attention mechanisms, and multi-scale head tasks. Additionally, we incorporate a difference-guided feature extraction module that enhances temporal representations. Extensive ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each architectural component. When evaluated on the nuScenes dataset, BEVPredFormer was on par or surpassed State-Of-The-Art methods, highlighting its potential for robust and efficient Autonomous Driving perception.

AI.tldr: BEVPredFormer is a camera-only BEV instance prediction model using gated transformers and divided spatio-temporal attention with a difference-guided module; it achieves competitive results on nuScenes without recurrence.
AI.motivation: Traditional modular perception pipelines accumulate errors and latency; dynamic driving scenes require efficient processing of dense spatial-temporal information; a unified, camera-only BEV approach can improve robustness and efficiency.
AI.method: A recurrent-free architecture with gated transformer layers, divided spatio-temporal attention, attention-based 3D projection of camera data, multi-scale head tasks, and a difference-guided feature extraction module.
AI.result: On nuScenes, BEVPredFormer matches or exceeds state-of-the-art methods; extensive ablations validate each component's contribution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method demonstrates effective, efficient camera-only BEV instance prediction for autonomous driving, with strong temporal-spatial modeling and potential for real-time deployment.

title: HyperFitS -- Hypernetwork Fitting Spectra for metabolic quantification of ${}^1$H MR spectroscopic imaging
summary: Purpose: Proton magnetic resonance spectroscopic imaging ($^1$H MRSI) enables the mapping of whole-brain metabolites concentrations in-vivo. However, a long-standing problem for its clinical applicability is the metabolic quantification, which can require extensive time for spectral fitting. Recently, deep learning methods have been able to provide whole-brain metabolic quantification in only a few seconds. However, neural network implementations often lack configurability and require retraining to change predefined parameter settings. Methods: We introduce HyperFitS, a hypernetwork for spectral fitting for metabolite quantification in whole-brain $^1$H MRSI that flexibly adapts to a broad range of baseline corrections and water suppression factors. Metabolite maps of human subjects acquired at 3T and 7T with isotropic resolutions of 10 mm, 3.4 mm and 2 mm by water-suppressed and water-unsuppressed MRSI were quantified with HyperFitS and compared to conventional LCModel fitting. Results: Metabolic maps show a substantial agreement between the new and gold-standard methods, with significantly faster fitting times by HyperFitS. Quantitative results further highlight the impact of baseline parametrization on metabolic quantification, which can alter results by up to 30%. Conclusion: HyperFitS shows strong agreement with state-of-the-art conventional methods, while reducing processing times from hours to a few seconds. Compared to prior deep learning based spectral fitting methods, HyperFitS enables a wide range of configurability and can adapt to data quality acquired with multiple protocols and field strengths without retraining.

AI.tldr: HyperFitS uses a hypernetwork to enable configurable, rapid whole-brain 1H MRSI spectral fitting for metabolite quantification, achieving results comparable to LCModel with substantial speed gains and cross-protocol adaptability.
AI.motivation: DL-based spectral fitting for MRSI can be fast but often lacks configurability and requires retraining for different baselines, water suppression, and protocols; a flexible model is needed for clinical applicability across field strengths and resolutions.
AI.method: Introduce HyperFitS, a hypernetwork for spectral fitting in whole-brain 1H MRSI. It flexibly adapts to baseline corrections and water suppression factors. Applied to human brain MRSI data acquired at 3T and 7T with isotropic resolutions of 10 mm, 3.4 mm, and 2 mm, and with both water-suppressed and water-unsuppressed acquisitions. Quantification performed and compared to conventional LCModel fitting.
AI.result: Metabolic maps show substantial agreement with LCModel; HyperFitS achieves significantly faster fitting times. Baseline parametrization can impact quantification by up to ~30% depending on configuration.
AI.conclusion: HyperFitS provides strong agreement with LCModel while dramatically reducing processing time (hours to seconds) and offering greater configurability than previous DL-based methods, enabling adaptation to data quality across protocols and field strengths without retraining.

title: PolyReal: A Benchmark for Real-World Polymer Science Workflows
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in general domains but struggle with complex, real-world science. We posit that polymer science, an interdisciplinary field spanning chemistry, physics, biology, and engineering, is an ideal high-stakes testbed due to its diverse multimodal data. Yet, existing benchmarks related to polymer science largely overlook real-world workflows, limiting their practical utility and failing to systematically evaluate MLLMs across the full, practice-grounded lifecycle of experimentation. We introduce PolyReal, a novel multimodal benchmark grounded in real-world scientific practices to evaluate MLLMs on the full lifecycle of polymer experimentation. It covers five critical capabilities: (1) foundational knowledge application; (2) lab safety analysis; (3) experiment mechanism reasoning; (4) raw data extraction; and (5) performance & application exploration. Our evaluation of leading MLLMs on PolyReal reveals a capability imbalance. While models perform well on knowledge-intensive reasoning (e.g., Experiment Mechanism Reasoning), they drop sharply on practice-based tasks (e.g., Lab Safety Analysis and Raw Data Extraction). This exposes a severe gap between abstract scientific knowledge and its practical, context-dependent application, showing that these real-world tasks remain challenging for MLLMs. Thus, PolyReal helps address this evaluation gap and provides a practical benchmark for assessing AI systems in real-world scientific workflows.

AI.tldr: PolyReal introduces a multimodal benchmark to evaluate MLLMs across the full polymer experimentation lifecycle, revealing a capability imbalance: models handle knowledge-based reasoning well but struggle with practice-based tasks like lab safety and raw data extraction.
AI.motivation: To test MLLMs on real-world scientific workflows by focusing on polymer science, an interdisciplinary, data-rich domain, where successful deployment requires both abstract reasoning and practical, context-aware capabilities.
AI.method: Propose PolyReal with five capabilities (foundational knowledge application, lab safety analysis, experiment mechanism reasoning, raw data extraction, performance & application exploration) and evaluate leading MLLMs on these tasks to assess their alignment with real-world experiments.
AI.result: Foundational knowledge and mechanism reasoning are relatively strong; lab safety analysis and raw data extraction show sharp performance drops, revealing a gap between abstract knowledge and practical application in real-world workflows.
AI.conclusion: PolyReal provides a practical, lifecycle-grounded benchmark for evaluating MLLMs in real-world scientific workflows and highlights the need to enhance models' practical capabilities beyond theoretical knowledge.

title: DSBD: Dual-Aligned Structural Basis Distillation for Graph Domain Adaptation
summary: Graph domain adaptation (GDA) aims to transfer knowledge from a labeled source graph to an unlabeled target graph under distribution shifts. However, existing methods are largely feature-centric and overlook structural discrepancies, which become particularly detrimental under significant topology shifts. Such discrepancies alter both geometric relationships and spectral properties, leading to unreliable transfer of graph neural networks (GNNs). To address this limitation, we propose Dual-Aligned Structural Basis Distillation (DSBD) for GDA, a novel framework that explicitly models and adapts cross-domain structural variation. DSBD constructs a differentiable structural basis by synthesizing continuous probabilistic prototype graphs, enabling gradient-based optimization over graph topology. The basis is learned under source-domain supervision to preserve semantic discriminability, while being explicitly aligned to the target domain through a dual-alignment objective. Specifically, geometric consistency is enforced via permutation-invariant topological moment matching, and spectral consistency is achieved through Dirichlet energy calibration, jointly capturing structural characteristics across domains. Furthermore, we introduce a decoupled inference paradigm that mitigates source-specific structural bias by training a new GNN on the distilled structural basis. Extensive experiments on graph and image benchmarks demonstrate that DSBD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: DSBD introduces a dual-aligned structural basis distillation framework for graph domain adaptation, explicitly modeling cross-domain topology shifts via differentiable probabilistic prototype graphs, dual geometric-spectral alignment, and decoupled inference for robust transfer.
AI.motivation: GDA is hampered by topology shifts that distort geometric and spectral graph properties; existing feature-centric methods fail to adapt structural information across domains.
AI.method: Constructs a differentiable structural basis by synthesizing continuous probabilistic prototype graphs, learned with source supervision to preserve discriminability; aligns to target via a dual-alignment objective combining permutation-invariant topological moment matching (geometric) and Dirichlet energy calibration (spectral); proposes decoupled inference by training a new GNN on the distilled basis.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on graph and image benchmarks show DSBD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: DSBD provides an effective mechanism to model and adapt cross-domain structural variation for GDA, offering gradient-based topology optimization, robust cross-domain alignment, and a decoupled inference paradigm that reduces source-specific bias.

title: Modality-Specific Hierarchical Enhancement for RGB-D Camouflaged Object Detection
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures, including supplementary material. Accepted by IEEE ICME 2026
summary: Camouflaged object detection (COD) is challenging due to high target-background similarity, and recent methods address this by complementarily using RGB-D texture and geometry cues. However, RGB-D COD methods still underutilize modality-specific cues, which limits fusion quality. We believe this is because RGB and depth features are fused directly after backbone extraction without modality-specific enhancement. To address this limitation, we propose MHENet, an RGB-D COD framework that performs modality-specific hierarchical enhancement and adaptive fusion of RGB and depth features. Specifically, we introduce a Texture Hierarchical Enhancement Module (THEM) to amplify subtle texture variations by extracting high-frequency information and a Geometry Hierarchical Enhancement Module (GHEM) to enhance geometric structures via learnable gradient extraction, while preserving cross-scale semantic consistency. Finally, an Adaptive Dynamic Fusion Module (ADFM) adaptively fuses the enhanced texture and geometry features with spatially varying weights. Experiments on four benchmarks demonstrate that MHENet surpasses 16 state-of-the-art methods qualitatively and quantitatively. Code is available at https://github.com/afdsgh/MHENet.

AI.tldr: MHENet improves RGB-D camouflaged object detection by applying modality-specific hierarchical enhancement for texture and geometry features (THEM and GHEM) and an adaptive dynamic fusion (ADFM) to fuse enhanced features, achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: RGB and depth features are often fused directly after backbone extraction, which underutilizes modality-specific cues and limits fusion quality.
AI.method: Introduce Texture Hierarchical Enhancement Module (THEM) to amplify high-frequency texture information, Geometry Hierarchical Enhancement Module (GHEM) to enhance geometric structures via learnable gradient extraction, and Adaptive Dynamic Fusion Module (ADFM) to fuse enhanced texture and geometry features with spatially varying weights, preserving cross-scale semantic consistency.
AI.result: MHENet surpasses 16 state-of-the-art methods on four COD benchmarks, with qualitative and quantitative improvements; code is provided at the authors' GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Modality-specific hierarchical enhancement plus adaptive fusion effectively boosts RGB-D camouflaged object detection performance, demonstrating strong generalization across benchmarks.

title: Understanding the Role of Hallucination in Reinforcement Post-Training of Multimodal Reasoning Models
summary: The recent success of reinforcement learning (RL) in large reasoning models has inspired the growing adoption of RL for post-training Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to enhance their visual reasoning capabilities. Although many studies have reported improved performance, it remains unclear whether RL training truly enables models to learn from visual information. In this work, we propose the Hallucination-as-Cue Framework, an analytical framework designed to investigate the effects of RL-based post-training on multimodal reasoning models from the perspective of model hallucination. Specifically, we introduce hallucination-inductive, modality-specific corruptions that remove or replace essential information required to derive correct answers, thereby forcing the model to reason by hallucination. By applying these corruptions during both training and evaluation, our framework provides a unique perspective for diagnosing RL training dynamics and understanding the intrinsic properties of datasets. Through extensive experiments and analyses across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks, we reveal that the role of model hallucination for RL-training is more significant than previously recognized. For instance, we find that RL post-training under purely hallucination-inductive settings can still significantly improve models' reasoning performance, and in some cases even outperform standard training. These findings challenge prevailing assumptions about MLLM reasoning training and motivate the development of more modality-aware RL-based training designs.

AI.tldr: RL post-training can improve multimodal reasoning even when trained with and evaluated under hallucination-inducing corruptions; Hallucination-as-Cue Framework reveals that model hallucination plays a significant (and sometimes dominant) role in RL-based improvement, challenging the assumption that purely grounded visual information is learned.
AI.motivation: To determine whether RL-based post-training truly enables MLLMs to learn from visual information, and to understand how hallucination interacts with RL training dynamics and dataset properties in multimodal reasoning.
AI.method: Propose Hallucination-as-Cue Framework. Introduce hallucination-inductive, modality-specific corruptions that remove or replace essential information. Apply these corruptions during training and evaluation to diagnose RL dynamics and dataset properties. Conduct extensive experiments across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks to assess the role of hallucination in RL training.
AI.result: Hallucination plays a more significant role in RL training than previously recognized. RL post-training under hallucination-inductive settings can significantly boost reasoning performance and, in some cases, surpass standard training.
AI.conclusion: These findings challenge prevailing assumptions about MLLM reasoning training and motivate developing modality-aware RL-based training designs and more robust evaluation that accounts for hallucination.

title: MMTalker: Multiresolution 3D Talking Head Synthesis with Multimodal Feature Fusion
summary:
Speech-driven three-dimensional (3D) facial animation synthesis aims to build a mapping from one-dimensional (1D) speech signals to time-varying 3D facial motion signals. Current methods still face challenges in maintaining lip-sync accuracy and producing realistic facial expressions, primarily due to the highly ill-posed nature of this cross-modal mapping. In this paper, we introduce a novel 3D audio-driven facial animation synthesis method through multi-resolution representation and multi-modal feature fusion, called MMTalker which can accurately reconstruct the rich details of 3D facial motion.
  We first achieve the continuous representation of 3D face with details by mesh parameterization and non-uniform differentiable sampling. The mesh parameterization technique establishes the correspondence between UV plane and 3D facial mesh and is used to offer ground truth for the continuous learning. Differentiable non-uniform sampling enables precise facial detail acquisition by setting learnable sampling probability in each triangular face. Next, we employ residual graph convolutional network and dual cross-attention mechanism to extract discriminative facial motion feature from multiple input modalities. This proposed multimodal fusion strategy takes full use of the hierarchical features of speech and the explicit spatiotemporal geometric features of facial mesh. Finally, a lightweight regression network predicts the vertex-wise geometric displacements of the synthesized talking face by jointly processing the sampled points in the canonical UV space and the encoded facial motion features.
  Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that significant improvements are achieved over state-of-the-art methods, especially in the synchronization accuracy of lip and eye movements.

AI.tldr: MMTalker: a multi-resolution 3D audio-driven facial animation method that uses mesh UV parameterization, differentiable non-uniform sampling, residual graph convolutions and dual cross-attention to fuse speech with mesh features, achieving superior lip-sync and eye motion realism.
AI.motivation: To overcome the ill-posed cross-modal mapping from 1D speech to time-varying 3D facial motion and to improve lip-sync accuracy and realism by leveraging continuous mesh representation and multi-modal feature fusion.
AI.method: Employs mesh parameterization to establish UV-3D correspondence; differentiable non-uniform sampling with learnable per-face probabilities; residual graph convolutional networks and dual cross-attention to extract and fuse discriminative motion features from speech and explicit mesh geometry; a lightweight regression network predicts vertex-wise displacements by jointly processing sampled points in UV space and encoded facial motion features.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in lip-sync, with notable enhancements in eye movement synchronization, validated through comprehensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: MMTalker delivers detailed, accurate 3D facial motion from speech through a comprehensive framework of continuous mesh representation and multimodal fusion, achieving robust lip-sync and expressive facial dynamics.

title: Learning from Synthetic Data via Provenance-Based Input Gradient Guidance
summary: Learning methods using synthetic data have attracted attention as an effective approach for increasing the diversity of training data while reducing collection costs, thereby improving the robustness of model discrimination. However, many existing methods improve robustness only indirectly through the diversification of training samples and do not explicitly teach the model which regions in the input space truly contribute to discrimination; consequently, the model may learn spurious correlations caused by synthesis biases and artifacts. Motivated by this limitation, this paper proposes a learning framework that uses provenance information obtained during the training data synthesis process, indicating whether each region in the input space originates from the target object, as an auxiliary supervisory signal to promote the acquisition of representations focused on target regions. Specifically, input gradients are decomposed based on information about target and non-target regions during synthesis, and input gradient guidance is introduced to suppress gradients over non-target regions. This suppresses the model's reliance on non-target regions and directly promotes the learning of discriminative representations for target regions. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of the proposed method across multiple tasks and modalities, including weakly supervised object localization, spatio-temporal action localization, and image classification.

AI.tldr: Provenance-guided input-gradient suppression during training with synthetic data to focus learning on target regions, improving discrimination; validated across weakly supervised object localization, spatio-temporal action localization, and image classification.
AI.motivation: Synthetic data increases training diversity but can induce spurious correlations due to synthesis biases; explicit guidance is needed to focus representations on target regions.
AI.method: During training, decompose input gradients using synthesis provenance (target vs non-target regions) and apply gradient guidance to suppress non-target-region gradients; use provenance as an auxiliary supervisory signal to train discriminative representations for target regions.
AI.result: Empirical validation shows effectiveness and generality across multiple tasks/modalities, including weakly supervised object localization, spatio-temporal action localization, and image classification.
AI.conclusion: Provenance-based gradient suppression directly promotes learning of discriminative representations for target regions, yielding more robust and generalizable models across tasks.

title: Reflective Context Learning: Studying the Optimization Primitives of Context Space
comment: Under review at COLM. Github: https://github.com/nvassilyev/RCL
summary: Generally capable agents must learn from experience in ways that generalize across tasks and environments. The fundamental problems of learning, including credit assignment, overfitting, forgetting, local optima, and high-variance learning signals, persist whether the learned object lies in parameter space or context space. While these challenges are well understood in classical machine learning optimization, they remain underexplored in context space, leading current methods to be fragmented and ad hoc. We present Reflective Context Learning (RCL), a unified framework for agents that learn through repeated interaction, reflection on behavior and failure modes, and iterative updates to context. In RCL, reflection converts trajectories and current context into a directional update signal analogous to gradients, while mutation applies that signal to improve future behavior in context space. We recast recent context-optimization approaches as instances of this shared learning problem and systematically extend them with classical optimization primitives, including batching, improved credit-assignment signal, auxiliary losses, failure replay, and grouped rollouts for variance reduction. On AppWorld, BrowseComp+, and RewardBench2, these primitives improve over strong baselines, with their relative importance shifting across task regimes. We further analyze robustness to initialization, the effects of batch size, sampling and curriculum strategy, optimizer-state variants, and the impact of allocating stronger or weaker models to different optimization components. Our results suggest that learning through context updates should be treated not as a set of isolated algorithms, but as an optimization problem whose mechanisms can be studied systematically and improved through transferable principles.

AI.tldr: Introduces Reflective Context Learning (RCL), a unified framework for context-space optimization in agents. It uses reflection to generate directional updates from trajectories and context, and mutation to apply those updates. Reframes recent context-optimization methods as instances of a shared problem and augments them with classical optimization primitives (batching, improved credit signals, auxiliary losses, failure replay, grouped rollouts). Empirically improves baselines on AppWorld, BrowseComp+, and RewardBench2; analyzes robustness and resource allocation, arguing for treating context updates as transferable optimization principles.
AI.motivation: Context-space learning faces core ML challenges—credit assignment, overfitting, forgetting, local optima, and high-variance signals—that persist irrespective of whether learning happens in parameter space or context space. The paper argues these challenges are underexplored in context space and that a unified, principled framework is needed to generalize across tasks/environments.
AI.method: Proposes Reflective Context Learning (RCL) where reflection converts trajectories and current context into a directional update signal analogous to gradients, and mutation applies that signal to improve future behavior in context space. Recasts recent context-optimization approaches as instances of a shared learning problem and augments them with classical optimization primitives: batching, improved credit-assignment signal, auxiliary losses, failure replay, and grouped rollouts for variance reduction. Empirically evaluates on AppWorld, BrowseComp+, and RewardBench2, and analyzes robustness to initialization, batch size, sampling/curriculum strategies, optimizer-state variants, and the impact of allocating stronger/weaker models to different optimization components.
AI.result: The proposed primitives yield improvements over strong baselines on the tested environments, with relative importance of components varying by task regime. Robustness analyses indicate sensitivity to initialization, batch size, sampling/curriculum choices, and model allocation strategies, but overall support treating context updates as a transferable optimization problem rather than a collection of isolated algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Learning through context updates should be treated as an optimization problem whose mechanisms can be studied and improved via transferable, classical optimization principles. The framework unifies existing context-optimization methods under RCL and provides a principled path for systematic improvements.

title: CrossWeaver: Cross-modal Weaving for Arbitrary-Modality Semantic Segmentation
summary: Multimodal semantic segmentation has shown great potential in leveraging complementary information across diverse sensing modalities. However, existing approaches often rely on carefully designed fusion strategies that either use modality-specific adaptations or rely on loosely coupled interactions, thereby limiting flexibility and resulting in less effective cross-modal coordination. Moreover, these methods often struggle to balance efficient information exchange with preserving the unique characteristics of each modality across different modality combinations. To address these challenges, we propose CrossWeaver, a simple yet effective multimodal fusion framework for arbitrary-modality semantic segmentation. Its core is a Modality Interaction Block (MIB), which enables selective and reliability-aware cross-modal interaction within the encoder, while a lightweight Seam-Aligned Fusion (SAF) module further aggregates the enhanced features. Extensive experiments on multiple multimodal semantic segmentation benchmarks demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance with minimal additional parameters and strong generalization to unseen modality combinations.

AI.tldr: CrossWeaver presents a simple, flexible fusion framework for arbitrary-modality semantic segmentation, using a Modality Interaction Block (MIB) for selective, reliability-aware cross-modal interaction in the encoder and a lightweight Seam-Aligned Fusion (SAF) to aggregate features, achieving state-of-the-art results with few extra parameters and strong generalization to unseen modality combinations.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal fusion approaches are either modality-specific or rely on loosely coupled interactions, which limits cross-modal coordination and fails to balance information exchange with preserving each modality's distinctive characteristics across diverse modality pairs.
AI.method: Introduce Modality Interaction Block (MIB) for selective, reliability-aware cross-modal interaction within the encoder, complemented by a lightweight Seam-Aligned Fusion (SAF) module to aggregate enhanced features. Designed to work with arbitrary modalities without extensive modality-specific adaptations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple multimodal semantic segmentation benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance with minimal additional parameters and strong generalization to unseen modality combinations.
AI.conclusion: CrossWeaver offers a simple yet effective fusion framework for arbitrary modalities, achieving strong cross-modal coordination and efficiency, and promising broad applicability across multimodal segmentation tasks.

title: Gradient Boosting within a Single Attention Layer
summary: Transformer attention computes a single softmax-weighted average over values -- a one-pass estimate that cannot correct its own errors. We introduce \emph{gradient-boosted attention}, which applies the principle of gradient boosting \emph{within} a single attention layer: a second attention pass, with its own learned projections, attends to the prediction error of the first and applies a gated correction. Under a squared reconstruction objective, the construction maps onto Friedman's gradient boosting machine, with each attention pass as a base learner and the per-dimension gate as the shrinkage parameter. We show that a single Hopfield-style update erases all query information orthogonal to the stored-pattern subspace, and that further iteration under local contraction can collapse distinct queries in the same region to the same fixed point. We also show that separate projections for the correction pass can recover residual information inaccessible to the shared-projection approach of Tukey's twicing. On a 10M-token subset of WikiText-103, gradient-boosted attention achieves a test perplexity of $67.9$ compared to $72.2$ for standard attention, $69.6$ for Twicing Attention, and $69.0$ for a parameter-matched wider baseline, with two rounds capturing most of the benefit.

AI.tldr: Gradient-boosted attention: within a single layer, a second attention pass uses gradient-boosting to correct the first-pass errors via a gated correction; achieves perplexity improvement on WikiText-103 (67.9 vs 72.2 standard).
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of standard attention being a single-pass, error-prone estimate that cannot self-correct within the layer.
AI.method: Introduce a second attention pass with separate learned projections that attends to the first-pass prediction error and applies a gated correction. Under a squared reconstruction objective, maps to Friedman's gradient boosting; each pass is a base learner with per-dimension shrinkage; notes include Hopfield-style update effects and the potential benefits of separate projections for the correction pass.
AI.result: Empirical improvement on a 10M-token subset of WikiText-103: perplexity 67.9 with gradient-boosted attention vs 72.2 for standard attention; 69.6 for Twicing Attention; 69.0 for a wider baseline; two rounds capture most benefits.
AI.conclusion: A principled in-layer refinement method using gradient boosting ideas that reduces error and enhances language-modeling performance with modest additional computation; separate projection paths for correction further enhance residual information recovery.

title: Collaborative Multi-Mode Pruning for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have advanced rapidly within the unified Transformer architecture, yet their deployment on resource-constrained devices remains challenging due to high computational complexity. While pruning has emerged as an effective technique for compressing VLMs, existing approaches predominantly focus on a single mode by pruning either parameters or tokens, neglecting fully exploring the inherent redundancy in each mode, which leads to substantial performance degradation at high pruning ratios. To address the above limitations, we propose Collaborative Multi-Mode Pruning (CoMP), a novel framework tailored for VLMs by performing joint parameter and token pruning. Specifically, we first design a Collaborative Importance Metric (CIM) that investigates the mutual interference between the coupled parameters and tokens. It incorporates distinct significance of tokens into the computation of parameter importance scores, while simultaneously mitigating the affect of pruned parameters on token importance scores. Moreover, we develop a Multi-Mode Pruning Strategy (MPS) that decomposes the overall pruning process into a sequence of pruning stages, while in each stage we estimate the priory of different pruning modes based on their pruning cost and adaptively shift to the optimal one. Additionally, MPS integrates the historical cost and random exploration, in order to achieve a stable pruning process and avoid local optimum. Extensive experiments across various vision-language tasks and models demonstrate that our method effectively promotes the performance under high pruning ratios by comparing to the state-of-the-art approaches. The source code is available at https://github.com/Wuzimeng/CoMP.git.

AI.tldr: CoMP introduces Collaborative Multi-Mode Pruning for vision-language models, jointly pruning parameters and tokens with a Collaborative Importance Metric and a Multi-Mode Pruning Strategy, achieving better retention under high pruning ratios than single-mode pruning methods; code released at the project repository.
AI.motivation: VLMs are large and resource-hungry, making deployment on constrained devices challenging. Existing pruning methods focus on a single mode (parameters or tokens) and fail to exploit redundancy across both modes, leading to significant performance loss at high pruning ratios.
AI.method: CIM calculates parameter importance while accounting for the influence of token pruning, and tokens’ importance while accounting for pruned parameters; MPS decomposes pruning into stages, estimating priors for different modes based on pruning cost and adaptively switching to the most promising mode, while incorporating historical cost and random exploration to stabilize optimization. The framework jointly prunes parameters and tokens in a staged, interdependent manner.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse vision-language tasks and models show that CoMP maintains higher performance at high pruning ratios compared with state-of-the-art single-mode pruning methods, demonstrating robustness and effectiveness across tasks and architectures; source code is provided at the given GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: CoMP effectively leverages the redundancy in both parameters and tokens and provides a practical, generalizable pruning framework for VLMs, capable of sustaining performance under aggressive pruning while reducing resource requirements.

title: Real-Time Surrogate Modeling for Personalized Blood Flow Prediction and Hemodynamic Analysis
summary: Cardiovascular modeling has rapidly advanced over the past few decades due to the rising needs for health tracking and early detection of cardiovascular diseases. While 1-D arterial models offer an attractive compromise between computational efficiency and solution fidelity, their application on large populations or for generating large \emph{in silico} cohorts remains challenging. Certain hemodynamic parameters like the terminal resistance/compliance, are difficult to clinically estimate and often yield non-physiological hemodynamics when sampled naively, resulting in large portions of simulated datasets to be discarded. In this work, we present a systematic framework for training machine learning (ML) models, capable of instantaneous hemodynamic prediction and parameter estimation. We initially start with generating a parametric virtual cohort of patients which is based on the multivariate correlations observed in the large Asklepios clinical dataset, ensuring that physiological parameter distributions are respected. We then train a deep neural surrogate model, able to predict patient-specific arterial pressure and cardiac output (CO), enabling rapid a~priori screening of input parameters. This allows for immediate rejection of non-physiological combinations and drastically reduces the cost of targeted synthetic dataset generation (e.g. hypertensive groups). The model also provides a principled means of sampling the terminal resistance to minimize the uncertainties of unmeasurable parameters. Moreover, by assessing the model's predictive performance we determine the theoretical information which suffices for solving the inverse problem of estimating the CO. Finally, we apply the surrogate on a clinical dataset for the estimation of central aortic hemodynamics i.e. the CO and aortic systolic blood pressure (cSBP).

AI.tldr: A deep-learning surrogate framework for 1-D arterial models that generates physiologically plausible virtual cohorts, enables instantaneous predictions of arterial pressure and cardiac output, and facilitates parameter estimation, with principled sampling of terminal resistance and validation on central aortic hemodynamics.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of generating large, physiologically realistic 1-D arterial model datasets for population-scale studies. Naive sampling of hard-to-estimate parameters (e.g., terminal resistance/compliance) yields non-physiological results and high discard rates; there is a need for fast, data-driven surrogates that respect physiological correlations.
AI.method: Create a parametric virtual cohort anchored to multivariate correlations observed in the Asklepios clinical dataset. Train a deep neural surrogate to predict patient-specific arterial pressure and cardiac output. Implement a priori screening to reject input combinations that produce non-physiological hemodynamics. Develop a principled sampling strategy for terminal resistance to reduce uncertainty. Analyze the information content required to solve the inverse problem of CO estimation. Validate by applying the surrogate to a clinical dataset to estimate central aortic hemodynamics (CO and cSBP).
AI.result: The surrogate enables instantaneous predictions and dramatically reduces the cost of targeted synthetic data generation. It filters out non-physiological inputs, provides principled terminal-resistance sampling to minimize uncertainty, and quantifies the information needed for CO estimation. It is applied to clinical data to estimate central aortic hemodynamics (CO and cSBP).
AI.conclusion: ML surrogates, informed by population correlations and a priori screening, offer a scalable route to large, physiologically consistent in silico cohorts and rapid, reliable estimation of central hemodynamics in clinical data.

title: Visual Prototype Conditioned Focal Region Generation for UAV-Based Object Detection
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) based object detection is a critical but challenging task, when applied in dynamically changing scenarios with limited annotated training data. Layout-to-image generation approaches have proved effective in promoting detection accuracy by synthesizing labeled images based on diffusion models. However, they suffer from frequently producing artifacts, especially near layout boundaries of tiny objects, thus substantially limiting their performance. To address these issues, we propose UAVGen, a novel layout-to-image generation framework tailored for UAV-based object detection. Specifically, UAVGen designs a Visual Prototype Conditioned Diffusion Model (VPC-DM) that constructs representative instances for each class and integrates them into latent embeddings for high-fidelity object generation. Moreover, a Focal Region Enhanced Data Pipeline (FRE-DP) is introduced to emphasize object-concentrated foreground regions in synthesis, combined with a label refinement to correct missing, extra and misaligned generations. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, and consistently promotes accuracy when integrated with distinct detectors. The source code is available at https://github.com/Sirius-Li/UAVGen.

AI.tldr: UAVGen introduces a layout-to-image diffusion framework for UAV object detection, using a Visual Prototype Conditioned Diffusion Model (VPC-DM) and a Focal Region Enhanced Data Pipeline (FRE-DP) to generate high-fidelity, class-representative images and refine labels, improving detection under limited data and achieving strong, detector-agnostic gains; code released.
AI.motivation: Address UAV object detection with scarce annotated data and artifacts in layout-to-image diffusion, especially around tiny object boundaries; existing diffusion-based synthesis degrades detector performance, requiring higher-fidelity, region-focused generation.
AI.method: VPC-DM constructs representative class prototypes and embeds them into latent diffusion processes to produce faithful object instances; FRE-DP focuses synthesis on foreground regions to reduce background artifacts; a label refinement step corrects missing, extra, and misaligned generations to ensure annotation-consistent data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the proposed UAVGen method substantially outperforms state-of-the-art layout-to-image approaches and yields consistent accuracy gains across different detectors; the authors release source code at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: UAVGen effectively mitigates artifacts and data scarcity in UAV-based object detection by integrating prototype-conditioned diffusion with region-focused data synthesis and label refinement, delivering robust synthetic data for detector training.

title: Hierarchical Planning with Latent World Models
summary: Model predictive control (MPC) with learned world models has emerged as a promising paradigm for embodied control, particularly for its ability to generalize zero-shot when deployed in new environments. However, learned world models often struggle with long-horizon control due to the accumulation of prediction errors and the exponentially growing search space. In this work, we address these challenges by learning latent world models at multiple temporal scales and performing hierarchical planning across these scales, enabling long-horizon reasoning while substantially reducing inference-time planning complexity.
