Our approach serves as a modular planning abstraction that applies across diverse latent world-model architectures and domains. We demonstrate that this hierarchical approach enables zero-shot control on real-world non-greedy robotic tasks, achieving a 70% success rate on pick-&-place using only a final goal specification, compared to 0% for a single-level world model. In addition, across physics-based simulated environments including push manipulation and maze navigation, hierarchical planning achieves higher success while requiring up to 4x less planning-time compute.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical planning over latent world models at multiple temporal scales improves long-horizon control, enabling zero-shot real-world tasks and reduced planning time in simulations.
AI.motivation: Overcome accumulation of prediction errors and combinatorial planning cost in single-scale learned world models for long-horizon control; achieve robust zero-shot deployment across varied tasks and domains.
AI.method: Train latent world models at multiple temporal scales and perform hierarchical planning across scales; provide a modular planning abstraction compatible with various latent models and domains; apply to real-world robotic pick-and-place and physics-based simulations (push, maze).
AI.result: Zero-shot pick-and-place with only a final goal spec achieves 70% success (vs 0% with single-level models); in simulated tasks, hierarchical planning yields higher success with up to 4x reduction in planning-time compute.
AI.conclusion: Multi-scale latent models with hierarchical planning significantly enhance long-horizon control and planning efficiency, offering a modular abstraction that generalizes across architectures and domains and improves real-world robotic task success.

title: Exploring Motion-Language Alignment for Text-driven Motion Generation
summary: Text-driven human motion generation aims to synthesize realistic motion sequences that follow textual descriptions. Despite recent advances, accurately aligning motion dynamics with textual semantics remains a fundamental challenge. In this paper, we revisit text-to-motion generation from the perspective of motion-language alignment and propose MLA-Gen, a framework that integrates global motion priors with fine-grained local conditioning. This design enables the model to capture common motion patterns, while establishing detailed alignment between texts and motions. Furthermore, we identify a previously overlooked attention sink phenomenon in human motion generation, where attention disproportionately concentrates on the start text token, limiting the utilization of informative textual cues and leading to degraded semantic grounding. To analyze this issue, we introduce SinkRatio, a metric for measuring attention concentration, and develop alignment-aware masking and control strategies to regulate attention during generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently improves both motion quality and motion-language alignment over strong baselines. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A text-to-motion framework (MLA-Gen) combines global motion priors with fine-grained local conditioning to improve motion quality and alignment with text; it reveals and mitigates an attention sink phenomenon using SinkRatio, with alignment-aware masking to regulate attention; experiments show consistent gains over baselines; code will be released upon acceptance.
AI.motivation: Despite progress, aligning motion dynamics with textual semantics remains difficult; existing methods struggle to utilize informative textual cues due to misallocation of attention and lack of strong global priors.
AI.method: MLA-Gen integrates global motion priors with local conditioning, and identifies an attention sink phenomenon. It introduces SinkRatio to measure attention concentration, and develops alignment-aware masking and control strategies to regulate attention during generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in motion quality and motion-language alignment over strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework advances text-to-motion generation by aligning global and local cues and by regulating attention distribution, leading to improved semantic grounding; code will be released after acceptance.

title: Enhancing Robustness of Federated Learning via Server Learning
summary: This paper explores the use of server learning for enhancing the robustness of federated learning against malicious attacks even when clients' training data are not independent and identically distributed. We propose a heuristic algorithm that uses server learning and client update filtering in combination with geometric median aggregation. We demonstrate via experiments that this approach can achieve significant improvement in model accuracy even when the fraction of malicious clients is high, even more than $50\%$ in some cases, and the dataset utilized by the server is small and could be synthetic with its distribution not necessarily close to that of the clients' aggregated data.

AI.tldr: A heuristic defense for Byzantine-robust federated learning using server-side learning, client update filtering, and geometric median aggregation, achieving higher accuracy under non-IID data and many malicious clients, even with a small/synthetic server dataset.
AI.motivation: Federated learning is vulnerable to malicious clients (Byzantine faults). Non-IID data further complicates robust aggregation. When server data is scarce or synthetic, standard robust aggregations may fail; hence a new heuristic combining server-side learning with filtering and geometric median is proposed.
AI.method: Propose a heuristic algorithm that integrates server learning, client update filtering, and geometric median aggregation to defend against Byzantine clients in FL with non-IID data.
AI.result: Experimental results show substantial accuracy gains even when the malicious client fraction exceeds 50%, and with a small synthetic server dataset whose distribution need not match client data.
AI.conclusion: Server-learning integrated with update filtering and geometric-median aggregation provides robust FL performance under heavy Byzantine presence and non-IID data, even with limited server-side data.

title: A Data-Centric Vision Transformer Baseline for SAR Sea Ice Classification
summary: Accurate and automated sea ice classification is important for climate monitoring and maritime safety in the Arctic. While Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) is the operational standard because of its all-weather capability, it remains challenging to distinguish morphologically similar ice classes under severe class imbalance. Rather than claiming a fully validated multimodal system, this paper establishes a trustworthy SAR only baseline that future fusion work can build upon. Using the AI4Arctic/ASIP Sea Ice Dataset (v2), which contains 461 Sentinel-1 scenes matched with expert ice charts, we combine full-resolution Sentinel-1 Extra Wide inputs, leakage-aware stratified patch splitting, SIGRID-3 stage-of-development labels, and training-set normalization to evaluate Vision Transformer baselines. We compare ViT-Base models trained with cross entropy and weighted cross-entropy against a ViT-Large model trained with focal loss. Among the tested configurations, ViT-Large with focal loss achieves 69.6% held-out accuracy, 68.8% weighted F1, and 83.9% precision on the minority Multi-Year Ice class. These results show that focal-loss training offers a more useful precision-recall trade-off than weighted cross-entropy for rare ice classes and establishes a cleaner baseline for future multimodal fusion with optical, thermal, or meteorological data.

AI.tldr: A trustworthy SAR-only baseline using ViT on the AI4Arctic/ASIP Sea Ice Dataset v2, showing focal loss improves minority-class precision and overall metrics, enabling future multimodal fusion.
AI.motivation: Sea ice classification suffers from severe class imbalance and relies on all-weather SAR data; the paper seeks a robust, SAR-only baseline to anchor future multimodal fusion efforts.
AI.method: Full-resolution Sentinel-1 Extra Wide inputs; leakage-aware stratified patch splitting; SIGRID-3 stage-of-development labels; training-set normalization; Vision Transformer baselines (ViT-Base with cross-entropy and weighted CE; ViT-Large with focal loss). Evaluation on held-out data.
AI.result: ViT-Large with focal loss achieves 69.6% held-out accuracy, 68.8% weighted F1, and 83.9% precision on the minority Multi-Year Ice class.
AI.conclusion: Focal-loss training provides a better precision-recall balance for rare ice classes and establishes a clean, SAR-only baseline for future multimodal fusion with optical, thermal, or meteorological data.

title: Effect of Input Resolution on Retinal Vessel Segmentation Performance: An Empirical Study Across Five Datasets
summary: Most deep learning pipelines for retinal vessel segmentation resize fundus images to satisfy GPU memory constraints and enable uniform batch processing. However, the impact of this resizing on thin vessel detection remains underexplored. When high resolution images are downsampled, thin vessels are reduced to subpixel structures, causing irreversible information loss even before the data enters the network. Standard volumetric metrics such as the Dice score do not capture this loss because thick vessel pixels dominate the evaluation. We investigated this effect by training a baseline UNet at multiple downsampling ratios across five fundus datasets (DRIVE, STARE, CHASE_DB1, HRF, and FIVES) with native widths ranging from 565 to 3504 pixels, keeping all other settings fixed. We introduce a width-stratified sensitivity metric that evaluates thin (half-width <3 pixels), medium (3 to 7 pixels), and thick (>7 pixels) vessel detection separately, using native resolution width estimates derived from a Euclidean distance transform. Results show that for high-resolution datasets (HRF, FIVES), thin vessel sensitivity improves monotonically as images are downsampled toward the encoder's effective operating range, peaking at processed widths between 256 and 876 pixels. For low-to-mid resolution datasets (DRIVE, STARE, CHASE_DB1), thin vessel sensitivity is highest at or near native resolution and degrades with any downsampling. Across all five datasets, aggressive downsampling reduced thin vessel sensitivity by up to 15.8 percentage points (DRIVE) while Dice remained relatively stable, confirming that Dice alone is insufficient for evaluating microvascular segmentation.

AI.tldr: Downsampling fundus images to fit memory budgets degrades thin vessel detection in a nonuniform, dataset-dependent way, while Dice remains stable; width-stratified sensitivity reveals losses in microvasculature not captured by standard metrics.
AI.motivation: Evaluate how common image resizing for memory efficiency affects detection of thin retinal vessels, and whether Dice score adequately reflects this core performance metric.
AI.method: Train a baseline UNet on five fundus datasets (DRIVE, STARE, CHASE_DB1, HRF, FIVES) across multiple downsampling ratios. Define native vessel widths (subpixel to thick) via Euclidean distance transform and group into thin (<3 px), medium (3–7 px), and thick (>7 px). Introduce a width-stratified sensitivity metric for each group and compare with Dice.
AI.result: Thin vessel sensitivity improves with downsampling for high-resolution datasets (HRF, FIVES) when processed widths lie between 256–876 px, but degrades with any downsampling for low-to-mid resolution datasets (DRIVE, STARE, CHASE_DB1), with native-resolution performance best. Aggressive downsampling reduced thin-vessel sensitivity by up to 15.8 percentage points on DRIVE, while Dice remained relatively stable across downsampling levels.
AI.conclusion: Dice alone fails to capture microvascular performance; downsampling impacts thin vessels nonuniformly and depends on native resolution. A width-stratified assessment is necessary, and strategies to preserve subpixel information or maintain higher effective resolutions should be considered to avoid information loss in thin vessels.

title: Not All Frames Deserve Full Computation: Accelerating Autoregressive Video Generation via Selective Computation and Predictive Extrapolation
summary: Autoregressive (AR) video diffusion models enable long-form video generation but remain expensive due to repeated multi-step denoising. Existing training-free acceleration methods rely on binary cache-or-recompute decisions, overlooking intermediate cases where direct reuse is too coarse yet full recomputation is unnecessary. Moreover, asynchronous AR schedules assign different noise levels to co-generated frames, yet existing methods process the entire valid interval uniformly. To address these AR-specific inefficiencies, we present SCOPE, a training-free framework for efficient AR video diffusion. SCOPE introduces a tri-modal scheduler over cache, predict, and recompute, where prediction via noise-level Taylor extrapolation fills the gap between reuse and recomputation with explicit stability controls backed by error propagation analysis. It further introduces selective computation that restricts execution to the active frame interval. On MAGI-1 and SkyReels-V2, SCOPE achieves up to 4.73x speedup while maintaining quality comparable to the original output, outperforming all training-free baselines.

AI.tldr: SCOPE is a training-free method for efficient autoregressive video diffusion that uses a tri-modal scheduler (cache, predict, recompute) and Taylor-extrapolated prediction to bridge reuse and recomputation, with selective computation on the active frame interval, achieving up to 4.73x speedup on MAGI-1 and SkyReels-V2 while preserving quality.
AI.motivation: AR video diffusion models are expensive due to repeated multi-step denoising. Training-free accelerations relying on binary cache/recompute miss intermediate reuse opportunities and do not account for varying noise levels in asynchronous AR schedules.
AI.method: Introduce SCOPE with a tri-modal scheduler (cache, predict, recompute). Use noise-level Taylor extrapolation for prediction to interpolate between reuse and recomputation with explicit stability controls via error propagation analysis. Implement selective computation to run only on the active frame interval.
AI.result: On MAGI-1 and SkyReels-V2, SCOPE achieves up to 4.73x speedup with quality comparable to the original, outperforming all training-free baselines.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE provides a training-free, stability-aware, AR-specific acceleration framework for video diffusion, combining predictive interpolation with selective execution to substantially reduce compute while maintaining output quality.

title: Can VLMs Truly Forget? Benchmarking Training-Free Visual Concept Unlearning
summary:
VLMs trained on web-scale data retain sensitive and copyrighted visual concepts that deployment may require removing. Training-based unlearning methods share a structural flaw: fine-tuning on a narrow forget set degrades general capabilities before unlearning begins, making it impossible to attribute subsequent performance drops to the unlearning procedure itself. Training-free approaches sidestep this by suppressing concepts through prompts or system instructions, but no rigorous benchmark exists for evaluating them on visual tasks.
  We introduce VLM-UnBench, the first benchmark for training-free visual concept unlearning in VLMs. It covers four forgetting levels, 7 source datasets, and 11 concept axes, and pairs a three-level probe taxonomy with five evaluation conditions to separate genuine forgetting from instruction compliance. Across 8 evaluation settings and 13 VLM configurations, realistic unlearning prompts leave forget accuracy near the no-instruction baseline; meaningful reductions appear only under oracle conditions that disclose the target concept to the model. Object and scene concepts are the most resistant to suppression, and stronger instruction-tuned models remain capable despite explicit forget instructions. These results expose a clear gap between prompt-level suppression and true visual concept erasure.

AI.tldr: A benchmarking study (VLM-UnBench) shows training-free prompts fail to truly erase visual concepts in VLMs; only oracle disclosures cause notable forgetting; reveals a gap between suppression and real erasure across datasets, concepts, and model configurations.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of robust benchmarks for training-free visual concept unlearning in vision-language models and the shortcomings of training-based unlearning due to performance degradation.
AI.method: Introduce VLM-UnBench with four forgetting levels, seven source datasets, and 11 concept axes; employ a three-level probe taxonomy and five evaluation conditions to separate genuine forgetting from instruction compliance; evaluate across 8 settings and 13 VLM configurations using prompt-based suppression and instruction cues.
AI.result: Realistic unlearning prompts keep forget accuracy near the no-instruction baseline; meaningful forgetting only under oracle conditions that reveal the target concept; object- and scene-level concepts are most resistant; stronger instruction-tuned models still perform despite explicit forget instructions.
AI.conclusion: There is a persistent gap between prompt-level suppression and true visual concept erasure; the benchmark reveals limitations of current training-free methods and underscores the need for rigorous, attribution-friendly evaluation and potentially new unlearning mechanisms.

title: Rendering Multi-Human and Multi-Object with 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Reconstructing dynamic scenes with multiple interacting humans and objects from sparse-view inputs is a critical yet challenging task, essential for creating high-fidelity digital twins for robotics and VR/AR. This problem, which we term Multi-Human Multi-Object (MHMO) rendering, presents two significant obstacles: achieving view-consistent representations for individual instances under severe mutual occlusion, and explicitly modeling the complex and combinatorial dependencies that arise from their interactions. To overcome these challenges, we propose MM-GS, a novel hierarchical framework built upon 3D Gaussian Splatting. Our method first employs a Per-Instance Multi-View Fusion module to establish a robust and consistent representation for each instance by aggregating visual information across all available views. Subsequently, a Scene-Level Instance Interaction module operates on a global scene graph to reason about relationships between all participants, refining their attributes to capture subtle interaction effects. Extensive experiments on challenging datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms strong baselines, producing state-of-the-art results with high-fidelity details and plausible inter-instance contacts.

AI.tldr: MM-GS: A hierarchical 3D-Gaussian-Splatting framework for Multi-Human Multi-Object rendering from sparse views, featuring per-instance multi-view fusion and a scene-level interaction module to model inter-instance relationships; achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and plausible contacts.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing dynamic scenes with multiple interacting humans and objects from sparse views is hard due to severe occlusions and combinatorial interdependencies; existing methods struggle to maintain view-consistent instance representations and to model interactions explicitly.
AI.method: Propose MM-GS built on 3D Gaussian Splatting; first Per-Instance Multi-View Fusion aggregates information across all views to produce robust per-instance representations; second Scene-Level Instance Interaction uses a global scene graph to reason about relationships and refine per-instance attributes to capture interaction effects.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on challenging datasets show significant improvements over strong baselines, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity and plausible inter-instance contacts.
AI.conclusion: MM-GS demonstrates that decoupling per-instance fusion from global interaction reasoning enables robust MHMO rendering under sparse views, with potential benefits for robotics and VR/AR digital twins.

title: Explicit Time-Frequency Dynamics for Skeleton-Based Gait Recognition
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, to appear in ICASSP 2026
summary: Skeleton-based gait recognizers excel at modeling spatial configurations but often underuse explicit motion dynamics that are crucial under appearance changes. We introduce a plug-and-play Wavelet Feature Stream that augments any skeleton backbone with time-frequency dynamics of joint velocities. Concretely, per-joint velocity sequences are transformed by the continuous wavelet transform (CWT) into multi-scale scalograms, from which a lightweight multi-scale CNN learns discriminative dynamic cues. The resulting descriptor is fused with the backbone representation for classification, requiring no changes to the backbone architecture or additional supervision. Across CASIA-B, the proposed stream delivers consistent gains on strong skeleton backbones (e.g., GaitMixer, GaitFormer, GaitGraph) and establishes a new skeleton-based state of the art when attached to GaitMixer. The improvements are especially pronounced under covariate shifts such as carrying bags (BG) and wearing coats (CL), highlighting the complementarity of explicit time-frequency modeling and standard spatio-temporal encoders.

AI.tldr: Plug-and-play Wavelet Feature Stream augments skeleton-based gait recognizers with time-frequency dynamics of joint velocities, yielding state-of-the-art results on CASIA-B and improved robustness to covariates.
AI.motivation: Skeleton-based methods capture spatial configurations but often underutilize explicit motion dynamics; incorporating time-frequency dynamics can improve discrimination and robustness to appearance changes.
AI.method: Attach a Wavelet Feature Stream that computes per-joint velocity sequences, applies continuous wavelet transform to generate multi-scale scalograms, processes them with a lightweight multi-scale CNN to produce a dynamic descriptor, and fuse it with the backbone representation; no changes to backbone architecture or supervision required.
AI.result: Consistent gains across strong skeleton backbones (GaitMixer, GaitFormer, GaitGraph); achieves a new skeleton-based state of the art when paired with GaitMixer; particularly strong improvements under covariate shifts like carrying bags and wearing coats.
AI.conclusion: Explicit time-frequency modeling of joint velocities complements standard spatio-temporal encoders, enabling improved gait recognition robustness with a plug-and-play integration.

title: GenSmoke-GS: A Multi-Stage Method for Novel View Synthesis from Smoke-Degraded Images Using a Generative Model
summary: This paper describes our method for Track 2 of the NTIRE 2026 3D Restoration and Reconstruction (3DRR) Challenge on smoke-degraded images. In this task, smoke reduces image visibility and weakens the cross-view consistency required by scene optimization and rendering. We address this problem with a multi-stage pipeline consisting of image restoration, dehazing, MLLM-based enhancement, 3DGS-MCMC optimization, and averaging over repeated runs. The main purpose of the pipeline is to improve visibility before rendering while limiting scene-content changes across input views. Experimental results on the challenge benchmark show improved quantitative performance and better visual quality than the provided baselines. The code is available at https://github.com/plbbl/GenSmoke-GS. Our method achieved a ranking of 1 out of 14 participants in Track 2 of the NTIRE 3DRR Challenge, as reported on the official competition website: https://www.codabench.org/competitions/13993/#/results-tab.

AI.tldr: Multi-stage restoration pipeline for smoke-degraded images in Track 2 of NTIRE 2026 3DRR, combining restoration, dehazing, MLLM-based enhancement, 3DGS-MCMC optimization, and ensembling; achieves top-1 (1/14) with improved metrics and visuals.
AI.motivation: Smoke reduces visibility and weakens cross-view consistency needed for scene optimization and rendering; a robust restoration is required to recover scene content without introducing view-specific artifacts.
AI.method: A multi-stage pipeline: image restoration, dehazing, MLLM-based enhancement, 3DGS-MCMC optimization, and averaging over repeated runs; designed to boost visibility prior to rendering while constraining content changes across input views.
AI.result: On the challenge benchmark, the method shows improved quantitative performance and better visual quality than baselines; ranked 1st among 14 participants in Track 2.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline effectively enhances visibility for 3D restoration under smoke while maintaining cross-view consistency; code is publicly available and the approach demonstrates strong competitive performance in NTIRE 3DRR Track 2.

title: QVAD: A Question-Centric Agentic Framework for Efficient and Training-Free Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Video Anomaly Detection (VAD) is a fundamental challenge in computer vision, particularly due to the open-set nature of anomalies. While recent training-free approaches utilizing Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown promise, they typically rely on massive, resource-intensive foundation models to compensate for the ambiguity of static prompts. We argue that the bottleneck in VAD is not necessarily model capacity, but rather the static nature of inquiry. We propose QVAD, a question-centric agentic framework that treats VLM-LLM interaction as a dynamic dialogue. By iteratively refining queries based on visual context, our LLM agent guides smaller VLMs to produce high-fidelity captions and precise semantic reasoning without parameter updates. This ``prompt-updating" mechanism effectively unlocks the latent capabilities of lightweight models, enabling state-of-the-art performance on UCF-Crime, XD-Violence, and UBNormal using a fraction of the parameters required by competing methods. We further demonstrate exceptional generalizability on the single-scene ComplexVAD dataset. Crucially, QVAD achieves high inference speeds with minimal memory footprints, making advanced VAD capabilities deployable on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, question-centric LLM-VLM framework (QVAD) that dynamically updates prompts to guide small VLMs for video anomaly detection, achieving state-of-the-art on multiple datasets with low resource usage.
AI.motivation: Static prompts and reliance on massive foundation models hinder efficiency in video anomaly detection, especially under open-set conditions. There is a need to leverage lightweight models via adaptive querying to improve performance without retraining.
AI.method: QVAD treats VLM-LLM interaction as a dynamic dialogue. An LLM agent iteratively refines queries based on visual context and guides smaller VLMs to produce high-fidelity captions and precise semantic reasoning without parameter updates. A prompt-updating mechanism enables latent capabilities of lightweight models, and the system emphasizes inference speed and small memory usage for edge deployment.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on UCF-Crime, XD-Violence, and UBNormal using a fraction of parameters required by competing methods; generalizes well to the single-scene ComplexVAD dataset; demonstrates high inference speed and minimal memory footprint, suitable for edge devices.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, query-based interaction between VLMs and an LLM unlocks the latent potential of lightweight models for video anomaly detection, reducing dependence on large foundation models while delivering strong performance and edge-friendly deployment.

title: PR3DICTR: A modular AI framework for medical 3D image-based detection and outcome prediction
summary: Three-dimensional medical image data and computer-aided decision making, particularly using deep learning, are becoming increasingly important in the medical field. To aid in these developments we introduce PR3DICTR: Platform for Research in 3D Image Classification and sTandardised tRaining. Built using community-standard distributions (PyTorch and MONAI), PR3DICTR provides an open-access, flexible and convenient framework for prediction model development, with an explicit focus on classification using three-dimensional medical image data. By combining modular design principles and standardization, it aims to alleviate developmental burden whilst retaining adjustability. It provides users with a wealth of pre-established functionality, for instance in model architecture design options, hyper-parameter solutions and training methodologies, but still gives users the opportunity and freedom to ``plug in'' their own solutions or modules. PR3DICTR can be applied to any binary or event-based three-dimensional classification task and can work with as little as two lines of code.

AI.tldr: PR3DICTR offers an open, modular, and standardized platform for 3D medical image classification built on PyTorch and MONAI, enabling rapid model development with minimal code and plug-in flexibility.
AI.motivation: 3D medical image classification tasks suffer from fragmented tooling, high development burden, and lack of standardization; researchers need a flexible platform to experiment with architectures, training strategies, and hyperparameters.
AI.method: A modular software framework built on PyTorch/MONAI that provides pre-built components for model architectures, hyperparameters, and training methods; supports plug-in modules and claims to enable work with as little as two lines of code for binary or event-based 3D classification.
AI.result: The framework centralizes functionality and standardizes workflows; reduces development time and effort; enables rapid prototyping and experimentation in 3D image classification.
AI.conclusion: PR3DICTR addresses bottlenecks in 3D medical image classification research by offering a flexible, reusable platform that streamlines model development while preserving customization.

title: STEAR: Layer-Aware Spatiotemporal Evidence Intervention for Hallucination Mitigation in Video Large Language Models
summary: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) remain prone to spatiotemporal hallucinations, often generating visually unsupported details or incorrect temporal relations. Existing mitigation methods typically treat hallucination as a uniform decoding failure, applying globally shared correction rules. We instead observe that decoder layers contribute differently to visual grounding and later linguistic composition, indicating that intervention must be layer-aware. Based on this insight, we propose STEAR, a layer-aware spatiotemporal evidence intervention framework. STEAR identifies high-risk decoding steps and selects token-conditioned visual evidence from grounding-sensitive middle layers. It uses this shared evidence for two coupled purposes: restoring missing local grounding in middle layers, and constructing temporally perturbed patch-level counterfactuals to falsify inconsistent reasoning during late-layer decoding. Consequently, STEAR mitigates both spatial and temporal hallucinations within an efficient single-encode inference framework. Experiments across representative Video-LLM backbones and challenging benchmarks demonstrate that STEAR consistently reduces hallucinations while improving faithfulness, temporal consistency, and robustness. Our results confirm that reliable video decoding relies on intervening on precise evidence at the right layer, rather than enforcing a global penalty. The code is provided in the Supplementary Material.

AI.tldr: STEAR is a layer-aware intervention for Video-LLMs that mitigates spatiotemporal hallucinations by using token-conditioned visual evidence from grounding-sensitive middle layers, serving dual purposes: restoring local grounding in middle layers and generating temporally perturbed counterfactuals for late-layer decoding within a single-encode framework.
AI.motivation: Video-LLMs suffer spatiotemporal hallucinations due to uniform global decoding failures; decoder layers contribute differently to grounding and lateral linguistic composition, necessitating layer-aware interventions.
AI.method: Identify high-risk decoding steps and extract token-conditioned visual evidence from grounding-sensitive middle layers. Use this evidence to (1) restore missing local grounding in middle layers and (2) construct temporally perturbed patch-level counterfactuals to challenge inconsistent late-layer reasoning. Implemented within a single-encode inference framework to reduce hallucinations efficiently.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across representative Video-LLMs and benchmarks shows consistent reductions in hallucinations with improvements in faithfulness, temporal consistency, and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Precise evidence interventions at the correct layer, rather than global penalties, are essential for reliable video decoding. The approach achieves effective hallucination mitigation within a single-encode framework, with code provided in supplementary materials.

title: Can Nano Banana 2 Replace Traditional Image Restoration Models? An Evaluation of Its Performance on Image Restoration Tasks
summary: Recent advances in generative AI raise the question of whether general-purpose image editing models can serve as unified solutions for image restoration. In this work, we conduct a systematic evaluation of Nano Banana 2 for image restoration across diverse scenes and degradation types. Our results show that prompt design plays a critical role, where concise prompts with explicit fidelity constraints achieve the best trade-off between reconstruction accuracy and perceptual quality. Compared with state-of-the-art restoration models, Nano Banana 2 achieves superior performance in full-reference metrics while remaining competitive in perceptual quality, which is further supported by user studies. We also observe strong generalization in challenging scenarios, such as small faces, dense crowds, and severe degradations. However, the model remains sensitive to prompt formulation and may require iterative refinement for optimal results. Overall, our findings suggest that general-purpose generative models hold strong potential as unified image restoration solvers, while highlighting the importance of controllability and robustness. All test results are available on https://github.com/yxyuanxiao/NanoBanana2TestOnIR.

AI.tldr: General-purpose image editing/generative models can serve as restoration solvers; Nano Banana 2 shows strong full-reference performance with prompt design, robust in challenging scenes, but sensitive to prompts and may require iteration.
AI.motivation: Assess whether a general-purpose generative model can act as a unified image restoration solution across diverse degradation types and scenes, and understand how prompting controls restoration quality vs perceptual fidelity.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of Nano Banana 2 on image restoration across varied degradations and scenes; analyses of prompt design strategies, trade-offs between reconstruction accuracy (full-reference metrics) and perceptual quality; comparisons to state-of-the-art restoration models; user studies; tests on challenging cases (small faces, dense crowds, severe degradations); availability of results via a public repo.
AI.result: Concise prompts with explicit fidelity constraints yield best trade-off; Nano Banana 2 achieves superior full-reference metrics and competitive perceptual quality; generalizes well in challenging cases; strong potential as a unified restoration solver; requires careful prompt formulation and potential iterative refinement for optimal results; user studies corroborate, and results are reproducible via the provided link.
AI.conclusion: General-purpose generative models hold promise as unified image restoration solvers when guided with well-crafted prompts and fidelity constraints; controllability and robustness remain critical to practical adoption; further work should address prompts sensitivity and automation of prompt refinement.

title: Gram-MMD: A Texture-Aware Metric for Image Realism Assessment
summary: Evaluating the realism of generated images remains a fundamental challenge in generative modeling. Existing distributional metrics such as the Frechet Inception Distance (FID) and CLIP-MMD (CMMD) compare feature distributions at a semantic level but may overlook fine-grained textural information that can be relevant for distinguishing real from generated images. We introduce Gram-MMD (GMMD), a realism metric that leverages Gram matrices computed from intermediate activations of pretrained backbone networks to capture correlations between feature maps. By extracting the upper-triangular part of these symmetric Gram matrices and measuring the Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) between an anchor distribution of real images and an evaluation distribution, GMMD produces a representation that encodes textural and structural characteristics at a finer granularity than global embeddings. To select the hyperparameters of the metric, we employ a meta-metric protocol based on controlled degradations applied to MS-COCO images, measuring monotonicity via Spearman's rank correlation and Kendall's tau. We conduct experiments on both the KADID-10k database and the RAISE realness assessment dataset using various backbone architectures, including DINOv2, DC-AE, Stable Diffusion's VAE encoder, VGG19, and the AlexNet backbone from LPIPS, among others. We also demonstrate on a cross-domain driving scenario (KITTI / Virtual KITTI / Stanford Cars) that CMMD can incorrectly rank real images as less realistic than synthetic ones due to its semantic bias, while GMMD preserves the correct ordering. Our results suggest that GMMD captures complementary information to existing semantic-level metrics.

AI.tldr: GMMD is a new realism metric for generated images that uses Gram matrices of intermediate backbone features and MMD to capture texture and structure, complementing semantic metrics like FID/CMMD; it shows robustness to semantic bias and better ordering in cross-domain tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing distributional realism metrics (e.g., FID, CMMD) focus on semantic-level feature distributions and can miss fine-grained textural cues essential for distinguishing real vs. generated images; a texture/structure-aware metric could provide complementary information and reduce bias.
AI.method: Compute Gram matrices from intermediate activations of pretrained backbones; extract upper-triangular part of these Gram matrices to form a representation; measure MMD between an anchor distribution of real images and an evaluation distribution. Hyperparameters are selected via a meta-metric protocol using controlled degradations on MS-COCO, assessing monotonicity with Spearman's rho and Kendall's tau.
AI.result: GMMD captures textural and structural information at finer granularity than global embeddings, providing complementary information to semantic metrics. In experiments on KADID-10k and RAISE, with various backbones (DINOv2, DC-AE, Stable Diffusion VAE encoder, VGG19, AlexNet from LPIPS, etc.), GMMD outperforms or complements existing metrics and avoids semantic bias that can misrank real vs. synthetic in cross-domain KITTI/Virtual KITTI/Stanford Cars scenarios where CMMD is biased.
AI.conclusion: GMMD offers a robust, texture/structure-aware realism metric that complements semantic-based metrics and can mitigate biases in cross-domain evaluations. It warrants further exploration and validation across diverse image domains and efficiency considerations.

title: SparseSplat: Towards Applicable Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting with Pixel-Unaligned Prediction
summary: Recent progress in feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has notably improved rendering quality. However, the spatially uniform and highly redundant 3DGS map generated by previous feed-forward 3DGS methods limits their integration into downstream reconstruction tasks. We propose SparseSplat, the first feed-forward 3DGS model that adaptively adjusts Gaussian density according to scene structure and information richness of local regions, yielding highly compact 3DGS maps. To achieve this, we propose entropy-based probabilistic sampling, generating large, sparse Gaussians in textureless areas and assigning small, dense Gaussians to regions with rich information. Additionally, we designed a specialized point cloud network that efficiently encodes local context and decodes it into 3DGS attributes, addressing the receptive field mismatch between the general 3DGS optimization pipeline and feed-forward models. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that SparseSplat can achieve state-of-the-art rendering quality with only 22% of the Gaussians and maintain reasonable rendering quality with only 1.5% of the Gaussians. Project page: https://victkk.github.io/SparseSplat-page/.

AI.tldr: SparseSplat is a feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting model that adaptively allocates Gaussian density based on local scene information using entropy-based sampling, paired with a specialized point cloud network. It achieves state-of-the-art rendering with a fraction of Gaussians (~22%) and maintains quality with even less (~1.5%), enabling more compact 3DGS maps for downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Previous feed-forward 3DGS methods produce spatially uniform, highly redundant 3DGS maps that hinder integration into downstream reconstruction pipelines. There is a need for compact, adaptive representations that preserve rendering quality.
AI.method: Introduce entropy-based probabilistic sampling to generate large, sparse Gaussians in textureless regions and small, dense Gaussians in information-rich regions. Develop a specialized point cloud network that efficiently encodes local context and decodes it into 3DGS attributes, addressing receptive-field mismatches with the conventional 3DGS optimization pipeline.
AI.result: Empirical results show SparseSplat achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality using only ~22% of the Gaussians, and maintains reasonable rendering quality with as little as ~1.5% of Gaussians.
AI.conclusion: SparseSplat delivers a compact, high-quality 3DGS representation suitable for downstream tasks by adaptively varying Gaussian density via entropy-guided sampling and a context-aware encoding-decoding network.

title: MI-Pruner: Crossmodal Mutual Information-guided Token Pruner for Efficient MLLMs
summary: For multimodal large language models (MLLMs), visual information is relatively sparse compared with text. As a result, research on visual pruning emerges for efficient inference. Current approaches typically measure token importance based on the attention scores in the visual encoder or in the LLM decoder, then select visual tokens with high attention scores while pruning others. In this paper, we pursue a different and more surgical approach. Instead of relying on mechanism-specific signals, we directly compute Mutual Information (MI) between visual and textual features themselves, prior to their interaction. This allows us to explicitly measure crossmodal dependency at the feature levels. Our MI-Pruner is simple, efficient and non-intrusive, requiring no access to internal attention maps or architectural modifications. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms previous attention-based pruning methods with minimal latency.

AI.tldr: MI-Pruner: prune visual tokens by mutual information between visual and textual features before interaction; outperforms attention-based pruning with low latency.
AI.motivation: Visual information is sparse in multimodal LLMs; existing pruning relies on attention scores and is tied to specific mechanisms. A direct, feature-level crossmodal dependency metric could be more effective and non-intrusive.
AI.method: Compute mutual information between visual features and textual features prior to fusion, selecting visual tokens with higher MI with textual representations; requires no access to attention maps and no architectural changes.
AI.result: Empirically, MI-Pruner surpasses attention-based pruning methods with minimal latency.
AI.conclusion: MI-based crossmodal feature dependency is a simple, efficient, and effective pruning signal for MLLMs, offering a non-intrusive alternative to attention-based pruning.

title: Revealing Physical-World Semantic Vulnerabilities: Universal Adversarial Patches for Infrared Vision-Language Models
summary: Infrared vision-language models (IR-VLMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for multimodal perception in low-visibility environments, yet their robustness to adversarial attacks remains largely unexplored. Existing adversarial patch methods are mainly designed for RGB-based models in closed-set settings and are not readily applicable to the open-ended semantic understanding and physical deployment requirements of infrared VLMs. To bridge this gap, we propose Universal Curved-Grid Patch (UCGP), a universal physical adversarial patch framework for IR-VLMs. UCGP integrates Curved-Grid Mesh (CGM) parameterization for continuous, low-frequency, and deployable patch generation with a unified representation-driven objective that promotes subspace departure, topology disruption, and stealth. To improve robustness under real-world deployment and domain shift, we further incorporate Meta Differential Evolution and EOT-augmented TPS deformation modeling. Rather than manipulating labels or prompts, UCGP directly disrupts the visual representation space, weakening cross-modal semantic alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UCGP consistently compromises semantic understanding across diverse IR-VLM architectures while maintaining cross-model transferability, cross-dataset generalization, real-world physical effectiveness, and robustness against defenses. These findings reveal a previously overlooked robustness vulnerability in current infrared multimodal systems.

AI.tldr: A universal physical adversarial patch, UCGP, for infrared vision-language models (IR-VLMs) that uses Curved-Grid Mesh parameterization and a unified representation-driven objective to disrupt cross-modal semantic alignment; enhanced for real-world deployment via Meta Differential Evolution and EOT-based TPS deformation; demonstrates strong cross-model, cross-dataset, and physical robustness, revealing a vulnerability in infrared multimodal systems.
AI.motivation: IR-VLMs enable perception in low-visibility environments, but their robustness to adversarial patches remains underexplored. Existing patches target RGB models in closed-set settings and are not suitable for open-ended, physically deployable infrared VLMs. There is a need for a universal, deployable adversarial patch framework that can degrade semantic understanding across IR-VLM architectures under real-world conditions.
AI.method: Introduce Universal Curved-Grid Patch (UCGP) with Curved-Grid Mesh (CGM) parameterization for continuous, low-frequency, deployable patches. Employ a unified representation-driven objective emphasizing subspace departure, topology disruption, and stealth. Improve robustness with Meta Differential Evolution and EOT-augmented TPS deformation modeling. The attack operates in the visual representation space, not through labels or prompts, aiming to degrade cross-modal semantic alignment.
AI.result: UCGP consistently degrades semantic understanding across diverse IR-VLM architectures, with strong cross-model transferability and cross-dataset generalization. Demonstrates real-world physical effectiveness and robustness against defenses, indicating a notable vulnerability in infrared multimodal systems.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a previously overlooked robustness vulnerability in infrared multimodal systems. The proposed UCGP framework shows the feasibility of universal, physically realizable patches that undermine IR-VLMs and highlights the need for defense strategies and robust training to mitigate such attacks.

title: Salt: Self-Consistent Distribution Matching with Cache-Aware Training for Fast Video Generation
summary: Distilling video generation models to extremely low inference budgets (e.g., 2--4 NFEs) is crucial for real-time deployment, yet remains challenging. Trajectory-style consistency distillation often becomes conservative under complex video dynamics, yielding an over-smoothed appearance and weak motion. Distribution matching distillation (DMD) can recover sharp, mode-seeking samples, but its local training signals do not explicitly regularize how denoising updates compose across timesteps, making composed rollouts prone to drift. To overcome this challenge, we propose Self-Consistent Distribution Matching Distillation (SC-DMD), which explicitly regularizes the endpoint-consistent composition of consecutive denoising updates. For real-time autoregressive video generation, we further treat the KV cache as a quality parameterized condition and propose Cache-Distribution-Aware training. This training scheme applies SC-DMD over multi-step rollouts and introduces a cache-conditioned feature alignment objective that steers low-quality outputs toward high-quality references. Across extensive experiments on both non-autoregressive backbones (e.g., Wan~2.1) and autoregressive real-time paradigms (e.g., Self Forcing), our method, dubbed \textbf{Salt}, consistently improves low-NFE video generation quality while remaining compatible with diverse KV-cache memory mechanisms. Source code will be released at \href{https://github.com/XingtongGe/Salt}{https://github.com/XingtongGe/Salt}.

AI.tldr: Salt: Self-Consistent Distribution Matching Distillation with cache-aware training for real-time low-NFE video generation, yielding improved quality across non-autoregressive and autoregressive backbones.
AI.motivation: Need to distill video generators to very low inference budgets (2–4 NFEs) for real-time use. Trajectory-style distillation is overly conservative; distribution matching can recover sharp samples but lacks multi-step consistency; a method to regularize endpoint-consistent composition and leverage KV-cache as a quality parameter is desired.
AI.method: Introduce SC-DMD to enforce endpoint-consistent composition of successive denoising updates. Extend with Cache-Distribution-Aware training: apply SC-DMD over multi-step rollouts and add a cache-conditioned feature alignment objective aligning outputs with high-quality references, enabling the KV cache to act as a quality parameter. Evaluate on non-autoregressive Wan 2.1 and autoregressive Self Forcing backbones; aim for compatibility with various KV-cache mechanisms.
AI.result: Empirically improves low-NFE video generation quality across backbones, with Salt achieving consistent gains and compatibility with diverse KV-cache memory strategies. Source code to be released.
AI.conclusion: Salt provides a robust, cache-aware distillation framework that yields higher-quality real-time video generation at low NFEs and is broadly compatible with existing backbones and caching mechanisms.

title: CAMEO: A Conditional and Quality-Aware Multi-Agent Image Editing Orchestrator
summary: Conditional image editing aims to modify a source image according to textual prompts and optional reference guidance. Such editing is crucial in scenarios requiring strict structural control (i.e., anomaly insertion in driving scenes and complex human pose transformation). Despite recent advances in large-scale editing models (i.e., Seedream, Nano Banana, etc), most approaches rely on single-step generation. This paradigm often lacks explicit quality control, may introduce excessive deviation from the original image, and frequently produces structural artifacts or environment-inconsistent modifications, typically requiring manual prompt tuning to achieve acceptable results. We propose \textbf{CAMEO}, a structured multi-agent framework that reformulates conditional editing as a quality-aware, feedback-driven process rather than a one-shot generation task. CAMEO decomposes editing into coordinated stages of planning, structured prompting, hypothesis generation, and adaptive reference grounding, where external guidance is invoked only when task complexity requires it. To overcome the lack of intrinsic quality control in existing methods, evaluation is embedded directly within the editing loop. Intermediate results are iteratively refined through structured feedback, forming a closed-loop process that progressively corrects structural and contextual inconsistencies. We evaluate CAMEO on anomaly insertion and human pose switching tasks. Across multiple strong editing backbones and independent evaluation models, CAMEO consistently achieves 20\% more win rate on average compared to multiple state-of-the-art models, demonstrating improved robustness, controllability, and structural reliability in conditional image editing.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent, feedback-driven conditional image editing framework (CAMEO) that plans, prompts, hypothesizes, and grounds references with embedded quality control, enabling iterative refinement instead of one-shot editing, achieving higher robustness and ~20% win-rate gains.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of intrinsic quality control and structural artifacts in single-step editing methods; ensure strict structural control in complex edits.
AI.method: Decompose editing into planning, structured prompting, hypothesis generation, and adaptive reference grounding; invoke external guidance only as needed; embed evaluation inside the editing loop; closed-loop refinement of intermediate outputs.
AI.result: Consistent performance gains: ~20% higher win rate across anomaly insertion and human pose switching tasks across multiple backbones and evaluation models, indicating improved robustness, controllability, and structural reliability.
AI.conclusion: CAMEO offers a principled, quality-aware editing paradigm that integrates evaluation and feedback to reduce artifacts and deviations from source imagery, improving the reliability of conditional editing.

title: EffiMiniVLM: A Compact Dual-Encoder Regression Framework
summary: Predicting product quality from multimodal item information is critical in cold-start scenarios, where user interaction history is unavailable and predictions must rely on images and textual metadata. However, existing vision-language models typically depend on large architectures and/or extensive external datasets, resulting in high computational cost. To address this, we propose EffiMiniVLM, a compact dual-encoder vision-language regression framework that integrates an EfficientNet-B0 image encoder and a MiniLM-based text encoder with a lightweight regression head. To improve training sample efficiency, we introduce a weighted Huber loss that leverages rating counts to emphasize more reliable samples, yielding consistent performance gains. Trained using only 20% of the Amazon Reviews 2023 dataset, the proposed model contains 27.7M parameters and requires 6.8 GFLOPs, yet achieves a CES score of 0.40 with the lowest resource cost in the benchmark. Despite its small size, it remains competitive with significantly larger models, achieving comparable performance while being approximately 4x to 8x more resource-efficient than other top-5 methods and being the only approach that does not use external datasets. Further analysis shows that scaling the data to 40% alone allows our model to overtake other methods, which use larger models and datasets, highlighting strong scalability despite the model's compact design.

AI.tldr: A compact dual-encoder vision-language model (EffiMiniVLM) for cold-start product quality prediction that achieves competitive performance with substantially lower compute and no external data.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational cost and model size of vision-language approaches in cold-start product quality tasks while preserving accuracy, enabling deployment on resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: EffiMiniVLM uses an EfficientNet-B0 image encoder and a MiniLM-based text encoder with a lightweight regression head. It employs a weighted Huber loss that uses rating counts to emphasize more reliable samples. Trained on 20% of the Amazon Reviews 2023 dataset; model size 27.7M parameters; 6.8 GFLOPs.
AI.result: CES score of 0.40 with the lowest resource cost in the benchmark; 27.7M params and 6.8 GFLOPs; competitive with significantly larger models; ~4x to 8x more resource-efficient than top-5 methods; the only approach not using external datasets; scaling to 40% data further improves performance, surpassing methods that rely on larger models/datasets.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that compact multimodal models can achieve competitive performance with strong scalability and much lower resource consumption, making them viable for cold-start product quality tasks and resource-constrained deployments.

title: SFFNet: Synergistic Feature Fusion Network With Dual-Domain Edge Enhancement for UAV Image Object Detection
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
summary: Object detection in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) images remains a highly challenging task, primarily caused by the complexity of background noise and the imbalance of target scales. Traditional methods easily struggle to effectively separate objects from intricate backgrounds and fail to fully leverage the rich multi-scale information contained within images. To address these issues, we have developed a synergistic feature fusion network (SFFNet) with dual-domain edge enhancement specifically tailored for object detection in UAV images. Firstly, the multi-scale dynamic dual-domain coupling (MDDC) module is designed. This component introduces a dual-driven edge extraction architecture that operates in both the frequency and spatial domains, enabling effective decoupling of multi-scale object edges from background noise. Secondly, to further enhance the representation capability of the model's neck in terms of both geometric and semantic information, a synergistic feature pyramid network (SFPN) is proposed. SFPN leverages linear deformable convolutions to adaptively capture irregular object shapes and establishes long-range contextual associations around targets through the designed wide-area perception module (WPM). Moreover, to adapt to the various applications or resource-constrained scenarios, six detectors of different scales (N/S/M/B/L/X) are designed. Experiments on two challenging aerial datasets (VisDrone and UAVDT) demonstrate the outstanding performance of SFFNet-X, achieving 36.8 AP and 20.6 AP, respectively. The lightweight models (N/S) also maintain a balance between detection accuracy and parameter efficiency. The code will be available at https://github.com/CQNU-ZhangLab/SFFNet.

AI.tldr: SFFNet combines dual-domain edge enhancement with a synergistic feature pyramid to tackle UAV object detection, achieving high AP on VisDrone and UAVDT and offering lightweight variants.
AI.motivation: To address background noise and scale imbalance in UAV imagery by exploiting multi-scale information and robust edge representations.
AI.method: MDDC module: dual-domain (frequency and spatial) edge extraction for decoupling multi-scale object edges from background; SFPN: linear deformable convolutions for irregular shapes; WPM for long-range context; six detectors at scales N/S/M/B/L/X; overall architecture SFFNet.
AI.result: SFFNet-X achieves 36.8 AP on VisDrone and 20.6 AP on UAVDT; lightweight N/S variants maintain accuracy-efficiency trade-off; code release planned.
AI.conclusion: SFFNet provides effective multi-scale edge-aware feature fusion for UAV detection, with strong empirical results and scalable variants.

title: The Eleventh NTIRE 2026 Efficient Super-Resolution Challenge Report
comment: CVPR 2026 NTIRE Workshop Paper, Efficient Super Resolution Technical Report
summary: This paper reviews the NTIRE 2026 challenge on efficient single-image super-resolution with a focus on the proposed solutions and results. The aim of this challenge is to devise a network that reduces one or several aspects, such as runtime, parameters, and FLOPs, while maintaining PSNR of around 26.90 dB on the DIV2K_LSDIR_valid dataset, and 26.99 dB on the DIV2K_LSDIR_test dataset. The challenge had 95 registered participants, and 15 teams made valid submissions. They gauge the state-of-the-art results for efficient single-image super-resolution.

AI.tldr: NTIRE 2026 Efficient SISR challenge benchmarks lightweight networks against PSNR targets on DIV2K LSDIR; 95 registered, 15 valid submissions; results define the current SOTA for efficiency.
AI.motivation: To advance single-image super-resolution under real-time and resource constraints by reducing runtime, parameters, and FLOPs while maintaining competitive PSNR.
AI.method: A comprehensive review and synthesis of proposed solutions and results from the NTIRE 2026 efficient SISR challenge, including the evaluation framework, participating teams, and dataset variants (DIV2K LSDIR valid/test).
AI.result: PSNR targets are around 26.90 dB on the valid set and 26.99 dB on the test set; 95 registered participants and 15 teams with valid submissions; establishes a state-of-the-art baseline for efficient SISR performance.
AI.conclusion: The challenge provides a rigorous benchmark for efficient SISR, highlighting progress toward lighter models without sacrificing PSNR and guiding future architectural innovations.

title: ProtoFlow: Mitigating Forgetting in Class-Incremental Remote Sensing Segmentation via Low-Curvature Prototype Flow
summary: Remote sensing segmentation in real deployment is inherently continual: new semantic categories emerge, and acquisition conditions shift across seasons, cities, and sensors. Despite recent progress, many incremental approaches still treat training steps as isolated updates, which leaves representation drift and forgetting insufficiently controlled. We present ProtoFlow, a time-aware prototype dynamics framework that models class prototypes as trajectories and learns their evolution with an explicit temporal vector field. By jointly enforcing low-curvature motion and inter-class separation, ProtoFlow stabilizes prototype geometry throughout incremental learning. Experiments on standard class- and domain-incremental remote sensing benchmarks show consistent gains over strong baselines, including up to 1.5-2.0 points improvement in mIoUall, together with reduced forgetting. These results suggest that explicitly modeling temporal prototype evolution is a practical and interpretable strategy for robust continual remote sensing segmentation.

AI.tldr: ProtoFlow treats class prototypes as time-evolving trajectories governed by a learned temporal vector field, enforcing low-curvature motion and inter-class separation to stabilize representations in continual remote sensing segmentation, yielding consistent gains and reduced forgetting.
AI.motivation: Continual remote sensing faces evolving class sets and shifting acquisition conditions across time, seasons, cities, and sensors. Standard incremental updates cause representation drift and forgetting; a temporal modeling of prototypes could maintain stable geometry and improve transfer across tasks.
AI.method: Represent class prototypes as trajectories in feature space. Learn an explicit temporal vector field that governs prototype evolution over time. Enforce two regularizers: (1) low-curvature motion to prevent abrupt prototype changes, and (2) inter-class separation to maintain discriminability. Train jointly to stabilize prototype geometry during incremental learning.
AI.result: On standard class- and domain-incremental remote sensing benchmarks, ProtoFlow achieves consistent gains over strong baselines, including 1.5–2.0 points improvement in mIoU_all, with reduced forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Explicit temporal prototype evolution is a practical and interpretable strategy for robust continual remote sensing segmentation, combining stability with discriminability.

title: VOSR: A Vision-Only Generative Model for Image Super-Resolution
summary: Most of the recent generative image super-resolution (SR) methods rely on adapting large text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models pretrained on web-scale text-image data. While effective, this paradigm starts from a generic T2I generator, despite that SR is fundamentally a low-resolution (LR) input-conditioned image restoration task. In this work, we investigate whether an SR model trained purely on visual data can rival T2I-based ones. To this end, we propose VOSR, a Vision-Only generative framework for SR. We first extract semantically rich and spatially grounded features from the LR input using a pretrained vision encoder as visual semantic guidance. We then revisit classifier-free guidance for training generative models and show that the standard unconditional branch is ill-suited to restoration models trained from scratch. We therefore replace it with a restoration-oriented guidance strategy that preserves weak LR anchors. Built upon these designs, we first train a multi-step VOSR model from scratch and then distill it into a one-step model for efficient inference. VOSR requires less than one-tenth of the training cost of representative T2I-based SR methods, yet in both multi-step and one-step settings, it achieves competitive or even better perceptual quality and efficiency, while producing more faithful structures with fewer hallucinations on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks. Our results, for the first time, show that high-quality generative SR can be achieved without multimodal pretraining. The code and models can be found at https://github.com/cswry/VOSR.

AI.tldr: Vision-Only SR (VOSR): trains a generative SR model from visual data without multimodal pretraining, using LR-guided semantic features and restoration-oriented guidance; achieves competitive quality with an order of magnitude lower training cost and distills to a fast one-step model.
AI.motivation: SR research often relies on large pre-trained text-to-image diffusion models pretrained on web-scale data. The work investigates whether a vision-only SR model trained from scratch on visual data can match or exceed T2I-based SR methods, reducing dependency on multimodal pretraining.
AI.method: Extract semantic, spatial features from the LR input via a pretrained vision encoder as guidance. Replace the standard unconditional classifier-free guidance with a restoration-oriented guidance that preserves LR anchors. Train a multi-step VOSR from scratch, then distill into a one-step model for faster inference.
AI.result: VOSR requires <10% of the training cost of representative T2I-based SR methods. It achieves competitive or better perceptual quality and efficiency in both multi-step and one-step forms, with more faithful structures and fewer hallucinations on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates for the first time that high-quality generative SR can be achieved without multimodal pretraining, using a vision-only approach. The approach reduces training cost substantially while maintaining or improving quality; code and models are released.

title: CoME-VL: Scaling Complementary Multi-Encoder Vision-Language Learning
summary: Recent vision-language models (VLMs) typically rely on a single vision encoder trained with contrastive image-text objectives, such as CLIP-style pretraining. While contrastive encoders are effective for cross-modal alignment and retrieval, self-supervised visual encoders often capture richer dense semantics and exhibit stronger robustness on recognition and understanding tasks. In this work, we investigate how to scale the fusion of these complementary visual representations for vision-language modeling. We propose CoME-VL: Complementary Multi-Encoder Vision-Language, a modular fusion framework that integrates a contrastively trained vision encoder with a self-supervised DINO encoder. Our approach performs representation-level fusion by (i) entropy-guided multi-layer aggregation with orthogonality-constrained projections to reduce redundancy, and (ii) RoPE-enhanced cross-attention to align heterogeneous token grids and produce compact fused visual tokens. The fused tokens can be injected into a decoder-only LLM with minimal changes to standard VLM pipelines. Extensive experiments across diverse vision-language benchmarks demonstrate that CoME-VL consistently outperforms single-encoder baselines. In particular, we observe an average improvement of 4.9% on visual understanding tasks and 5.4% on grounding tasks. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on RefCOCO for detection while improving over the baseline by a large margin. Finally, we conduct ablation studies on layer merging, non-redundant feature mixing, and fusion capacity to evaluate how complementary contrastive and self-supervised signals affect VLM performance.

AI.tldr: A multi-encoder fusion framework, CoME-VL, combines a contrastive vision encoder with a self-supervised DINO encoder to enhance vision-language modeling via representation-level fusion and RoPE-enhanced cross-attention, yielding consistent gains across benchmarks and state-of-the-art results on RefCOCO detection.
AI.motivation: Contrastive encoders excel at cross-modal alignment but self-supervised encoders capture denser semantics and robustness. A principled fusion of these complementary visual representations aims to leverage their strengths for improved VLM performance without overhauling standard pipelines.
AI.method: 1) Entropy-guided multi-layer aggregation with orthogonality-constrained projections to reduce redundancy across encoder outputs; 2) RoPE-enhanced cross-attention to align heterogeneous token grids and produce compact fused visual tokens; 3) inject fused tokens into a decoder-only LLM with minimal changes to standard VLM pipelines.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse vision-language benchmarks shows CoME-VL outperforms single-encoder baselines, with average improvements of 4.9% on visual understanding tasks and 5.4% on grounding tasks; achieves state-of-the-art on RefCOCO for detection; ablation studies highlight the importance of layer merging, non-redundant feature mixing, and fusion capacity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-encoder fusion approach effectively leverages complementary signals from contrastive and self-supervised visual representations, yielding consistent gains in VLM performance and establishing a new reference point for fusion-based vision-language models.
